topics.title: Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Interpretative Models in Biological Signal Processing** are formal mathematical, statistical, or computational structures used to describe, predict, and explain the underlying physiological phenomena that generate biomedical signals. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, these models form the bridge between raw physiological measurements (ECG, EEG, EMG, etc.) and clinically meaningful information by encoding the *generative mechanism* of the signal into a tractable analytical form.

An interpretative model typically consists of three components:

1. **Source Model (Physiological Generator):** Represents the underlying bioelectric, biomechanical, or biochemical activity (e.g., the cardiac dipole for ECG, neural current sources for EEG).
2. **Observation Model (Forward Model):** Describes how the source propagates through biological tissue, sensors, and noise to produce the recorded signal.
3. **Parameter Set:** A finite set of coefficients $(\theta = \{\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_p\})$ that adapt the generic model to a specific patient or signal epoch.

Mathematically, an interpretative model is represented as:

$$x(n) = f\bigl(s(n),\, h(n),\, \theta\bigr) + v(n)$$

where $x(n)$ is the observed biomedical signal, $s(n)$ is the latent source, $h(n)$ is the impulse response of the propagation medium, $\theta$ is the parameter vector, and $v(n)$ is additive observation noise (often assumed **white Gaussian noise** with variance $\sigma_v^2$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Interpretative models are *not* merely curve-fitting tools. They are **mechanistic representations** that incorporate the physics, biology, and statistics of the signal generation process, allowing both **prediction** (forward simulation) and **inference** (parameter estimation from data).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are listening to music playing inside a closed room. You cannot see the orchestra, but you hear the sound at the door. The *interpretative model* is your mental framework that says:

- **What** is producing the sound (the orchestra = the source model ŌĆö say, "string section + percussion").
- **How** the sound reaches your ear (the walls, the air, the door = the propagation/observation model).
- **How loud** the music would be if the door were opened (the inverse model ŌĆö back-calculating the source from the door's recording).

In the same way, an interpretative model for an ECG signal says: *"The heart is an electrical dipole rotating in 3D space; this dipole generates potentials that travel through the thorax ŌĆö a volume conductor ŌĆö and are recorded as voltage differences on the skin."* The model parameters (dipole location, orientation, magnitude) are then estimated from the recorded ECG.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> - A **model** is a *story* told in mathematics.
> - **Interpretation** means the story has a physiological meaning ŌĆö every parameter corresponds to something real (heart rate, neural firing rate, muscle fibre conduction velocity).
> - Without interpretative models, signal processing is *blind* filtering; with them, it becomes *diagnostic intelligence*.

## 1.3 Why Interpretative Models Matter in Biomedical Engineering

| Reason | Description |
|---|---|
| **Noise Robustness** | Model-based filtering (e.g., Wiener, Kalman) outperforms generic filters when noise statistics are unknown. |
| **Feature Extraction** | Parameters $\theta$ directly yield diagnostic features (e.g., AR coefficients ŌåÆ spectral peak of EEG rhythms). |
| **Signal Compression** | A parametric model compresses long signals into a few parameters with controllable fidelity. |
| **Simulation & Training** | Models generate realistic synthetic signals for testing algorithms and training clinicians. |
| **Inverse Problem Solving** | Enables source localization (EEG/MEG), arrhythmia classification, and seizure detection. |

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

The most commonly used metrics in the KTU 2024 evaluation of interpretative models are:

- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x(n) - \hat{x}(n)\bigr)^2$
- **Percent Root Mean Square Difference (PRD):** $\text{PRD} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x-\hat{x})^2}{\sum x^2}} \times 100\%$
- **Model Order Selection:** **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)** and **Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constants & Ranges (Bold for Recall):**
> - **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** ECG $\approx 250$ŌĆō$500$ Hz, EEG $\approx 100$ŌĆō$1000$ Hz, EMG $\approx 1000$ŌĆō$2000$ Hz.
> - **Model Order ($p$):** Typically $4$ŌĆō$12$ for ECG, $6$ŌĆō$20$ for EEG.
> - **Noise Variance ($\sigma_v^2$):** Usually $10$ŌĆō$40$ dB below signal power for clean clinical recordings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Interpretative Models

Interpretative models in biological signal processing are classified along several orthogonal axes. Mastering this taxonomy is critical for KTU Part A questions.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Determinism

- **Deterministic Models:** The output is fully determined by the input and parameters. Used when the source mechanism is well-known (e.g., dipole model for ECG).
$$x(n) = f(s(n), \theta)$$

- **Stochastic Models:** The output is treated as a realization of a random process. Used when underlying variability is intrinsic (e.g., heart rate variability, spontaneous EEG).
$$x(n) = H(z)\, w(n), \quad w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$$

### 2.1.2 Classification by Linearity

- **Linear Models:** Superposition holds. Examples: **AR, MA, ARMA** models.
- **Nonlinear Models:** Capture complex dynamics. Examples: **Volterra series, Bilinear models, Neural Network models, HMMs**.

### 2.1.3 Classification by Parameterization

- **Parametric (White-Box):** Fixed structure, finite parameters $\theta$. Examples: AR($p$), State-Space.
- **Non-Parametric (Black-Box):** Structure not fixed; parameters grow with data. Examples: Histograms, Kernel density estimates.
- **Semi-Parametric (Grey-Box):** Hybrid ŌĆö partial physiological knowledge + learned components.

### 2.1.4 Classification by Domain

- **Time-Domain Models:** AR, ARMA ŌĆö capture temporal evolution.
- **Frequency-Domain Models:** Spectral models ŌĆö capture rhythmic content.
- **Time-Frequency Models:** Wavelet-based, Cohen-class distributions.
- **State-Space Models:** Capture hidden dynamics via state variables.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Biomedical Signal Modeling

### Pillar 1: The Source Model (Generative Physiology)

A **source model** characterizes the bioelectric generator. The three canonical source models are:

| Source Type | Mathematical Form | Biomedical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Dipole** | $\vec{p} = I \cdot \vec{d}$ | ECG, MEG, EEG evoked potentials |
| **Multipole Expansion** | $\vec{p} + \vec{Q} + \ldots$ | High-resolution EEG source localization |
| **Distributed Source** | $\vec{J}(r, t)$ over a volume | Cortical imaging, inverse EEG/MEG |
| **Moving Dipole** | $\vec{p}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t), \theta(t), \phi(t))$ | Dynamic cardiac mapping |

### Pillar 2: The Volume Conductor (Propagation Model)

The propagation medium (head, thorax, limb) is modeled using **Maxwell's equations** simplified under quasi-static assumptions:

$$\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$$

For a homogeneous, isotropic medium, the forward solution (potential at electrode $i$ from dipole $\vec{p}$ at location $\vec{r}_0$) is:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}_i) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$$

This linear relationship allows the **Lead Field Matrix** $\mathbf{L}$ to be precomputed:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L} \cdot \vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$$

### Pillar 3: The Observation / Measurement Model

Adds the effects of sensors, amplifiers, quantization, and noise:

$$x(n) = G \cdot \Phi(n) + v(n)$$

where $G$ is the sensor gain and $v(n)$ captures all noise sources (thermal, motion artifact, mains interference).

## 2.3 The Linear Parametric Model Family (AR, MA, ARMA, ARMAX)

This is the **most heavily tested family** in KTU examinations.

### 2.3.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

where $w(n)$ is white noise with variance $\sigma_w^2$, and $a_k$ are AR coefficients. The transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

**Use case in biomedicine:** Modeling the spectrum of EEG rhythms, heart rate variability, and vocal-tract-like analysis of PCG.

### 2.3.2 Moving Average (MA) Model

$$x(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k\, w(n-k)$$

### 2.3.3 ARMA Model (Combined)

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k\, w(n-k)$$

### 2.3.4 ARMAX Model (with exogenous input)

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k\, w(n-k) + \sum_{k=1}^{r} c_k\, u(n-k)$$

Used when an external stimulus $u(n)$ drives the system (e.g., evoked potentials).

### 2.3.5 YuleŌĆōWalker Equations (Critical for AR Estimation)

The autocorrelation $R_{xx}(k) = E[x(n) x(n-k)]$ satisfies:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{xx}(0) &= -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(k) + \sigma_w^2 \\
R_{xx}(m) &= -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(m-k), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, p
\end{aligned}$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ R_{xx}(p)
\end{bmatrix}
= -
\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_{xx}(p-1) & R_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}$$

The PSD of an AR($p$) process is:

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$$

## 2.4 State-Space Models (Modern Powerhouse)

A discrete-time **linear Gaussian state-space model** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{s}(n+1) &= \mathbf{A}\, \mathbf{s}(n) + \mathbf{B}\, u(n) + \mathbf{w}(n) \\
\mathbf{x}(n) &= \mathbf{C}\, \mathbf{s}(n) + \mathbf{D}\, u(n) + \mathbf{v}(n)
\end{aligned}$$

- $\mathbf{s}(n) \in \mathbb{R}^{n_s}$: hidden state vector
- $\mathbf{A}$: state transition matrix
- $\mathbf{C}$: observation matrix
- $\mathbf{w}(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{Q})$, $\mathbf{v}(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$

The **Kalman Filter** recursively estimates $\hat{\mathbf{s}}(n \mid n)$ using:

$$\hat{\mathbf{s}}(n \mid n) = \hat{\mathbf{s}}(n \mid n-1) + \mathbf{K}(n) \left[\mathbf{x}(n) - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{s}}(n \mid n-1)\right]$$

with Kalman gain:

$$\mathbf{K}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n \mid n-1) \mathbf{C}^T \left[\mathbf{C} \mathbf{P}(n \mid n-1) \mathbf{C}^T + \mathbf{R}\right]^{-1}$$

## 2.5 Bayesian Framework for Model Interpretation

The Bayesian paradigm provides the most principled approach to interpretative modeling. The goal is to compute the **posterior distribution** over parameters:

$$p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

- $p(\theta)$: **prior** (encodes physiological knowledge).
- $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$: **likelihood** (how well the model explains the data).
- $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$: **posterior** (updated belief after observing data).
- **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimate:** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \arg\max_\theta \, p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x})$
- **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE):** $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bayesian?** It allows incorporation of *a priori* physiological knowledge (e.g., normal heart rate range 60ŌĆō100 bpm) into the model, regularizing the estimation problem and improving robustness to noise.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ | AR($p$) model | Discrete-time |
| 2 | $H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | AR transfer function | Z-domain |
| 3 | $P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j\omega k} \vert^2}$ | AR Power Spectral Density | W/Hz |
| 4 | $\mathbf{R}_{xx}\, \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_{xx}$ | YuleŌĆōWalker normal equations | Correlation matrix |
| 5 | $\Phi(\vec{r}_i) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$ | Dipole forward model | Volts |
| 6 | $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L}\, \vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$ | Lead field observation | $\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 3}$ |
| 7 | $\text{AIC} = 2k - 2\ln(\hat{L})$ | Model order selection | Akaike |
| 8 | $\text{BIC} = k \ln(N) - 2\ln(\hat{L})$ | Bayesian model order | $N$ = samples |
| 9 | $\mathbf{K}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n \mid n-1) \mathbf{C}^T [\mathbf{C}\mathbf{P}\mathbf{C}^T + \mathbf{R}]^{-1}$ | Kalman gain | Filter |
| 10 | $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$ | Bayes' rule | Posterior |
| 11 | $\nabla \cdot (\sigma \nabla \Phi) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J}_s$ | Quasi-static volume conductor | Field eqn. |
| 12 | $\text{PRD} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x - \hat{x})^2}{\sum x^2}} \times 100\%$ | Compression fidelity | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin ECG compression standards, EEG-driven brain-computer interfaces, seizure prediction algorithms, and EMG-based prosthetic control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the central theoretical results in full detail. **No step is omitted** ŌĆö every algebraic transition is justified for KTU 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Derivation: YuleŌĆōWalker Equations for AR($p$) Model

### Starting Point
The AR($p$) model:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

with $w(n) \sim \mathcal{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ (white noise), and $E[w(n) w(m)] = \sigma_w^2 \delta(n-m)$.

### Step 1: Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation

$$E[x(n) x(n-m)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k) x(n-m)] + E[w(n) x(n-m)]$$

By definition, $E[x(n) x(n-m)] = R_{xx}(m)$. Thus:

$$R_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(m-k) + E[w(n) x(n-m)]$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the cross-correlation $E[w(n) x(n-m)]$

Since $x(n-m)$ depends on $w(n-m), w(n-m-1), \ldots, w(n-m-p+1)$, and $w(n)$ is white noise:

- If $m > 0$: $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with $x(n-m)$, so $E[w(n) x(n-m)] = 0$.
- If $m = 0$: $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$, so $E[w(n) x(n)] = E[w(n)\{ -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)\}] = \sigma_w^2$ (since $E[w(n) x(n-k)] = 0$ for $k \geq 1$).
- If $m < 0$: By stationarity, $R_{xx}(-m) = R_{xx}(m)$, so the same analysis applies via symmetry.

Therefore:

$$E[w(n) x(n-m)] = \begin{cases} \sigma_w^2, & m = 0 \\ 0, & m \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Write the YuleŌĆōWalker equations

For $m = 0$:

$$R_{xx}(0) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(k) + \sigma_w^2$$

For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$R_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(m-k)$$

### Step 4: Assemble into matrix form

Using the autocorrelation symmetry $R_{xx}(-k) = R_{xx}(k)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(1) \\
R_{xx}(2) \\
\vdots \\
R_{xx}(p)
\end{bmatrix}
= -\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R_{xx}(p-1) & R_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}$$

This is a **Toeplitz system** $\mathbf{r} = -\mathbf{R}\, \mathbf{a}$, solvable in $O(p^2)$ by the **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin algorithm**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Spectral Density of AR($p$) Process

### Step 1: Take the Z-transform of the AR equation

$$X(z) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} X(z) + W(z)$$

$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} \right] = W(z)$$

$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{W(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

### Step 2: Apply the WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem

For a stationary process, the PSD is the DTFT of the autocorrelation:

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \left\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \right\vert^2 P_{ww}(\omega)$$

Since $w(n)$ is white noise, $P_{ww}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2$ (constant).

### Step 3: Substitute to obtain the AR PSD

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Peaks in $P_{xx}(\omega)$ occur at the roots of the denominator (the *poles* of $H(z)$). These peaks correspond to **dominant rhythms** ŌĆö e.g., the alpha rhythm at $\approx 10$ Hz in EEG, the QRS complex at $\approx 15$ Hz in ECG.

## 3.3 Derivation: Forward Model for a Current Dipole in Homogeneous Volume Conductor

### Step 1: Quasi-static assumption

For frequencies below $\approx 1$ MHz, biological tissue behaves as a **resistive** medium. The quasi-static approximation yields:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0, \quad \vec{E} = -\nabla \Phi, \quad \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

which combine to Laplace's equation: $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$ in source-free regions.

### Step 2: Solve Laplace's equation for a point current dipole

A current dipole $\vec{p} = I \vec{d}$ at origin creates a current density $\vec{J} = \vec{p}\, \delta(\vec{r})$. The potential is the well-known solution:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{4\pi \sigma r^2} = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r} \vert^3}$$

where $\hat{r} = \vec{r}/\vert \vec{r} \vert$ and $r = \vert \vec{r} \vert$.

### Step 3: Translate to dipole at $\vec{r}_0$ and electrode at $\vec{r}_i$

By translation invariance of Laplace's equation, replace $\vec{r} \to \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0$:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}_i) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$$

### Step 4: Vectorize for multiple electrodes

For $M$ electrodes, define the **Lead Field Matrix** $\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 3}$ whose $i$-th row is:

$$\mathbf{L}_i = \frac{(\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)^T}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$$

Then the measurement vector is:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L}\, \vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$$

where $\mathbf{v}$ is the $M \times 1$ noise vector.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is $\mathbf{L}$ important?** It is the *forward operator* mapping the invisible 3D dipole to the visible multi-channel recording. The **inverse problem** in EEG/MEG is to estimate $\vec{p}$ from $\mathbf{x}$ via $\hat{\vec{p}} = \mathbf{L}^{+} \mathbf{x}$ (using pseudoinverse or regularized inversion).

## 3.4 Derivation: MAP Estimate for a Linear Model with Gaussian Prior

### Setup
Linear observation model:
$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{H}\, \theta + \mathbf{v}, \quad \mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_v)$$

Gaussian prior on parameters:
$$\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_0, \mathbf{C}_\theta)$$

### Step 1: Likelihood

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{N/2} \vert \mathbf{R}_v \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta)^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta)\right]$$

### Step 2: Prior

$$p(\theta) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} \vert \mathbf{C}_\theta \vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(\theta - \theta_0)^T \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} (\theta - \theta_0)\right]$$

### Step 3: Posterior (Bayes' theorem)

$$p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$$

Taking negative log and dropping constants:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta)^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta) + \frac{1}{2}(\theta - \theta_0)^T \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}(\theta - \theta_0)$$

### Step 4: Minimize $J(\theta)$ by setting $\nabla_\theta J = 0$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta) + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}(\theta - \theta_0) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\left[\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{H} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}\right] \theta = \mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \theta_0$$

### Step 5: Closed-form MAP estimate

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \left[\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{H} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}\right]^{-1} \left[\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \theta_0\right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $\mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \to \mathbf{0}$ (no prior information), the MAP estimate reduces to the **weighted least-squares** solution. When $\mathbf{R}_v \to \mathbf{0}$ (noiseless data), $\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} \to \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{x}$ (the pure algebraic inverse).

## 3.5 Model Order Selection ŌĆö AIC and BIC Derivations

### Akaike Information Criterion
Derived from the KullbackŌĆōLeibler divergence between the true and fitted models:

$$\text{AIC} = -2 \ln(\hat{L}) + 2k$$

where $\hat{L}$ is the maximized likelihood and $k$ is the number of free parameters.

### Bayesian Information Criterion
Derived as an approximation to the marginal likelihood under a Laplace approximation:

$$\text{BIC} = -2 \ln(\hat{L}) + k \ln(N)$$

where $N$ is the sample size.

**Practical rule:** BIC penalizes model complexity more strongly than AIC for $N \geq 8$. KTU examiners often expect students to compute AIC for a small AR model and select the optimal order.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Given the autocorrelation sequence $R_{xx}(0) = 1.0$, $R_{xx}(1) = 0.6$, $R_{xx}(2) = 0.3$, estimate the parameters of an AR(2) model.

### Solution

**Step 1: Set up the YuleŌĆōWalker matrix equation** (for $m = 1, 2$):

$$\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2)
\end{bmatrix}
= -\begin{bmatrix}
R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) \\
R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2
\end{bmatrix}$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
0.6 \\ 0.3
\end{bmatrix}
= -\begin{bmatrix}
1.0 & 0.6 \\
0.6 & 1.0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Solve via matrix inversion**

The inverse of $\begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$ has determinant $1.0 - 0.36 = 0.64$:

$$\begin{bmatrix}
1.0 & 0.6 \\
0.6 & 1.0
\end{bmatrix}^{-1}
= \frac{1}{0.64}\begin{bmatrix}
1.0 & -0.6 \\
-0.6 & 1.0
\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}
1.5625 & -0.9375 \\
-0.9375 & 1.5625
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute the coefficient vector**

$$\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} 1.5625 & -0.9375 \\ -0.9375 & 1.5625 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.3 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} 1.5625 \cdot 0.6 - 0.9375 \cdot 0.3 \\ -0.9375 \cdot 0.6 + 1.5625 \cdot 0.3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute each:

- Top: $0.9375 - 0.28125 = 0.65625$
- Bottom: $-0.5625 + 0.46875 = -0.09375$

So:

$$\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} 0.65625 \\ -0.09375 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.65625 \\ 0.09375 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the driving noise variance** using $m = 0$:

$$\sigma_w^2 = R_{xx}(0) + a_1 R_{xx}(1) + a_2 R_{xx}(2)$$

$$\sigma_w^2 = 1.0 + (-0.65625)(0.6) + (0.09375)(0.3) = 1.0 - 0.39375 + 0.028125 = 0.634375$$

**Step 5: Verify the model is stable**

Poles of $H(z)$ are roots of $1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} = 0$, i.e., $z^2 - 0.65625 z + 0.09375 = 0$. Discriminant: $0.4307 - 0.375 = 0.0557 > 0$, so two real roots: $z_1 \approx 0.567$, $z_2 \approx 0.089$. Both inside unit circle $\Rightarrow$ stable. Ō£ō

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define an interpretative model in biomedical signal processing. Distinguish between parametric and non-parametric models with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interpretative model** in biomedical signal processing is a formal mathematical or statistical representation of the physiological mechanism that generates a biosignal, with parameters that have direct physiological meaning.

| Aspect | Parametric | Non-Parametric |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Fixed functional form | Data-driven, no fixed form |
| Parameters | Finite, fixed number | Grow with sample size |
| Example | AR($p$) model for EEG spectrum | Histogram estimate of HRV distribution |
| Interpretability | High (each $\theta_i$ meaningful) | Low |

> **Mark Allocation (KTU Standard):** Definition ŌĆö 1 mark; parametric features ŌĆö 1 mark; non-parametric features + example ŌĆö 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`
**What is the role of the lead field matrix in the forward model of EEG? Write the observation equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **lead field matrix** $\mathbf{L} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 3}$ maps the 3D neural current dipole $\vec{p}$ to the $M$-channel scalp potential measurements. It is computed from the geometry and conductivity of the head model (scalp, skull, brain) using:

$$\mathbf{L}_i = \frac{(\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)^T}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$$

The observation equation is:

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L}\, \vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$$

> **Mark Allocation:** Definition of $\mathbf{L}$ ŌĆö 1 mark; forward formula ŌĆö 1 mark; observation equation ŌĆö 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each, OR one design problem of 14 marks. Students answer **one** of two alternatives.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **YuleŌĆōWalker equations** for an AR($p$) model and explain how they are used to estimate the model coefficients from the autocorrelation sequence. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given $R_{xx}(0) = 4$, $R_{xx}(1) = 3$, $R_{xx}(2) = 2$, $R_{xx}(3) = 1$, estimate the parameters of an **AR(3)** model and verify its stability. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1: Write the AR($p$) model equation.**

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n), \quad w(n) \sim \mathcal{WN}(0, \sigma_w^2)$$

**Step 2: Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation.**

$$E[x(n) x(n-m)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k) x(n-m)] + E[w(n) x(n-m)]$$

Recognising the autocorrelation $R_{xx}(m) = E[x(n)x(n-m)]$:

$$R_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(m-k) + E[w(n) x(n-m)]$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the cross-term.**

- For $m = 0$: $E[w(n) x(n)] = \sigma_w^2$
- For $m \neq 0$: $E[w(n) x(n-m)] = 0$ (whiteness assumption)

**Step 4: Write the final YuleŌĆōWalker equations.**

$$R_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R_{xx}(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m), \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, p$$

**Step 5: Express in matrix form for $m = 1, \ldots, p$.**

$$\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R_{xx}(1) \\ R_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ R_{xx}(p) \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{r}} = -\underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} R_{xx}(0) & R_{xx}(1) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-1) \\ R_{xx}(1) & R_{xx}(0) & \cdots & R_{xx}(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ R_{xx}(p-1) & R_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & R_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{R}} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{a}}$$

**Step 6: Coefficient estimation.**

Since $\mathbf{R}$ is a **symmetric positive-definite Toeplitz matrix**, the solution is:

$$\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{r}$$

In practice, the **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin recursion** is used for $O(p^2)$ computation, also yielding $\sigma_w^2$ as a by-product.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö Common Mistakes:**
> - Forgetting the negative sign in $\mathbf{r} = -\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a}$. (Lose 1 mark)
> - Not justifying why $E[w(n) x(n-m)] = 0$ for $m \neq 0$. (Lose 1 mark)
> - Writing the equations only for $m = 0$ and omitting the general $m = 1, \ldots, p$ case. (Lose 2 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Given:** $R_{xx}(0) = 4$, $R_{xx}(1) = 3$, $R_{xx}(2) = 2$, $R_{xx}(3) = 1$.

**Step 1: Set up the YuleŌĆōWalker matrix system for AR(3), $m = 1, 2, 3$.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Solve the Toeplitz system using the LevinsonŌĆōDurbin recursion (or direct inversion).**

For brevity, use the **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin algorithm**:

- $a_{1,1} = -R_{xx}(1)/R_{xx}(0) = -3/4 = -0.75$
- $\Delta_2 = R_{xx}(2) + a_{1,1} R_{xx}(1) = 2 + (-0.75)(3) = 2 - 2.25 = -0.25$
- $a_{2,2} = -\Delta_2 / (R_{xx}(0) - a_{1,1} R_{xx}(1)) = -(-0.25)/(4 - (-0.75)(3)) = 0.25/(4 + 2.25) = 0.25/6.25 = 0.04$
- $a_{2,1} = a_{1,1} + a_{2,2} a_{1,1} = -0.75 + (0.04)(-0.75) = -0.75 - 0.03 = -0.78$
- $\Delta_3 = R_{xx}(3) + a_{2,1} R_{xx}(2) + a_{2,2} R_{xx}(1) = 1 + (-0.78)(2) + (0.04)(3) = 1 - 1.56 + 0.12 = -0.44$
- Denominator: $R_{xx}(0) - a_{2,1} R_{xx}(1) - a_{2,2} R_{xx}(2) = 4 - (-0.78)(3) - (0.04)(2) = 4 + 2.34 - 0.08 = 6.26$
- $a_{3,3} = -(-0.44)/6.26 = 0.0703$
- $a_{3,1} = a_{2,1} + a_{3,3} a_{2,2} = -0.78 + (0.0703)(0.04) = -0.78 + 0.00281 = -0.7772$
- $a_{3,2} = a_{2,2} + a_{3,3} a_{2,1} = 0.04 + (0.0703)(-0.78) = 0.04 - 0.0548 = -0.0148$

So $\mathbf{a} = [-0.7772,\ -0.0148,\ 0.0703]^T$.

**Step 3: Stability verification.**

The characteristic polynomial is:

$$1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + a_3 z^{-3} = 1 - 0.7772 z^{-1} - 0.0148 z^{-2} + 0.0703 z^{-3}$$

Multiply by $z^3$:

$$z^3 - 0.7772 z^2 - 0.0148 z + 0.0703 = 0$$

Using numerical root finding (or Jury test), all roots have magnitude $< 1$, confirming **stability**. The model is minimum-phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Students often forget to verify stability. KTU examiners award up to 2 marks for this verification.
> - Sign errors in the recursion (forgetting the negative sign) lead to a 0.5ŌĆō1 mark deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **forward model** for a current dipole in a homogeneous volume conductor. Derive the potential at an arbitrary electrode. (7 marks)

**(b)** Develop the **Bayesian MAP estimation** framework for a linear observation model with Gaussian prior. Discuss its advantages over the MLE approach for biomedical applications. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Step 1: State the quasi-static assumption.**

For biological media at signal frequencies below $\approx 1$ MHz, the medium is treated as purely resistive. Maxwell's equations reduce to:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = 0, \quad \vec{E} = -\nabla \Phi, \quad \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

These combine to Laplace's equation: $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$ in source-free regions.

**Step 2: Define a current dipole.**

A current dipole $\vec{p} = I \vec{d}$ at the origin represents a point source of current. The current density is:

$$\vec{J}_s(\vec{r}) = \vec{p}\, \delta(\vec{r})$$

**Step 3: Solve Laplace's equation with a point source.**

The Green's function for Laplace's equation in unbounded 3D is $G(\vec{r}) = 1/(4\pi \vert \vec{r} \vert)$. The potential is:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{4\pi \sigma} \int \vec{J}_s \cdot \nabla G\, dV$$

Evaluating at the point dipole:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{4\pi \sigma r^2} = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r} \vert^3}$$

**Step 4: Translate to electrode position $\vec{r}_i$ and dipole position $\vec{r}_0$.**

By translation invariance:

$$\Phi(\vec{r}_i) = \frac{\vec{p} \cdot (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0)}{4\pi \sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3}$$

**Step 5: Generalize to multiple electrodes via Lead Field Matrix.**

For $M$ electrodes, $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L} \vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$, where the $i$-th row of $\mathbf{L}$ is the gradient above.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write the formula for the potential of a *point charge* ($1/r$) instead of a *dipole* ($1/r^2$). The dipole potential decays as $1/r^2$, while the field decays as $1/r^3$. Examiners check this carefully.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Step 1: Define the linear observation model.**

$$\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{H}\theta + \mathbf{v}, \quad \mathbf{v} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_v)$$

**Step 2: Specify the Gaussian prior on parameters.**

$$\theta \sim \mathcal{N}(\theta_0, \mathbf{C}_\theta)$$

This encodes physiological knowledge (e.g., normal heart rate range, expected dipole location within the brain).

**Step 3: Form the posterior via Bayes' rule.**

$$p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)}{p(\mathbf{x})} \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$$

**Step 4: Take negative log and differentiate.**

The cost function:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{H}\theta \Vert_{\mathbf{R}_v^{-1}}^2 + \frac{1}{2}\Vert \theta - \theta_0 \Vert_{\mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}}^2$$

Setting $\nabla_\theta J = 0$ yields:

$$\hat{\theta}_{\text{MAP}} = \left[\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{H} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1}\right]^{-1} \left[\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{R}_v^{-1} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \theta_0\right]$$

**Step 5: Comparison with MLE.**

| Aspect | MLE | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Uses prior | No | Yes |
| Robust to noise | Lower | Higher (regularized) |
| Physiological plausibility | Not guaranteed | Enforced via prior |
| Computational cost | Lower | Slightly higher (matrix add) |
| Sparse data | May overfit | Generalizes better |

**Step 6: Biomedical advantages.**

- In EEG source localization, the prior restricts solutions to physiologically plausible cortical locations.
- In ECG arrhythmia classification, priors encode known normal rhythm statistics.
- The MAP estimate equals MLE only when $\mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \to \mathbf{0}$ (vague prior).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Confusing MAP with MLE ŌĆö the presence of $\mathbf{C}_\theta^{-1} \theta_0$ in the numerator is the *signature* of the prior. Omit it and lose 2 marks.
> - Failing to mention the role of the prior in regularizing ill-posed inverse problems (e.g., EEG/MEG).
> - Forgetting the matrix dimensions when writing the final formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 ŌĆö Interpretative Models)**

- [x] An **interpretative model** combines a *source model* (physiology) + *forward model* (propagation) + *observation model* (sensors + noise).
- [x] **Three pillars**: Source, Volume Conductor, Observation ŌĆö must all be addressed in any KTU answer.
- [x] **AR($p$) model**: $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$; estimated via **YuleŌĆōWalker** equations.
- [x] **YuleŌĆōWalker** matrix form: $\mathbf{R} \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$; solved by **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin** in $O(p^2)$.
- [x] **AR PSD**: $P_{xx}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j\omega k} \vert^2$ ŌĆö peaks indicate dominant rhythms.
- [x] **Forward model (dipole)**: $\Phi(\vec{r}_i) = \vec{p} \cdot (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0) / (4\pi\sigma \vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_0 \vert^3)$ ŌĆö note $1/r^2$ decay.
- [x] **Lead field matrix** $\mathbf{L}$: maps 3D dipole to multi-channel recordings; $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{L}\vec{p} + \mathbf{v}$.
- [x] **State-space models**: $\{\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{R}\}$; recursively estimated by **Kalman filter**.
- [x] **Bayes' theorem**: $p(\theta \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto p(\mathbf{x} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta)$; MAP = regularized MLE.
- [x] **AIC** = $-2\ln \hat{L} + 2k$; **BIC** = $-2\ln \hat{L} + k \ln N$ ŌĆö used for model order selection.
- [x] **Stability criterion**: All poles of $H(z)$ must lie **inside the unit circle** in the $z$-plane.
- [x] **Typical model orders**: ECG AR(4)ŌĆōAR(12); EEG AR(6)ŌĆōAR(20); EMG AR(4)ŌĆōAR(8).
- [x] **PRD** is the standard compression-fidelity metric; $< 5\%$ is clinically acceptable.
- [x] **Always** state assumptions: stationarity, whiteness of $w(n)$, linearity, volume conductor type.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Candidates:** The single most common reason for losing marks in this module is **vague definitions**. Always tie every mathematical object to its physiological counterpart. Examiners reward *interpretability* ŌĆö that is the entire theme of Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Scheme ŌĆō PEBRT865, Module 1)**
> The topic **"Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing"** introduces the essential mathematical apparatus ŌĆö *Linear Algebra, Probability, Stochastic Processes, and Transform Theory* ŌĆö that underpins every operation performed on biomedical signals such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG.

### 1.1 What are "Mathematical Instruments" in Signal Processing?

In the context of **Advanced Biomedical Signal Processing (ABSP)**, *mathematical instruments* refer to the formal collection of analytical tools, structures, and operators used to **represent, transform, analyze, and interpret** physiological signals.

A biomedical signal $x(t)$ (for continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (for discrete-time) is rarely analyzed in its raw form. Instead, it is mapped into mathematical spaces where features (such as QRS-complex timing, EEG rhythms, or EMG bursts) become discernible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 terminology)**
> A *mathematical instrument* is a formally defined operator, transformation, or algebraic structure ŌĆö such as a linear map, a probability measure, a stochastic descriptor, or an integral transform ŌĆö that enables the rigorous manipulation of signals in a Hilbert or Banach space.

### 1.2 The Five Pillars of Mathematical Instruments

| # | Instrument | One-line Purpose |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Linear Algebra** | Representation of signals as vectors and their projections onto basis spaces. |
| 2 | **Probability Theory** | Quantification of randomness inherent in biomedical recordings. |
| 3 | **Stochastic Processes** | Modeling signals whose behaviour evolves with statistical regularity. |
| 4 | **Transform Theory** (Fourier / Z / Hilbert) | Decomposition of signals into canonical basis functions. |
| 5 | **Statistical Signal Descriptors** | Mean, variance, autocorrelation, PSD for characterization. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Surgeon's Toolkit"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a cardiologist examining an ECG trace. The raw trace is a wiggly line on paper. To extract diagnostic meaning, the cardiologist uses:
> - A *ruler* (linear measurements) ŌåÆ **Linear Algebra**
> - A *stethoscope's sense of "normal vs. abnormal rhythm"* ŌåÆ **Probability & Statistics**
> - A *memory of how a normal beat evolves* ŌåÆ **Stochastic Processes**
> - A *prism splitting white light into colors* ŌåÆ **Fourier Transform**
> - A *baseline-removal tool* ŌåÆ **Hilbert Transform / Detrending**
>
> Mathematical instruments are these "clinical tools" ŌĆö but in their mathematically rigorous, generalized form.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics are **frequently referenced** in biomedical signal processing and are **bolded** in KTU exam solutions:

- **Sampling Frequency $f_s$** ŌĆö typically **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** for ECG; **100ŌĆō500 Hz** for EEG; **Ōēź 1 kHz** for EMG.
- **Standard Reference Potentials:** ECG calibration pulse = **1 mV, 200 ms**.
- **Spectral Bands of EEG:** Delta (0.5ŌĆō4 Hz), Theta (4ŌĆō8 Hz), Alpha (8ŌĆō13 Hz), Beta (13ŌĆō30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- **Nyquist Limit:** $f_{\text{Nyq}} = f_s / 2$.
- **Universally used symbols:** $E[\cdot]$ for expectation, $\text{Var}(\cdot)$ for variance, $\sigma^2$ for power, $R_{xx}[\tau]$ for autocorrelation.

### 1.5 Why this Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 1 lays the **axiomatic foundation** for all subsequent modules (filter design, spectral analysis, wavelet analysis, adaptive filtering, and biomedical applications). Every derivations, proofs, and algorithms in later modules are *applications* of these instruments. Examiners routinely test:
> - Definitions of stationarity, ergodicity, autocorrelation.
> - Eigen decomposition of biomedical covariance matrices (PCA for ECG feature extraction).
> - Power Spectral Density (PSD) derivations.
> - Hilbert transform properties for envelope extraction.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section builds the **theoretical backbone** of the topic, going one level deeper than the intuitive overview.

### 2.1 Linear Algebraic Foundations

A discrete-time biomedical signal of length $N$ can be represented as a **column vector** in $\mathbb{R}^N$ (or $\mathbb{C}^N$ if complex):

$$
x = \begin{bmatrix} x[0] \\ x[1] \\ \vdots \\ x[N-1] \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{C}^{N}
$$

**Key Constructs:**

- **Inner Product** (engineered for *similarity* between two signals):
$$
\langle x, y \rangle = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, y^{*}[n] = x^{H} y
$$
where $H$ denotes Hermitian (conjugate transpose). The inner product defines a **Hilbert space** ŌĆö the arena of signal processing.

- **Norm** (signal "energy" or "magnitude"):
$$
\|x\| = \sqrt{\langle x, x \rangle} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^{2}}
$$

- **Eigen Decomposition** of the autocorrelation matrix $R_{xx} \in \mathbb{C}^{N \times N}$:
$$
R_{xx} = U \Lambda U^{H}
$$
where $U$ contains orthonormal eigenvectors and $\Lambda$ contains eigenvalues. This is the *mathematical heart of PCA* ŌĆö used for **ECG beat classification** and **EEG channel reduction**.

### 2.2 Probability & Random Variables

A continuous random variable $X$ representing a noisy ECG sample is characterized by:

- **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**
$$
F_X(x) = P(X \le x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f_X(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

- **Probability Density Function (PDF):**
$$
f_X(x) = \frac{dF_X(x)}{dx}
$$

- **Expectation (Mean):**
$$
E[X] = \mu_X = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x \, f_X(x) \, dx
$$

- **Variance:**
$$
\text{Var}(X) = \sigma_X^{2} = E\!\left[(X - \mu_X)^{2}\right] = E[X^{2}] - \mu_X^{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Insight:** Additive sensor noise on ECG is well modeled as **AWGN** ŌĆö *Additive White Gaussian Noise* with zero mean and variance $\sigma^{2}$.

### 2.3 Stochastic Processes & Stationarity

A stochastic process $\{X(t), t \in T\}$ is a *family of random variables indexed by time*. For biomedical signals, $X(t)$ could be the instantaneous amplitude of an EMG signal.

**Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS):** All statistical moments are time-invariant.

**Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS):** Only the first two moments are time-invariant:
$$
E[X(t)] = \mu \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

$$
E[X(t) X(t+\tau)] = R_{XX}(\tau) \quad \text{(depends only on lag } \tau\text{)}
$$

**Ergodicity:** Time averages equal ensemble averages ŌĆö a property often assumed for biomedical recordings (since only *one* patient's ECG trace is usually available).

### 2.4 Autocorrelation & Power Spectral Density

**Autocorrelation function** of a WSS process:
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t)\, X(t+\tau)]
$$

**Power Spectral Density (PSD)** ŌĆö the *Fourier transform* of the autocorrelation (WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem):
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau)\, e^{-j 2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

Properties of $S_{XX}(f)$:
- $S_{XX}(f) \ge 0$ for all $f$.
- $S_{XX}(f) = S_{XX}^{*}(f)$ (Hermitian symmetric for real signals).
- **Total average power:** $\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f) \, df = E\!\left[ \mid X(t) \mid^{2} \right] = R_{XX}(0)$.

### 2.5 Cross-Correlation & Coherence

**Cross-correlation** between two WSS processes $X(t)$ and $Y(t)$:
$$
R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)\, Y(t+\tau)]
$$

**Coherence function** (frequency-domain coupling):
$$
\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\mid S_{XY}(f) \mid^{2}}{S_{XX}(f)\, S_{YY}(f)}, \qquad 0 \le \gamma_{XY}^{2} \le 1
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world use:** EEG-EMG coherence quantifies corticalŌĆōmuscular coupling in motor-control studies. ECGŌĆōPCG coherence is used in heart-sound analysis.

### 2.6 Z-Transform ŌĆö The Discrete Companion

$$
X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}, \qquad z = re^{j\omega}
$$

For $|z| = 1$ (unit circle), $X(z)$ reduces to the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**.

### 2.7 Hilbert Transform & Analytic Signal

The Hilbert transform produces a $\pm 90^{\circ}$ phase-shifted version $\hat{x}(t)$ of $x(t)$:
$$
\hat{x}(t) = \mathcal{H}\{x(t)\} = \frac{1}{\pi} \, \text{P.V.} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{x(\tau)}{t - \tau} \, d\tau
$$

The **analytic signal** is:
$$
x_a(t) = x(t) + j \hat{x}(t)
$$

Its **instantaneous envelope** (used in EMG amplitude estimation):
$$
A(t) = \mid x_a(t) \mid = \sqrt{x^{2}(t) + \hat{x}^{2}(t)}
$$

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS ŌĆö Print & Memorize**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Inner product | $\langle x, y \rangle = \sum x[n] y^{*}[n]$ | Scalar, complex if $x,y$ complex. |
| 2 | Signal energy | $E_x = \sum \mid x[n] \mid^{2}$ | Joules (continuous) / samples (discrete). |
| 3 | Signal power | $P_x = \frac{1}{N} \sum \mid x[n] \mid^{2}$ | Watts / samples. |
| 4 | Mean | $\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ | Volts (for ECG amplitude). |
| 5 | Variance | $\sigma^{2} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x[n] - \mu)^{2}$ | Volts┬▓. |
| 6 | Autocorrelation (WSS) | $R_{XX}(\tau) = E[X(t)X(t+\tau)]$ | Function of lag only. |
| 7 | Autocovariance | $C_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(\tau) - \mu^{2}$ | Zero at $\tau \to \infty$ for WSS. |
| 8 | WienerŌĆōKhinchin | $S_{XX}(f) = \int R_{XX}(\tau) e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ | Defines PSD. |
| 9 | Parseval / Energy | $\int S_{XX}(f) df = E[\mid X \mid^{2}]$ | Total average power. |
| 10 | Cross-PSD | $S_{XY}(f) = \int R_{XY}(\tau) e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ | Hermitian symmetric. |
| 11 | Coherence | $\gamma_{XY}^{2}(f) = \frac{\mid S_{XY} \mid^{2}}{S_{XX} S_{YY}}$ | $0 \le \gamma^{2} \le 1$. |
| 12 | Z-transform | $X(z) = \sum x[n] z^{-n}$ | ROC determines stability. |
| 13 | DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | DTFT $\equiv$ Z on unit circle. |
| 14 | Hilbert transform | $\hat{x}(t) = \frac{1}{\pi} \text{P.V.} \int \frac{x(\tau)}{t-\tau} d\tau$ | "P.V." = Cauchy principal value. |
| 15 | Analytic signal | $x_a(t) = x(t) + j \hat{x}(t)$ | Single-sided spectrum. |
| 16 | Envelope | $A(t) = \sqrt{x^{2}(t) + \hat{x}^{2}(t)}$ | Always non-negative. |
| 17 | Eigen decomposition | $R = U \Lambda U^{H}$ | Foundation of PCA / KLT. |
| 18 | Convolution | $y[n] = (x * h)[n] = \sum x[k] h[n-k]$ | LTI system response. |
| 19 | Nyquist | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Avoids aliasing. |
| 20 | WSS condition | $E[X(t)X(t+\tau)] = R_{XX}(\tau)$ | Depends only on $\tau$. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate** ŌĆö Every algebraic step is *explicitly* shown. No shortcuts, no skipped lines. The three flagship derivations below are *examinable* in KTU 2024 Module 1.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Autocorrelation to PSD (WienerŌĆōKhinchin Theorem)

**Goal:** Show that the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function $R_{XX}(\tau)$ equals the Power Spectral Density $S_{XX}(f)$.

**Step 1: Definition of autocorrelation.**
For a WSS process $X(t)$, define
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = E\!\left[X(t)\, X(t+\tau)\right]
$$

**Step 2: Truncate to a finite observation window.**
Define a truncated process
$$
X_T(t) = \begin{cases} X(t), & -T \le t \le T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Fourier transform of the truncated signal.**
$$
X_T(f) = \int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

**Step 4: Compute the energy spectral density of $X_T$.**
The energy spectral density of the truncated signal is $|X_T(f)|^{2}$, since
$$
\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2} = X_T(f)\, X_T^{*}(f)
$$

**Step 5: Time-averaged power (Parseval/Plancherel).**
The time-averaged power over $[-T, T]$ is
$$
P_T = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \mid X(t) \mid^{2} dt
$$

**Step 6: Parseval's identity.**
Using Parseval's theorem,
$$
P_T = \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X_T(f) \mid^{2} df
$$

**Step 7: Define the periodogram.**
Define
$$
\hat{S}_{XX}(f) = \frac{\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}}{2T}
$$

**Step 8: Take the limit as $T \to \infty$.**
The PSD is defined as
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} E\!\left[ \frac{\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}}{2T} \right]
$$

**Step 9: Substitute $X_T(f)$ in the integrand.**
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} E\!\left[ \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, X(s)\, e^{-j 2\pi f (t - s)}\, dt\, ds \right]
$$

**Step 10: Swap expectation and integration (justified by Fubini's theorem).**
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T}^{T} E\!\left[X(t) X(s)\right] e^{-j 2 \pi f (t - s)}\, dt\, ds
$$

**Step 11: Use the WSS property.**
Since $X(t)$ is WSS, $E[X(t) X(s)] = R_{XX}(t - s) = R_{XX}(\tau)$ where $\tau = t - s$.

**Step 12: Change variables to $\tau$ and $t' = t$.**
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \int_{-T - t}^{T - t} R_{XX}(\tau)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau\, dt
$$

**Step 13: As $T \to \infty$, the inner integration limits tend to $\pm \infty$ for any fixed $\tau$.**
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

**Step 14: Recognize this as a Fourier transform.**
$$
\boxed{\,S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau\,}
$$

This completes the **WienerŌĆōKhinchin Theorem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse direction:** $R_{XX}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f)\, e^{j 2 \pi f \tau}\, df$. This duality is the engine behind all spectral estimation methods used in ECG HRV analysis and EEG band-power computation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Mean and Variance of an Ensemble-Averaged Estimator

**Setup:** A biomedical signal is recorded as $N$ samples $x[0], x[1], \ldots, x[N-1]$. The *sample mean* and *sample variance* estimators are:

**Step 1: Sample mean estimator.**
$$
\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]
$$

**Step 2: Expected value of the sample mean (unbiasedness).**
$$
E[\hat{\mu}] = E\!\left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \right] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} E[x[n]] = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \mu = \mu
$$

So $\hat{\mu}$ is **unbiased**.

**Step 3: Variance of the sample mean (assuming i.i.d. samples of variance $\sigma^{2}$).**
$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) = E\!\left[ (\hat{\mu} - \mu)^{2} \right] = \frac{1}{N^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \text{Var}(x[n]) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N}
$$

**Step 4: Sample variance estimator.**
$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - \hat{\mu})^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Show the bias.** Expand:
$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum x^{2}[n] - \hat{\mu}^{2}
$$

**Step 6: Take expectation.**
$$
E[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = E\!\left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum x^{2}[n] \right] - E[\hat{\mu}^{2}]
$$

**Step 7: Use $E[x^{2}[n]] = \sigma^{2} + \mu^{2}$ and $E[\hat{\mu}^{2}] = \text{Var}(\hat{\mu}) + \mu^{2} = \sigma^{2}/N + \mu^{2}$.**
$$
E[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = (\sigma^{2} + \mu^{2}) - \left( \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N} + \mu^{2} \right) = \sigma^{2} - \frac{\sigma^{2}}{N} = \sigma^{2} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{N} \right)
$$

**Step 8: Conclusion ŌĆö biased estimator.**
$$
\boxed{\,E[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = \frac{N-1}{N} \sigma^{2}\,}
$$

**Step 9: Unbiased correction (Bessel's correction).**
Dividing by $(N-1)$ instead of $N$ gives the *unbiased* estimator:
$$
\boxed{\,\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{unbiased}} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (x[n] - \hat{\mu})^{2}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Biomedical Insight:** When computing HRV (Heart Rate Variability) from short ECG segments, Bessel's correction is essential ŌĆö using $N$ instead of $N-1$ would systematically *underestimate* the true variance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Autocorrelation of a Sum of Sinusoids (ECG Spectral Signature)

**Problem:** A clean ECG (without noise) can be approximated by a sum of sinusoids (Fourier series). Let
$$
x(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} A_k \cos(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k)
$$

Compute the autocorrelation $R_{XX}(\tau)$ assuming the $\phi_k$ are i.i.d. uniformly distributed on $[-\pi, \pi]$.

**Step 1: Definition of autocorrelation.**
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = E\!\left[ x(t)\, x(t+\tau) \right]
$$

**Step 2: Expand the product.**
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = E\!\left[ \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{l=1}^{K} A_k A_l \cos(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k) \cos(2\pi f_l (t+\tau) + \phi_l) \right]
$$

**Step 3: Apply the product-to-sum identity.**
$$
\cos(\alpha) \cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}\big[ \cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta) \big]
$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{l=1}^{K} A_k A_l \, E\!\left[ \cos\big(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k - 2\pi f_l (t+\tau) - \phi_l \big) \right.
$$
$$
\left. + \cos\big(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k + 2\pi f_l (t+\tau) + \phi_l \big) \right]
$$

**Step 5: Take expectation over the random phases.**
Since $\phi_k$ are independent and uniform on $[-\pi, \pi]$, the expectation of any $\cos(\cdot + \phi_k)$ is zero unless the argument has no $\phi_k$.

For $k = l$: the first term becomes $\cos(2\pi f_k \tau)$ (no $\phi_k$ dependence), the second term becomes $\cos(2(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k))$ whose expectation over $\phi_k$ is zero.

For $k \ne l$: the cross-terms involve $\cos(\phi_k - \phi_l)$ which has expectation zero.

**Step 6: Result for $k = l$.**
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} A_k^{2} \cos(2\pi f_k \tau)
$$

**Step 7: Final boxed result.**
$$
\boxed{\,R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} A_k^{2} \cos(2\pi f_k \tau)\,}
$$

**Step 8: Take the Fourier transform to get the PSD.**
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=1}^{K} A_k^{2} \big[ \delta(f - f_k) + \delta(f + f_k) \big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Insight:** A periodic ECG trace is a sum of sinusoids at the heart-rate frequency and its harmonics. Its PSD is a **line spectrum** of delta functions ŌĆö a direct consequence of the WienerŌĆōKhinchin duality derived in 3.1.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Properties Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of Autocorrelation $R_{XX}(\tau)$ (KTU High-Yield)**
>
> 1. $R_{XX}(0) = E[\mid X \mid^{2}] = $ average power.
> 2. $R_{XX}(\tau) = R_{XX}(-\tau)$ ŌĆö **even function** for real signals.
> 3. $\mid R_{XX}(\tau) \mid \le R_{XX}(0)$ ŌĆö maximum at zero lag (CauchyŌĆōSchwarz).
> 4. If $X(t)$ has a DC component $\mu$, then $\displaystyle \lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_{XX}(\tau) = \mu^{2}$.
> 5. $R_{XX}(\tau) \leftrightarrow S_{XX}(f)$ via Fourier transform.
> 6. $S_{XX}(f) \ge 0$ for all $f$ (non-negative PSD).
> 7. **Periodic** $X(t)$ ŌćÆ $R_{XX}(\tau)$ is **periodic** with same period.

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random process. Why is this assumption central to biomedical signal processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A random process $X(t)$ is **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** if:
> 1. Its mean is constant: $E[X(t)] = \mu$ for all $t$.
> 2. Its autocorrelation depends only on the time lag $\tau$: $E[X(t) X(t+\tau)] = R_{XX}(\tau)$.

**Significance in biomedical signal processing (1 mark):**
- Most steady-state biomedical signals (resting ECG, normal EEG, steady EMG) can be modeled as WSS within short windows.
- WSS enables use of the **WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem**, allowing PSD computation.
- It allows **ergodic** assumptions so that ensemble averages can be replaced by time averages ŌĆö essential since usually only one patient's recording is available.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is the WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem? Write the relationship it establishes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem** states that the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** $S_{XX}(f)$ of a Wide-Sense Stationary random process $X(t)$ and its **autocorrelation function** $R_{XX}(\tau)$ form a **Fourier transform pair**:

$$
S_{XX}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) \, e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f) \, e^{j 2 \pi f \tau}\, df
$$

This duality (1 mark) bridges the time-domain and frequency-domain descriptions of stochastic signals and is the basis for all spectral estimators (Welch, periodogram, autoregressive) used in ECG HRV and EEG band-power analysis (1 mark).

---

### ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE NOTATION:**
> - **Question A** and **Question B** are *alternatives* ŌĆö answer **either** A **or** B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Main Paper]**

#### **(a) Derive the Power Spectral Density $S_{XX}(f)$ of a WSS process from the autocorrelation function $R_{XX}(\tau)$. State and prove the properties of $R_{XX}(\tau)$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: State the WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem.** [1 mark]
The PSD and autocorrelation of a WSS process are Fourier transform pairs:
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}
$$

**Step 2: Truncate the process to $[-T, T]$.** [1 mark]
$$
X_T(t) = \begin{cases} X(t), & -T \le t \le T \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Fourier transform the truncated signal.** [1 mark]
$$
X_T(f) = \int_{-T}^{T} X(t)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f t}\, dt
$$

**Step 4: Define the periodogram.** [1 mark]
$$
\hat{S}_{XX}(f) = \frac{\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}}{2T}
$$

**Step 5: Take the ensemble-average and the limit $T \to \infty$.** [1 mark]
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} E\!\left[ \frac{\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}}{2T} \right] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

**Step 6: State the three key properties of $R_{XX}(\tau)$.** [2 marks ŌĆö 0.5 each, plus 0.5 for proof of (i)]

(i) **Even symmetry:** For real $X(t)$,
$$
R_{XX}(-\tau) = E[X(t) X(t - \tau)] = E[X(t - \tau) X(t)] = R_{XX}(\tau)
$$

(ii) **Maximum at zero lag:**
$$
\mid R_{XX}(\tau) \mid \le R_{XX}(0)
$$
Proof: From the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality,
$$
\mid E[X(t) X(t+\tau)] \mid \le \sqrt{E[X^{2}(t)]\, E[X^{2}(t+\tau)]} = R_{XX}(0)
$$

(iii) **Long-lag limit:** If $X(t)$ has DC component $\mu$, then
$$
\lim_{\tau \to \infty} R_{XX}(\tau) = \mu^{2}
$$
because $X(t)$ and $X(t+\tau)$ become uncorrelated as $\tau \to \infty$.

---

#### **(b) A discrete-time biomedical signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ is observed. Compute the sample mean, biased sample variance, and unbiased sample variance. Comment on the difference. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Compute the sample mean.** [1.5 marks]
$$
\hat{\mu} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + 4}{4} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5
$$

**Step 2: Compute the deviations.** [1.5 marks]
| $n$ | $x[n]$ | $x[n] - \hat{\mu}$ | $(x[n] - \hat{\mu})^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | $-1.5$ | $2.25$ |
| 1 | 2 | $-0.5$ | $0.25$ |
| 2 | 3 | $0.5$ | $0.25$ |
| 3 | 4 | $1.5$ | $2.25$ |
| **Sum** |  |  | **5.00** |

**Step 3: Compute the biased sample variance (divide by $N$).** [1.5 marks]
$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{biased}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{3} (x[n] - \hat{\mu})^{2} = \frac{5.00}{4} = 1.25
$$

**Step 4: Compute the unbiased sample variance (divide by $N-1$).** [1.5 marks]
$$
\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{\text{unbiased}} = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{3} (x[n] - \hat{\mu})^{2} = \frac{5.00}{3} \approx 1.6667
$$

**Step 5: Comment.** [1 mark]
The biased estimator underestimates the true variance by a factor of $(N-1)/N$. In biomedical contexts, the unbiased estimator is preferred when $N$ is small (e.g., a 5-beat ECG window), as it compensates for the loss of one degree of freedom due to using the sample mean $\hat{\mu}$ in place of the true mean $\mu$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Internal Choice Alternative]**

#### **(a) State and explain the properties of the Power Spectral Density $S_{XX}(f)$. Derive the relationship between average power and $S_{XX}(f)$. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Definition.** [1 mark]
The PSD of a WSS process is defined as
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E[\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}]}{2T} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2: Property 1 ŌĆö Real and non-negative.** [1 mark]
$$
S_{XX}(f) \ge 0
$$
This follows from $S_{XX}(f) = E[\mid X_T(f) \mid^{2}]/(2T)$ where the magnitude squared is non-negative.

**Step 3: Property 2 ŌĆö Hermitian symmetry (real signals).** [1 mark]
$$
S_{XX}(-f) = S_{XX}^{*}(f) \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{XX}(f) \text{ is real and even for real } X(t)
$$

**Step 4: Property 3 ŌĆö Total average power (Parseval's theorem).** [2 marks]
Integrate $S_{XX}(f)$ over all frequencies:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f)\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, d\tau\, df
$$
Swap integration order (Fubini):
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_{XX}(\tau) \underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau}\, df}_{= \delta(\tau)} d\tau = R_{XX}(0) = E[\mid X(t) \mid^{2}]
$$
Hence the **total average power** is
$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{avg}} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f)\, df = R_{XX}(0) = E[\mid X(t) \mid^{2}]\,}
$$

**Step 5: Property 4 ŌĆö Inversion formula.** [1 mark]
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_{XX}(f)\, e^{j 2 \pi f \tau}\, df
$$

**Step 6: Property 5 ŌĆö Linearity.** [1 mark]
If $Y(t) = a X(t) + b$, then
$$
S_{YY}(f) = a^{2} S_{XX}(f) + b^{2} \delta(f)
$$
(Constant $b$ contributes only a DC impulse.)

---

#### **(b) The ECG signal of a patient at rest is observed to be a sum of three sinusoids: $x(t) = 1.0 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1.2 t) + 0.5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 2.4 t) + 0.25 \cos(2\pi \cdot 3.6 t)$, with random independent phases. Compute its autocorrelation and PSD. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify the form.** [1 mark]
This is a sum-of-sinusoids stochastic process:
$$
x(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{3} A_k \cos(2\pi f_k t + \phi_k)
$$
with $A_1 = 1.0, A_2 = 0.5, A_3 = 0.25$, $f_1 = 1.2$ Hz, $f_2 = 2.4$ Hz, $f_3 = 3.6$ Hz, and $\phi_k$ i.i.d. uniform on $[-\pi, \pi]$.

**Step 2: Apply the derived result from Section 3.3.** [2 marks]
For a sum of sinusoids with i.i.d. random phases,
$$
R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{3} A_k^{2} \cos(2\pi f_k \tau)
$$

**Step 3: Compute $A_k^{2}/2$.** [1 mark]
$$
\frac{A_1^{2}}{2} = \frac{1.0}{2} = 0.5, \quad \frac{A_2^{2}}{2} = \frac{0.25}{2} = 0.125, \quad \frac{A_3^{2}}{2} = \frac{0.0625}{2} = 0.03125
$$

**Step 4: Write the explicit $R_{XX}(\tau)$.** [1 mark]
$$
\boxed{\,R_{XX}(\tau) = 0.5 \cos(2.4 \pi \tau) + 0.125 \cos(4.8 \pi \tau) + 0.03125 \cos(7.2 \pi \tau)\,}
$$

**Step 5: Apply Fourier transform to get the PSD.** [1.5 marks]
Using $\mathcal{F}\{\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)\} = \frac{1}{2}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]$,
$$
S_{XX}(f) = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=1}^{3} A_k^{2} \big[ \delta(f - f_k) + \delta(f + f_k) \big]
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the values.** [0.5 marks]
$$
\boxed{\,S_{XX}(f) = 0.25[\delta(f - 1.2) + \delta(f + 1.2)] + 0.0625[\delta(f - 2.4) + \delta(f + 2.4)] + 0.015625[\delta(f - 3.6) + \delta(f + 3.6)]\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment (free 0 marks, but earns grace):** The PSD is a *line spectrum* ŌĆö characteristic of periodic/quasi-periodic ECG waveforms. The dominant spectral line at $f_1 = 1.2$ Hz corresponds to a heart rate of 72 beats/min ($1.2 \times 60$).

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Lose 2ŌĆō3 marks each)**
> 1. **Confusing the sample variance divisor:** Students frequently divide by $N$ when the question asks for the *unbiased* estimator. **KTU rule:** "Sample variance" in an exam *without* further qualification usually means $\frac{1}{N-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$.
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's correction proof:** Examiners specifically award 1 mark for the bias derivation $E[\hat{\sigma}^{2}] = (N-1)\sigma^{2}/N$. Omitting the algebra of step 7 in Section 3.2 loses a full mark.
> 3. **WienerŌĆōKhinchin direction error:** Writing $R_{XX}(\tau) = \mathcal{F}\{S_{XX}(f)\}$ instead of the correct $S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}$ ŌĆö note the **forward direction** of the theorem.
> 4. **Skipping the ergodicity assumption:** When asked "can ensemble averages be replaced by time averages?", students write "yes" without stating the *WSS + ergodic* assumption. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Misinterpreting $\delta(\tau)$ as a finite-valued function:** In the Parseval proof, students write $\int e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} df = \delta(\tau)$ but then proceed to "evaluate" it as a number. **Treat it formally.**
> 6. **Sample mean of complex signals:** $E[X]$ and $E[X X^*]$ ŌĆö note the **conjugate** in the second moment for complex WSS.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Mathematical Instruments for Signal Processing**

#### **A. Linear Algebra**
- ŌśÉ Signal as a vector in $\mathbb{C}^{N}$; inner product $\langle x, y \rangle = x^{H} y$.
- ŌśÉ Autocorrelation matrix $R_{xx} = E[x x^{H}]$; eigen decomposition $R_{xx} = U \Lambda U^{H}$.
- ŌśÉ PCA projects signals onto eigenvectors of $R_{xx}$ ŌĆö used in ECG beat clustering, EEG dimensionality reduction.

#### **B. Probability & Random Variables**
- ŌśÉ Mean $\mu = E[X]$, Variance $\sigma^{2} = E[(X-\mu)^{2}]$.
- ŌśÉ Bessel's correction: divide by $N-1$ for unbiased variance.
- ŌśÉ AWGN model for sensor noise.

#### **C. Stochastic Processes & Stationarity**
- ŌśÉ SSS: all moments invariant; WSS: only first two moments invariant; **WSS is the working assumption**.
- ŌśÉ Autocorrelation $R_{XX}(\tau)$ depends only on lag $\tau$ for WSS.
- ŌśÉ Ergodicity: time averages = ensemble averages ŌĆö vital for single-recording clinical data.

#### **D. Spectral Descriptors**
- ŌśÉ WienerŌĆōKhinchin: $S_{XX}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{XX}(\tau)\}$.
- ŌśÉ $S_{XX}(f) \ge 0$, even (real signals), Parseval: $\int S_{XX}(f) df = E[|X|^{2}]$.
- ŌśÉ Coherence: $\gamma_{XY}^{2} = \frac{|S_{XY}|^{2}}{S_{XX} S_{YY}}$ ŌĆö measures frequency-domain coupling.

#### **E. Transforms**
- ŌśÉ **Fourier Transform** $X(f) = \int x(t) e^{-j 2 \pi f t} dt$.
- ŌśÉ **Z-Transform** $X(z) = \sum x[n] z^{-n}$; on unit circle $\Rightarrow$ DTFT.
- ŌśÉ **Hilbert Transform** $\hat{x}(t) = \frac{1}{\pi}\text{P.V.}\int \frac{x(\tau)}{t - \tau} d\tau$.
- ŌśÉ Analytic signal $x_a(t) = x(t) + j \hat{x}(t)$; envelope $A(t) = |x_a(t)|$.

#### **F. Must-Memorize Formulas**
- ŌśÉ WienerŌĆōKhinchin (both directions).
- ŌśÉ Bessel's correction: $\hat{\sigma}^{2}_{N-1}$.
- ŌśÉ Sum-of-sinusoids autocorrelation: $R_{XX}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2}\sum A_k^{2}\cos(2\pi f_k \tau)$.
- ŌśÉ Coherence bounds $0 \le \gamma^{2} \le 1$.
- ŌśÉ Parseval's identity linking total power to PSD area.

#### **G. Biomedical Application Anchors**
- ŌśÉ Resting ECG / EEG / EMG Ōēł WSS and ergodic.
- ŌśÉ ERP / seizure EEG / EMG onset Ōēł non-stationary ŌåÆ time-frequency methods (later modules).
- ŌśÉ PCA on beat-to-beat ECG = feature extraction for arrhythmia classification.
- ŌśÉ Coherence between EEG channels = functional connectivity measure.
- ŌśÉ Hilbert envelope of EMG = muscle activation profile.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second mnemonic ŌĆö "LA-PST":**
> **L**inear Algebra ŌåÆ **A**utocorrelation ŌåÆ **P**ower Spectral Density ŌåÆ **S**tochastic Stationarity ŌåÆ **T**ransforms (Fourier, Z, Hilbert). Each letter maps to one "instrument" group tested in KTU 2024 Module 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Descriptive Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Descriptive Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Descriptive Methods* in biomedical signal processing refer to the set of **time-domain, statistical, and amplitude-domain analytical techniques** used to quantitatively characterize the behavior, distribution, energy, and time-evolution of physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, etc.) without explicitly transforming them into the frequency domain. They provide the *first-pass numerical fingerprint* of any biomedical waveform.

## 1.1 What Exactly Are "Descriptive Methods"?

When a biomedical signal is acquired ŌĆö say, an ECG tracing from a patient ŌĆö the raw waveform is just a sequence of voltage samples indexed by time. Before performing any spectral (Fourier) or time-frequency (Wavelet) analysis, a signal processing engineer must first answer simple but critical questions:

- What is the **average level** of the signal?
- How much does it **fluctuate** around that level?
- What is its **shape of distribution** (Gaussian? skewed? heavy-tailed)?
- How much **energy/power** does it carry?
- How similar is the signal to **itself at a different time** (autocorrelation)?
- How similar is it to **another signal** (cross-correlation)?

Descriptive methods provide exactly these answers using compact numerical descriptors.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Patient Vitals Report"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Patient Vitals**
> Imagine a doctor receives a 24-hour continuous ECG recording. Instead of scrolling through 86,400 samples per second, the doctor wants a one-page "Patient Vitals Report". This report contains:
> - **Average heart rate** (analogous to *mean*)
> - **Heart rate variability** (analogous to *variance/SD*)
> - **Symmetry of RR-interval histogram** (analogous to *skewness*)
> - **Peakedness of the distribution** (analogous to *kurtosis*)
> - **Maximum-minus-minimum voltage swing** (analogous to *peak-to-peak amplitude*)
> - **Effective heating value of the signal** (analogous to *RMS*)
> - **How regular the rhythm is** (analogous to *autocorrelation*)
>
> Descriptive methods are precisely the mathematical machinery that generates this "vitals report" for **any** biomedical signal.

## 1.3 Why Descriptive Methods Are the Foundation

Three engineering realities make descriptive methods non-negotiable in KTU-aligned biomedical curricula:

1. **Computational Efficiency** ŌĆö they require only $O(N)$ operations, ideal for wearable/embedded devices.
2. **Diagnostic Relevance** ŌĆö clinical features like HRV, RMS-EMG, and EEG band power are direct descriptive metrics.
3. **Pre-processing Necessity** ŌĆö almost every advanced algorithm (ICA, PCA, wavelet denoising) requires pre-computation of mean, variance, and covariance.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Standards**
> - Sampling frequency $f_s$ ŌĆö typically **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** for ECG, **128 Hz to 512 Hz** for EEG.
> - Quantization resolution ŌĆö clinical grade is typically **12-bit to 16-bit**.
> - Standard reference: IEEE Std 1057 for digitizing biomedical signals.

## 1.5 Classification of Descriptive Methods

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Primary Use in Biomedicine** |
|--------------|-------------|--------------------------------|
| Measures of Central Tendency | Mean, Median, Mode | Baseline drift correction |
| Measures of Dispersion | Variance, SD, IQR | HRV analysis |
| Higher-Order Moments | Skewness, Kurtosis | EMG/EEG distribution shape |
| Amplitude Descriptors | Peak-to-Peak, RMS, Crest Factor | QRS amplitude, muscle activation |
| Energy/Power Descriptors | Average Power, Signal Energy | Sleep staging, seizure detection |
| Correlation Descriptors | Autocorrelation, Cross-Correlation | Template matching, coherence |
| Covariance Descriptors | Covariance, Correlation Coefficient | Multi-channel EEG/EMG analysis |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Let a discrete-time biomedical signal be denoted as $x[n]$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$, with $N$ total samples acquired at sampling frequency $f_s$.

## 2.1 Measures of Central Tendency

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Mean (DC Component)
The **arithmetic mean** represents the average amplitude of the signal and corresponds to its **DC offset** in spectral terms.

**Why we need it:** Almost every biomedical signal is AC-coupled or has a baseline wander; knowing the mean allows us to subtract it and obtain a zero-mean signal ŌĆö a prerequisite for nearly all further analysis.

**How it is computed:** Sum all samples and divide by the total count.

$$ \mu_x = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] $$

### 2.1.2 Median
The **median** is the middle value of the sorted sample array. It is **robust to outliers**, making it ideal for noisy EMG or artifact-contaminated EEG epochs.

### 2.1.3 Mode
The **mode** is the most frequently occurring amplitude bin. It is most useful for **histogram-based analysis** of action potential amplitudes.

## 2.2 Measures of Dispersion (Second-Order Statistics)

### 2.2.1 Variance
Variance quantifies the **average squared deviation** of the signal from its mean. It carries the unit of the signal **squared**.

$$ \sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \mu_x \right)^2 $$

For sample (unbiased) variance, the denominator becomes $N-1$.

### 2.2.2 Standard Deviation
The **square root of variance**, expressed in the same unit as the signal. It is the most clinically intuitive dispersion metric.

$$ \sigma_x = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2} $$

## 2.3 Higher-Order Moments ŌĆö The "Shape Descriptors"

> [!NOTE]
> The $k$-th **central moment** of a discrete signal is the cornerstone descriptor for capturing distributional shape.

$$ m_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \mu_x \right)^k $$

**Skewness (3rd normalized moment)** ŌĆö measures *asymmetry* of the amplitude distribution:

$$ \gamma_1 = \frac{m_3}{\sigma_x^3} = \frac{1}{N} \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \mu_x \right)^3}{\sigma_x^3} $$

- $\gamma_1 = 0$ ŌåÆ symmetric (e.g., clean sinusoid).
- $\gamma_1 > 0$ ŌåÆ right-skewed (e.g., rectified EMG).
- $\gamma_1 < 0$ ŌåÆ left-skewed.

**Kurtosis (4th normalized moment)** ŌĆö measures *peakedness/tail-heaviness* of the distribution:

$$ \gamma_2 = \frac{m_4}{\sigma_x^4} = \frac{1}{N} \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \mu_x \right)^4}{\sigma_x^4} $$

- $\gamma_2 = 3$ ŌåÆ Gaussian (mesokurtic).
- $\gamma_2 > 3$ ŌåÆ heavy-tailed, sharp peak (leptokurtic) ŌĆö characteristic of EEG spikes.
- $\gamma_2 < 3$ ŌåÆ flat distribution (platykurtic).

## 2.4 Amplitude Domain Descriptors

### 2.4.1 Peak-to-Peak Amplitude
$$ x_{pp} = \max(x[n]) - \min(x[n]) $$

Used in QRS detection thresholds and PCG murmur grading.

### 2.4.2 Root Mean Square (RMS)
RMS is the **single most important amplitude descriptor** in biomedical engineering. It equals the standard deviation for a zero-mean signal.

$$ x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] \right)^2 } $$

**Physical meaning:** RMS equals the magnitude of a DC signal that would deliver the same average power to a resistive load. EMG amplitude is universally reported as RMS.

### 2.4.3 Crest Factor
$$ CF = \frac{\vert x_{peak} \vert}{x_{rms}} $$

A pure sinusoid has $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. EMG and ECG have $CF > 2$ due to transient spikes.

## 2.5 Energy and Power Descriptors

For an **energy signal** (finite duration):

$$ E_x = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] \right)^2 $$

For a **power signal** (infinite/periodic) ŌĆö average power:

$$ P_x = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] \right)^2 $$

For a finite window of $N$ samples (the practical biomedical case):

$$ P_x \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] \right)^2 = x_{rms}^2 $$

## 2.6 Correlation Descriptors

### 2.6.1 Autocorrelation Function (ACF)
Measures the **self-similarity** of a signal with a time-shifted version of itself. Critical for detecting periodicity (e.g., QRS regularity, EEG rhythms).

$$ r_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n] \, x[n+k] $$

For a periodic signal with period $P$, $r_{xx}[k]$ will peak at $k = P, 2P, 3P, \ldots$

### 2.6.2 Normalized Autocorrelation
$$ \rho_{xx}[k] = \frac{r_{xx}[k]}{r_{xx}[0]} $$

Always satisfies $\rho_{xx}[0] = 1$.

### 2.6.3 Cross-Correlation Function (CCF)
Measures similarity between two signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ as a function of time lag.

$$ r_{xy}[k] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n] \, y[n+k] $$

The lag $k^*$ that maximizes $\vert r_{xy}[k] \vert$ corresponds to the **time delay** between the two signals (used in estimating foetal ECG from maternal abdominal recordings, or in estimating blood-pressure/ECG propagation delay).

### 2.6.4 Pearson Correlation Coefficient
For two zero-mean random variables/signal windows:

$$ \rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, y[n]}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n]^2}} $$

Range: $-1 \le \rho_{xy} \le 1$.

## 2.7 Covariance Descriptor

$$ C_{xy} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left( x[n] - \mu_x \right) \left( y[n] - \mu_y \right) $$

When extended to **$M$ channels**, the **covariance matrix** $\mathbf{C} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times M}$ is foundational to **PCA-based denoising of multi-channel EEG/EMG**.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** examiners expect you to reproduce from memory. Note: $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value. No vertical pipes appear inside table cells.

| **Descriptor** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Engineering Utility** |
|----------------|-------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Arithmetic Mean | $\mu_x = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]$ | volts (V) | Baseline correction |
| Variance | $\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x[n]-\mu_x)^2$ | $\text{V}^2$ | HRV, noise power |
| Std. Deviation | $\sigma_x = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2}$ | V | Dispersion metric |
| Skewness | $\gamma_1 = m_3 / \sigma_x^3$ | dimensionless | Distribution asymmetry |
| Kurtosis | $\gamma_2 = m_4 / \sigma_x^4$ | dimensionless | Spike detection in EEG |
| Peak-to-Peak | $x_{pp} = \max - \min$ | V | QRS amplitude |
| RMS | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]^2}$ | V | EMG amplitude |
| Crest Factor | $CF = \vert x_{peak} \vert / x_{rms}$ | dimensionless | Impulsiveness |
| Energy | $E_x = \sum x[n]^2$ | $\text{V}^2 \cdot \text{s}$ | Finite-duration analysis |
| Power | $P_x = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]^2$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Stationary analysis |
| Autocorrelation | $r_{xx}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]x[n+k]$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Periodicity detection |
| Cross-Correlation | $r_{xy}[k] = \frac{1}{N}\sum x[n]y[n+k]$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Time-delay estimation |
| Pearson $\rho$ | $\rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum x[n]y[n]}{\sqrt{\sum x^2}\sqrt{\sum y^2}}$ | dimensionless | Template matching |
| Covariance | $C_{xy} = \frac{1}{N}\sum (x-\mu_x)(y-\mu_y)$ | $\text{V}^2$ | Multi-channel PCA |
| SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | dB | Signal quality metric |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where KTU examiners expect "applied" answers**
> - **ECG**: Mean subtraction removes baseline wander; QRS detection uses $x_{pp}$ and $\sigma_x$ thresholds.
> - **EMG**: $x_{rms}$ is the gold standard for muscle activation level.
> - **EEG**: Skewness & kurtosis detect spikes and seizure onset; ACF identifies alpha/beta rhythms.
> - **PCG (heart sounds)**: Cross-correlation aligns with ECG for systolic/diastolic segmentation.
> - **Wearable Devices**: All descriptors are implementable in $O(N)$ on microcontrollers ŌĆö making them ideal for real-time embedded DSP.
