## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define resting membrane potential. Derive the Nernst equation and compute the equilibrium potential for KŌü║ given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM at 37┬░C.*

**Model Answer:**
The *resting membrane potential* is the steady-state voltage difference (typically **ŌłÆ70 mV** inside relative to outside) maintained across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is not firing. It arises from the combined action of the NaŌü║/KŌü║ ATPase pump and selective ionic permeability.

Derivation: The Nernst equation is derived by equating the chemical free energy of an ionic concentration gradient with the electrical work of moving the ion across the membrane.

$$
\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0
$$

$$
RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right) + zFE = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; E = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\frac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}}\right)
$$

For KŌü║ ($z = +1$, T = 310 K):

$$
E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}(140/5) = 61.5 \times 1.447 \approx -89 \text{ mV}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Compare the temporal and spatial resolution of EEG, fMRI, MEG, PET, and fNIRS. Why is EEG still the most widely used modality despite its poor spatial resolution?*

**Model Answer:**

| Modality | Temporal Res. | Spatial Res. | Cost |
|----------|---------------|--------------|------|
| EEG | 1 ms | 2ŌĆō3 cm | Low |
| MEG | 1 ms | 2ŌĆō5 mm | Very high |
| fMRI | 1ŌĆō6 s | 1ŌĆō3 mm | High |
| PET | 30 s ŌĆō 5 min | 4ŌĆō6 mm | Very high |
| fNIRS | 1 s | 1ŌĆō3 cm | Low |

EEG remains the most widely used modality because it offers **excellent temporal resolution (ms)**, **low cost**, **portability** (ambulatory and bedside recording), **non-invasiveness**, and **direct measurement of neuronal activity** (unlike the indirect hemodynamic/metabolic surrogates of fMRI/PET). Its spatial resolution is constrained by the **smearing effect of the skull** (a low-conductivity volume conductor) and the **inverse problem** of source localization.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 model paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the GoldmanŌĆōHodgkinŌĆōKatz (GHK) voltage equation. With standard mammalian ionic concentrations, show that the resting potential evaluates to approximately **ŌłÆ70 mV**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the four phases of a neuronal action potential. Sketch the corresponding ionic currents ($I_{Na}$, $I_K$) and gating variables ($m$, $h$, $n$) as functions of time. State the significance of the absolute and relative refractory periods.

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the constant-field assumption (Goldman, 1943). The membrane current for a monovalent ion is:

$$
I_{ion} = P_{ion}\frac{F^2 V_m}{RT}\left(\frac{[ion]_i - [ion]_o e^{-F V_m/RT}}{1 - e^{-F V_m/RT}}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** At rest, $I_{total} = I_K + I_{Na} + I_{Cl} = 0$. For $z = -1$ (ClŌü╗), flipping the exponential term:

$$
\frac{F V_m}{RT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $RT/F$:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Insert numerical values ($[K^+]_i=140, [K^+]_o=5; [Na^+]_i=15, [Na^+]_o=145; [Cl^-]_i=4, [Cl^-]_o=110$; $P_K:P_{Na}:P_{Cl} = 1:0.04:0.45$; $\frac{RT}{F}\ln 10 = 61.5$ mV at 37┬░C):

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{(1)(5)+(0.04)(145)+(0.45)(4)}{(1)(140)+(0.04)(15)+(0.45)(110)}\right)
$$

$$
V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}(12.6 / 190.1) = 61.5 \times (-1.178) = -72.4 \text{ mV} \approx -70 \text{ mV}
$$

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

| Phase | Voltage | Active Current | Gating |
|-------|---------|----------------|--------|
| 1. Resting | ŌłÆ70 mV | None (steady leak) | $m=0$, $h=1$, $n=0$ |
| 2. Depolarization | ŌłÆ55 to +40 mV | $I_{Na}$ inrush | $m$ rises rapidly, $h$ slow |
| 3. Repolarization | +40 to ŌłÆ70 mV | $I_K$ efflux | $n$ peaks, $m$ falls, $h$ falls |
| 4. Hyperpolarization | < ŌłÆ70 mV | $I_K$ tail | $n$ decays, $h$ recovers |

**Significance of refractory periods:**
- **Absolute refractory period (0ŌĆō2 ms):** NaŌü║ channels are inactivated; *no stimulus, however strong, can elicit another AP*. This enforces **unidirectional propagation** along the axon.
- **Relative refractory period (2ŌĆō4 ms):** Some NaŌü║ channels have recovered, but KŌü║ channels are still open; a *larger-than-normal* stimulus is required. This sets the **maximum firing rate** of a neuron.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 model paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **BOLD signal** mechanism in functional MRI. Explain the canonical Hemodynamic Response Function (HRF) and why BOLD is considered an *indirect* measure of neural activity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the typical signal-processing pipeline of a **functional neuroimaging** experiment (acquisition ŌåÆ preprocessing ŌåÆ statistical analysis ŌåÆ inference). Compare the strengths and weaknesses of EEG-fMRI simultaneous recording.

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**BOLD mechanism:**
The Blood-Oxygen-Level-Dependent (BOLD) signal exploits the differing magnetic properties of **oxyhemoglobin (diamagnetic)** and **deoxyhemoglobin (paramagnetic)**. When a brain region becomes active:
1. Cerebral blood flow (CBF) increases locally (~30ŌĆō50%).
2. Oxygen extraction increases only modestly.
3. The net result: **deoxyhemoglobin concentration decreases** in the venous bed.
4. Lower deoxyhemoglobin ŌćÆ less local field inhomogeneity ŌćÆ longer T2* ŌćÆ **higher MR signal**.

**Mathematical model:**

$$
\frac{\Delta S}{S_0} \approx -\text{TE}\cdot\Delta R_2^*
$$

**Canonical HRF:**

$$
h(t) = t^{a_1-1} e^{-t/b_1} - c\,t^{a_2-1} e^{-t/b_2}
$$

with $(a_1, b_1, a_2, b_2, c) = (6, 0.9, 16, 1.2, 1/6)$.

- Initial **dip** (~2 s) ŌĆö early oxygen extraction.
- Main **peak** at ~5ŌĆō6 s ŌĆö CBF overshoot.
- **Post-stimulus undershoot** at ~15 s ŌĆö CBF normalizes before CBV.

BOLD is **indirect** because it measures a **vascular/metabolic** consequence of neural activity, lagging by ~6 seconds. The relationship between neural firing (action potentials / synaptic activity) and BOLD is non-trivial ŌĆö typically a linear convolution model is assumed, but non-linearities, vascular steal effects, and the *neurovascular coupling* assumption remain active research areas.

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.2):**
The pipeline consists of five stages:
1. **Source:** Neuronal ionic currents and synaptic dipoles.
2. **Propagation:** Volume conductor physics / hemodynamic coupling / tracer kinetics.
3. **Acquisition:** Sensor arrays (EEG, MEG, MRI, PET, fNIRS).
4. **Preprocessing:** Filtering, artifact removal (ICA, ASR), motion correction, spatial normalization to MNI/Talairach atlas.
5. **Analysis & Inference:** GLM (fMRI), source localization (EEG/MEG), spectral analysis, statistical parametric mapping (SPM), multiple-comparison correction (FDR, FWE).

**EEG-fMRI simultaneous recording ŌĆö strengths and weaknesses:**

| Aspect | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Temporal | EEG adds ms resolution to slow fMRI | Gradient artifacts in EEG |
| Spatial | fMRI locates EEG sources | Ballistocardiogram artifacts |
| Information | Multiplexed electrical + hemodynamic | Complex synchronized preprocessing |
| Cost | Single session reduces scan time | MR-compatible EEG caps expensive |
| Application | Epilepsy focus localization, sleep, BCI | Patient safety ŌĆö currents must be limited |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Forgetting that $\frac{RT}{F}\ln 10 = 61.5$ mV applies **only at 37┬░C (310 K)**. At room temperature (298 K) it is 59.1 mV. Always state the temperature!
> 2. **Log base:** Some students use $\log_e$ instead of $\log_{10}$ in the Nernst equation ŌĆö instant zero. Always use base-10 with the 61.5 factor.
> 3. **Direction of inequality:** In the GHK equation, the numerator has the **OUT** concentrations for cations but **IN** for anions. Reversing this is a classic 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **BOLD delay:** Students often state "BOLD is instantaneous" ŌĆö it is not. There is a 6-second hemodynamic lag. Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **HRF shape:** The HRF has *two* components ŌĆö the **main positive peak** *and* the **post-stimulus undershoot**. Drawing only the peak is incomplete.
> 6. **MEG units:** Always quote magnetic field strength in **femtotesla (fT)** or **picotesla (pT)**; stating only "Tesla" without a prefix is technically correct but signals conceptual weakness.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision ŌĆö Memorize These 15 Points:**

- ŌśÉ **Nernst equation** at 37┬░C: $E = 61.5/z \cdot \log_{10}([ion]_{in}/[ion]_{out})$; $E_K \approx -90$ mV, $E_{Na} \approx +60$ mV.
- ŌśÉ **GHK resting potential** evaluates to **ŌłÆ70 mV** using $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$.
- ŌśÉ **Action potential peak** = **+30 to +40 mV**; duration **~1ŌĆō2 ms**.
- ŌśÉ **HodgkinŌĆōHuxley exponents**: NaŌü║ activation is $m^3 h$, KŌü║ activation is $n^4$.
- ŌśÉ **Volume conductor forward model** uses quasi-static Maxwell equations ŌåÆ Laplace equation in homogeneous regions.
- ŌśÉ **EEG 10-20 system** ŌĆö electrodes at 10% / 20% of inion-nasion distance; Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz, A1, A2.
- ŌśÉ **EEG bandwidth:** 0.5ŌĆō100 Hz; sampling Ōēź 200 Hz clinical, Ōēź 1000 Hz research.
- ŌśÉ **MEG** uses **SQUID** sensors; measures femtotesla fields; *insensitive to radial sources* (a high-yield fact).
- ŌśÉ **fMRI BOLD** lags neural activity by **~6 seconds**; spatial resolution **1ŌĆō3 mm**.
- ŌśÉ **PET tracer**: ┬╣ŌüĖF-FDG half-life = **109.7 min**; emits **511 keV** annihilation photons.
- ŌśÉ **fNIRS** penetration depth = **1.5ŌĆō3 cm**; uses modified **BeerŌĆōLambert law**.
- ŌśÉ **Diffusion MRI (DWI)** uses StejskalŌĆōTanner equation $S = S_0 e^{-bD}$ to map white-matter tracts.
- ŌśÉ **Nyquist for fMRI:** $f_{Nyq} = 1/(2 \cdot \text{TR})$; with TR = 2 s ŌćÆ **0.25 Hz**.
- ŌśÉ **Refractory periods:** Absolute (0ŌĆō2 ms) ŌåÆ no new AP; Relative (2ŌĆō4 ms) ŌåÆ stronger stimulus needed.
- ŌśÉ **EEG-fMRI fusion** combines ms temporal + mm spatial resolution ŌĆö the modern "gold standard" for presurgical epilepsy workup.

---

**End of Module 1 ŌĆö Electrophysiology and Functional Neuroimaging Notes**
*Total marks coverage: 2├Ś Part A (3 M each) + 1├Ś Part B (14 M) = 20 marks (fully aligned with KTU 2024 ESE pattern).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Wavelet Analysis

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelet Analysis** is a time-frequency decomposition technique that uses a family of basis functions, called **wavelets**, obtained by dilating (scaling) and translating (shifting) a single prototype function, the **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$. Unlike the Fourier Transform, which represents a signal as a sum of infinite-duration sinusoids, the Wavelet Transform represents a signal as a sum of localized, finite-energy wavelets, providing simultaneous information about *when* and *at what frequency* a particular event occurs in the signal.

Mathematically, a wavelet $\psi(t)$ must satisfy the **admissibility condition** and the **zero-mean (DC) condition**:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \psi(t)\, dt = 0$$

This zero-average property is the defining characteristic that distinguishes wavelets from standard oscillating functions. It guarantees that the wavelet transform is invertible and that the wavelet captures *changes* or *transients* rather than steady-state behavior.

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Musical Score vs. The Magnifying Glass"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box ŌĆö Why Wavelets?**
> Imagine you are listening to a long symphony recording.
> - The **Fourier Transform** is like telling you *which musical notes* (frequencies) are present in the entire symphony ŌĆö but you cannot tell *at what moment* a particular trumpet fanfare was played.
> - The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is like a fixed-width magnifying glass that slides over the recording. The size of the lens is fixed, so you either see the whole orchestra in low detail or a tiny passage in high detail ŌĆö but not both.
> - The **Wavelet Transform** is a *variable-lens magnifying glass*. It zooms in tightly (high frequency, short time window) to catch the sharp cymbal crash at second 47, and zooms out (low frequency, long time window) to capture the slow bass line of the introduction. **This adaptive resolution is the soul of wavelets.**

In biomedical signals ŌĆö ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG ŌĆö the events of clinical interest (QRS complexes, spikes, bursts, murmurs) are *short, transient, and localized* in time. Wavelet analysis is therefore the *natural* mathematical microscope for such signals.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mother Wavelet | $\psi(t)$ | Dimensionless, $\int \vert\psi(t)\vert^2 dt = 1$ |
| Scale Parameter | $a$ | $a > 0$, dimensionless (or seconds) |
| Translation Parameter | $b$ | $b \in \mathbb{R}$ (seconds) |
| Centre Frequency | $f_c$ | **0.8ŌĆō0.85 Hz** (for Morlet) |
| Sampling Rate (clinical) | $f_s$ | **250 Hz ŌĆō 1 kHz** (typical ECG) |
| Decomposition Levels | $J$ | **3 to 8** (typical for ECG) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)

The **Continuous Wavelet Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as the inner product of $x(t)$ with a family of scaled and translated wavelets:

$$CWT_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt$$

where:
- $a$ = **scale parameter** ($a > 0$); small $a \Rightarrow$ compressed (high-frequency) wavelet; large $a \Rightarrow$ stretched (low-frequency) wavelet.
- $b$ = **translation parameter** (time shift).
- $\psi^{*}(t)$ = complex conjugate of the mother wavelet.
- $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ = **energy normalization factor** to keep the energy of $\psi$ constant across all scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pseudo-frequency Mapping:** Although $a$ is dimensionless in mathematics, in engineering we convert it to a *pseudo-frequency* using the centre frequency $f_c$ of the mother wavelet:
>
> $$f_a = \frac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$$
>
> where $\Delta t$ is the sampling period. This allows the time-scale plane $(a, b)$ to be interpreted as the conventional time-frequency plane $(f, t)$.

## 2.2 Admissibility & Reconstruction Conditions

For a function $\psi(t)$ to qualify as a mother wavelet, it must satisfy the **admissibility condition**:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{+\infty} \frac{\vert\hat{\psi}(f)\vert^2}{f}\, df < \infty$$

where $\hat{\psi}(f)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$. This implies $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$, i.e., the wavelet has no DC component.

**Inverse CWT (Reconstruction):**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} CWT_x(a, b)\, \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) \frac{da\, db}{a^2}$$

## 2.3 The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)

For computational tractability, the scale and translation parameters are sampled on a **dyadic grid**:

$$a = a_0^m, \quad b = n\, b_0\, a_0^m$$

With the canonical choice $a_0 = 2$ and $b_0 = 1$ (Mallat's dyadic sampling), the DWT becomes:

$$DWT_x(m, n) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^m}} \sum_{k} x[k]\, \psi\!\left(\frac{k - 2^m n}{2^m}\right)$$

The DWT is implemented efficiently via **Mallat's Algorithm** (1989) using a pair of **Quadrature Mirror Filters (QMF)**:
- $h[n]$ = **low-pass filter** ŌåÆ produces the **approximation coefficients** $A_m$.
- $g[n]$ = **high-pass filter** ŌåÆ produces the **detail coefficients** $D_m$.
- The filters are related by: $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ (spectral inversion).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE ŌĆö These are the equations that recur most often in KTU board exams for this module.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CWT Definition | $CWT(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a\vert}}\int x(t)\psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)dt$ | $a$ = scale, $b$ = shift |
| 2 | Mother Wavelet Condition | $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\psi(t)dt = 0$ | Zero mean ŌåÆ captures transients |
| 3 | Admissibility | $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\vert\hat{\psi}(f)\vert^2}{f}df < \infty$ | Guarantees invertibility |
| 4 | Pseudo-frequency | $f_a = \frac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$ | Converts scale to Hz |
| 5 | QMF Relation | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$ | LP ŌåÆ HP by spectral inversion |
| 6 | Energy Conservation | $\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}}\iint \vert CWT(a,b)\vert^2 \frac{da\,db}{a^2}$ | Parseval-like identity |
| 7 | Dyadic Sampling | $a = 2^m,\ b = n\cdot 2^m$ | Octave-band decomposition |
| 8 | Inverse DWT | $x[n] = \sum_m \sum_n \left(A_m[n] + D_m[n]\right)$ | Perfect reconstruction (orthogonal wavelets) |
| 9 | Heisenberg Area | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | CWT satisfies this bound |
| 10 | Morlet Wavelet | $\psi(t) = e^{-t^2/2}\cos(5t)$ | $f_c \approx 0.8$ Hz (used in EEG) |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG ŌĆö R-peak & QRS detection:** DWT with **Daubechies-4 (db4)** at level 4 decomposes the QRS into a dominant detail coefficient. A simple threshold on $D_4$ locates R-peaks with sub-millisecond accuracy.
- **EEG ŌĆö Epileptic spike detection:** The **modulus maxima** of the CWT are used to identify transient spike-and-wave discharges.
- **EMG ŌĆö Muscle fatigue analysis:** Wavelet packet energy distribution across scales tracks the shift of the EMG power spectrum toward lower frequencies during fatigue.
- **Denoising:** Thresholding the detail coefficients (Donoho's **VisuShrink** / **SureShrink**) at levels where SNR is low yields a clean signal.
- **Compression:** Set small detail coefficients to zero (e.g., EZW, SPIHT algorithms) ŌĆö used in JPEG2000 and the **DICOM-wavelet** medical image format.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From the Fourier Transform to the Wavelet Transform

The CWT can be derived rigorously from the Fourier transform of the dilated-and-shifted wavelet.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the scaled, shifted wavelet in the frequency domain.**

Let $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a\vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$. Its Fourier transform is:

$$\hat{\psi}_{a,b}(f) = \sqrt{\vert a\vert}\, \hat{\psi}(a f)\, e^{-j 2\pi f b}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Parseval's theorem to convert the time-domain inner product into a frequency-domain inner product.**

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \int x(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt = \int \hat{x}(f)\, \hat{\psi}_{a,b}^{*}(f)\, df$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the expression for $\hat{\psi}_{a,b}(f)$.**

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \int \hat{x}(f)\, \sqrt{\vert a\vert}\, \hat{\psi}^{*}(a f)\, e^{+j 2\pi f b}\, df$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Make the substitution $f' = a f$, so $df = \frac{df'}{a}$.**

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \sqrt{\vert a\vert} \int \hat{x}\!\left(\frac{f'}{a}\right) \hat{\psi}^{*}(f')\, e^{j 2\pi f' (b/a)}\, \frac{df'}{a}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Simplify the constant and obtain the final form.**

$$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a\vert}} \int \hat{x}\!\left(\frac{f'}{a}\right) \hat{\psi}^{*}(f')\, e^{j 2\pi f' (b/a)}\, df'$$

This derivation shows that **a small scale $a$ corresponds to a stretched spectrum $\hat{x}(f/a)$** ŌĆö i.e., the CWT behaves like a **band-pass filter** whose centre frequency is inversely proportional to $a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The CWT is essentially a bank of band-pass filters with constant-Q (constant relative bandwidth). Each scale $a$ corresponds to an octave band of the original signal spectrum.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mallat's Decomposition ŌĆö The Two-Channel Subband Filter Bank

The DWT of a discrete signal $x[n]$ is computed by convolving it with the QMF pair and downsampling by 2.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Low-pass (approximation) branch.**

$$A_1[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k]\, x[2n - k]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö High-pass (detail) branch.**

$$D_1[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g[k]\, x[2n - k]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recursive decomposition of the approximation branch.**

$$A_{m+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k]\, A_m[2n - k], \quad D_{m+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k]\, A_m[2n - k]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Perfect reconstruction (inverse DWT).**

$$A_m[k] = \sum_{n} h[2n - k]\, A_{m+1}[n] + \sum_{n} g[2n - k]\, D_{m+1}[n]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Energy of the original signal is preserved (orthogonal wavelets).**

$$\sum_{k} \vert x[k]\vert^2 = \sum_{k} \vert A_J[k]\vert^2 + \sum_{m=1}^{J} \sum_{k} \vert D_m[k]\vert^2$$

This is the **Parseval identity for the DWT** and is fundamental for energy-based feature extraction in biomedical signals.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Haar Wavelet Decomposition of a Short ECG Segment

**Problem:** Compute the 1-level DWT of the 8-sample ECG segment $x = [72,\ 75,\ 78,\ 80,\ 85,\ 90,\ 92,\ 95]$ using the **Haar wavelet**.

**Haar Filters (length $N = 2$):**

$$h = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\ \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right], \quad g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},\ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute approximation coefficients $A_1[n]$.**

$$A_1[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(72 + 75) = \frac{147}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 103.94$$

$$A_1[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(78 + 80) = \frac{158}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 111.72$$

$$A_1[2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(85 + 90) = \frac{175}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 123.74$$

$$A_1[3] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(92 + 95) = \frac{187}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 132.23$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute detail coefficients $D_1[n]$.**

$$D_1[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(72 - 75) = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -2.12$$

$$D_1[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(78 - 80) = \frac{-2}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -1.41$$

$$D_1[2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(85 - 90) = \frac{-5}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -3.54$$

$$D_1[3] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(92 - 95) = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{2}} \approx -2.12$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation.**

The approximations $A_1$ capture the **slow trend (baseline wander)** of the ECG, while the details $D_1$ capture the **fast changes (slopes of the waveform)**. After multiple levels, the high-amplitude details at level 4 or 5 typically align with the **QRS complex**.

| Coefficient | Symbol | Value | Biophysical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1[0]$ | Approx. | 103.94 | Baseline at t=0 |
| $A_1[1]$ | Approx. | 111.72 | Baseline at t=2 |
| $D_1[2]$ | Detail | -3.54 | Largest local slope (rising edge) |
| $D_1[0]$ | Detail | -2.12 | Local slope change |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö DWT Decomposition (3-Level Wavelet Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS["Input Signal xn"] --> nodeLP1["Low-Pass Filter hn"]
    nodeS --> nodeHP1["High-Pass Filter gn"]
    nodeLP1 --> nodeDS1["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeHP1 --> nodeDS2["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeDS1 --> nodeA1["Approximation A1"]
    nodeDS2 --> nodeD1["Detail D1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeLP2["Low-Pass hn Level 2"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeHP2["High-Pass gn Level 2"]
    nodeLP2 --> nodeDS3["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeHP2 --> nodeDS4["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeDS3 --> nodeA2["Approximation A2"]
    nodeDS4 --> nodeD2["Detail D2"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeLP3["Low-Pass hn Level 3"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeHP3["High-Pass gn Level 3"]
    nodeLP3 --> nodeDS5["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeHP3 --> nodeDS6["Downsample by 2"]
    nodeDS5 --> nodeA3["Approximation A3"]
    nodeDS6 --> nodeD3["Detail D3"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö CWT as an Adaptive Time-Frequency Microscope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSig["Biomedical Signal x of t"] --> nodeCWT["CWT Engine"]
    nodeMW["Mother Wavelet psi t"] --> nodeCWT
    nodeCWT --> nodeScaleAxis["Scale Axis a small to large"]
    nodeCWT --> nodeTimeAxis["Time Axis b shifts"]
    nodeScaleAxis --> nodeSFC["Scalogram CWT mag squared"]
    nodeTimeAxis --> nodeSFC
    nodeSFC --> nodeFeat["Feature Extraction"]
    nodeFeat --> nodeQRS["QRS Detection"]
    nodeFeat --> nodeSpike["Epileptic Spike Detection"]
    nodeFeat --> nodeDenoise["Wavelet Denoising"]
    nodeFeat --> nodeClass["Signal Classification"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Wavelet Denoising Pipeline (Thresholding)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn["Noisy Signal s of t equals x of t plus n of t"] --> nodeDec["DWT Decomposition to Level J"]
    nodeDec --> nodeApprox["Approximation Aj Unchanged"]
    nodeDec --> nodeDet["Detail Coefficients D1 to DJ"]
    nodeDet --> nodeTh["Threshold via VisuShrink or SureShrink"]
    nodeTh --> nodeMod["Modified Details D1 prime to DJ prime"]
    nodeApprox --> nodeRec["IDWT Reconstruction"]
    nodeMod --> nodeRec
    nodeRec --> nodeOut["Denoised Signal x hat of t"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) Subband Frequency Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF0["Full Band 0 to fs by 2"] --> nodeD1["D1 fs by 4 to fs by 2"]
    nodeF0 --> nodeA1["A1 0 to fs by 4"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeD2["D2 fs by 8 to fs by 4"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2["A2 0 to fs by 8"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeD3["D3 fs by 16 to fs by 8"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["A3 0 to fs by 16 Deep Approximation"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeApp1["Reveals QRS High Freq"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeApp2["Reveals T Wave Mid Freq"]
    nodeD3 --> nodeApp3["Reveals P Wave Low Mid"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeApp4["Reveals Baseline Wander DC"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the MRA Diagram:** Each level of the DWT halves the bandwidth. The deepest approximation $A_J$ contains the slowest components (baseline wander, respiration), while the finest detail $D_1$ contains the sharpest transients (QRS spikes, EMG bursts). For an ECG sampled at $f_s = 360$ Hz, decomposition to level 4 places the QRS energy squarely in $D_3$ (45ŌĆō90 Hz) and $D_4$ (22.5ŌĆō45 Hz).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]`
**Define the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a signal $x(t)$. Explain the role of the scale and translation parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

The CWT of a signal $x(t)$ with respect to a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is defined as:

$$CWT_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt, \quad a \neq 0, \ b \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Role of parameters:**
- **Scale $a$:** Compresses or stretches the wavelet. Small $a$ ŌåÆ high-frequency, fine-resolution analysis. Large $a$ ŌåÆ low-frequency, coarse-resolution analysis.
- **Translation $b$:** Shifts the wavelet along the time axis, localizing the analysis in time. Together, $(a, b)$ provide a *time-scale* representation analogous to the *time-frequency* representation of the STFT, but with adaptive resolution.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three properties that a function $\psi(t)$ must satisfy to be a valid mother wavelet.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Admissibility condition:** $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert\hat{\psi}(f)\vert^2}{f} df < \infty$. This guarantees that the CWT is invertible.
2. **Zero mean (DC-free):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t)\, dt = 0$. Implies the wavelet has no zero-frequency component, so it captures *changes* (transients) rather than steady offsets.
3. **Finite energy (square-integrability):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \psi(t)\vert^2 dt < \infty$. Ensures the wavelet is localized in time and decays to zero.
4. **(Bonus) Compact support or fast decay:** Many practical wavelets (Daubechies, Haar) are compactly supported, enabling finite-impulse-response filter implementations.

---

## **PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make and lose marks on:
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ normalization** in the CWT definition (deducted 1ŌĆō2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing scale $a$ with frequency $f$.** Examiners expect you to state the *pseudo-frequency formula* explicitly when discussing time-frequency correspondence.
> 3. **Writing $g[n] = -h[n]$ instead of $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$** for the QMF relation ŌĆö the sign alternation and time-reversal are *both* essential.
> 4. **Not specifying the mother wavelet** when discussing an application. The choice of wavelet (db4 vs. sym8 vs. biorthogonal) directly affects detection accuracy.
> 5. **Skipping the admissibility condition** while deriving inverse CWT ŌĆö this is the foundation of the reconstruction integral.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]`

**$(a)$** Derive the Continuous Wavelet Transform of a signal $x(t)$. Explain how the admissibility condition ensures the invertibility of the transform. (7 Marks)

**$(b)$** With a neat block diagram, explain the Mallat's algorithm for the computation of the Discrete Wavelet Transform using quadrature mirror filters. Show the perfect reconstruction condition. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Definition.** The CWT is defined as the inner product of the signal with a family of scaled and translated wavelets:

$$CWT_x(a, b) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, \psi_{a, b}^{*}(t)\, dt, \quad \psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Parseval's bridge.** By Parseval's theorem:

$$CWT_x(a, b) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \hat{x}(f)\, \hat{\psi}_{a, b}^{*}(f)\, df$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Inverse CWT.**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} CWT_x(a, b)\, \psi_{a, b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Role of $C_{\psi}$.** The constant $C_{\psi}$ is the **admissibility constant**:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert\hat{\psi}(f)\vert^2}{f} df < \infty$$

It must be **finite and non-zero** for the inverse integral to converge. The condition $C_{\psi} < \infty$ forces $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$, i.e., the wavelet has no DC component, which means it is a *band-pass* function. This is why wavelets detect transients and not constant offsets.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verification of energy conservation (Parseval-like identity for CWT).**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert CWT_x(a, b)\vert^2\, \frac{da\, db}{a^2}$$

This shows the CWT preserves signal energy ŌĆö a critical property for both compression and feature extraction.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Filter bank structure.** Mallat's algorithm implements the DWT as a tree of two-channel subband filters:
- **Low-pass filter $h[n]$** ŌåÆ extracts the **approximation** (coarse) coefficients.
- **High-pass filter $g[n]$** ŌåÆ extracts the **detail** (fine) coefficients.

**Step 2 ŌĆö QMF relation.**

$$g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$$

The high-pass filter is obtained by alternating-sign reversal of the time-reversed low-pass filter, ensuring spectral mirror symmetry about $f_s/4$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decomposition equations (refer to diagram in Section 4.1).**

$$A_m[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k]\, A_{m-1}[2n - k]$$

$$D_m[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g[k]\, A_{m-1}[2n - k]$$

with $A_0[n] = x[n]$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Reconstruction (IDWT).**

$$A_{m-1}[k] = \sum_{n} h[2n - k]\, A_m[n] + \sum_{n} g[2n - k]\, D_m[n]$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Perfect reconstruction condition.** The filters must satisfy:

- **No aliasing:** $H(z)H(z^{-1}) + G(z)G(z^{-1}) = 2$ (all-pass).
- **No amplitude distortion:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert^2 + \vert G(e^{j\omega})\vert^2 = 2$.
- **No phase distortion:** The product $H(z)G(z^{-1})$ must be an *odd* polynomial in $z$, i.e., $H(z)G(z^{-1}) = -z^{-L}$ (eliminating cross-aliasing between $A$ and $D$).

When all three conditions hold, the reconstructed signal $\hat{x}[n] = x[n]$ (up to a delay), and the DWT is said to be *orthogonal* (e.g., Daubechies, Symlet) or *biorthogonal* (e.g., B-spline, CDF 9/7).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]`

**$(a)$** Compare the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) and the Wavelet Transform. Discuss the concept of multi-resolution analysis and explain how the choice of mother wavelet affects the analysis of an ECG signal. (7 Marks)

**$(b)$** With a suitable example, explain wavelet-based signal denoising. Discuss the VisuShrink and SureShrink thresholding techniques. (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö STFT vs. Wavelet comparison.**

| Feature | STFT | Wavelet (CWT/DWT) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis function | Fixed window + sinusoid | Variable-scale wavelet |
| Resolution | Fixed (Heisenberg-bound) | Adaptive (multi-resolution) |
| Best for | Stationary signals | Non-stationary / transient signals |
| Time-frequency tiling | Uniform rectangles | Logarithmic tiling |
| Output | Spectrogram | Scalogram |

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA).** MRA, formalized by Mallat and Meyer (1989), states that any finite-energy signal $x(t)$ can be decomposed into a hierarchy of nested subspaces $V_J \subset V_{J+1} \subset \cdots \subset L^2(\mathbb{R})$ and their orthogonal complements (wavelet subspaces) $W_m$, where $V_{m+1} = V_m \oplus W_m$. The signal is then:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k} c_J[k]\, \phi_{J, k}(t) + \sum_{m=1}^{J} \sum_{k} d_m[k]\, \psi_{m, k}(t)$$

Here $\phi$ is the **scaling function** and $\psi$ the **wavelet function**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Effect of mother wavelet choice on ECG analysis.**
- **Haar:** Simple, but produces blocky artifacts; not ideal for smooth ECG morphology.
- **Daubechies-4 (db4):** The gold standard for ECG QRS detection. Its shape closely matches the QRS morphology, maximizing the energy compaction in the $D_4$ detail band.
- **Symlet-8 (sym8):** Near-symmetric; used when phase distortion must be minimized.
- **Morlet:** Continuous, complex-valued; preferred for time-frequency analysis of EEG and PCG (heart sounds).
- **Coiflet-4:** Used for ECG compression in telemedicine, as both scaling and wavelet functions have many zero moments.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Practical ECG example.** Decomposing an ECG with db4 to level 4:
- $D_1$ (90ŌĆō180 Hz) ŌĆö captures high-frequency noise and EMG interference.
- $D_2$ (45ŌĆō90 Hz) ŌĆö captures the **QRS complex**.
- $D_3$ (22.5ŌĆō45 Hz) ŌĆö captures the **T wave**.
- $D_4$ (11.25ŌĆō22.5 Hz) ŌĆö captures the **P wave** and QRS tail.
- $A_4$ (0ŌĆō11.25 Hz) ŌĆö captures **baseline wander and respiration**.

The detection algorithm then thresholds $|D_2|$ to locate the R-peak, with accuracy better than **┬▒2 ms** for clean ECG signals.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Wavelet denoising pipeline.** Given a noisy signal $s[n] = x[n] + \eta[n]$, where $\eta$ is Gaussian white noise of variance $\sigma^2$:

1. Compute the DWT of $s$ to level $J$: obtain $\{A_J, D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_J\}$.
2. Apply a threshold $\lambda$ to the detail coefficients (soft or hard).
3. Reconstruct via IDWT.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Hard vs. soft thresholding.**

$$\hat{d}_{m,k}^{\text{hard}} = \begin{cases} d_{m,k}, & \vert d_{m,k}\vert \geq \lambda \\ 0, & \vert d_{m,k}\vert < \lambda \end{cases}$$

$$\hat{d}_{m,k}^{\text{soft}} = \text{sign}(d_{m,k}) \cdot \max(\vert d_{m,k}\vert - \lambda,\ 0)$$

Hard thresholding preserves sharp edges but causes discontinuities; soft thresholding produces smoother signals at the cost of slight amplitude attenuation.

**Step 3 ŌĆö VisuShrink (Donoho, 1994).** A *universal* threshold derived from minimax theory:

$$\lambda_{\text{visu}} = \sigma \sqrt{2 \ln N}$$

where $N$ is the signal length and $\sigma$ is the noise standard deviation, estimated as $\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\text{MAD}(D_1)}{0.6745}$ (MAD = median absolute deviation of the finest detail coefficients).

**Step 4 ŌĆö SureShrink (Donoho & Johnstone, 1995).** A *data-driven* threshold that minimizes Stein's Unbiased Risk Estimate (SURE):

$$\text{SURE}(\lambda) = N - 2 \sum_{k=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{\vert d_k\vert \leq \lambda\} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \min(\vert d_k\vert^2, \lambda^2)$$

$$\lambda_{\text{SURE}} = \arg\min_{\lambda \geq 0} \text{SURE}(\lambda)$$

SureShrink is **adaptive** ŌĆö it shrinks coefficients more in smooth regions and less near singularities, making it ideal for denoising ECG signals where QRS complexes must be preserved.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Comparison.**

| Method | Threshold | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| VisuShrink | Universal, constant | High-noise, smooth signals |
| SureShrink | Adaptive, level-dependent | Signals with sparse transients (ECG, EMG) |
| BayesShrink | Minimizes Bayesian risk | Subband-dependent SNR |
| NeighCoef | Spatial correlation | Image-like 2D signals |

---

## **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- **Wavelet** = a small, oscillating, zero-mean, finite-energy function $\psi(t)$ used as a *mathematical microscope* for non-stationary signals.
- **CWT** uses *continuous* $a$ and $b$; provides redundant but smooth time-scale representation; computationally heavy.
- **DWT** uses *dyadic* sampling $a = 2^m$, $b = n \cdot 2^m$; computed via Mallat's filter bank; computationally efficient (O(N)).
- **Admissibility condition** $C_{\psi} < \infty$ guarantees invertibility; zero-mean condition $\int \psi = 0$ is its necessary consequence.
- **Pseudo-frequency** $f_a = \frac{f_c}{a \cdot \Delta t}$ converts scales to Hz for engineering interpretation.
- **QMF pair**: $g[n] = (-1)^n h[N-1-n]$. The HP filter is the sign-alternating, time-reversed LP filter.
- **MRA**: $V_{m+1} = V_m \oplus W_m$. Approximations live in $V_m$; details live in $W_m$.
- **Parseval's identity for DWT**: $\sum \vert x\vert^2 = \sum \vert A_J\vert^2 + \sum_{m=1}^{J}\sum \vert D_m\vert^2$.
- **Mother wavelet selection for ECG** = db4 (most common) or sym8 (low-phase-distortion). For EEG, use **Morlet** (CWT). For compression, use **Coiflet** or **biorthogonal 9/7**.
- **Denoising pipeline**: DWT ŌåÆ threshold detail coefficients (VisuShrink or SureShrink) ŌåÆ IDWT.
- **VisuShrink** $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$; **SureShrink** is data-driven and adaptive per subband.
- **Heisenberg uncertainty**: $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$. CWT achieves this bound; it cannot be improved.
- **Biomedical applications**: ECG QRS detection, EEG spike detection, EMG fatigue analysis, PCG S1/S2 separation, denoising, compression (JPEG2000, DICOM-wavelet), heart-rate variability via wavelet-based spectral estimation.
- **Common wavelet families (must know for viva)**: Haar, Daubechies (dbN), Symlet (symN), Coiflet (coifN), Biorthogonal (bior), Morlet, Mexican Hat (Ricker), Meyer.
- **For 14-mark derivations**: always show the *admissibility condition*, the *QMF relation*, and the *perfect reconstruction conditions* (no aliasing, no amplitude distortion, no phase distortion).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Short-Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Short-Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ö¼ The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) ŌĆö A Foundational Time-Frequency Tool

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is a classical, linear, quadratic-distribution-class time-frequency representation (TFR) used to analyze how the frequency content of a non-stationary signal evolves over time. It is mathematically obtained by multiplying the input signal $x(t)$ with a short-duration, sliding window function $w(t)$ and then computing the Fourier Transform of the windowed segment.

The continuous-time STFT is formally defined as:

$$
X(\tau, f) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t - \tau)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

where:
- $x(t)$ is the input (biomedical) signal under analysis.
- $w(t-\tau)$ is the analysis window centered at time $\tau$.
- $w^{*}(\cdot)$ denotes the complex conjugate of the window (relevant when complex windows like Gabor are used; for real windows it is simply $w$).
- $X(\tau, f)$ is a 2-D complex function of both time $\tau$ and frequency $f$.
- $e^{-j 2 \pi f t}$ is the complex exponential kernel of the Fourier Transform.

The magnitude squared $\vert X(\tau,f) \vert^2$ is known as the **spectrogram**, a widely used energy density representation in biomedical engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The STFT forms the conceptual backbone for the **spectrogram**, the **Gabor transform**, and the motivation for the **Wavelet Transform**. Any Part-B question on time-frequency analysis will almost certainly begin with the STFT.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Reading Magnifier" Intuition

Imagine trying to read a long, scrolling banner of text with your naked eye. The **Fourier Transform** is like squinting at the entire banner from far away ŌĆö you can tell *roughly* what letters appear, but you cannot tell *where* they appear on the banner. The **STFT**, however, is like sweeping a small magnifying glass along the banner: at every position (time $\tau$) you look through the glass and read only a *small portion* of the banner, performing a frequency analysis on that portion. As you slide the magnifier, you build a 2-D map: *what frequencies are present, and at what time*. The **width of the magnifying glass** is analogous to the **window length**: a narrow window gives good *time resolution* but poor *frequency resolution*, and vice-versa.

## 1.3 Why STFT? ŌĆö The Stationarity Problem

Most real-world biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, speech, EMG) are **non-stationary** ŌĆö their spectral content changes with time (e.g., QRS complex in ECG, sleep spindles in EEG, Murmurs in PCG). A plain Fourier Transform averages frequency information over the entire signal duration, hiding these temporal dynamics. The STFT explicitly addresses this by imposing *local stationarity* within short, overlapping windows.

## 1.4 Key Physical and Mathematical Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_w$ | Window duration (length) | **20 ŌĆō 500 ms** for biomedical signals |
| $\Delta t$ | Time resolution | $\approx T_w$ |
| $\Delta f$ | Frequency resolution | $\approx 1 / T_w$ |
| $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ | Time-frequency area | $\geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ (Gabor limit) |
| $f_s$ | Sampling rate | **250 Hz ŌĆō 1 kHz** typical for ECG/EEG |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ­¤¦Ā Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Mechanics

The STFT operates through a tightly choreographed sequence of operations. Each step is explained below with its physical/mathematical *why*.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Windowing the signal:**
The infinite-duration signal $x(t)$ is multiplied point-by-point with a short, time-localized window $w(t-\tau)$ shifted to time $\tau$. This isolates a finite segment of the signal around $\tau$.  
*Why:* The Fourier Transform assumes the segment under analysis is approximately stationary; truncation is the only practical way to enforce this locally.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Fourier Transform of the windowed segment:**
The standard Fourier Transform is applied to the windowed segment $x(t)w(t-\tau)$. The output is a *local spectrum* valid at time $\tau$.  
*Why:* This converts a 1-D time slice into a 1-D frequency signature.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Slide the window and repeat:**
The window is translated to the next time instant $\tau + \Delta\tau$, and Steps 1ŌĆō2 are repeated. The collection of all such local spectra forms a 2-D surface $X(\tau, f)$.  
*Why:* This builds the time-frequency map of the entire signal.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Spectrogram formation:**
$\text{Spectrogram}(\tau, f) = \vert X(\tau, f) \vert^2$.  
*Why:* The squared magnitude is an *energy* quantity and is more useful for visual interpretation in clinical applications.

## 2.2 The Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off (HeisenbergŌĆōGabor Uncertainty)

The single most examinable concept in STFT is the **uncertainty principle** that governs the joint time-frequency resolution. For any window $w(t)$ with energy normalized to unity, the product of its time spread and frequency spread is bounded below by:

$$
\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \;\geq\; \frac{1}{4\pi}
$$

The equality holds only for the **Gaussian window** (Gabor limit). This means:
- A **wide window** (long $T_w$) ŌåÆ narrow main-lobe in frequency ŌåÆ **good frequency resolution**, poor time resolution.
- A **narrow window** (short $T_w$) ŌåÆ narrow main-lobe in time ŌåÆ **good time resolution**, poor frequency resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Trap:** The trade-off is *unavoidable* for a *fixed* window shape. You cannot achieve arbitrarily good resolution in *both* domains simultaneously with the STFT. This is precisely why multi-resolution tools like the Wavelet Transform were developed.

## 2.3 Common Window Functions ŌĆö A Comparative Study

| Window | Main-lobe width | Side-lobe level (dB) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $2/T_w$ (narrowest) | **ŌłÆ13 dB** (worst) | Transient detection, high time resolution |
| Hann (Hanning) | $4/T_w$ | **ŌłÆ31 dB** | General-purpose biomedical signals |
| Hamming | $4/T_w$ | **ŌłÆ43 dB** | Speech, ECG ŌĆö suppresses spectral leakage |
| Blackman | $6/T_w$ | **ŌłÆ58 dB** | High dynamic range, PCG analysis |
| Gaussian | variable | **ŌłÆ42 dB** | Optimal joint resolution (Gabor limit) |
| Kaiser ($\beta$ tunable) | tunable | tunable | Adaptive biomedical applications |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A popular 14-mark question asks to justify the choice of Hamming over Rectangular window for analyzing ECG. The model answer must mention that the Hamming window's **lower side-lobe level (ŌłÆ43 dB)** drastically reduces **spectral leakage**, which is critical when detecting the low-amplitude P and T waves buried near the high-amplitude QRS.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) w^{*}(t-\tau) e^{-j 2 \pi f t} dt$ | Continuous-Time STFT |
| 2 | $X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n+mH) w(n) e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}$ | Discrete STFT (DFT-based) |
| 3 | $S(\tau, f) = \vert X(\tau, f) \vert^2$ | Spectrogram (energy density) |
| 4 | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | GaborŌĆōHeisenberg uncertainty |
| 5 | $x(t) = \frac{1}{E_w}\int\!\!\int X(\tau, f) w(t-\tau) e^{j 2 \pi f t} d\tau\, df$ | Inverse STFT (with $E_w = \int \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$) |
| 6 | $\text{STFT}\{x(t-\tau_0)\} = X(\tau-\tau_0, f) e^{-j 2 \pi f \tau_0}$ | Time-shift property |
| 7 | $\text{STFT}\{x(t) e^{j 2 \pi f_0 t}\} = X(\tau, f - f_0)$ | Frequency-shift property |
| 8 | $R_x(\tau, \nu) = \iint X(\tau_1, f_1) X^{*}(\tau_2, f_2) \cdot d\tau_1 d\tau_2$ | Ambiguity function (related concept) |
| 9 | $E = \int\!\!\int S(\tau, f) d\tau\, df = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | Energy preservation (Parseval for STFT) |
| 10 | $H = \frac{N - M}{M}$ (overlap) | Hop size $H$ in samples for overlap-add |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering, the STFT spectrogram is the workhorse of:
- **Phonocardiogram (PCG) analysis** ŌĆö visualizing S1, S2, murmurs, and clicks.
- **EEG seizure detection** ŌĆö localizing transient rhythmic activity in time-frequency.
- **Speech audiometry** ŌĆö formant tracking for hearing aids.
- **EMG fatigue analysis** ŌĆö observing median frequency shift over time.
- **Vibration analysis of heart sounds & Doppler ultrasound.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The STFT assumes *quasi-stationarity within the window*. For signals with very fast transients (e.g., QRS complex, ~80 ms), the window length must be chosen shorter than the transient but long enough to capture the dominant frequency (e.g., 10ŌĆō40 Hz for QRS main energy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤ōÉ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the STFT from the Standard Fourier Transform

Starting from the classical Fourier Transform definition of a signal $x(t)$:

$$
X(f) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f t}\, dt \qquad \text{(Global Spectrum)}
$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Impose a time-localized window.**  
Multiply $x(t)$ by a shifted window $w(t-\tau)$ to obtain a *localized* segment:

$$
x_{\tau}(t) \;=\; x(t) \cdot w(t - \tau)
$$

This restricts the signal to the region near $t = \tau$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Fourier Transform to the localized segment.**  

$$
X(\tau, f) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x_{\tau}(t)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f t}\, dt \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w(t - \tau)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f t}\, dt
$$

This is precisely the **continuous-time STFT**. Hence, mathematically, the STFT is the Fourier Transform of the *windowed* (localized) signal.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Spectrogram as the energy distribution.**  
Since $X(\tau, f)$ is complex, we define a real, non-negative energy density:

$$
S(\tau, f) \;=\; \vert X(\tau, f) \vert^2 \;=\; X(\tau, f) \cdot X^{*}(\tau, f)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Inverse STFT (signal reconstruction).**  
The original signal $x(t)$ can be perfectly recovered using the **inverse STFT**:

$$
x(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{E_w} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\tau, f)\, w(t - \tau)\, e^{j 2 \pi f t}\, d\tau\, df
$$

where $E_w = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert w(t) \vert^2 dt$ is the energy of the window. *Note:* This reconstruction is exact only if the window satisfies the admissibility condition (no zeros in its Fourier Transform). Reconstruction is **redundant** ŌĆö overlapping windows create multiple representations of the same signal.

## 3.2 Derivation of the HeisenbergŌĆōGabor Uncertainty Inequality

Define the effective time duration and frequency duration of a window $w(t)$ with energy $E_w$ as:

$$
(\Delta t)^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{E_w} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} t^{2}\, \vert w(t) \vert^2\, dt
$$

$$
(\Delta f)^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{E_w} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f^{2}\, \vert W(f) \vert^2\, df
$$

By applying the **CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality** to the integral $\int t\, w(t) \cdot W^{*}(f)\, df$ and using Parseval's theorem, one arrives at:

$$
\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \;\geq\; \frac{1}{4\pi}
$$

**Equality** is achieved **if and only if** $w(t)$ is a **Gaussian function**: $w(t) = A \, e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$. This is the **Gabor logon** ŌĆö the most compact time-frequency atom achievable under the STFT framework.

## 3.3 Discrete-Time STFT (DSTFT) ŌĆö Practical Implementation

For a discrete signal $x(n)$ sampled at $f_s$ and a discrete window $w(n)$ of length $N$, the discrete STFT is given by:

$$
X(m, k) \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n + mH)\, w(n)\, e^{-j 2 \pi k n / N}
$$

where:
- $m$ is the frame index (time axis discretization).
- $k$ is the frequency bin index.
- $H$ is the hop size (number of samples the window advances per frame).
- The overlap is $N - H$ samples.

For perfect reconstruction via overlap-add, the window must satisfy the **COLA (Constant Overlap-Add)** condition:

$$
\sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} w(n - mH) \;=\; \text{constant}
$$

The **Hann window with 50% overlap** is a textbook COLA example.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute the magnitude of the STFT at $\tau = 0$ for the signal $x(t) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 10\, t)$ using a rectangular window of width $T_w = 0.2$ s.

**Solution:**

$$
X(0, f) = \int_{-0.1}^{+0.1} \cos(20\pi t)\, e^{-j 2 \pi f t}\, dt
$$

Expanding the cosine via Euler's formula, we have:

$$
X(0, f) = \frac{1}{2}\int_{-0.1}^{0.1} \left( e^{j 20\pi t} + e^{-j 20\pi t} \right) e^{-j 2 \pi f t} dt
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{2}\left[ \int_{-0.1}^{0.1} e^{j 2\pi(10 - f) t} dt + \int_{-0.1}^{0.1} e^{-j 2\pi(10 + f) t} dt \right]
$$

Evaluating each integral:

$$
\int_{-0.1}^{0.1} e^{j 2\pi(10 - f) t} dt = \frac{\sin\!\big(0.2\pi(10 - f)\big)}{\pi(10 - f)}
$$

$$
\int_{-0.1}^{0.1} e^{-j 2\pi(10 + f) t} dt = \frac{\sin\!\big(0.2\pi(10 + f)\big)}{\pi(10 + f)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
X(0, f) = 0.1 \left[ \text{sinc}\!\big(0.2(10 - f)\big) + \text{sinc}\!\big(0.2(10 + f)\big) \right]
$$

The magnitude $\vert X(0, f) \vert$ is **maximum at $f = \pm 10$ Hz**, confirming the expected dominant frequency, with the peak's sharpness determined by $1/T_w = 5$ Hz resolution.

# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the STFT Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire STFT signal-flow architecture, including its biomedical signal-processing context.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Biomedical Signal x t] --> B[Segmentation into Overlapping Frames]
    B --> C[Apply Sliding Window w t minus tau]
    C --> D[Compute Local Fourier Transform]
    D --> E[Complex STFT Matrix X of tau f]
    E --> F[Compute Magnitude Squared]
    F --> G[Spectrogram S tau f]
    G --> H[Time Frequency Visualization]
    H --> I[Clinical Feature Extraction]
    I --> J[QRS Detection or Seizure Localization or PCG Murmur]
    E --> K[Inverse STFT Synthesis]
    K --> L[Reconstructed Signal x hat t]
```

## 4.2 Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Choose Window Length T w] --> B{Resolution Priority}
    B -->|Need sharp time localization| C[Narrow Window T w Small]
    C --> C1[Good Delta t, Poor Delta f]
    B -->|Need sharp frequency localization| D[Wide Window T w Large]
    D --> D1[Poor Delta t, Good Delta f]
    B -->|Need optimal joint resolution| E[Gaussian Window]
    E --> E1[Delta t times Delta f equals 1 over 4 pi, Gabor Limit]
```

## 4.3 STFT vs. CWT vs. FT ŌĆö Functional Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Signal x t] --> B[Classical Fourier Transform]
    A --> C[Short Time Fourier Transform]
    A --> D[Continuous Wavelet Transform]
    B --> B1[1D Global Spectrum, No Time Info]
    C --> C1[2D Time Frequency Map, Fixed Resolution]
    D --> D1[2D Time Scale Map, Adaptive Resolution]
    C1 --> E[Limitation: Uniform Resolution]
    D1 --> F[Strength: Multi Resolution]
```

## 4.4 STFT Feature-Extraction Workflow for ECG

```mermaid

    A[Raw ECG Signal] --> B[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[Frame Blocking with Hamming Window]
    C --> D[Compute STFT per Frame]
    D --> E[Spectrogram Generation]
    E --> F[Detect QRS Energy in 10 to 25 Hz Band]
    F --> G[Detect T Wave Energy in 1 to 7 Hz Band]
    G --> H[Output HRV and Morphology Features]
```
