# ­¤ōØ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. State the role of the window function $w(t)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The STFT of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, w^{*}(t-\tau)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

where $\tau$ is the time-shift (window-center) parameter and $f$ is the frequency parameter.

**Role of the window function:** The window $w(t)$ is a short-duration, typically real-valued, time-localized function (e.g., Hamming, Hann, Gaussian) that isolates a finite segment of $x(t)$ around the time instant $\tau$. By restricting the analysis to this segment, the window imposes a *local stationarity* assumption, allowing the Fourier Transform to compute a meaningful local spectrum. The choice of window length $T_w$ directly governs the **time-frequency resolution trade-off**: short $T_w$ gives good time resolution but poor frequency resolution, and vice-versa.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**What is the spectrogram? How is it related to the STFT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **spectrogram** is a real, non-negative, two-dimensional energy density function of time and frequency, defined as the **squared magnitude of the STFT**:

$$
S(\tau, f) = \vert X(\tau, f) \vert^2
$$

While the STFT $X(\tau, f)$ is a complex-valued function preserving both amplitude and phase information, the spectrogram discards the phase and represents only the **signal energy** localized in the time-frequency plane. It is the most widely used visualization tool in biomedical TFR applications (e.g., for visualizing EEG seizure onset or PCG murmurs), because clinicians intuitively interpret brightness as energy concentration.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) Starting from the classical Fourier Transform, derive the continuous-time Short-Time Fourier Transform of a signal $x(t)$. Clearly explain the role of the window function and the significance of the time-frequency resolution trade-off. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the HeisenbergŌĆōGabor uncertainty principle for the STFT. With a neat sketch, explain how the choice of window length affects the time and frequency resolutions. Mention the optimal window and justify your answer. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Derivation of STFT (7 marks):**

The classical Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ is:

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-j 2\pi f t} dt
$$

This provides a global frequency description, but for non-stationary biomedical signals, time-localized spectral information is required. To achieve this, we multiply $x(t)$ with a time-shifted window $w(t-\tau)$:

$$
x_\tau(t) = x(t) w(t-\tau)
$$

The window $w(t-\tau)$ is non-zero only over a short interval of duration $T_w$ centered at $t = \tau$. Applying the Fourier Transform to the windowed segment:

$$
X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) w(t-\tau) e^{-j 2\pi f t} dt
$$

This is the **continuous-time STFT**. By varying $\tau$, we obtain a family of local spectra, which together form the 2-D time-frequency representation.

**Role of the window:**  
(i) It enforces *local stationarity* within the windowed segment.  
(ii) Its length $T_w$ controls the time-frequency resolution trade-off.  
(iii) Its shape (Hamming, Hann, etc.) controls spectral leakage.

**Significance of the trade-off:** $\Delta t \approx T_w$ and $\Delta f \approx 1/T_w$, hence $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ is roughly constant. Narrowing $T_w$ improves time resolution but worsens frequency resolution and vice versa.

**(b) HeisenbergŌĆōGabor Uncertainty Principle (7 marks):**

For any window $w(t)$ with finite energy $E_w$, the time-spread $\Delta t$ and frequency-spread $\Delta f$ satisfy:

$$
\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}
$$

**Equality** holds only for the **Gaussian window** $w(t) = e^{-\alpha t^2}$.

**Effect of window length:**

| Window length | $\Delta t$ | $\Delta f$ | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short (e.g., 20 ms) | Small (good) | Large (poor) | Detecting transients, QRS complex |
| Long (e.g., 500 ms) | Large (poor) | Small (good) | Stationary tonal analysis |

**Sketch description:** Draw a 2-D time-frequency plane showing two rectangles ŌĆö one tall and narrow (short window, good time but poor frequency resolution) and one short and wide (long window, good frequency but poor time resolution). The Gaussian logon achieves the most compact rectangle of area $\frac{1}{4\pi}$, and is therefore the optimal STFT window.

**Justification of Gaussian optimality:** The Gaussian function is the unique function that saturates the Heisenberg inequality. It also has the property that its Fourier Transform is itself a Gaussian, preserving symmetry between time and frequency domains ŌĆö an essential feature for time-frequency analysis.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the discrete-time Short-Time Fourier Transform (DSTFT) with its mathematical formulation. Discuss the importance of window overlap and the COLA condition for perfect signal reconstruction. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare the STFT with the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) in terms of resolution, basis functions, and suitability for biomedical signal analysis. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Discrete-Time STFT and COLA Condition (7 marks):**

For a discrete signal $x(n)$ sampled at $f_s$ and an $N$-point window $w(n)$, the DSTFT is:

$$
X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n + mH) w(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

where $m$ is the frame index, $k$ is the frequency bin, and $H$ is the hop size (number of samples advanced per frame).

**Window overlap:** Successive frames share $N - H$ samples. A 50% overlap ($H = N/2$) is standard. Overlap is critical because:  
(i) It avoids losing transient events that fall on window boundaries.  
(ii) It provides smoother time evolution of the spectrogram.  
(iii) It enables perfect reconstruction.

**COLA (Constant Overlap-Add) condition:** For perfect reconstruction of $x(n)$ from the DSTFT, the window must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} w(n - mH) = C \quad \text{(a constant, for all } n\text{)}
$$

The Hann window with 50% overlap satisfies COLA. The rectangular window does *not* satisfy COLA except at 0% or 100% overlap, and hence is unsuitable for high-fidelity overlap-add reconstruction.

**(b) STFT vs. CWT (7 marks):**

| Feature | STFT | CWT |
|---|---|---|
| Basis function | Fixed-modulation window $w(t)$ | Dilated and translated mother wavelet $\psi((t-\tau)/a)$ |
| Resolution | Uniform $\Delta t, \Delta f$ across all frequencies | Adaptive: good time resolution at high $f$, good frequency resolution at low $f$ |
| Tiling of T-F plane | Rectangular grid of equal-area tiles | Variable-area tiles (short windows at high $f$, long windows at low $f$) |
| Complexity | $O(N \log N)$ per frame | $O(N \cdot \text{scales})$ ŌĆö higher |
| Best for | Quasi-stationary signals, ECG R-peak analysis, PCG tones | Signals with broad frequency range, transients of varying scales, EEG spikes |
| Reconstruction | Yes (with COLA) | Yes (with admissible wavelet) |

**Suitability for biomedical signals:**  
The STFT is the preferred choice when the signal has **quasi-stationary segments of known duration** (e.g., systolic phase of PCG, sustained EEG rhythms). The CWT is preferred when analyzing **multi-scale transients** like the QRS complex, action potentials, or seizure spikes, where high-frequency components are brief and low-frequency components are prolonged.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Resulting in Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the complex conjugate** of the window in the STFT integral ŌĆö costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing STFT with spectrogram** ŌĆö STFT is complex; spectrogram is real and non-negative. Mixing up the two loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Stating $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1$** ŌĆö the correct constant is $\frac{1}{4\pi}$ (Gabor). Writing $1$ or $0.5$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Saying "STFT has no resolution limit"** ŌĆö the Heisenberg principle applies; the resolution is *bounded* below. Lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Omitting the COLA condition** when discussing DSTFT reconstruction ŌĆö lose 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Confusing window length with overlap** ŌĆö overlap = $N - H$ (in samples), where $H$ is the hop size. Many students write overlap = $H$. This loses 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **STFT is the Fourier Transform of a windowed, locally-stationary segment of a signal.**
- The window function $w(t-\tau)$ is the heart of the STFT ŌĆö it dictates resolution and leakage.
- **Spectrogram** = $\vert \text{STFT} \vert^2$ ŌĆö real, non-negative, energy density.
- The **time-frequency resolution trade-off** is governed by **HeisenbergŌĆōGabor uncertainty**: $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$.
- The **Gaussian window** achieves the lower bound and is the *optimal* STFT window.
- **Hamming window** is widely used in biomedical signal processing because of its **ŌłÆ43 dB** side-lobe level.
- The **DSTFT** is computed as $X(m, k) = \sum x(n+mH) w(n) e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ with hop size $H$ and frame index $m$.
- **COLA condition** is mandatory for perfect overlap-add reconstruction from the DSTFT.
- **STFT properties**: time shift ŌåÆ phase modulation; frequency shift ŌåÆ frequency translation; linearity holds.
- The **STFT is redundant** ŌĆö multiple windowed representations of the same signal exist.
- **STFT vs CWT**: STFT has *fixed* resolution; CWT has *adaptive* multi-resolution.
- The STFT is the **clinical standard** for PCG, EEG, and EMG time-frequency visualization in hospitals today.
- **Practical window lengths**: ECG ŌåÆ 20ŌĆō50 ms; EEG ŌåÆ 100ŌĆō500 ms; PCG ŌåÆ 20ŌĆō100 ms; EMG bursts ŌåÆ 50ŌĆō250 ms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time-Frequency Resolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Time-Frequency Resolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Time-Frequency Resolution** is defined as the simultaneous ability of a signal analysis transform to resolve and distinguish signal features in **both the time domain** (when an event occurs) and the **frequency domain** (what oscillatory content is present). It is mathematically bounded by the **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle**, which states that a signal cannot be localized to an arbitrarily small region in the joint time-frequency plane.

In the context of biomedical signal processing, time-frequency resolution quantifies **how precisely a transform (such as the STFT, Wavelet Transform, or Wigner-Ville Distribution) can pinpoint (a)** the *instant* a transient bio-event (e.g., QRS complex, EEG spike, or EMG burst) occurs, and **b)** the *spectral composition* of that event at that instant.

The two constituent resolutions are:
- **Time Resolution (╬öt):** Ability to distinguish two events closely spaced in time.
- **Frequency Resolution (╬öf):** Ability to distinguish two spectral components closely spaced in frequency.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the *trade-off* between ╬öt and ╬öf, the role of **window length N**, and the comparison of constant-bandwidth (STFT) versus constant-Q (Wavelet) tilings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **filming a hummingbird's wings with a camera**:
- If you use a **very fast shutter speed (short window)**, you freeze the wings sharply in time ŌĆö excellent *time resolution* ŌĆö but the resulting image is dark, blurry, and you cannot tell the color (frequency) of the feathers.
- If you use a **very slow shutter speed (long window)**, the wings appear as a colorful blur ŌĆö excellent *frequency/color resolution* ŌĆö but the exact moment a wing flap occurred is lost.

You **cannot have both simultaneously**. This is the essence of the time-frequency resolution trade-off. In biomedical terms, you must choose: do you want to know *exactly when* a heart murmur happened, or *exactly what pitch* it had? Longer analysis ŌåÆ better frequency detail; shorter analysis ŌåÆ better time detail.

The **mathematical constant** governing this trade-off is bounded by:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi} \quad \text{(in radians)}$$

which is a manifestation of the **Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle** in signal processing.

> [!TIP] **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams**
> Remember the constant **1/(4ŽĆ) Ōēł 0.0796** for radian frequency form, or equivalently **┬Į** in normalized form. The product $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ can NEVER go below this floor, no matter what window you choose.

---

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering

Biomedical signals ŌĆö ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, EOG ŌĆö are **non-stationary**. Their spectral content (e.g., dominant rhythm in EEG alpha vs. beta bands) changes from millisecond to millisecond. Time-frequency resolution is therefore the **central design parameter** for any clinical diagnostic algorithm built on spectral analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Trade-Off: Step-by-Step Logic

The operational logic of time-frequency resolution can be decomposed into five ordered steps that the KTU examiner frequently tests:

1. **Choice of Analysis Window $w(t)$:** Every time-frequency transform applies a localized window $w(t)$ of finite duration to the infinite-duration signal $x(t)$.
2. **Window Duration Governs ╬öt:** A short window $\Rightarrow$ small $\Delta t$ (sharp time localization) $\Rightarrow$ broad main lobe in frequency $\Rightarrow$ poor $\Delta f$.
3. **Window Duration Governs ╬öf (inversely):** A long window $\Rightarrow$ large $\Delta t$ $\Rightarrow$ narrow main lobe in frequency $\Rightarrow$ excellent $\Delta f$.
4. **Heisenberg Floor:** The product $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ is bounded below by a constant; increasing one resolution necessarily degrades the other.
5. **Window Shape Matters:** Although the *area* of the resolution cell is fixed, its *shape* (rectangular, Hamming, Gaussian, Kaiser) redistributes resolution between time and frequency.

## 2.2 The Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle

For any finite-energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier transform $X(\omega)$, the **time variance** $\sigma_t^2$ and **frequency variance** $\sigma_\omega^2$ satisfy:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \frac{\int t^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt}{\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt}$$

$$\sigma_\omega^2 = \frac{\int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}{\int \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}$$

The **Heisenberg-Gabor inequality** is:

$$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Equality Condition**
> Equality $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega = \frac{1}{2}$ holds **if and only if** $x(t)$ is a **Gaussian function**. This is why the Gabor transform and Morlet wavelet (Gaussian-enveloped) achieve the *optimal* joint resolution.

## 2.3 Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) Resolution

The STFT is defined as:

$$X(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot w^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

For a discrete-time signal sampled at $f_s$ with window length $N$ samples, the practical resolution bounds are:

$$\Delta t \approx \frac{N}{f_s} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

$$\Delta f \approx \frac{f_s}{N} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

This confirms the inverse relationship: **doubling N doubles ╬öt but halves ╬öf**, keeping the product constant:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 1 \quad \text{(for STFT with rectangular window, approximate)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Variable | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heisenberg-Gabor Inequality (radian) | $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ | Variances in time and frequency | Most frequently asked 3-mark question |
| 2 | Heisenberg-Gabor (Hz) | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | Hz units | Engineering (practical) form |
| 3 | STFT Definition | $X(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) w^{*}(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | Shift $\tau$, frequency $\omega$ | Foundation of Module 2 |
| 4 | STFT Time Resolution | $\Delta t \approx N / f_s$ | $N$ = window length, $f_s$ = sampling freq. | Direct proportionality to N |
| 5 | STFT Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f \approx f_s / N$ | Inverse proportionality to N | The "see-saw" with $\Delta t$ |
| 6 | Time-Bandwidth Product | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 1$ (STFT rect. window) | Constant for fixed transform | Trade-off proof |
| 7 | Spectrogram | $S(\tau, \omega) = \vert X(\tau, \omega) \vert^2$ | Magnitude-squared of STFT | Used in EEG/ECG analysis |
| 8 | Optimal Window (Gabor) | $w(t) = e^{-t^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | Gaussian envelope | Achieves Heisenberg lower bound |
| 9 | Wavelet Constant-Q | $\Delta f / f = \text{constant}$ | Logarithmic scaling | Multi-resolution analysis |
| 10 | Number of Frequency Bins | $K = f_s / \Delta f = N$ | Equal to window length | DFT relationship |

> [!NOTE] **Units Reminder**
> $\sigma_\omega$ is in **rad/s**; convert to Hz using $\sigma_f = \sigma_\omega / (2\pi)$. The product $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f$ has units of seconds┬ĘHz = dimensionless.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG Analysis:** Detecting QRS complexes (requires high $\Delta t$, ~10 ms) vs. analyzing ST-segment T-wave alternans (requires high $\Delta f$).
- **EEG Spectral Mapping:** Distinguishing alpha (8ŌĆō13 Hz) from beta (13ŌĆō30 Hz) rhythms needs sufficient $\Delta f$, while transient epileptic spikes need sharp $\Delta t$.
- **PCG (Heart Sound) Analysis:** Identifying S1/S2 lub-dub timing vs. diagnosing murmurs by frequency content.
- **EMG Fatigue Analysis:** Time-frequency ridges reveal motor unit recruitment patterns.
- **Doppler Ultrasound / Blood Flow:** Time-varying spectral shifts require joint resolution to diagnose stenosis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle (Heuristic via Schwarz Inequality)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 2 and is worth 7 marks in a typical Part B question.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Setup the Parseval-type integrals.**
Define the energy of the signal and its Fourier transform:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the time and frequency means.**
Assume the signal is centered so that the mean time $\bar{t} = 0$ and mean frequency $\bar{\omega} = 0$. Then the variances are:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \frac{1}{E} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

$$\sigma_\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi E} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.**
Consider the product of two $L^2$ functions: $f(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ and $g(t) = x'(t) = dx/dt$. By Cauchy-Schwarz:

$$\left( \int \vert f(t) \vert^2 dt \right) \left( \int \vert g(t) \vert^2 dt \right) \geq \left\vert \int f(t) g^{*}(t) dt \right\vert^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the three integrals.**

$$\int \vert f(t) \vert^2 dt = \int t^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = E \sigma_t^2$$

For $g(t) = x'(t)$, by Parseval's theorem:

$$\int \vert g(t) \vert^2 dt = \int \vert x'(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega = E \sigma_\omega^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the cross-term.**

$$\int f(t) g^{*}(t) dt = \int t \cdot x(t) \cdot x'^{*}(t) dt$$

The cross-term simplifies (using $x \cdot x'^{*} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{d}{dt} \vert x \vert^2$):

$$\int t \cdot \frac{1}{2} \frac{d}{dt} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = -\frac{1}{2} \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = -\frac{E}{2}$$

(The boundary term vanishes because finite-energy signals decay as $\vert t \vert \to \infty$.)

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the inequality.**

$$(E \sigma_t^2)(E \sigma_\omega^2) \geq \left\vert -\frac{E}{2} \right\vert^2 = \frac{E^2}{4}$$

Dividing both sides by $E^2$:

$$\sigma_t^2 \cdot \sigma_\omega^2 \geq \frac{1}{4}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}}$$

This is the **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle** in radian-frequency form.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Equality condition.**
Equality in Cauchy-Schwarz holds when $f(t) = c \cdot g(t)$, i.e., when $t \cdot x(t) = c \cdot x'(t)$. Solving this ODE yields:

$$x(t) = A \cdot e^{-t^2 / (2c)}$$

which is a **Gaussian pulse**. Hence, the Gaussian achieves the theoretical lower bound of joint resolution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: STFT Time and Frequency Resolution from Window Length

Given a discrete signal $x[n]$ of length $L$, sampled at $f_s$, segmented into frames using a window $w[n]$ of length $N$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Time-domain localization.**
The window isolates a portion of $x[n]$ of duration:

$$\Delta t = \frac{N}{f_s} \quad \text{seconds}$$

This is because $N$ samples cover $N / f_s$ seconds of the signal.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency-domain localization via DFT.**
Applying the DFT of length $N$ to the windowed segment produces $N$ frequency bins, spanning the Nyquist range $[0, f_s/2]$ (or $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$ for two-sided). The spacing between adjacent bins is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} \quad \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the product.**

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \frac{N}{f_s} \cdot \frac{f_s}{N} = 1$$

The product is exactly **unity** (dimensionless: seconds ┬Ę Hz = 1) for the rectangular window.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Example numerical demonstration.**
Let $f_s = 1000$ Hz and $N = 256$ samples.

$$\Delta t = \frac{256}{1000} = 0.256 \text{ s}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{1000}{256} \approx 3.906 \text{ Hz}$$

If we **double** $N$ to 512:

$$\Delta t = 0.512 \text{ s} \quad (\text{time resolution worsens by 2├Ś})$$

$$\Delta f = 1.953 \text{ Hz} \quad (\text{frequency resolution improves by 2├Ś})$$

This **numerically confirms** the trade-off. The product remains 1 in both cases.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resolution Cell Area in the Time-Frequency Plane

The "resolution cell" is the conceptual rectangle of dimensions $\Delta t \times \Delta f$ that a transform places at each point $(\tau, \omega)$ of the time-frequency plane.

**Step 1 ŌĆö STFT tiling (constant bandwidth).**

For STFT, $\Delta f$ is constant for all $\omega$. The tiling consists of identical rectangles:

$$\text{Area}_{\text{STFT}} = \Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Wavelet tiling (constant-Q).**

For the wavelet transform, the relative bandwidth is constant:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta f}{f} = \text{constant} \Rightarrow \Delta f \propto f$$

This means high-frequency bins have larger $\Delta f$ (wider cells) and low-frequency bins have smaller $\Delta f$ (narrower cells). The time resolution $\Delta t$ is **inversely proportional** to the central frequency.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compare the two tilings using a worked example.**

For a 4-level wavelet decomposition of an EEG signal bandlimited to 0ŌĆō30 Hz:

| Level | Frequency Band (Hz) | $\Delta f$ (Hz) | $\Delta t$ (ms) | Resolution Cell |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Detail) | 15 ŌĆō 30 | 15 | ~33 | Wide freq, sharp time |
| 2 (Detail) | 7.5 ŌĆō 15 | 7.5 | ~66 | Moderate both |
| 3 (Detail) | 3.75 ŌĆō 7.5 | 3.75 | ~133 | Sharp freq, blurry time |
| 4 (Approx.) | 0 ŌĆō 3.75 | 3.75 | ~266 | Very sharp freq, very blurry time |

> [!TIP] **Interpretation for EEG**
> Spike detection (transient, broadband) is best at **Level 1**; rhythm identification (sustained alpha) is best at **Levels 3ŌĆō4**. This is the practical power of constant-Q analysis.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Time-Frequency Transforms

| Transform | $\Delta t$ Behavior | $\Delta f$ Behavior | Optimal Application | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| STFT (short N) | Excellent (small) | Poor (large) | Transient detection (QRS) | Smearing of spectra |
| STFT (long N) | Poor (large) | Excellent (small) | Harmonic analysis (PCG) | Misses rapid events |
| Wavelet (high scale) | Poor | Excellent | Low-frequency rhythms | Computationally heavy |
| Wavelet (low scale) | Excellent | Poor | Spike / burst detection | Aliasing in coarse scales |
| Wigner-Ville | Moderate | Moderate | High-resolution analysis | Cross-term interference |
| Gabor Transform | Optimal (Gaussian) | Optimal (Gaussian) | Theoretical benchmark | Same as STFT essentially |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: STFT Tiling of the Time-Frequency Plane (Constant Bandwidth)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Input Signal x t] --> B[Apply Window w t of length N]
    B --> C[Compute DFT of length N]
    C --> D[STFT Coefficient X tau omega]
    D --> E{Tiling Position in TF Plane}
    E --> F1[Cell at tau1 omega1]
    E --> F2[Cell at tau2 omega2]
    E --> F3[Cell at tau3 omega3]
    E --> F4[Cell at tau4 omega4]
    F1 --> G1[Width Delta t = N over fs]
    F1 --> G2[Height Delta f = fs over N]
    G1 --> H[IDENTICAL RECTANGULAR CELLS]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Constant Bandwidth Tiling]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> Every cell of the STFT tiling is a rectangle of dimensions $\Delta t \times \Delta f$. The cells are **identical in size** (uniform tiling), which is the hallmark of STFT.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Wavelet Tiling of the Time-Frequency Plane (Constant-Q)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Input Signal x t] --> B[Wavelet Decomposition Tree]
    B --> C1[Level 1 Detail 15 to 30 Hz]
    B --> C2[Level 2 Detail 7.5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C3[Level 3 Detail 3.75 to 7.5 Hz]
    B --> C4[Level 4 Approximation 0 to 3.75 Hz]
    C1 --> D1[Wide Delta f Narrow Delta t]
    C2 --> D2[Moderate Delta f Moderate Delta t]
    C3 --> D3[Narrow Delta f Wide Delta t]
    C4 --> D4[Very Narrow Delta f Very Wide Delta t]
    D1 --> E[Logarithmic Octave Band Tiling]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Constant Q Property Delta f over f is fixed]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Trade-Off Visualization Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Short Window N small] --> B[Delta t EXCELLENT]
    A --> C[Delta f POOR]
    D[Long Window N large] --> E[Delta t POOR]
    D --> F[Delta f EXCELLENT]
    B --> G{Trade Off Floor}
    C --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Heisenberg Bound Delta t times Delta f is greater than or equal to 1 over 4 pi]
    I[Gaussian Window] --> J[OPTIMAL Joint Resolution]
    J --> H
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of a Time-Frequency Analyzer

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[Biomedical Signal Source ECG EEG EMG] --> S2[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    S2 --> S3[ADC Sampling at fs]
    S3 --> S4[Frame Buffer of length N]
    S4 --> S5[Window Function Selector]
    S5 --> S51[Rectangular]
    S5 --> S52[Hamming]
    S5 --> S53[Gaussian Optimal]
    S51 --> S6[FFT Engine]
    S52 --> S6
    S53 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[STFT Coefficient X tau omega]
    S7 --> S8[Magnitude Squared equals Spectrogram]
    S8 --> S9[Time Frequency Display Scalogram]
    S9 --> S10[Clinical Diagnosis Output]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define Time-Frequency Resolution. State the Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle in terms of time and frequency variances.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Time-Frequency Resolution** is the ability of a signal analysis method to simultaneously resolve features in both the time domain and the frequency domain.
>
> The **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle** states that for any finite-energy signal $x(t)$, the product of standard deviations in time ($\sigma_t$) and frequency ($\sigma_\omega$) is bounded below by $\frac{1}{2}$:
>
> $$\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$
>
> This means we cannot localize a signal to an arbitrarily small region in the joint time-frequency plane. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**What is the significance of choosing a Gaussian window in the Short-Time Fourier Transform?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Gaussian window $w(t) = e^{-t^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ is significant because:

1. It is the **only** function that achieves equality in the Heisenberg-Gabor inequality: $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega = \frac{1}{2}$. **(1 mark)**
2. It provides the **optimal joint time-frequency localization**, i.e., the resolution cell has the **minimum possible area**. **(1 mark)**
3. It minimizes side-lobe leakage in the frequency domain, giving cleaner spectral estimates for biomedical signals. **(1 mark)**

This is why the **Gabor transform** (STFT with Gaussian window) is the theoretical benchmark for time-frequency analysis.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) An ECG signal is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. Compute the time and frequency resolutions of the STFT if the window length is (i) $N = 128$ and (ii) $N = 512$. Comment on the trade-off in each case.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

The derivation proceeds as follows:

1. Define energy $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$. **(1 mark)**
2. Define variances $\sigma_t^2 = \frac{1}{E} \int t^2 \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ and $\sigma_\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi E} \int \omega^2 \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$. **(1 mark)**
3. Set $f(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ and $g(t) = x'(t)$. Apply Cauchy-Schwarz: $\int \vert f \vert^2 \int \vert g \vert^2 \geq \vert \int f g^* \vert^2$. **(2 marks)**
4. Compute integrals: $\int \vert f \vert^2 = E \sigma_t^2$, $\int \vert g \vert^2 = E \sigma_\omega^2$, and the cross-term $\int t \cdot x(t) x'^{*}(t) \, dt = -E/2$. **(2 marks)**
5. Substitute and simplify to get $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$. **(1 mark)**

(Full derivation already shown in SECTION 3.1.)

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Given: $f_s = 500$ Hz.

**Case (i): $N = 128$**

$$\Delta t = \frac{N}{f_s} = \frac{128}{500} = 0.256 \text{ s}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{500}{128} \approx 3.906 \text{ Hz}$$

**Case (ii): $N = 512$**

$$\Delta t = \frac{512}{500} = 1.024 \text{ s}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{500}{512} \approx 0.977 \text{ Hz}$$

**Trade-off Comment (2 marks):**

- In **Case (i)**, the short window gives **better time resolution (0.256 s)** but **poorer frequency resolution (3.906 Hz)**. This is suitable for detecting **QRS complexes** or sharp transients.
- In **Case (ii)**, the long window gives **worse time resolution (1.024 s)** but **excellent frequency resolution (0.977 Hz)**. This is suitable for analyzing fine **spectral details** like murmur harmonics.
- In both cases, $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 1$, confirming the **Heisenberg trade-off**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 ŌĆō Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Explain the time-frequency tiling diagrams of the STFT and Wavelet Transform. How do they differ in resolution characteristics?** **(7 marks)**

**(b) An EEG signal with band 0.5ŌĆō40 Hz is sampled at 200 Hz. A 4-level wavelet packet decomposition is performed. Determine the frequency band, $\Delta f$, and $\Delta t$ at each level. Assume the wavelet uses dyadic sampling.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

1. **STFT Tiling (3 marks):** The STFT produces a **uniform rectangular tiling** of the time-frequency plane. Each cell has constant width $\Delta t = N/f_s$ and constant height $\Delta f = f_s/N$. Hence, the **bandwidth is constant** for all frequencies.

2. **Wavelet Tiling (3 marks):** The wavelet transform produces a **logarithmic octave-band tiling**. Higher frequencies have wider cells (good time, poor frequency), and lower frequencies have taller cells (poor time, good frequency). The relative bandwidth $Q = \Delta f / f$ is **constant**.

3. **Practical Difference (1 mark):** STFT is preferred for **stationary or slowly varying** signals; wavelet is preferred for **transient-rich** signals like EEG spikes and EMG bursts.

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Given: $f_s = 200$ Hz, 4-level dyadic wavelet packet decomposition.

The signal bandwidth is $0$ to $f_s/2 = 100$ Hz. At each level, the bandwidth is halved.

| Level | Frequency Band (Hz) | $\Delta f$ (Hz) | Samples / cycle | $\Delta t$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 ŌĆō 100 | 50 | 200/50 = 4 | 20 |
| 2 | 25 ŌĆō 50 | 25 | 200/25 = 8 | 40 |
| 3 | 12.5 ŌĆō 25 | 12.5 | 200/12.5 = 16 | 80 |
| 4 | 6.25 ŌĆō 12.5 | 6.25 | 200/6.25 = 32 | 160 |

(Each row worth 1.5 marks; trade-off comment worth 1 mark.)

**Comment:** Lower levels (higher frequencies) give better time resolution for spike detection; higher levels (lower frequencies) give better frequency resolution for rhythm analysis ŌĆö confirming the **constant-Q property**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion (ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks):** Students frequently write $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{2}$ using **Hz**, but the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ applies only to **radian frequency** $\omega$. For Hz, the correct bound is $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$. Always check the units.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Constant in STFT (ŌĆō1 mark):** Many students write $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 1$ without stating it is for the **rectangular window**; for Hamming or Hanning windows, the product is slightly larger (~1.3 to 1.5).
>
> 3. **Swapping ╬öt and ╬öf (ŌĆō2 marks):** A common error is to state that a long window gives *better* time resolution. Memorize: **long window ŌćÆ poor ╬öt, good ╬öf** (and vice versa).
>
> 4. **Confusing Resolution with Accuracy (ŌĆō1 mark):** Resolution refers to the **ability to distinguish two close features**, not the absolute accuracy of a measurement. A transform can be highly accurate yet have poor resolution.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Equality Condition (ŌĆō1 mark):** In derivations, never forget to mention that the Gaussian is the unique minimizer of the uncertainty product.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Heisenberg-Gabor Inequality** (radian): $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$; (Hz): $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$.
> - **Gaussian** is the **only** function achieving the lower bound.
> - **STFT Time Resolution:** $\Delta t = N / f_s$ ŌĆö proportional to window length.
> - **STFT Frequency Resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / N$ ŌĆö inversely proportional to window length.
> - **Time-Bandwidth Product** (STFT, rectangular window) = 1.
> - **STFT Tiling:** Uniform rectangular cells ŌćÆ **constant bandwidth** across all frequencies.
> - **Wavelet Tiling:** Logarithmic octave bands ŌćÆ **constant-Q** $\Delta f / f = \text{constant}$.
> - **Wigner-Ville Distribution:** Best resolution but suffers from **cross-term interference**.
> - **Practical biomedical rule of thumb:** Use **short windows** for QRS / spike detection; use **long windows** for rhythm / harmonic analysis.
> - **Higher-order windows** (Hamming, Kaiser) reduce spectral leakage at the cost of a slightly larger resolution cell.
> - **DFT bins** = $N$; hence $\Delta f = f_s / N$ directly.
> - **Optimal joint localization** ŌćÆ Gabor / Morlet (Gaussian) kernels.
> - **Units check:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ is **dimensionless** (seconds ┬Ę Hz = 1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiresolution Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiresolution Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Multiresolution Analysis (MRA)**, as formalized by **Stephane Mallat (1989)** and **Yves Meyer**, is a mathematical framework used to represent a signal $f(t) \in L^2(\mathbb{R})$ as a limit of successive approximations at progressively coarser resolutions. Formally, an MRA is a sequence of closed subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ that satisfies a set of axioms defining a nested, shift-invariant, and scale-invariant approximation scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MRA is the *theoretical backbone* of the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT). Every KTU numerical or descriptive question on DWT in this module assumes mastery of MRA fundamentals.

### 1.2 The Five MRA Axioms (Mathematical Closure Properties)

A sequence of subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ qualifies as a Multiresolution Analysis if and only if the following axioms are satisfied:

1. **Nestedness (Monotonicity):** $\cdots \subset V_2 \subset V_1 \subset V_0 \subset V_{-1} \subset V_{-2} \subset \cdots$
2. **Density (Completeness in the limit):** $\overline{\bigcup_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
3. **Separation (Trivial intersection):** $\bigcap_{j \in \mathbb{Z}} V_j = \{0\}$
4. **Scaling Property:** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$
5. **Translation Invariance:** $f(t) \in V_0 \implies f(t - n) \in V_0 \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Function:** There must exist a function $\phi(t) \in V_0$, called the **scaling function** (also *father wavelet*), whose integer translates $\{\phi(t-n)\}_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}$ form a **Riesz basis** (or orthonormal basis) for $V_0$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Zoom Lens" View

Imagine a **pathologist** examining a tissue biopsy slide under a microscope:

- At **low magnification (coarse resolution, $V_J$)**, she sees the overall tissue architecture.
- At **medium magnification ($V_{J-1}$)**, she sees cellular patterns and groupings.
- At **high magnification ($V_{J-K}$, finest resolution)**, she sees the nuclear structure of individual cells.

**MRA works exactly like this** ŌĆö the original biomedical signal is decomposed into a *coarse approximation* (overall trend) plus a hierarchy of *detail layers* (fine features). At each level, you can choose the "magnification" best suited to diagnose the condition (e.g., detecting the QRS complex in ECG, or spike-and-wave in EEG).

| MRA Term | Microscopy Analogy | Biomedical Signal Example |
|---|---|---|
| $V_0$ (finest space) | 100x oil-immersion lens | Raw, unfiltered ECG sample |
| $V_{-J}$ (coarsest space) | Naked eye | Smoothed baseline of ECG |
| Scaling function $\phi(t)$ | Binoculars | Approximation coefficients |
| Wavelet function $\psi(t)$ | Magnifier toggle | Detail coefficients (R-peak, T-wave) |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Introduced

- **Approximation Coefficients ($c_{j,k}$):** Captures the *slow-varying, low-frequency* content of the signal at scale $j$.
- **Detail Coefficients ($d_{j,k}$):** Captures the *fast-varying, high-frequency* content at scale $j$.
- **Scale Index $j$:** Negative $j$ ŌåÆ finer resolution; Positive $j$ ŌåÆ coarser resolution.
- **Wavelet Subspace $W_j$:** Orthogonal complement of $V_j$ in $V_{j-1}$, i.e., $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Scale Relation (Dilation Equation)

The scaling function at a coarser scale can be expressed in terms of scaled and translated versions of itself at the next finer scale:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) \, \phi(2t - n)$$

The wavelet function (mother wavelet) is related to the scaling function via the wavelet filter $g(n)$:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} g(n) \, \phi(2t - n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** These two relations are the bridge between *continuous-time scaling/wavelet functions* and the *discrete-time filters* ($h$ and $g$) used in Mallat's algorithm. Exam questions frequently ask to derive one from the other or to compute the filter coefficients.

### 2.2 The Orthogonality Constraint (Quadrature Mirror Filter)

For an orthogonal wavelet basis, the filters must satisfy the **power complementary condition**:

$$\vert H(\omega) \vert^2 + \vert H(\omega + \pi) \vert^2 = 2$$

And the wavelet filter is related to the scaling filter by:

$$g(n) = (-1)^n h(1 - n)$$

### 2.3 The Decomposition of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ via MRA

The full signal space is decomposed as a direct sum of orthogonal wavelet subspaces:

$$L^2(\mathbb{R}) = V_J \oplus W_J \oplus W_{J-1} \oplus \cdots \oplus W_{J-M+1}$$

Any signal $f(t)$ can therefore be reconstructed as:

$$f(t) = \sum_{k} c_{J,k} \, \phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=J-M+1}^{J} \sum_{k} d_{j,k} \, \psi_{j,k}(t)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical KTU Use** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}t - k)$ | Scaled/translated basis | Writing basis functions |
| 2 | $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j}t - k)$ | Wavelet basis | Detail representation |
| 3 | $c_{j,k} = \sum_{n} h(n - 2k) \, c_{j-1,n}$ | Approx. coeff. recursion | Mallat decomposition |
| 4 | $d_{j,k} = \sum_{n} g(n - 2k) \, c_{j-1,n}$ | Detail coeff. recursion | Mallat decomposition |
| 5 | $c_{j-1,n} = \sum_{k} h(n - 2k) \, c_{j,k} + \sum_{k} g(n - 2k) \, d_{j,k}$ | Reconstruction | Mallat synthesis |
| 6 | $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ | Subspace relation | Prove orthogonality |
| 7 | $\int \phi(t) \, dt = 1$ (normalization) | DC gain of scaling function | Check admissibility |
| 8 | $\int \psi(t) \, dt = 0$ (admissibility) | DC gain of wavelet | Zero-mean condition |
| 9 | $\sum_{n} h(n) = \sqrt{2}$ | Low-pass DC gain | Verify low-pass nature |
| 10 | $\sum_{n} h(n) (-1)^n = 0$ | High-pass attenuation | Verify high-pass nature |
| 11 | $\sum_{n} h^2(n) = 1$ | Energy preservation | Orthonormality check |
| 12 | $\vert H(\omega) \vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi) \vert^2 = 2$ | Power complementary | QMF condition |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG QRS Detection:** The detail coefficients $\{d_{j,k}\}$ at scale $j=1$ to $j=4$ isolate the high-frequency QRS complex, while $\{c_{4,k}\}$ captures the baseline wander ŌĆö perfect for arrhythmia classification.
- **EEG Spike Detection:** MRA-based de-noising preserves sharp epileptiform transients while suppressing background noise.
- **EMG Analysis:** Multi-scale decomposition separates voluntary motor unit activity from artifact noise.
- **Compression:** Set small wavelet coefficients to zero (thresholding) for high-fidelity lossy compression of medical records (JPEG2000 uses CDF 9/7 biorthogonal wavelets ŌĆö an MRA-built system).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Two-Scale Relation for the Scaling Function

**Statement to Prove:** If $\phi(t) \in V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, and $\{\phi_{-1,n}(t)\}$ is an orthonormal basis of $V_{-1}$, then $\phi(t)$ can be written as $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h(n) \phi(2t - n)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from subspace membership:**
Since $\phi(t) \in V_0$ and $V_0 \subset V_{-1}$, the function $\phi(t)$ lies in the finer subspace $V_{-1}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand in the basis of $V_{-1}$:**
Any function in $V_{-1}$ can be written as a linear combination of the basis $\{\phi_{-1,n}(t)\}$:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) \, \phi_{-1,n}(t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the definition of $\phi_{-1,n}(t)$:**
By definition, the basis of $V_{-1}$ is given by $\phi_{-1,n}(t) = 2^{1/2} \phi(2t - n)$. Substituting:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) \cdot \sqrt{2} \, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Form:**
Factoring out the constant $\sqrt{2}$:

$$\boxed{\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) \, \phi(2t - n)}$$

> This is the canonical **dilation equation**. The coefficients $h(n)$ are the *scaling (low-pass) filter* coefficients of the DWT.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Wavelet and Scaling Filters

**Given:** $g(n) = (-1)^n h(1 - n)$, prove that $\{g(n)\}$ corresponds to a high-pass filter that is the mirror of $h(n)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the DTFT of $g(n)$:**

$$G(\omega) = \sum_{n} g(n) e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n} (-1)^n h(1 - n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $m = 1 - n$, so $n = 1 - m$:**

$$G(\omega) = \sum_{m} (-1)^{1-m} h(m) e^{-j\omega(1-m)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Factor:**

$$G(\omega) = -e^{-j\omega} \sum_{m} (-1)^{-m} h(m) e^{j\omega m} = -e^{-j\omega} \sum_{m} h(m) e^{j\omega m} e^{-j\pi m}$$

(Using $(-1)^{-m} = e^{-j\pi m}$ for integer $m$)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine exponentials:**

$$G(\omega) = -e^{-j\omega} \sum_{m} h(m) e^{j(\omega - \pi) m} = -e^{-j\omega} H(\omega - \pi)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{G(\omega) = e^{-j\omega} H^{*}(\pi - \omega) \quad \text{(for real filters, no conjugation)}}$$

> **Conclusion:** Modulating $H(\omega)$ by $\pi$ shifts its spectrum to the high-frequency band, confirming $G$ is the high-pass QMF counterpart of $H$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mallat's Decomposition Algorithm

**Given** approximation coefficients $c_{j-1,k}$, derive the next-coarser-level approximation and detail coefficients.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Projection onto $V_j$:**
The projection of $f(t)$ onto $V_j$ is:

$$P_j f(t) = \sum_{k} c_{j,k} \, \phi_{j,k}(t)$$

where the coefficients are obtained by inner product:

$$c_{j,k} = \langle f(t), \phi_{j,k}(t) \rangle$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the two-scale relation:**
Substitute $\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}t - k)$ and use the dilation equation for $\phi$:

$$c_{j,k} = \sum_{n} h(n) \int f(t) \, 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}(2t) - (2k + n)) \, dt$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize the inner product pattern:**
The integral is exactly $c_{j-1, 2k+n}$. Summing over $n$ (let $m = 2k + n$, so $n = m - 2k$):

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mallat's Approximation Equation:**

$$\boxed{c_{j,k} = \sum_{n} h(n - 2k) \, c_{j-1,n}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Mallat's Detail Equation (analogous derivation using $\psi$):**

$$\boxed{d_{j,k} = \sum_{n} g(n - 2k) \, c_{j-1,n}}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Reconstruction (Inverse DWT):**

$$c_{j-1,n} = \sum_{k} h(n - 2k) \, c_{j,k} + \sum_{k} g(n - 2k) \, d_{j,k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The down-sampling by 2** (the $2k$ shift in the index) is the mathematical origin of the $\downarrow 2$ operator in every MRA block diagram.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Computing Level-1 MRA Coefficients

**Given:** Scaling filter $h = [h_0, h_1, h_2, h_3] = [\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 0, 0]$ (Haar-like truncated filter, ignore edge effects).
Wavelet filter $g(n) = (-1)^n h(1-n)$:

$g_0 = (-1)^0 h(1) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, $g_1 = (-1)^1 h(0) = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, rest $= 0$.

**Input signal (level-0 approx.):** $c_{0} = [4, 6, 10, 12, 8, 4, 6, 10]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply low-pass $h$ and downsample by 2:**

For $k=0$: $c_{1,0} = h_0 c_{0,0} + h_1 c_{0,1} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4+6) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=1$: $c_{1,1} = h_0 c_{0,2} + h_1 c_{0,3} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10+12) = \tfrac{22}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=2$: $c_{1,2} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8+4) = \tfrac{12}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=3$: $c_{1,3} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(6+10) = \tfrac{16}{\sqrt{2}}$

So $c_1 = \left[\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{22}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{12}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{16}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply high-pass $g$ and downsample by 2:**

For $k=0$: $d_{1,0} = g_0 c_{0,0} + g_1 c_{0,1} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 - 6) = -\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=1$: $d_{1,1} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 - 12) = -\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=2$: $d_{1,2} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8 - 4) = \tfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}$

For $k=3$: $d_{1,3} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(6 - 10) = -\tfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}$

So $d_1 = \left[-\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}, -\tfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Energy check (Parseval):**

$\sum c_0^2 = 16+36+100+144+64+16+36+100 = 512$

$\sum c_1^2 = 50 + 242 + 72 + 128 = 492$

$\sum d_1^2 = 2 + 2 + 8 + 8 = 20$

Total = $492 + 20 = 512$ Ō£ō **Energy preserved.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: One-Level MRA Decomposition & Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal c(j-1, n)"] --> B1["Low-Pass Filter h(n)"]
    A --> B2["High-Pass Filter g(n)"]
    B1 --> C1["Down-sample by 2"]
    B2 --> C2["Down-sample by 2"]
    C1 --> D1["Approximation c(j, k)"]
    C2 --> D2["Detail d(j, k)"]
    D1 --> E1["Up-sample by 2"]
    D2 --> E2["Up-sample by 2"]
    E1 --> F1["Low-Pass Filter h(n)"]
    E2 --> F2["High-Pass Filter g(n)"]
    F1 --> G["Adder: Reconstructed Signal"]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H["Output c(j-1, n) Reconstructed"]
```

### 4.2 Multi-Level Decomposition Tree (Wavelet Packet-Like)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["Original Signal c(0)"] --> LP1["Low-Pass h, Down 2"]
    S --> HP1["High-Pass g, Down 2"]
    LP1 --> A1["c(1) Approx Level 1"]
    HP1 --> D1["d(1) Detail Level 1"]
    A1 --> LP2["Low-Pass h, Down 2"]
    A1 --> HP2["High-Pass g, Down 2"]
    LP2 --> A2["c(2) Approx Level 2"]
    HP2 --> D2["d(2) Detail Level 2"]
    A2 --> LP3["Low-Pass h, Down 2"]
    A2 --> HP3["High-Pass g, Down 2"]
    LP3 --> A3["c(3) Approx Level 3"]
    HP3 --> D3["d(3) Detail Level 3"]
    D3 --> OUT1["Fine Detail"]
    D2 --> OUT2["Mid Detail"]
    D1 --> OUT3["Coarse Detail"]
    A3 --> OUT4["Smoothed Baseline"]
```

### 4.3 Signal Decomposition Intuition (Block View of Subspace Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph V0["V(0) Full Signal Space L2(R)"]
        subgraph V1["V(1) Coarser Approximation"]
            subgraph V2["V(2) Even Coarser Approximation"]
                A["c(2,k) phi(2,k)"]
            end
            W2["W(2) Detail at Level 2"]
        end
        W1["W(1) Detail at Level 1"]
    end
    V0 --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> A
```

### 4.4 Reconstruction Architecture (Inverse MRA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cj["c(j, k) Approx Coeff"] --> UH["Up-Sample by 2"]
    Dj["d(j, k) Detail Coeff"] --> UG["Up-Sample by 2"]
    UH --> FH["Reconstruction Filter h(n)"]
    UG --> FG["Reconstruction Filter g(n)"]
    FH --> SUM["Adder Node"]
    FG --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["Reconstructed c(j-1, n)"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Only?** The above diagrams render the **functional block architecture** of Mallat's pyramidal algorithm ŌĆö the exact form examiners expect students to reproduce in KTU 14-mark derivations. Time-domain sketching of wavelet shapes is rarely graded numerically; the *block flow* is the KTU expectation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**State and briefly explain the five axioms that a sequence of subspaces must satisfy to qualify as a Multiresolution Analysis (MRA).**

**Model Answer:**
An MRA is a sequence of closed subspaces $\{V_j\}_{j \in \mathbb{Z}}$ of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ satisfying:

1. **Nestedness:** $V_{j+1} \subset V_j$ for all $j$
2. **Density:** $\overline{\bigcup_j V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
3. **Separation:** $\bigcap_j V_j = \{0\}$
4. **Scaling:** $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j-1}$
5. **Translation invariance:** $f(t) \in V_0 \implies f(t-n) \in V_0, \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}$

Additionally, there must exist a scaling function $\phi \in V_0$ whose translates form a Riesz basis of $V_0$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**Write the two-scale (dilation) equation relating the scaling function $\phi(t)$ to its translates at the next finer scale. Why is this equation important in the DWT implementation?**

**Model Answer:**
The two-scale relation is:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h(n) \, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Importance:** It establishes the relationship between the continuous scaling function and the discrete filter coefficients $h(n)$ (scaling/low-pass filter). Using this relation, the DWT is implemented via filter banks and Mallat's pyramidal algorithm, enabling fast $O(N)$ decomposition. Without this relation, the DWT would have no efficient computational implementation.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE ŌĆö Answer ANY ONE full question from each choice pair.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the approximation coefficients $c_{j,k}$ and the coarser-level coefficients $c_{j-1,n}$ using the two-scale relation. Show that:

$$c_{j,k} = \sum_{n} h(n - 2k) \, c_{j-1,n}$$

**(b)** For a 4-tap orthogonal low-pass filter with $h = \left[\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\right]$, compute the first-level approximation and detail coefficients for the input $c_0 = [8, 4, 6, 2, 10, 8, 4, 6]$. Verify Parseval's energy theorem.

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1:** By definition, $c_{j,k} = \langle f(t), \phi_{j,k}(t) \rangle = \int f(t) \, 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j}t - k) \, dt$

**Step 2:** Replace $\phi(2^{-j}t - k)$ using the two-scale relation written at the appropriate scale:
$\phi_{j,k}(t) = \sum_n h(n) \, 2^{-(j-1)/2} \phi(2^{-(j-1)}t - (2k + n))$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:
$c_{j,k} = \sum_n h(n) \int f(t) \, 2^{-(j-1)/2} \phi(2^{-(j-1)}t - (2k + n)) \, dt$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner integral as $c_{j-1, 2k+n}$. Letting $m = 2k + n$ and rearranging:
$c_{j,k} = \sum_m h(m - 2k) \, c_{j-1, m}$ Ō£ō **(7 marks awarded for complete derivation)**

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

Given $h_0 = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.3536$, $h_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$, $h_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$, $h_3 = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.3536$.

**Wavelet filter** $g(n) = (-1)^n h(1-n)$:
- $g_0 = (-1)^0 h(1) = 0.5$
- $g_1 = (-1)^1 h(0) = -0.3536$
- $g_2 = (-1)^2 h(-1) = 0$ (zero padding)
- $g_3 = (-1)^3 h(-2) = 0$

**Approximation coefficients** (apply $h$ and downsample by 2):

$c_{1,0} = h_0 c_{0,0} + h_1 c_{0,1} + h_2 c_{0,2} + h_3 c_{0,3}$
$= 0.3536(8) + 0.5(4) + 0.5(6) + 0.3536(2) = 2.8288 + 2 + 3 + 0.7072 = 8.536$

$c_{1,1} = 0.3536(6) + 0.5(2) + 0.5(10) + 0.3536(8) = 2.1216 + 1 + 5 + 2.8288 = 10.95$

$c_{1,2} = 0.3536(4) + 0.5(6) + 0.5(0) + 0.3536(0) = 1.4144 + 3 = 4.414$

(Assuming zero-padding for missing samples)

**Detail coefficients:**

$d_{1,0} = g_0 c_{0,0} + g_1 c_{0,1} = 0.5(8) - 0.3536(4) = 4 - 1.4144 = 2.586$

$d_{1,1} = 0.5(6) - 0.3536(2) = 3 - 0.7072 = 2.293$

$d_{1,2} = 0.5(10) - 0.3536(8) = 5 - 2.8288 = 2.171$

**Parseval's Check:**

Input energy: $64+16+36+4+100+64+16+36 = 336$

Output energy: $c_1^2 = 72.86 + 119.90 + 19.48 = 212.24$
$d_1^2 = 6.687 + 5.258 + 4.713 = 16.66$
Total = $212.24 + 16.66 = 228.9$ (Discrepancy due to edge effects / truncation at sample 4)

> Normal caveat: Exact Parseval holds only for *circular* convolution; linear convolution with truncation loses edge energy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of wavelet subspace $W_j$ and prove that $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$. State the role of the wavelet function $\psi(t)$ in this decomposition.

**(b)** Explain the equivalence between MRA-based wavelet decomposition and a two-channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank. Sketch the block diagram of Mallat's pyramidal algorithm with one level of decomposition and reconstruction.

---

**Model Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

The wavelet subspace $W_j$ is defined as the *orthogonal complement* of $V_j$ in $V_{j-1}$:

$$V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j, \quad V_j \perp W_j$$

**Proof Sketch:**
- *Part 1 ŌĆö Direct Sum:* Any $f \in V_{j-1}$ can be uniquely written as $f = f_{\text{approx}} + f_{\text{detail}}$ where $f_{\text{approx}} \in V_j$ and $f_{\text{detail}} \in W_j$.
- *Part 2 ŌĆö Orthogonality:* $\langle V_j, W_j \rangle = 0$ by definition of orthogonal complement.
- *Part 3 ŌĆö Generators:* The basis of $V_j$ is $\{\phi_{j,k}\}_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$; the basis of $W_j$ is $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}$.

**Role of $\psi(t)$:** The mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ generates $W_j$ via scaling and translation: $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \psi(2^{-j}t - k)$. It captures the "detail" or "difference" information lost when moving from the finer space $V_{j-1}$ to the coarser $V_j$. It is a band-pass function satisfying $\int \psi(t) \, dt = 0$ (zero-mean admissibility).

---

**Model Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Equivalence with QMF Bank:**
The MRA decomposition can be implemented entirely using a two-channel Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank as follows:

- The low-pass filter $h(n)$ extracts the *approximation* (coarse, low-frequency) content.
- The high-pass filter $g(n)$ extracts the *detail* (high-frequency) content.
- The QMF condition $\vert H(\omega) \vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi) \vert^2 = 2$ ensures that the two filter outputs are *mirror images* about $\omega = \pi/2$, hence "quadrature mirror."
- Down-sampling by 2 after filtering is permitted by the Nyquist criterion (no aliasing due to the QMF property).
- Reconstruction uses the synthesis filters $h(n)$ and $g(n)$ followed by up-sampling by 2 and summation.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1)*

The decomposition computes $c_{j,k} = \sum_n h(n-2k) c_{j-1,n}$ and $d_{j,k} = \sum_n g(n-2k) c_{j-1,n}$.

The reconstruction computes $c_{j-1,n} = \sum_k h(n-2k) c_{j,k} + \sum_k g(n-2k) d_{j,k}$.

This pyramidal structure, iterated $J$ times, gives a complete MRA-based DWT of depth $J$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in the two-scale relation when going from continuous to discrete is the #1 deduction point (-1 to -2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing index directions** ŌĆö remember: in MRA, $j = 0$ is the *finest* (original) resolution, and $j = -1$ is *coarser* (low-pass filtered). Many students write the equations backwards.
> 3. **Mistaking the QMF condition** for "high-pass + low-pass = 1" ŌĆö it is $\vert H(\omega)\vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi)\vert^2 = 2$, not a sum of magnitudes.
> 4. **Skipping the down-sampling by 2** in the Mallat equations ŌĆö this is the *cause* of the $n-2k$ shift in indices and is frequently tested.
> 5. **Missing orthogonality proof** of $V_j \perp W_j$ in 7-mark questions ŌĆö KTU examiners specifically want this justification.

---

### ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MRA =** nested subspaces + scaling property + Riesz basis of integer translates of $\phi(t)$.
- **Dilation equation:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h(n) \phi(2t - n)$ ŌĆö links continuous $\phi$ to discrete filter $h(n)$.
- **Wavelet equation:** $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n g(n) \phi(2t - n)$, with $g(n) = (-1)^n h(1-n)$.
- **Subspace decomposition:** $V_{j-1} = V_j \oplus W_j$, and $L^2(\mathbb{R}) = V_J \oplus \bigoplus_{j<J} W_j$.
- **Mallat's Algorithm:** Decompose via $c_{j,k} = \sum_n h(n-2k) c_{j-1,n}$ and $d_{j,k} = \sum_n g(n-2k) c_{j-1,n}$; reconstruct via the inverse formula.
- **QMF Condition:** $\vert H(\omega) \vert^2 + \vert H(\omega+\pi) \vert^2 = 2$ ŌĆö this is the *orthogonality* criterion of the filter bank.
- **Admissibility:** $\int \phi(t) dt = 1$ (low-pass), $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ (band-pass).
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Energy is preserved across decomposition levels (sum of squares of $c_j$ and $d_j$ = energy of $c_{j-1}$).
- **Time-frequency tiling:** Each scale $j$ covers a *dyadic* frequency band $[2^{j+1}\pi, 2^{j+2}\pi]$ in the wavelet domain.
- **Biomedical relevance:** MRA-based DWT is the standard for ECG denoising, EEG spike detection, EMG decomposition, and medical image compression (JPEG2000).
- **Daubechies family:** Compactly-supported orthonormal wavelets ŌĆö the most commonly used MRA wavelets in practice (Db4, Db6, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wavelet Transform (WT)** is a time-frequency analysis tool that decomposes a given continuous-time or discrete-time signal $x(t)$ into a set of basis functions generated by **scaling** (dilation) and **translation** (shifting) of a single prototype function $\psi(t)$, known as the **Mother Wavelet**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the WT is positioned as the successor to the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) for analyzing **non-stationary biomedical signals** (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG) because it provides **multi-resolution analysis (MRA)** ŌĆö high time resolution at high frequencies and high frequency resolution at low frequencies.

The general form of a wavelet basis function is given by:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right), \quad a \neq 0, \; a,b \in \mathbb{R}$$

where $a$ is the **scale parameter** and $b$ is the **translation parameter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight ŌĆö Definition You Must Memorize**
> The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a signal $x(t)$ with respect to mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is:
>
> $$\text{CWT}_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$
>
> where $\psi^{*}$ denotes the complex conjugate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Zooming Microscope"

Imagine you are examining a long ECG recording on a piece of paper. You are searching for the QRS complex.

- If you step far away (large scale $a$), you can see the **overall heart rate** (low-frequency content) ŌĆö you lose the ability to see individual beats.
- If you step very close (small scale $a$), you can see the **sharp QRS spike and fine P/T wave details** (high-frequency content) ŌĆö but you cannot see the rhythm.
- You can also slide the microscope left and right (translation $b$) to scan across the signal.

This dual zooming-and-sliding capability is what makes the wavelet transform the natural choice for biomedical signals, where both timing (e.g., R-peak detection) and frequency information (e.g., QRS spectral content) are clinically critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use STFT?**
> STFT uses a **fixed window size**, so it gives equal time and frequency resolution everywhere. Wavelets use a **variable window** ŌĆö short at high frequencies and long at low frequencies. This is called **constant-Q filtering** and is ideal for signals with abrupt transients like ECG QRS complexes.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Properties

The mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ **must** satisfy the **admissibility condition** to be a valid wavelet:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega \; < \; \infty$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$.

This condition implies that:
1. $\psi(t)$ has **finite energy**.
2. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi(t) \, dt = 0$ (zero mean / bandpass behavior).
3. The wavelet behaves like a **small wave** ŌĆö oscillatory and localized in time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Wavelet Families Used in Biomedical Signal Processing (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Haar Wavelet** ŌĆö simplest, discontinuous, useful for teaching.
> - **Daubechies (dbN)** ŌĆö most commonly used for ECG/EEG denoising.
> - **Morlet Wavelet** ŌĆö complex-valued, used for time-frequency maps.
> - **Symlet, Coiflet, Meyer** ŌĆö advanced families for specific applications.
> - **Mexican Hat (Ricker)** ŌĆö second derivative of Gaussian, used in spike detection in EEG.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Wavelet Transform

The WT operates on a fundamentally different principle from the Fourier Transform. Rather than asking *"What frequencies are present?"*, it asks *"What frequencies are present, and **when** do they occur?"* ŌĆö with the frequency window automatically adapting to the time scale being analyzed.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Choose a mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$. This choice determines the trade-off between time and frequency resolution and is matched to the signal's characteristics.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Generate the wavelet dictionary** by computing $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ for all relevant scales $a$ and translations $b$. Smaller $a$ = compressed (high-frequency) wavelet; larger $a$ = stretched (low-frequency) wavelet.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the inner product** (correlation) of the signal $x(t)$ with each scaled-translated wavelet. The result $\text{CWT}_x(a,b)$ is a 2-D function of $a$ and $b$, called the **scalogram** when squared.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Reconstruct** (for CWT) or **decompose** (for DWT) the signal by combining the wavelet coefficients weighted by the dual basis.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Apply MRA** to obtain a hierarchical, sparse representation ideal for denoising, compression, and feature extraction.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Property | Mathematical Form | Notes / Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Wavelet basis (continuous) | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | $a \neq 0$ is scale, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is shift |
| 2 | Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) | $\text{CWT}_x(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | Produces 2-D coefficient map |
| 3 | Inverse CWT (Reconstruction) | $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \text{CWT}_x(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \dfrac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$ | Requires admissibility constant $C_{\psi}$ |
| 4 | Admissibility Condition | $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Necessary for perfect reconstruction |
| 5 | Discrete Scale | $a = a_{0}^{m}, \; m \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $a_{0} > 1$ (typically $a_{0}=2$) |
| 6 | Discrete Translation | $b = n \, b_{0} \, a_{0}^{m}, \; n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Often $b_{0}=1$ |
| 7 | Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) | $W(m,n) = \sum_{k} x[k] \, \psi_{m,n}[k]$ | Discrete inner product |
| 8 | Haar Mother Wavelet | $\psi(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \le t < 0.5 \\ -1, & 0.5 \le t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Simplest, step-shaped |
| 9 | Haar Scaling Function | $\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Unit pulse on $[0,1)$ |
| 10 | MRA Expansion | $x(t) = \sum_{k} c_{J,k} \phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{k} d_{j,k} \psi_{j,k}(t)$ | $c_{J,k}$: approx. coeff.; $d_{j,k}$: detail coeff. |
| 11 | Dilation Equation (Scaling) | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} h[k] \, \phi(2t-k)$ | $h[k]$: low-pass filter |
| 12 | Wavelet Equation | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} g[k] \, \phi(2t-k)$ | $g[k]$: high-pass filter, $g[k]=(-1)^{k} h[1-k]$ |
| 13 | Approximation Coefficients | $c_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k-2n] \, c_{j}[k]$ | Downsampled low-pass output |
| 14 | Detail Coefficients | $d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k-2n] \, c_{j}[k]$ | Downsampled high-pass output |
| 15 | Energy / Parseval Relation | $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int\!\!\int \vert \text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2} \dfrac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$ | Energy conservation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical engineering, the Wavelet Transform is the **workhorse algorithm** for:

1. **ECG QRS detection** ŌĆö the sharp QRS complex corresponds to high-magnitude coefficients at small scales (high frequencies). Pan-Tompkins + Wavelet hybrid detectors are the clinical standard.
2. **EEG spike and seizure detection** ŌĆö Morlet and Daubechies wavelets localize transient epileptiform spikes with sub-second precision.
3. **Denoising ECG/EEG** ŌĆö wavelet thresholding (Donoho's soft/hard thresholding) removes baseline wander and 50 Hz powerline interference without distorting QRS morphology.
4. **Feature extraction for classification** ŌĆö wavelet packet energy, entropy, and coefficients feed classifiers for arrhythmia, sleep-apnea, and emotion detection.
5. **Data compression** ŌĆö the sparsity of wavelet coefficients in the MRA basis enables high-ratio compression of Holter ECG recordings (e.g., the MIT-BIH compression benchmark).
6. **PCG (heart sound) analysis** ŌĆö separating S1, S2, murmurs, and artifacts in phonocardiograms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Cross-Reference (KTU Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 module 2 unit on Wavelet Transform specifically tests: (i) CWT vs DWT distinction, (ii) MRA pyramid algorithm, (iii) choice of mother wavelet, and (iv) biomedical applications. Master the equations in Table rows 1, 2, 4, 10, 13, 14.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)

We start from the requirement that the analysis tool should be **localized in both time and frequency** ŌĆö the fundamental limitation of the global Fourier transform. The Wavelet Transform satisfies this by correlating the signal with scaled, shifted copies of a localized oscillatory function.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the inner product interpretation.**

For two finite-energy functions $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, the inner product in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ is:

$$\langle x, y \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y^{*}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the translated (shifted) mother wavelet.**

To make $\psi(t)$ scan across the time axis, we shift it by $b$:

$$\psi_{b}(t) = \psi(t - b)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Introduce dilation to obtain a frequency-varying window.**

To obtain a time-localized basis that varies with frequency, we compress/expand the wavelet by a scale $a$:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right)$$

The factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ is the **normalization constant** that ensures $\vert \vert \psi_{a,b} \vert \vert_{2} = \vert \vert \psi \vert \vert_{2}$ ŌĆö i.e., the energy of the wavelet is preserved across all scales (essential for an orthonormal-like framework).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the inner product to obtain CWT coefficients.**

Applying the inner product definition:

$$\text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) = \langle x, \psi_{a,b} \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the wavelet definition:

$$\text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive the admissibility condition.**

For the inverse transform to exist, the basis must be **complete** in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$. Applying Plancherel's theorem (energy conservation) in the Fourier domain, the completeness requirement leads to:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega < \infty$$

This forces $\Psi(0) = 0$, which in turn implies $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ ŌĆö the zero-mean property.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Derive the inverse CWT.**

The reconstruction formula is the adjoint of the CWT operator, weighted by $\frac{1}{C_{\psi}}$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^{2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\frac{1}{a^{2}}$ (not $\frac{1}{a}$) comes from the Jacobian of the change of variables $(a,b) \to (t,\omega)$ when switching between the time-scale plane and the time-frequency plane.

### 3.2 Derivation of the DWT and MRA Pyramid Equations

The Discrete Wavelet Transform is obtained by **dyadic sampling** of the scale-translation parameters.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Dyadic discretization.**

Choose $a_{0} = 2$, $b_{0} = 1$. Then:

$$a = 2^{m}, \quad b = n \cdot 2^{m}, \quad m,n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The basis functions become:

$$\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \, \psi(2^{-m} t - n)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define scaling and wavelet functions at level $j$.**

$$\phi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \, \phi(2^{-j} t - k)$$
$$\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{-j/2} \, \psi(2^{-j} t - k)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Multi-Resolution Analysis decomposition.**

Any finite-energy signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k} c_{J,k} \, \phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{k} d_{j,k} \, \psi_{j,k}(t)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute coefficients via inner products.**

$$c_{J,k} = \langle x(t), \phi_{J,k}(t) \rangle = \int x(t) \, \phi_{J,k}^{*}(t) \, dt$$
$$d_{j,k} = \langle x(t), \psi_{j,k}(t) \rangle = \int x(t) \, \psi_{j,k}^{*}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Pyramid (Mallat's) algorithm ŌĆö fast filter bank implementation.**

Using the orthogonality of $\{\phi, \psi\}$ and the dilation equation, projecting onto the next coarser level yields:

$$c_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k]$$
$$d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k]$$

This is the famous **Mallat pyramid algorithm**: at each level, convolve with a low-pass $h$ and a high-pass $g$ filter, then **downsample by 2**.

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Haar Wavelet Decomposition

**Problem:** Compute the first-level Haar DWT of the discrete signal $x = [1, 2, 3, 4]$ using the Haar filters $h = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ and $g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply low-pass filter $h$.**

$$c_{1}[0] = h[0] \cdot x[0] + h[1] \cdot x[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2) = \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$c_{1}[1] = h[0] \cdot x[2] + h[1] \cdot x[3] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4) = \frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply high-pass filter $g$.**

$$d_{1}[0] = g[0] \cdot x[0] + g[1] \cdot x[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1) - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2) = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$d_{1}[1] = g[0] \cdot x[2] + g[1] \cdot x[3] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3) - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4) = \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Result.**

$$c_{1} = \left[\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{7}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \approx [2.121, 4.950] \quad \text{(approximation ŌĆö low-frequency average)}$$
$$d_{1} = \left[\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \approx [-0.707, -0.707] \quad \text{(detail ŌĆö high-frequency change)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify energy conservation.**

$$E_{\text{input}} = 1^{2} + 2^{2} + 3^{2} + 4^{2} = 30$$
$$E_{\text{output}} = (2.121)^{2} + (4.950)^{2} + (0.707)^{2} + (0.707)^{2} = 4.5 + 24.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 30 \;\; \checkmark$$

This is a property of orthonormal wavelets ŌĆö the **Parseval relation** holds exactly in the wavelet domain.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Multi-Level Wavelet Decomposition Architecture (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates **Mallat's Pyramid Algorithm** as applied to a biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) for denoising or feature extraction. The architecture is a **tree-structured filter bank** with iterative decomposition of the approximation branch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inp["Input Biomedical Signal x t"]
    Inp --> L1["LPF h0 Low Pass"]
    Inp --> H1["HPF g0 High Pass"]
    L1 --> DS1["Downsample by 2"]
    H1 --> DS2["Downsample by 2"]
    DS1 --> cA1["cA1 Approximation Coefficients Level 1"]
    DS2 --> cD1["cD1 Detail Coefficients Level 1"]
    cA1 --> L2["LPF h0 Low Pass"]
    cA1 --> H2["HPF g0 High Pass"]
    L2 --> DS3["Downsample by 2"]
    H2 --> DS4["Downsample by 2"]
    DS3 --> cA2["cA2 Approximation Level 2"]
    DS4 --> cD2["cD2 Detail Level 2"]
    cA2 --> L3["LPF h0 Low Pass"]
    cA2 --> H3["HPF g0 High Pass"]
    L3 --> DS5["Downsample by 2"]
    H3 --> DS6["Downsample by 2"]
    DS5 --> cA3["cA3 Approximation Level 3"]
    DS6 --> cD3["cD3 Detail Level 3"]
    cA3 --> Th["Thresholding Denoising or Feature Selection"]
    cD1 --> Th
    cD2 --> Th
    cD3 --> Th
    Th --> Recon["Inverse DWT Reconstruction"]
    Recon --> Out["Denoised or Compressed Signal y t"]
```

This diagram maps the **decision process** a biomedical engineer follows when choosing a mother wavelet for a specific application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Start --> Q1{"Is the signal a sharp transient ECG QRS or EEG spike"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> W1["Choose Daubechies db4 or db6 Compact Support"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Need complex valued time frequency map"}

    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Need near symmetric basis for clean reconstruction"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> W4["Choose Symlet or Coiflet"]

    W1 --> App["Apply CWT or DWT MRA Decomposition"]

    W3 --> App
    W4 --> App
    W5 --> App
    App --> OutApp["Post processing Denoise Detect Classify Compress"]
```

### 4.3 Time-Frequency Tiling Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This block diagram compares the **time-frequency tiling** of STFT and Wavelet Transform ŌĆö a frequently tested concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STFT["STFT Time Frequency Plane Fixed Window"]
        S1["High Freq Coarse Time Coarse Freq"]
        S2["High Freq Coarse Time Coarse Freq"]
        S3["Low Freq Coarse Time Coarse Freq"]
        S4["Low Freq Coarse Time Coarse Freq"]
    end
    subgraph WT["Wavelet Transform Time Frequency Plane Adaptive Tiling"]
        W1["High Freq Fine Time Coarse Freq"]
        W2["High Freq Fine Time Coarse Freq"]
        W3["Low Freq Coarse Time Fine Freq"]
        W4["Low Freq Coarse Time Fine Freq"]
    end
    Note["STFT gives uniform resolution Wavelets give constant Q multi resolution tiling"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the Diagrams:**
> - The pyramid algorithm creates a **sparse** representation: most of the signal's energy is concentrated in a few large coefficients at coarse scales (low frequencies), while high-frequency transients (QRS, spikes) appear as isolated large coefficients at fine scales.
> - This sparsity is the foundation of **wavelet-domain thresholding** for denoising ŌĆö small coefficients are noise; large coefficients are signal features.
