## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define the mother wavelet. State and explain the admissibility condition. Why is it necessary for the Wavelet Transform?**
*`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper - May 2024]`* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$ is a finite-energy, oscillatory, zero-mean function used as the prototype to generate all basis functions of the Wavelet Transform via scaling and translation. All wavelets used in the transform are derived from it as $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$.

The **admissibility condition** states that for $\psi(t)$ to be a valid wavelet, the following integral must be finite:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega < \infty$$

where $\Psi(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$.

This condition is **necessary** because:
- It guarantees the **existence of the inverse wavelet transform** (i.e., perfect reconstruction).
- It implies $\Psi(0) = 0$, which forces $\int \psi(t) dt = 0$ ŌĆö the wavelet must have zero mean and behave like a small wave (bandpass), not a low-pass scaling function.
- It ensures the basis is **complete** in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$, so any finite-energy signal can be represented.

---

**Q2. Compare the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) and the Wavelet Transform with respect to time-frequency resolution and biomedical signal suitability.**
*`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Aspect | STFT | Wavelet Transform |
|--------------------|------|-------------------|
| Window size | **Fixed** for all frequencies | **Variable** (adaptive) |
| Time resolution at high frequency | Poor (long window) | **Good** (short wavelet at small scale) |
| Frequency resolution at low frequency | Poor (fixed resolution) | **Good** (long wavelet at large scale) |
| Basis function | Complex exponentials ├Ś shifted window | Scaled and translated mother wavelet |
| Best suited for | Quasi-stationary signals | Non-stationary, transient-rich signals |
| Biomedical suitability | Moderate ŌĆö cannot localize QRS precisely | **High** ŌĆö ideal for ECG QRS, EEG spikes, denoising |
| Computational cost | Lower | Slightly higher; DWT via Mallat is fast |

**Conclusion:** The Wavelet Transform is preferred for biomedical signals because it provides **multi-resolution analysis** ŌĆö fine time resolution for sharp transients (QRS, spikes) and fine frequency resolution for slow rhythms (baseline wander, delta waves).

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice within a module).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
*`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Module 2, 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a) Derive the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a signal $x(t)$ from first principles. State the admissibility condition and derive the inverse CWT formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Inner product framework.** The coefficient that measures similarity between $x(t)$ and an analysis function $y(t)$ is the inner product:
$$\langle x, y \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y^{*}(t) \, dt \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Time localization via translation.** To obtain time-localized information, translate the analysis function by $b$:
$$\psi_{b}(t) = \psi(t - b) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Scale adaptation via dilation.** To vary the frequency content analyzed, dilate the function by scale $a$:
$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the CWT.** Taking the inner product:
$$\boxed{\text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Admissibility condition.** For the inverse to exist, applying Plancherel in the frequency domain yields:
$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega < \infty \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Inverse CWT.** The reconstruction is the adjoint operator weighted by $1/C_{\psi}$:
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \text{CWT}_{x}(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^{2}} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Properties.** (i) Energy conservation, (ii) Translation invariance $\text{CWT}_{x(t-t_{0})}(a,b) = \text{CWT}_{x}(a,b-t_{0})$, (iii) Scale covariance.

---

**(b) Explain Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) and derive the Mallat pyramid algorithm equations for computing DWT coefficients. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö MRA concept.** MRA decomposes $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ into a chain of nested subspaces $V_{J} \subset V_{J-1} \subset \cdots \subset V_{0}$ with orthogonal wavelet complements $W_{j}$. Any signal is approximated at scale $J$ via scaling functions, with detail captured by wavelets at each level.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expansion formula.**
$$x(t) = \sum_{k} c_{J,k} \, \phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{k} d_{j,k} \, \psi_{j,k}(t) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Coefficient computation via inner product.**
$$c_{J,k} = \int x(t) \, \phi_{J,k}(t) \, dt, \quad d_{j,k} = \int x(t) \, \psi_{j,k}(t) \, dt \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Dilation (two-scale) equation.** The scaling function satisfies:
$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} h[k] \, \phi(2t - k) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

The wavelet function is generated from the same scaling function:
$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{k} g[k] \, \phi(2t - k), \quad g[k] = (-1)^{k} \, h[1-k] \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Derive Mallat's pyramid equations.** Projecting the signal onto $V_{j+1}$ using the dilation equation and using orthogonality of integer translates:
$$c_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k] \quad \text{...(v)}$$
$$d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k] \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

These two equations form the **filter bank** of the Mallat algorithm: convolve with $h$ and $g$, then **downsample by 2** (the $2n$ index). Reconstruction uses the synthesis filters with **upsampling by 2**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Complexity.** Mallat's algorithm achieves DWT in $O(N)$ time ŌĆö far faster than $O(N \log N)$ direct computation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
*`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper Module 2, 2024 Scheme]`*

**(a) Explain the different families of wavelets used in biomedical signal processing. Discuss the suitability of Daubechies wavelets for ECG signal denoising. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Haar Wavelet.** Discontinuous, step-shaped, simplest orthonormal basis. Useful for teaching and piecewise-constant signals. Not smooth, so not ideal for smooth ECG waveforms.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Daubechies (dbN) Wavelets.** Family of compactly-supported, orthonormal wavelets parameterized by the number of vanishing moments $N$. The most commonly used member is **db4** (used in MIT-BIH ECG benchmark) and **db6** for EEG.
- **Advantages:** Compact support (causal, finite impulse response), orthonormality, good time-frequency localization, efficient Mallat implementation.
- **For ECG denoising:** db4 has 4 vanishing moments ŌĆö enough to represent polynomial trends (baseline wander) and capture the QRS morphology. Its compact support preserves sharp edges of QRS without Gibbs oscillations.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Morlet Wavelet.** Complex-valued, $\psi(t) = e^{-t^{2}/2} \cos(5t)$. Used for time-frequency scalogram visualization of EEG and evoked potentials. Not orthonormal; used mainly for CWT analysis.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Symlet and Coiflet.** Modified Daubechies with near-symmetric phase response. Symlets (symN) are nearly linear-phase, reducing phase distortion in reconstructed signals. Coiflets have both $\phi$ and $\psi$ with vanishing moments. Used in high-fidelity ECG compression.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Mexican Hat (Ricker).** $\psi(t) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \pi^{-1/4} (1 - t^{2}) e^{-t^{2}/2}$. Second derivative of Gaussian. Excellent for **spike detection in EEG** (e.g., epileptiform discharges).

**Step 6 ŌĆö Daubechies-based ECG Denoising Procedure.**
1. Apply db4 DWT to noisy ECG for $L=4$ or $L=5$ levels.
2. Compute threshold $\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}$ (universal threshold, Donoho).
3. Apply **soft thresholding**: $d'_{j,k} = \text{sign}(d_{j,k}) \cdot \max(\vert d_{j,k} \vert - \lambda, 0)$.
4. Reconstruct via inverse DWT.
5. Result: clean ECG with suppressed baseline wander, PLI, and EMG noise, with QRS preserved.

**Conclusion:** Daubechies wavelets are the **gold standard** for ECG denoising due to their compact support, orthonormality, vanishing moments, and computational efficiency.

---

**(b) An ECG segment sampled at $f_{s} = 360$ Hz is decomposed using a 3-level Haar DWT. The detail coefficients at each level are: $D_{1} = [0.05, 0.10]$, $D_{2} = [0.20, 0.40]$, $D_{3} = [0.80, 1.50]$. The approximation at level 3 is $A_{3} = [3.0, 3.5]$. Apply Donoho's universal soft-thresholding rule with $\lambda = 0.5$ and reconstruct the denoised signal trend. Comment on which level captures the QRS complex. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply soft-thresholding rule $d' = \text{sign}(d) \cdot \max(\vert d \vert - \lambda, 0)$.**

For $\lambda = 0.5$:

- $D_{1} = [0.05, 0.10]$: $\vert 0.05 \vert = 0.05 < 0.5 \Rightarrow 0$; $\vert 0.10 \vert = 0.10 < 0.5 \Rightarrow 0$.  
  $D_{1}' = [0, 0]$

- $D_{2} = [0.20, 0.40]$: both $< 0.5 \Rightarrow 0$.  
  $D_{2}' = [0, 0]$

- $D_{3} = [0.80, 1.50]$:  
  $0.80 - 0.5 = 0.30 \Rightarrow +0.30$  
  $1.50 - 0.5 = 1.00 \Rightarrow +1.00$  
  $D_{3}' = [0.30, 1.00]$

- $A_{3}$ is kept (or thresholded separately if needed): $A_{3}' = [3.0, 3.5]$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Frequency bands of each level (Haar, $f_{s} = 360$ Hz).**

For a dyadic decomposition with $f_{s} = 360$ Hz, the frequency bands are:
- Level 1 ($D_{1}$): $90 - 180$ Hz ŌåÆ high-frequency noise (EMG, HF interference)
- Level 2 ($D_{2}$): $45 - 90$ Hz ŌåÆ medium-frequency noise (PLI residuals, motion artifact)
- Level 3 ($D_{3}$): $22.5 - 45$ Hz ŌåÆ **QRS complex band** (QRS has dominant energy here)
- $A_{3}$: $0 - 22.5$ Hz ŌåÆ baseline + P, T waves

**Step 3 ŌĆö Reconstruct the denoised trend.**

The reconstructed signal uses $A_{3}' + D_{1}' + D_{2}' + D_{3}'$ after inverse Haar DWT (upsampling + filtering). Numerically, upsampling and adding the surviving coefficients:
- The denoised signal at this scale has approximation $[3.0, 3.5]$ and a detail burst $[0.30, 1.00]$ at the QRS-frequency band.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation.**

- Levels 1 and 2 captured noise (coefficients below threshold ŌåÆ set to zero).
- Level 3 contained the **QRS complex** (large coefficients 0.80 and 1.50, surviving thresholding).
- The approximation $A_{3}$ carries the **baseline and slow P/T waves**.
- The denoised signal preserves the QRS morphology while suppressing high-frequency noise ŌĆö confirming the **multi-resolution efficacy** of wavelet denoising.

**Conclusion:** Soft-thresholding in the wavelet domain (Donoho, 1995) is an asymptotically near-optimal denoising method for Gaussian noise, and its band-selective nature makes it uniquely suitable for biomedical signals.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Wavelet Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization constant $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ in the CWT formula.** This is a 1-mark loss. Always include it.
> 2. **Confusing STFT and Wavelet tiling.** STFT has uniform rectangles; Wavelets have variable area (constant-Q). Students often draw the wrong diagram.
> 3. **Writing $g[k] = (-1)^{k} h[k]$ instead of $g[k] = (-1)^{k} h[1-k]$** for the QMF (quadrature mirror filter) relation. The index reversal $[1-k]$ is critical.
> 4. **Missing the factor $\frac{1}{a^{2}}$ in the inverse CWT** ŌĆö confusing it with $\frac{1}{a}$ (Fourier inverse uses $1/a$, but CWT inverse uses $1/a^{2}$ due to the Jacobian).
> 5. **Not stating the admissibility condition explicitly** when asked to "define mother wavelet" ŌĆö it is a compulsory sub-point.
> 6. **Mixing up CWT and DWT** in numerical problems. DWT uses $a = 2^{m}$, $b = n \cdot 2^{m}$, **not** continuous $a, b$.
> 7. **Skipping the energy conservation verification** in worked examples ŌĆö this is a 1-mark "free" point if done.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wavelet Transform (WT)** is a time-scale analysis tool that decomposes a signal into scaled and translated versions of a **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$.
- **CWT:** $CWT_{x}(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ ŌĆö produces 2-D time-scale map (scalogram).
- **DWT:** Discrete version with $a = 2^{m}$, $b = n \cdot 2^{m}$; computed efficiently via **Mallat's pyramid algorithm** in $O(N)$.
- **Admissibility condition** $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ ŌĆö necessary for perfect reconstruction; implies $\psi$ has zero mean.
- **Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA):** $x(t) = \sum c_{J,k} \phi_{J,k}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum d_{j,k} \psi_{j,k}(t)$ ŌĆö separates coarse approximation and fine details across scales.
- **Mallat's pyramid equations:** $c_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k-2n] c_{j}[k]$ and $d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k-2n] c_{j}[k]$ ŌĆö filter bank with downsampling by 2.
- **Dilation equations:** $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h[k]\phi(2t-k)$; $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum g[k]\phi(2t-k)$.
- **QMF relation:** $g[k] = (-1)^{k} h[1-k]$ (note the index reversal).
- **Common wavelets for biomedical signals:** **db4** (ECG denoising standard), **Morlet** (EEG time-frequency maps), **Haar** (teaching), **Symlet/Coiflet** (high-fidelity reconstruction), **Mexican Hat** (EEG spike detection).
- **STFT vs WT:** STFT has fixed window ŌåÆ uniform resolution; WT has variable window ŌåÆ multi-resolution, constant-Q.
- **Donoho's soft thresholding:** $d' = \text{sign}(d) \cdot \max(\vert d \vert - \lambda, 0)$ with $\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \log N}$ ŌĆö near-optimal wavelet-domain denoising.
- **Inverse CWT** uses $\frac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$ (Jacobian), not $\frac{da \, db}{a}$.
- **Energy conservation** holds in the wavelet domain (Parseval-like theorem).
- **Biomedical applications:** QRS detection, EEG spike/seizure detection, ECG/EEG denoising, signal compression (Holter), PCG analysis, feature extraction for ML classifiers.
- **QRS frequency band** is typically captured at level 3 (or 4) of a dyadic DWT decomposition with $f_{s} = 360$ Hz.

---

> **End of Module 2 ŌĆö Wavelet Transform Notes**
> *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1ŌĆōL4).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalization of the Short-Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generalization of the Short-Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Short-Time Fourier Transform (G-STFT)**
> The **Generalized Short-Time Fourier Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as a time-frequency representation obtained by computing the Fourier Transform of the signal multiplied by a sliding (shifted) analysis window function $h(t)$:
>
> $$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$
>
> where:
> - $X_{h}(\tau, \omega)$ is the **complex-valued G-STFT** of the signal (function of time-shift $\tau$ and angular frequency $\omega$).
> - $h(t)$ is the **analysis window function** (must belong to $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ ŌĆö the space of square-integrable functions).
> - $h^{*}(t - \tau)$ denotes the **complex conjugate** of the time-reversed and time-shifted window.
> - $\tau \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **time-shift (localization) parameter**.
> - $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **angular frequency parameter**.

The **inverse (or synthesis) G-STFT** reconstructs the original signal using a synthesis window $g(t)$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi \, C_{g,h}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau$$

provided the **reconstruction constant** satisfies:

$$C_{g,h} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} g^{*}(t) \, h(t) \, dt \neq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $h(t) = g(t)$ and the constant $C_{h,h} = \int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt$, the inverse reduces to the classical orthogonal case (orthonormal Gabor-type representation). The classical STFT is a special case where $g(t) = h(t)$ and $C_{g,h} = 1$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Sliding Camera"**
> Imagine you are photographing a long musical score using a narrow camera lens. As you slide the camera left to right (parameter $\tau$), you only see a small **slice** of the score at a time. Inside each slice, you identify the **notes (frequencies)** being played. By sliding the camera smoothly and recording notes at every position, you create a **time-frequency map** ŌĆö this is exactly what the G-STFT does with a signal. A wider lens = better frequency resolution but poorer time resolution; a narrow lens = better time localization but poorer frequency resolution.

**Engineering Significance:**
- ECG/EEG analysis: detecting transient QRS complexes and frequency shifts.
- Speech processing: extracting formants that change with phonemes.
- Vibration analysis in biomedical implants: identifying resonance frequencies over time.
- Evoked potential analysis: separating stimulus-locked transients from background noise.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Property |
|---|---|---|
| Heisenberg Uncertainty Product | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f$ | $\geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ |
| Energy of Window | $E_{h}$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt < \infty$ |
| Reconstruction Constant | $C_{g,h}$ | Must be finite and non-zero |
| Analysis Bandwidth | $B_{h}$ | $\frac{\int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega}{\int \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega}$ |

---

## 1.4 Why Generalize the Standard STFT?

The **classical STFT** (fixed window) suffers from a fixed time-frequency tiling ŌĆö it cannot adapt to signals that contain both slow transients and fast oscillations. The **Generalized STFT** lifts this restriction by allowing:

1. **Different analysis and synthesis windows** ($h(t) \neq g(t)$) ŌĆö enabling biorthogonal perfect-reconstruction systems.
2. **Variable window lengths** ŌĆö adaptive time-frequency resolution (the conceptual seed of the Wavelet Transform).
3. **Overcomplete (redundant) representations** ŌĆö providing robustness and improved denoising in biomedical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Gabor Transform** is the most celebrated case of G-STFT, where the window is chosen as a **Gaussian** $h(t) = e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$. The Gaussian uniquely minimizes the **Heisenberg uncertainty product**, making it the optimal choice for joint time-frequency localization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the G-STFT Operation

The Generalized STFT can be understood as a **three-stage sequential process**:

### Stage 1 ŌĆö Windowing (Time Localization)
Multiply the input signal $x(t)$ by a time-localized window $h^{*}(t-\tau)$ shifted to position $\tau$.

$$x_{\tau}(t) = x(t) \cdot h^{*}(t - \tau)$$

**Why?** The window restricts the analysis to a small neighborhood around $t = \tau$, making the signal "approximately stationary" within that interval.

### Stage 2 ŌĆö Fourier Transformation (Frequency Analysis)
Apply the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) to the windowed segment:

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x_{\tau}(t)\}$$

**Why?** Fourier Transform extracts the frequency content of the localized segment, producing a complex spectrum $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \in \mathbb{C}$.

### Stage 3 ŌĆö Sliding Mechanism (Time Sweep)
Sweep $\tau$ across all real values to build the complete 2D time-frequency plane.

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) : (\tau, \omega) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \mapsto \mathbb{C}$$

**Why?** This generates a complete 2D map showing how the spectral content evolves over time ŌĆö vital for non-stationary biomedical signals.

---

## 2.2 The Analysis Window ŌĆö Design Requirements

> [!NOTE]
> A valid analysis window $h(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ must satisfy:
> 1. **Finite energy:** $\int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt < \infty$
> 2. **Non-zero integral (DC condition):** $\int h(t) \, dt \neq 0$ (for faithful reconstruction)
> 3. **Compact support or rapid decay** (for computational efficiency in discrete STFT)
> 4. **Symmetry** (preferred, but not mandatory): $h(-t) = h(t)$ ensures linear phase

### Common Windows Used in Biomedical G-STFT

| Window | Time-Domain $h(t)$ | Frequency Property |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1$ for $\vert t \vert \le T/2$, else $0$ | Narrowest main lobe, highest sidelobes |
| Hann | $0.5[1 + \cos(2\pi t / T)]$ | Low sidelobe leakage, wider main lobe |
| Hamming | $0.54 + 0.46 \cos(2\pi t / T)$ | Improved sidelobe suppression |
| **Gaussian (Gabor)** | $e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$ | **Minimum uncertainty** ŌĆö optimal |
| Kaiser | $\frac{I_{0}(\beta \sqrt{1 - (2t/T)^{2}})}{I_{0}(\beta)}$ | Tunable trade-off parameter $\beta$ |

---

## 2.3 The Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off

The **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle** governs the G-STFT resolution:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

where $\Delta t$ and $\Delta \omega$ are the **standard deviations** of $\vert h(t) \vert^{2}$ and $\vert H(\omega) \vert^{2}$ respectively.

**Practical Implications:**
- **Short window** $\Rightarrow$ Good time resolution, poor frequency resolution (suited for transients like QRS, spikes in EEG).
- **Long window** $\Rightarrow$ Good frequency resolution, poor time resolution (suited for stationary segments like heart sound murmurs).
- **Adaptive / variable window** $\Rightarrow$ Optimal multi-resolution analysis (precursor to wavelets).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS ŌĆö G-STFT (Module 2 Essential)**

| # | Formula / Identity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | Forward G-STFT definition |
| 2 | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{g,h}} \iint X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$ | Inverse G-STFT |
| 3 | $C_{g,h} = \int g^{*}(t) \, h(t) dt \neq 0$ | Reconstruction constant |
| 4 | $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \left[ x(\omega) \ast H(\omega) \right]$ | Frequency-domain equivalence (modulation + filtering) |
| 5 | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ | Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty |
| 6 | $\Delta t = \left[ \frac{\int t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt}{\int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt} \right]^{1/2}$ | Time spread (RMS duration) |
| 7 | $\Delta \omega = \left[ \frac{\int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega}{\int \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega} \right]^{1/2}$ | Frequency spread (RMS bandwidth) |
| 8 | $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \langle x(t), \, h(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} \rangle$ | Inner-product form of G-STFT |
| 9 | $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \int X(\omega') \, H^{*}(\omega - \omega') \, d\omega'$ | Equivalent filter-bank form |
| 10 | $\int \int \vert X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \vert^{2} d\tau d\omega = 2\pi \int \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt \int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt$ | Generalized Parseval (energy preservation) |

> **Note on notation:** All vertical bars (absolute value, modulus) are rendered using `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.5 Filter-Bank Interpretation of G-STFT

The G-STFT can be equivalently implemented as a **bank of band-pass filters** $H^{*}(\omega - \omega_{k})$ followed by envelope detectors (modulators). Each filter output is a band-limited version of $x(t)$ centered at $\omega_{k}$. The sliding mechanism corresponds to **down-modulation** by $e^{-j\omega_{k} t}$:

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega_{k}) = e^{-j\omega_{k} \tau} \left[ X(\omega) \ast H(\omega) \right]_{\omega = \omega_{k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely the architecture of the **cochlea** in the human ear ŌĆö a logarithmic bank of band-pass filters analyzing sound. The G-STFT is therefore a *mathematical cochlea* used widely in digital hearing aids and audiological signal processing.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Generalized STFT

| Biomedical Application | Role of G-STFT |
|---|---|
| ECG Late Potential Detection | Resolves microvolt-level signals masked in QRS |
| EEG Seizure Onset Localization | Tracks sudden spectral bursts in time |
| Heart Sound Murmur Analysis | Reveals time-frequency evolution of S1, S2, murmurs |
| EMG Fatigue Analysis | Captures median frequency shifts in muscle contractions |
| Speech Phoneme Recognition | Tracks formant trajectories over time |
| Doppler Ultrasound | Time-frequency analysis of blood flow signals |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fourier Transform to Short-Time Fourier Transform

### **Starting Point: Standard Fourier Transform**

The CTFT of a signal $x(t)$ is:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

This represents the **global** frequency content of $x(t)$ ŌĆö there is **no time information** about when each frequency occurred.

---

### **Step 1: Impose Time-Localization via Windowing**

We want to restrict the integration to a small interval around a center time $\tau$. We do this by multiplying $x(t)$ with a window $h(t - \tau)$ centered at $\tau$:

$$\tilde{x}_{\tau}(t) = x(t) \cdot h(t - \tau)$$

The product $h(t - \tau)$ is **non-zero only near $t = \tau$**, so the contribution to the integral comes from a localized neighborhood.

> **Interpretation:** $h(t - \tau)$ acts as a "spotlight" focusing on a small time segment.

---

### **Step 2: Apply Fourier Transform to the Windowed Segment**

Take the CTFT of the windowed segment:

$$\mathcal{F}\{\tilde{x}_{\tau}(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ x(t) \cdot h(t - \tau) \right] e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

This is the spectrum of $x(t)$ *in the vicinity of* $t = \tau$.

---

### **Step 3: Introduce Complex Conjugation on the Window**

For mathematical convenience (and to make the operation a true **inner product**), we conjugate the window:

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot h^{*}(t - \tau) \cdot e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

> **Why conjugate?** This makes $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \langle x(t), h(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t} \rangle$, an inner product in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$. The result is a **projection coefficient** of $x$ onto the atom $h_{\tau, \omega}(t) = h(t - \tau) e^{j\omega t}$.

---

### **Step 4: Recognize the Modulation Term**

Factor out the exponential from the kernel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{h}(\tau, \omega) &= \int x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt \\
&= \int \left[ x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \right] h^{*}(t - \tau) \, dt \\
&= \left[ X(\omega) \ast H^{*}(\omega) \right] \cdot e^{-j\omega \tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:**
- Multiplication in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Convolution in frequency.
- The factor $h^{*}(t - \tau)$ has Fourier transform $H^{*}(\omega) \, e^{-j\omega \tau}$.
- So $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \left[ X(\omega) \ast H^{*}(\omega) \right]$, which is the **filter-bank** interpretation.

> **Conclusion:** The G-STFT in time equals a band-pass filtered version of the original signal's spectrum, multiplied by a phase factor $e^{-j\omega \tau}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Inverse G-STFT (Reconstruction Formula)

### **Goal:** Recover $x(t)$ from $X_{h}(\tau, \omega)$ using a synthesis window $g(t)$.

### **Step 1: Substitute and Inverse Fourier Transform**

Starting from the inverse Fourier transform:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \tilde{X}(\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega$$

We replace $\tilde{X}(\omega)$ with the G-STFT output at the current $t$ position. Considering the synthesis window $g(t - \tau)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, d\tau \right] e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \iint X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau \cdot \frac{1}{C_{g,h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The synthesis window $g(t - \tau)$ provides a **dual analysis** of the STFT coefficients, weighted by the reconstruction constant $C_{g,h}$.

### **Step 2: Derive the Reconstruction Constant**

For the inverse to exist, the inner product $\langle g, h \rangle$ must be non-zero. This arises from the requirement:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g^{*}(t) \, h(t) \, dt = C_{g,h} \neq 0$$

When $C_{g,h} = 1$, the analysis and synthesis windows are said to be **biorthogonal partners** ŌĆö the most useful case for filter-bank design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Heisenberg Uncertainty Bound for the G-STFT

### **Step 1: Define Energy-Weighted Spreads**

For window $h(t)$ with energy $E = \int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt$:

$$\Delta t^{2} = \frac{1}{E} \int t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt$$

$$\Delta \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi E} \int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega$$

### **Step 2: Apply the Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality**

Using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality on the product $\int t \cdot h(t) \cdot \frac{dh^{*}(t)}{dt} dt$:

$$\left\vert \int t \, h(t) \, \frac{dh^{*}}{dt} dt \right\vert^{2} \leq \left[ \int t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt \right] \left[ \int \left\vert \frac{dh}{dt} \right\vert^{2} dt \right]$$

By **Parseval's theorem**: $\int \vert \frac{dh}{dt} \vert^{2} dt = \int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega$.

### **Step 3: Compute the Inner Product**

By integration by parts:

$$\int t \, h(t) \, \frac{dh^{*}}{dt} dt = -\frac{1}{2} \int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt = -\frac{E}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\left( \frac{E}{2} \right)^{2} \leq \left( \Delta t^{2} E \right) \left( \Delta \omega^{2} \cdot 2\pi E \right)$$

### **Step 4: Simplify to Heisenberg Inequality**

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

> **Conclusion:** No window can achieve arbitrarily sharp simultaneous time and frequency localization. The **Gaussian** uniquely achieves equality ŌĆö this is the **Gabor Transform**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Time-Frequency Atoms Visualization

The atoms of the G-STFT are time-frequency shifted versions of the window:

$$h_{\tau, \omega}(t) = h(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t}$$

Their energy density in the time-frequency plane is concentrated in a **Heisenberg box** centered at $(\tau, \omega)$ with dimensions $(2\Delta t) \times (2\Delta \omega)$.

> **Key Fact:** All Heisenberg boxes have **equal area** $= 4 \Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq 2$. This is the geometric reason why the G-STFT tiles the time-frequency plane uniformly.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of G-STFT Variants

| Variant | Window $h(t)$ | Window $g(t)$ | Property | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Classical STFT | Fixed, often Hann | $h(t)$ | Biorthogonal if $C = 1$ | Speech spectrograms |
| Gabor Transform | Gaussian | $h(t)$ | Minimum uncertainty | Optimal TF localization |
| Biorthogonal G-STFT | $h(t)$ | $g(t) \neq h(t)$ | Perfect reconstruction | Filter banks |
| Adaptive STFT | Variable length | $h(t)$ | Multi-resolution | Wavelet precursor |
| Synchrosqueezing STFT | $h(t)$ | $h(t)$ | Reassignment in $f$ | Mode decomposition |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö G-STFT Analysis-Synthesis System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Multiplier Stage]
    W[Window Generator h of t minus tau] --> B
    B --> C[CTFT Integrator]
    C --> D[STFT Coefficient Matrix X tau omega]
    D --> E[Inverse CTFT over omega]
    E --> F[Multiplier with g of t minus tau]
    F --> G[Integrator over tau]
    G --> H[Divide by C gh]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Signal x hat of t]
    
    style A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style I fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Time-Frequency Tiling Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TFP[Time Frequency Plane]
        direction LR
        subgraph T1[Time Axis]
            T1A[tau equals 0]
            T1B[tau equals T]
            T1C[tau equals 2T]
            T1D[tau equals 3T]
        end
        subgraph F1[Frequency Axis]
            F1A[omega equals 0]
            F1B[omega equals omega 0]
            F1C[omega equals 2 omega 0]
        end
    end
    
    BOX1[Heisenberg Box 1] --> T1A
    BOX2[Heisenberg Box 2] --> T1B
    BOX3[Heisenberg Box 3] --> T1C
    BOX4[Heisenberg Box 4] --> T1D
    
    style BOX1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style BOX2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style BOX3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style BOX4 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
```

---

## 4.3 Filter-Bank Interpretation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[x of t] --> M1[Multiplier with e to the minus j omega 1 t]
    X --> M2[Multiplier with e to the minus j omega 2 t]
    X --> M3[Multiplier with e to the minus j omega 3 t]
    X --> MN[Multiplier with e to the minus j omega N t]
    
    M1 --> LPF1[Lowpass Filter h of t]
    M2 --> LPF2[Lowpass Filter h of t]
    M3 --> LPF3[Lowpass Filter h of t]
    MN --> LPFN[Lowpass Filter h of t]
    
    LPF1 --> D1[Downsample by tau]
    LPF2 --> D2[Downsample by tau]
    LPF3 --> D3[Downsample by tau]
    LPFN --> DN[Downsample by tau]
    
    D1 --> OUT1[STFT bin 1]
    D2 --> OUT2[STFT bin 2]
    D3 --> OUT3[STFT bin 3]
    DN --> OUTN[STFT bin N]
    
    style X fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    style OUT1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style OUT2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style OUT3 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style OUTN fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 G-STFT Operational Flowchart ŌĆö End-to-End Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Choose Analysis Window h of t] --> S2[Step 2: Slide Window Across Signal]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute CTFT of Each Windowed Segment]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Store 2D Matrix X tau omega]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Application Branch}
    
    S5 --> SA[Visualization as Spectrogram]
    S5 --> SB[Feature Extraction for ML]
    S5 --> SC[Reconstruction via Inverse G-STFT]
    S5 --> SD[Filter Bank Output]
    
    SC --> SC1[Apply Synthesis Window g of t]
    SC1 --> SC2[Integrate over tau and omega]
    SC2 --> SC3[Normalize by C gh]
    SC3 --> SC4[Reconstructed x hat of t]
    
    style S1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style S4 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style S5 fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
    style SC4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A QUESTIONS (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks Total)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Generalized Short-Time Fourier Transform (G-STFT) of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. What is the role of the analysis window $h(t)$ in the transform?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Generalized Short-Time Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ is defined as:
>
> $$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$
>
> The analysis window $h(t)$ serves three key roles:
> 1. **Time Localization:** It restricts the Fourier analysis to a small time interval around $t = \tau$.
> 2. **Trade-off Control:** Its width $\Delta t$ controls the time-frequency resolution trade-off via the Heisenberg inequality $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$.
> 3. **Stationarity Assumption:** It ensures the signal is approximately stationary within the localized segment, justifying the use of Fourier analysis locally.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle in the context of the G-STFT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle states that the product of the time spread $\Delta t$ and frequency spread $\Delta \omega$ of a windowed signal is bounded below:
>
> $$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$$
>
> where
> $$\Delta t = \left[ \frac{\int t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt}{\int \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt} \right]^{1/2}, \quad \Delta \omega = \left[ \frac{\int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega}{\int \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega} \right]^{1/2}$$
>
> **Implication:** No single window can simultaneously achieve arbitrarily sharp time and frequency localization. The Gaussian window uniquely satisfies the equality and is therefore the optimal choice ŌĆö leading to the Gabor Transform.

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice ŌĆö Answer ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **inverse Generalized STFT** reconstruction formula, starting from the forward G-STFT definition. Clearly state all assumptions and the role of the reconstruction constant $C_{g,h}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the analysis window $h(t) = e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$ (Gaussian, $\alpha > 0$), show that the time spread is $\Delta t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}$ and verify the **Heisenberg-Gabor lower bound** is achieved with equality.

---

### **Model Solution A:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Inverse G-STFT Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Begin with the forward G-STFT**

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2: Multiply by the synthesis window $g(t - \tau)$ and $e^{j\omega t}$, then integrate over $\omega$ and $\tau$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\iint X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau &= \iint \left[ \int x(t') \, h^{*}(t' - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t'} dt' \right] g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Fubini's theorem to exchange integrals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \int x(t') \left[ \int h^{*}(t' - \tau) \, g(t - \tau) \, d\tau \right] \left[ \int e^{j\omega(t - t')} d\omega \right] dt'
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the inner integral over $\omega$**

The identity $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(t - t')} d\omega = 2\pi \delta(t - t')$ (Dirac delta) is used:

$$\int e^{j\omega(t - t')} d\omega = 2\pi \delta(t - t')$$

**Step 5: Apply the sifting property of the delta function**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\iint X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau &= 2\pi \int x(t') \, \delta(t - t') \left[ \int h^{*}(t' - \tau) \, g(t - \tau) \, d\tau \right] dt' \\
&= 2\pi \, x(t) \int h^{*}(t - \tau) \, g(t - \tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute $u = t - \tau$ to recognize the inner product**

$$\int h^{*}(t - \tau) \, g(t - \tau) \, d\tau = \int h^{*}(u) \, g(u) \, du = C_{g,h}$$

**Step 7: Solve for $x(t)$ ŌĆö the Inverse G-STFT Formula**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi \, C_{g,h}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, g(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \, d\tau}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- $C_{g,h} = \int g^{*}(t) h(t) dt \neq 0$ (reconstruction is well-defined).
- Both $h(t), g(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ (finite energy).
- Signal $x(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ for proper convergence.

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Gaussian Window Uncertainty Verification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the energy of $h(t) = e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-2\alpha t^{2}} dt = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the time-spread numerator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt = \int t^{2} e^{-2\alpha t^{2}} dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the standard Gaussian integral identity $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} t^{2} e^{-\beta t^{2}} dt = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2\beta^{3/2}}$ with $\beta = 2\alpha$:

$$\int t^{2} e^{-2\alpha t^{2}} dt = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2(2\alpha)^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{4\alpha}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\Delta t$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t^{2} &= \frac{1}{E} \int t^{2} \vert h(t) \vert^{2} dt = \frac{\frac{1}{4\alpha}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}}}{\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}}} = \frac{1}{4\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\Delta t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Fourier transform of the Gaussian**

$$H(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{e^{-\alpha t^{2}}\} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\alpha}} \, e^{-\omega^{2}/(4\alpha)}$$

**Step 5: Compute $\Delta \omega$**

$$E_{\omega} = \int \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega = \frac{\pi}{\alpha} \int e^{-\omega^{2}/(2\alpha)} d\omega = \frac{\pi}{\alpha}\sqrt{2\pi\alpha} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{\alpha}}$$

$$\int \omega^{2} \vert H(\omega) \vert^{2} d\omega = \frac{\pi}{\alpha} \int \omega^{2} e^{-\omega^{2}/(2\alpha)} d\omega = \frac{\pi}{\alpha} \cdot \alpha \sqrt{2\pi\alpha} = \pi\sqrt{2\pi\alpha}$$

$$\Delta \omega^{2} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2\pi\alpha}}{\pi\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{\alpha}}} = \alpha \implies \Delta \omega = \sqrt{\alpha}$$

**Step 6: Compute the Heisenberg Product**

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}} \cdot \sqrt{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The Gaussian window achieves the **Heisenberg-Gabor lower bound with equality**, confirming it is the **optimal window** for joint time-frequency localization ŌĆö this is the foundation of the **Gabor Transform**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **filter-bank interpretation** of the G-STFT, showing that the G-STFT can be implemented as a bank of band-pass filters $H^{*}(\omega - \omega_{k})$ followed by down-modulation. Mention its biological analogue.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A biomedical signal $x(t)$ is analyzed using a Gaussian G-STFT with $\alpha = 2$. The signal contains a 50 Hz heart-sound component and a 200 Hz transient click. Compute the time spread $\Delta t$, the frequency spread $\Delta \omega$, and determine whether the transform can resolve the two events in time.

---

### **Model Solution B:**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Filter-Bank Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Start with the forward G-STFT**

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

**Step 2: Take the Fourier Transform of the inner-product form**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \, e^{j\omega' \tau} d\tau &= \int \int x(t) \, h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, e^{j\omega' \tau} \, d\tau \, dt \\
&= \int x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \left[ \int h^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{j\omega' \tau} d\tau \right] dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recognize the inner integral as the Fourier transform of the time-reversed window**

Let $u = t - \tau$, so $d\tau = -du$:

$$\int h^{*}(t - \tau) e^{j\omega' \tau} d\tau = e^{j\omega' t} \int h^{*}(u) e^{-j\omega' u} du = e^{j\omega' t} H^{*}(\omega')$$

**Step 4: Substitute back**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int X_{h}(\tau, \omega) e^{j\omega' \tau} d\tau &= H^{*}(\omega') \int x(t) \, e^{j(\omega' - \omega) t} dt = H^{*}(\omega') \cdot 2\pi \, X(\omega - \omega')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Conclude the filter-bank form**

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int X(\omega') H^{*}(\omega - \omega') e^{-j(\omega - \omega') \tau} d\omega' \cdot e^{j\omega \tau}$$

Equivalently:

$$X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \left[ X(\omega) \ast H^{*}(\omega) \right]_{\omega}$$

> **Interpretation:** $H^{*}(\omega - \omega_{k})$ acts as a **band-pass filter** centered at $\omega_{k}$. For each frequency $\omega_{k}$, the G-STFT outputs the envelope of the filtered signal at time $\tau$, scaled by $e^{-j\omega_{k} \tau}$.

**Biological Analogue:** The **cochlea** of the human inner ear implements a logarithmic filter bank with non-uniform frequency spacing (mel scale), performing precisely this kind of G-STFT-like analysis on incoming sound waves for auditory perception.

---

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Resolution Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Given:** Gaussian window with $\alpha = 2$, so $h(t) = e^{-2 t^{2}}$.

**Step 1: Compute Time Spread $\Delta t$**

Using the formula derived in Question A:

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.3536 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Compute Frequency Spread $\Delta \omega$**

$$\Delta \omega = \sqrt{\alpha} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \text{ rad/sec}$$

Converting to Hz: $\Delta f = \frac{\Delta \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\pi} \approx 0.2251 \text{ Hz}$

**Step 3: Determine Time Resolution for Resolving the Click**

The transient 200 Hz click is a sharp event. The smallest resolvable time difference is approximately $2\Delta t \approx 0.707$ seconds.

Since the 50 Hz heart sound is approximately periodic (period = $1/50 = 0.02$ s) and the 200 Hz click has a duration on the order of milliseconds, the **time spread of 0.35 seconds is too large** to resolve the click in time ŌĆö the click will be smeared across multiple heart-sound cycles.

**Step 4: Determine Frequency Resolution**

The frequency difference between the two events is $\Delta f_{\text{events}} = 200 - 50 = 150$ Hz. The frequency spread is $\Delta f = 0.2251$ Hz.

Since $150 \text{ Hz} \gg 0.2251 \text{ Hz}$, the two frequency components are **easily resolvable in frequency** ŌĆö but **not in time**.

**Step 5: Conclusion and Recommendation**

| Criterion | Value | Resolvable? |
|---|---|---|
| Time resolution | $2\Delta t \approx 0.71$ s | ŌØī No ŌĆö click is too brief |
| Frequency resolution | $\Delta f \approx 0.225$ Hz | Ō£ģ Yes ŌĆö 150 Hz separation is ample |

> **Conclusion:** The current Gaussian window achieves excellent frequency resolution but **poor time resolution** for transients. For ECG/heart-sound click detection, a **shorter window** (larger $\alpha$) should be used. This illustrates the **fundamental trade-off** governed by the Heisenberg-Gabor inequality.

---

## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the complex conjugate on $h(t)$** ŌĆö Many students write $h(t-\tau)$ instead of $h^{*}(t-\tau)$, which changes the meaning of the transform from a projection (inner product) to a mere windowed integral. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing up the analysis and synthesis windows** ŌĆö The forward G-STFT uses $h(t)$; the inverse uses $g(t)$. Confusing them leads to incorrect $C_{g,h}$. **(-2 marks)**
> 3. **Omitting the reconstruction constant** ŌĆö Failing to divide by $C_{g,h}$ in the inverse formula. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Confusing the Heisenberg inequality forms** ŌĆö Some textbooks write $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ (with frequency in Hz), while others use $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ (with angular frequency). KTU prefers the **angular frequency form** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps in inverse formula** ŌĆö KTU examiners want the **Fubini step** and the **delta function sifting** explicitly shown. Skipping them loses at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning the biological analogue** ŌĆö For biomedical applications (e.g., cochlear filter bank), the examiner expects explicit mention of the biological parallel. Omission costs 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 7. **Incorrect Gaussian Fourier transform** ŌĆö The FT of $e^{-\alpha t^{2}}$ is $\sqrt{\pi/\alpha} \cdot e^{-\omega^{2}/4\alpha}$, NOT $e^{-\omega^{2}/2\alpha}$. Confusing $1/(4\alpha)$ with $1/(2\alpha)$ is a common error. **(-1 mark)**

---

## ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ŌĆö G-STFT (Module 2)**

- Ō£ģ **Forward G-STFT:** $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) h^{*}(t-\tau) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ ŌĆö **memorize the conjugate on the window**.
- Ō£ģ **Inverse G-STFT:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{g,h}} \iint X_{h}(\tau, \omega) g(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} d\omega d\tau$ ŌĆö **never forget $C_{g,h}$**.
- Ō£ģ **Reconstruction Constant:** $C_{g,h} = \int g^{*}(t) h(t) dt \neq 0$ ŌĆö must be finite and non-zero.
- Ō£ģ **Heisenberg-Gabor Bound:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta \omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$ ŌĆö equality only for the **Gaussian** window.
- Ō£ģ **Filter-Bank Form:** $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = e^{-j\omega \tau} [X(\omega) \ast H^{*}(\omega)]$ ŌĆö interpretable as **modulation + convolution**.
- Ō£ģ **Inner-Product Form:** $X_{h}(\tau, \omega) = \langle x(t), h(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t} \rangle$ ŌĆö projection onto time-frequency atom.
- Ō£ģ **Time-frequency atoms:** $h_{\tau, \omega}(t) = h(t-\tau) e^{j\omega t}$ ŌĆö concentrated in Heisenberg box of area $\geq 2$.
- Ō£ģ **Generalized Parseval:** $\iint \vert X_{h}(\tau, \omega) \vert^{2} d\tau d\omega = 2\pi E_{x} E_{h}$ ŌĆö energy preservation.
- Ō£ģ **Common Windows:** Rectangular, Hann, Hamming, **Gaussian (optimal)**, Kaiser.
- Ō£ģ **Gabor Transform:** STFT with **Gaussian window** ŌĆö unique minimum-uncertainty representation.
- Ō£ģ **Biomedical Applications:** ECG late potentials, EEG seizure detection, heart-sound analysis, EMG fatigue, speech phoneme tracking, Doppler ultrasound.
- Ō£ģ **Biological Analogue:** **Cochlear filter bank** in the human inner ear performs G-STFT-like analysis.
- Ō£ģ **Trade-off Rule:** Short window = good time / bad frequency; long window = good frequency / bad time.
- Ō£ģ **Generalization to Wavelets:** Variable-length windows in the G-STFT conceptually lead to the **Wavelet Transform** (Module 3 preview).
- Ō£ģ **Perfect Reconstruction:** Achieved when $C_{g,h} = 1$ and the windows satisfy the **biorthogonality** condition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelet Transform and Discrete Filter Banks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wavelet Transform and Discrete Filter Banks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wavelet Transform ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelet Transform (KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition):** A mathematical transform that decomposes a signal $x(t)$ into a set of basis functions called *wavelets*, which are obtained by translating and dilating (scaling) a single prototype function $\psi(t)$, known as the **mother wavelet**. It provides simultaneous **timeŌĆōfrequency (or timeŌĆōscale) localization** that adapts to the frequency content of the signal.

The general form of a wavelet basis function is:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right)$$

where:
- $a \in \mathbb{R}, a \neq 0$ ŌåÆ **scale parameter** (inverse of frequency)
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ ŌåÆ **translation parameter** (time shift)
- $\psi(t)$ ŌåÆ mother wavelet satisfying the **admissibility condition**

### 1.2 Discrete Filter Banks ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Filter Bank (KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition):** A set of digital filters arranged in parallel (analysis) or series/parallel (synthesis) configuration, accompanied by **decimators** (down-samplers) and **interpolators** (up-samplers), used to split a signal into sub-band components (analysis) and reconstruct the original signal (synthesis) with controlled or zero distortion.

The canonical **two-channel quadrature mirror filter (QMF) bank** is the building block of all discrete wavelet transforms.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

**Analogy 1 ŌĆö Wavelet Transform as a "Variable Zoom Microscope":**
Imagine inspecting a printed ECG strip. A traditional Fourier Transform is like a satellite image: it tells you the *frequencies present* in the entire recording but cannot tell you *when* the QRS complex occurred. The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) is like a fixed-magnification microscope ŌĆö same window size everywhere, so it cannot zoom in tightly on a sharp R-peak *and* still see a slow ST-segment drift. **A wavelet transform is a microscope whose zoom level adapts automatically**: it uses a *narrow* wavelet to resolve sharp transients (like QRS) and a *wide* wavelet to capture slow trends (like baseline wander). This is the essence of **multi-resolution analysis (MRA)**.

**Analogy 2 ŌĆö Discrete Filter Bank as a Prism + Reassembly Workshop:**
Think of a glass prism that splits white light into red, green, and blue components (analysis). The filter bank does the *same* for a digital signal ŌĆö it splits it into low-frequency (approximation) and high-frequency (detail) components. After processing each colour separately (denoising, compression), a *synthesis* section recombines them back into white light (the original signal). The two-channel filter bank is the simplest such prism ŌĆö it splits the signal into only **two** sub-bands.

### 1.4 Standard Constants and Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Admissibility constant $C_{\psi}$** ŌĆö depends on the chosen mother wavelet; finite for admissible wavelets.
> - **Sampling rate $f_s$** of biomedical signals: typically **$\mathbf{360 \text{ Hz}}$ (MIT-BIH ECG)** or **$500 \text{ Hz}$ (clinical EEG)**.
> - **Number of decomposition levels $J$** in DWT ŌĆö controls the deepest frequency band; commonly **$J = 4$ to $9$** for ECG/EEG denoising.
> - **Decimation factor $M = 2$** for the standard dyadic two-channel filter bank.
> - **Energy preservation:** Parseval-like identity holds for orthonormal wavelet bases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Wavelet Transform

### 2.1.1 Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)
The CWT of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is the inner product with scaled and translated wavelets:

$$W_x(a, b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt$$

**Why?** Because the inner product measures similarity between $x(t)$ and the shifted/scaled wavelet, $W_x(a,b)$ tells *how much* of a particular frequency (controlled by $a$) is present *around* time $b$.

### 2.1.2 Inverse Continuous Wavelet Transform
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint W_x(a, b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^2}$$

where the **admissibility constant** is:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega \; < \; \infty$$

### 2.1.3 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)
By restricting $a = a_0^{m}$ and $b = n b_0 a_0^{m}$ (dyadic grid for $a_0 = 2, b_0 = 1$):

$$\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \, \psi(2^{-m} t - n)$$

The DWT coefficients are:

$$W_x(m, n) = \sum_{k} x[k] \, 2^{-m/2} \, \psi[2^{-m} k - n]$$

### 2.1.4 Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)
MRA expresses a signal as a sum of approximations at successive resolutions plus a final detail:
$$x(t) = A_J(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_j(t)$$

where $A_J$ is the coarse approximation and $D_j$ are detail signals at scale $j$.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown of Two-Channel Filter Bank

### 2.2.1 Analysis Bank
The input $x[n]$ is filtered by a **lowpass filter** $H_0(z)$ and a **highpass filter** $H_1(z)$, then **downsampled by 2** (decimation $\downarrow 2$):
$$X_0(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})\big]$$

### 2.2.2 Synthesis Bank
The sub-band signals are **upsampled by 2** ($\uparrow 2$) and filtered by $G_0(z), G_1(z)$, then summed to produce $\hat{x}[n]$.

### 2.2.3 Perfect Reconstruction (PR) Conditions
For **no aliasing, no amplitude distortion, and no phase distortion**, the following conditions must hold:

$$H_1(z) = H_0(-z) \quad \text{(alias cancellation ŌĆö QMF condition)}$$

$$G_0(z) = H_1(-z), \quad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) \quad \text{(synthesis pairing)}$$

$$\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2} + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega + \pi)}) \vert^{2} = 2 \quad \text{(power complementary)}$$

### 2.2.4 Polyphase Representation
Any filter $H(z)$ can be decomposed as $H(z) = H_0(z^2) + z^{-1} H_1(z^2)$, enabling efficient lattice implementations (Daubechies' factorization, CQF).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 ŌĆö High-Yield Formula Table (Memorize before ESE):**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Wavelet basis | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | Scaled & translated mother wavelet |
| 2 | CWT | $W_x(a,b) = \int x(t) \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt$ | Time-scale decomposition |
| 3 | Inverse CWT | $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint W_x(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da\,db}{a^2}$ | Reconstruction |
| 4 | Admissibility | $C_{\psi} = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Wavelet must oscillate, decay to 0 |
| 5 | DWT basis | $\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \psi(2^{-m} t - n)$ | Dyadic, orthonormal |
| 6 | MRA decomposition | $x(t) = A_J(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_j(t)$ | Approx + details |
| 7 | Scaling equation | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_0[n] \phi(2t - n)$ | Scaling function in terms of itself |
| 8 | Wavelet equation | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_1[n] \phi(2t - n)$ | Wavelet from scaling function |
| 9 | QMF condition | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Alias-cancelling mirror |
| 10 | PR amplitude | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | All-pass power |
| 11 | Decimation relation | $X_d(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})\big]$ | Down-sampler output |
| 12 | Nyquist of sub-band | $f_{c, j} = f_s / 2^{j+1}$ | Bandwidth halves each level |

## 2.4 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Wavelet + Filter Bank? |
|---|---|
| **ECG denoising (baseline wander, EMG, MA)** | MRA cleanly separates high-QRS energy from low-frequency drift |
| **EEG rhythm extraction (╬┤, ╬Ė, ╬▒, ╬▓)** | Dyadic decomposition maps to physiological frequency bands |
| **QRS / R-peak detection** | Sharp transients are localized by short-scale wavelets |
| **Feature extraction for arrhythmia classification** | Wavelet coefficients serve as input to classifiers (SVM, CNN) |
| **Compression of Holter recordings** | Thresholding small wavelet coefficients gives high CR with low PRD |
| **Phonocardiogram (PCG) S1/S2 detection** | Filter bank isolates murmur frequencies |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From STFT to CWT (Why the Wavelet Exists)

The STFT applies a *fixed* window $w(t)$ of duration $\sigma_t$:

$$\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) \, w^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j \omega t} \, dt$$

Heisenberg's uncertainty principle forces $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$, meaning **a single window cannot give both fine time and fine frequency resolution**.

**Step 1.** Rewrite the STFT inner product with the substitution $w(t) e^{j\omega t} \to \psi(t)$:

$$\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) \, \psi_{\omega, \tau}^{*}(t) \, dt$$

where $\psi_{\omega, \tau}(t) = w(t - \tau) e^{j \omega t}$. This is *not yet* a wavelet ŌĆö it still uses a fixed window.

**Step 2.** Notice the basis function $\psi_{\omega, \tau}(t)$ is *shifted in frequency* by $\omega$ and in time by $\tau$. Redefine parameters as:
- $a = \frac{1}{\omega}$ (scale = inverse frequency)
- $b = \tau$ (translation = time shift)

**Step 3.** Define the **mother wavelet** $\psi(t) = w(t) e^{j\Omega_c t}$ (a band-pass-windowed sinusoid). Then:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) = \sqrt{\vert \omega \vert} \, w\!\left(\frac{t-\tau}{1/\omega}\right) e^{j \omega_c \omega (t-\tau)}$$

**Step 4.** Recognize that the *width* of the wavelet window scales with $a$:
- Small $a$ ŌåÆ narrow wavelet ŌåÆ good **time** resolution ŌåÆ poor frequency resolution (high $\omega$)
- Large $a$ ŌåÆ wide wavelet ŌåÆ good **frequency** resolution ŌåÆ poor time resolution (low $\omega$)

**Conclusion.** This trade-off is the entire motivation for the wavelet transform. The CWT inherits an **adaptive timeŌĆōfrequency tiling**, which the STFT cannot match.

$$\boxed{\;\text{STFT: fixed area tiles} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{CWT: adaptive area tiles (constant } Q\text{)}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Perfect Reconstruction of Two-Channel Filter Bank

We derive the conditions under which the analysis-synthesis system yields $\hat{x}[n] = c \, x[n - n_0]$ (perfect reconstruction up to delay and gain).

**Step 1.** Write the sub-band signals after analysis and decimation in Z-domain:

$$V_0(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})\big]$$

$$V_1(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_1(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_1(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2})\big]$$

**Step 2.** Apply upsamplers (insertion of zeros) to obtain $V_0(z^2)$ and $V_1(z^2)$ (this drops the aliasing terms inside).

**Step 3.** Sum the synthesis-filtered signals:

$$\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z)\, V_0(z^2) + G_1(z)\, V_1(z^2)$$

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,X(z)\,\big[H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)\big] + \tfrac{1}{2}\,X(-z)\,\big[H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)\big]$$

**Step 4.** Define the **distortion term** $T(z)$ and the **aliasing term** $A(z)$:

$$T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)\big]$$
$$A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)\big]$$

Therefore $\hat{X}(z) = T(z) X(z) + A(z) X(-z)$.

**Step 5.** For **zero aliasing**, set $A(z) = 0$. The simplest choice is to set:

$$G_0(z) = H_1(-z), \qquad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$$

Substituting:

$$A(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(-z) H_1(-z) - H_1(-z) H_0(-z)\big] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6.** With aliasing cancelled, the distortion term becomes:

$$T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z) H_1(-z) - H_1(z) H_0(-z)\big]$$

**Step 7.** Impose the **QMF constraint** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z) H_0(z) - H_0(-z) H_0(-z)\big] = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)\big]$$

**Step 8.** For **flat magnitude** response, we further require:

$$\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2 \quad \text{(power complementarity)}$$

This gives $T(e^{j\omega}) = z^{-k}$ (a pure delay), and so:

$$\boxed{\;\hat{x}[n] = c\, x[n - k] \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Perfect Reconstruction}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wavelet Ōåö Filter Bank Equivalence

We show that the scaling and wavelet coefficients at dyadic scale $j$ are the outputs of a cascade of two-channel filter banks.

**Step 1.** Start from the **scaling function two-scale equation**:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h_0[n] \, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 2.** The approximation coefficient at scale $j$ is:

$$c_j[n] = \langle x(t), \phi_{j,n}(t) \rangle = \int x(t) \, 2^{-j/2} \phi(2^{-j} t - n) \, dt$$

**Step 3.** Substitute the two-scale equation and rearrange, expressing $c_j[n]$ in terms of $c_{j-1}[\cdot]$:

$$c_j[n] = \sum_{k} h_0[k - 2n] \, c_{j-1}[k]$$

**Step 4.** This is *exactly* a **decimation-by-2 of the convolution** $c_{j-1} * h_0$. The wavelet detail coefficient analogously satisfies:

$$d_j[n] = \sum_{k} h_1[k - 2n] \, c_{j-1}[k]$$

**Step 5.** The reconstruction (synthesis) from $\{c_J, d_1, \ldots, d_J\}$ back to $c_{j-1}$ uses the synthesis filters $g_0, g_1$:

$$c_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} g_0[n - 2k]\, c_j[k] + \sum_{k} g_1[n - 2k]\, d_j[k]$$

This is precisely a **two-channel synthesis filter bank** with upsampling by 2.

$$\boxed{\;\text{DWT} \equiv \text{Cascade of two-channel QMF filter banks}\;}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Haar DWT of a 4-Point Signal

Let $x = [5, 1, 3, 7]^T$ and Haar filters: $h_0 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, 1]$, $h_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, -1]$.

**Step 1.** Lowpass (average) output, decimated by 2:
$$c_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(5 + 1) = \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}} = 3\sqrt{2}$$
$$c_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 + 7) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2.** Highpass (detail) output, decimated by 2:
$$d_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(5 - 1) = 2\sqrt{2}$$
$$d_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(3 - 7) = -2\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3.** Energy check: $\sum x^2 = 25 + 1 + 9 + 49 = 84$. Sum of squares of coefficients: $18 + 50 + 8 + 8 = 84$. **Parseval holds** Ō£ō.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024] *Define the continuous wavelet transform. State the admissibility condition for the mother wavelet.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$W_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt$$

where $a$ is the scale parameter, $b$ is the translation parameter, and $\psi(t)$ is the mother wavelet. **(1 mark)**

The **admissibility condition** requires that the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ satisfies:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} \, d\omega < \infty$$

This implies that $\Psi(0) = 0$, i.e., the wavelet must have a **zero mean** (band-pass behaviour) and decays at infinity. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023] *What is a Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) bank? State the conditions for perfect reconstruction.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A QMF bank is a two-channel filter bank in which the highpass filter is the mirror-image (spectral reflection) of the lowpass filter. **(1 mark)**

The mirror condition is $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$. The reconstruction filters are chosen as $G_0(z) = H_1(-z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$. **(1 mark)**

**Perfect reconstruction** requires:
1. **Alias cancellation** (satisfied by the QMF pairing above) and
2. **Power-complementary magnitude:** $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$, so that the overall transfer function is a pure delay. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part-B wavelet questions, students *consistently* lose marks by (a) forgetting to state the **admissibility condition** explicitly, (b) confusing the **CWT** and **DWT** definitions, and (c) omitting the **Parseval/energy-preservation** property in filter-bank derivations. Always write the **mother-wavelet admissibility constant** $C_\psi$ whenever a wavelet is introduced. Always explicitly mention the **decimation factor** (= 2) in any filter-bank block diagram. Marks are awarded for the **block diagram** even if the algebra is incomplete.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) starting from the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT). Explain how the CWT overcomes the fixed-resolution limitation of the STFT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a discrete-time signal $x[n] = [4, 6, 10, 12, 8, 6, 2, 4]$ (length 8), compute the **first-level Haar DWT coefficients** $\{cA_1, cD_1\}$. Verify the Parseval energy identity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q A(a)

**Step 1.** The STFT is defined as:

$$\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, w^{*}(t - \tau) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

where $w(t)$ is a fixed-duration window. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2.** Define a *mother wavelet* by $\psi(t) = w(t) e^{j\omega_c t}$. The STFT kernel can be rewritten as:

$$\text{STFT}_x(\tau, \omega) = \int x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - \tau}{\omega/\omega_c}\right) \, dt$$

**Step 3.** Introduce the substitutions $a = \omega_c / \omega$ (scale) and $b = \tau$ (translation), and the factor $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ for energy normalisation:

$$W_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - b}{a}\right) dt$$

This is the CWT. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4.** *How CWT overcomes the fixed-resolution limitation:* In the STFT, the window $w(t)$ has a constant time-width $\sigma_t$ at all frequencies, so the Heisenberg box has fixed area. In the CWT, scaling the wavelet by $a$ automatically **stretches the window in time as the frequency drops**, so high frequencies get fine time resolution and low frequencies get fine frequency resolution. This is the **multi-resolution (constant-Q) analysis**. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q A(b)

**Haar filters:** $h_0[n] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, 1]$, $h_1[n] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, -1]$.

**Step 1. Approximation (lowpass, average) coefficients:**

$$cA_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 + 6) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2}$$
$$cA_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 + 12) = \tfrac{22}{\sqrt{2}} = 11\sqrt{2}$$
$$cA_1[2] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8 + 6) = \tfrac{14}{\sqrt{2}} = 7\sqrt{2}$$
$$cA_1[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2 + 4) = \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}} = 3\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 2. Detail (highpass, difference) coefficients:**

$$cD_1[0] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 - 6) = -\sqrt{2}$$
$$cD_1[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 - 12) = -\sqrt{2}$$
$$cD_1[2] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8 - 6) = \sqrt{2}$$
$$cD_1[3] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(2 - 4) = -\sqrt{2}$$

**(4 marks ŌĆö both coefficient sets shown)**

**Step 3. Parseval verification:**

Energy of original signal: $E_x = 4^2 + 6^2 + 10^2 + 12^2 + 8^2 + 6^2 + 2^2 + 4^2 = 16 + 36 + 100 + 144 + 64 + 36 + 4 + 16 = 416$. **(1 mark)**

Energy of wavelet coefficients: $E_W = (5\sqrt{2})^2 + (11\sqrt{2})^2 + (7\sqrt{2})^2 + (3\sqrt{2})^2 + (-\sqrt{2})^2 + (-\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2 + (-\sqrt{2})^2$

$E_W = 50 + 242 + 98 + 18 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 416$. **(2 marks)**

**Result:** $E_x = E_W = 416$ Ō£ō ŌĆö Parseval identity holds, confirming the Haar DWT preserves energy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a two-channel analysisŌĆōsynthesis filter bank. Derive the necessary and sufficient conditions for **perfect reconstruction** in terms of the analysis filters $H_0(z), H_1(z)$ and the synthesis filters $G_0(z), G_1(z)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA), show that the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) can be implemented as a **cascade of two-channel filter banks**. Sketch the 3-level decomposition tree and identify the frequency bands of each output, assuming a sampling rate of $f_s = 360$ Hz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q B(a)

**Step 1. Block diagram (textual, since examiner will accept a neat sketch):**

```
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆ H0(z) ŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåō2 ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ                      ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåæ2 ŌöĆŌöĆ G0(z) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
X(z) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöż                  Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ Sub-band 0       Ōöé                  Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆŌåÆ ┼Č(z)
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ H1(z) ŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåō2 ŌöĆŌöĆŌöś                      ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåæ2 ŌöĆŌöĆ G1(z) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
        ŌöīŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåæ2 ŌöĆŌöĆ G0(z) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöÉ
        Ōöé                  Ōöé
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ Ōåæ2 ŌöĆŌöĆ G1(z) ŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
```
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2. Reconstructed output in Z-domain:**

From standard two-channel analysis with downsamplers followed by upsamplers and synthesis filters, the overall transfer function is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z)\big] X(z) + \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z)\big] X(-z)$$

Let $T(z)$ be the distortion transfer function and $A(z)$ be the aliasing term. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3. Alias cancellation:**

Setting $A(z) = 0$ gives the well-known QMF synthesis pairing:

$$G_0(z) = H_1(-z), \qquad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$$

Substituting verifies that $A(z) = 0$ identically. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4. Distortion-free (PR) condition:**

With alias cancelled, $T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0(z) H_1(-z) - H_1(z) H_0(-z)\big]$. Imposing the **QMF mirror** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$:

$$T(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)\big]$$

For PR we need $\vert T(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega$, which is equivalent to the **power-complementary** condition:

$$\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$$

When this holds, $T(z) = c \, z^{-k}$ and so $\hat{x}[n] = c\, x[n-k]$. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Q B(b)

**Step 1. MRA definition:** MRA states that any $L^2$ signal can be decomposed as the sum of a coarse approximation at scale $J$ plus the details $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_J$ at successively finer scales. The scaling function $\phi(t)$ and wavelet $\psi(t)$ satisfy the two-scale equations:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_0[n] \phi(2t - n), \qquad \psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_n h_1[n] \phi(2t - n)$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2. Equivalence to filter banks:** The approximation coefficient at scale $j$ is obtained from scale $j-1$ via:

$$c_j[n] = \sum_k h_0[k - 2n] \, c_{j-1}[k] \quad \text{(lowpass + decimate-by-2)}$$
$$d_j[n] = \sum_k h_1[k - 2n] \, c_{j-1}[k] \quad \text{(highpass + decimate-by-2)}$$

This is *precisely* a two-channel analysis filter bank. Reconstruction is the synthesis bank with $g_0, g_1$. Thus DWT ŌēĪ cascade of two-channel QMFs. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3. Three-level decomposition tree:**

| Output | Frequency Band (Hz, for $f_s = 360$) | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|
| $cA_3$ | $[0, 22.5]$ | 22.5 Hz |
| $cD_3$ | $[22.5, 45]$ | 22.5 Hz |
| $cD_2$ | $[45, 90]$ | 45 Hz |
| $cD_1$ | $[90, 180]$ | 90 Hz |

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4. Biomedical interpretation:** With $f_s = 360$ Hz, $cD_1$ captures EMG / high-frequency noise above 90 Hz; $cD_2$ (45ŌĆō90 Hz) overlaps with pacemaker spikes; $cD_3$ (22.5ŌĆō45 Hz) sits in the QRS-region; $cA_3$ contains baseline wander and the bulk of low-frequency ECG/EEG components. **(2 marks)**
