## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 2: Wavelets & Filter Banks**

- ŌśÉ **CWT** decomposes a signal into a 2-D time-scale plane $(a, b)$ using $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\tfrac{t-b}{a}\right)$.
- ŌśÉ **Admissibility condition** $C_{\psi} = \int_0^\infty \tfrac{\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ is mandatory ŌĆö *write it on the first line* of every wavelet answer.
- ŌśÉ **Mother wavelet must have zero mean** ($\int \psi(t)\,dt = 0$) and decays to zero.
- ŌśÉ **DWT uses dyadic grid**: $a = 2^m, b = n 2^m$, giving $\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \psi(2^{-m} t - n)$.
- ŌśÉ **MRA** is the bridge between wavelets and filter banks.
- ŌśÉ **Two-scale equations**: $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum h_0[n] \phi(2t - n)$ and $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum h_1[n] \phi(2t - n)$.
- ŌśÉ **QMF condition** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ ensures **alias cancellation**.
- ŌśÉ **PR amplitude condition**: $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega+\pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$.
- ŌśÉ **DWT = cascade of two-channel QMFs** ŌĆö *single most tested relationship* in KTU ESE.
- ŌśÉ **Decimation factor is always 2** in standard dyadic banks; band width halves every level.
- ŌśÉ **Parseval / energy preservation** holds for orthonormal wavelets ŌĆö verify when asked.
- ŌśÉ **Haar wavelet**: simplest DWT filter, $h_0 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1,1]$, $h_1 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1,-1]$.
- ŌśÉ **Daubechies-4 (db4)**: longer support, smoother, used for ECG denoising in research literature.
- ŌśÉ **Biomedical application map**: ECG QRS ŌåÆ $cD_1, cD_2$ (high freq); EEG rhythms ŌåÆ $cA_j$ at appropriate levels; PCG S1/S2 ŌåÆ $cD_2$ to $cA_3$ bands.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU 2024 ESE**
> 1. Confusing the CWT inner product sign ŌĆö remember $W_x$ uses the **complex conjugate** $\psi^{*}$.
> 2. Writing the admissibility condition with $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert$ instead of $\vert \Psi(\omega) \vert^2$.
> 3. Forgetting the $1/a^2$ Jacobian in the inverse CWT formula.
> 4. Drawing filter banks without the **$\downarrow 2$** and **$\uparrow 2$** sampling-rate changers.
> 5. Omitting the constant $\tfrac{1}{2}$ from the decimation equation ŌĆö the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises from the identity $X(z^2) = \tfrac{1}{2}[X(z^{1/2}) + X(-z^{1/2})]$.
> 6. Calling $H_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$ the QMF condition (the **sign** of the mirror is *implementation-dependent*; the canonical form uses $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Pursuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching Pursuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤ōś Module 2 ŌĆö Matching Pursuit (MP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Focus:** Sparse signal representations of biomedical signals. **Matching Pursuit (MP)**, introduced by *Mallat and Zhang (1993)*, is the foundational greedy algorithm that powers modern sparse-coding frameworks used in EEG, ECG, and neural spike analysis.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Matching Pursuit (MP)** is a *greedy, iterative, adaptive sparse-decomposition algorithm* that approximates any finite-energy signal $f \in \mathcal{H}$ (a Hilbert space) as a linear combination of waveforms (called **atoms**) drawn from a **redundant dictionary** $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$.

At each iteration $n$, MP selects the atom $g_{\gamma_n} \in \mathcal{D}$ that has the **maximum absolute inner product** with the current residual $R^n f$, and then subtracts its projection from the residual. The dictionary $\mathcal{D}$ is said to be *redundant* when the number of atoms $|\Gamma|$ is much larger than the signal dimension, allowing for sparse, non-orthogonal expansions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight ŌĆö Three Pillars of MP:**
> 1. **Greedy** ŌĆö picks the best local atom at every step.
> 2. **Sparse** ŌĆö only $N \ll$ dimension atoms are kept.
> 3. **Adaptive** ŌĆö atoms are data-driven (not pre-fixed like in DFT/DWT).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Forensic Sketch Artist:**
> Imagine a police artist trying to reconstruct a face from memory using only a *huge* box of facial-feature stickers (eyes, noses, mouths, eyebrows). The box is **redundant** ŌĆö there are 500 eyes to choose from, 300 noses, etc.
>
> 1. He looks at the residual "blank canvas" (initially, the full unknown face).
> 2. He picks the single sticker that **best matches** the still-unexplained portion.
> 3. He sticks it on, **subtracts** that feature mentally, and looks at what remains.
> 4. He repeats until the canvas looks like the suspect.
>
> **MP works exactly the same way** ŌĆö the "canvas" is the residual signal, the "stickers" are dictionary atoms, and the rule is *"always pick the atom with the largest correlation to what is left."*

> [!TIP]
> A **dictionary atom** $g_{\gamma}$ is a unit-norm ($\|g_{\gamma}\| = 1$) elementary waveform, often a *modulated Gaussian* (Gabor atom): $g_{\gamma}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{s}}\, g\!\left(\frac{t-u}{s}\right)\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, where $\gamma = (s, u, \omega, \phi)$ indexes scale, translation, frequency, and phase.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in MP (KTU Board-Important)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value in Biomedical MP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sparsity level** | $N$ | **5ŌĆō30 atoms** for an EEG epoch |
| **Residual energy decay rate** | $\lambda$ | $O(n^{-1})$ (Mallat-Zhang bound) |
| **Reconstruction SNR** | $\text{SNR}_{\text{rec}}$ | **10ŌĆō25 dB** for spike detection |
| **Dictionary redundancy factor** | $K = \frac{\vert\Gamma\vert}{N_{\text{sig}}}$ | **5 to 20** |
| **Gabor atom count** | $\vert\Gamma\vert$ | **~1,000ŌĆō10,000** for 1-second epoch |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Concept ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The Matching Pursuit algorithm operates on the principle of **repeated orthogonal projection** onto a chosen atom. Each iteration produces:
- A *new best atom* (one more term in the expansion).
- A *smaller residual* (less of the signal remains unexplained).

### Step-by-Step Algorithm Logic

1. **Initialization**
   - Set the initial residual equal to the original signal: $R^{0} f = f$.
   - Set the iteration counter $n = 0$.
   - Initialize the sparse coefficient set: $C = \emptyset$.

2. **Inner-Product Sweep (Correlogram)**
   - For **every atom** $g_{\gamma} \in \mathcal{D}$, compute the absolute inner product $\vert\langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert$.
   - **Why?** The inner product measures *how much of $R^n f$ lies along the direction of $g_\gamma$*. The atom that gives the largest magnitude is the one that explains the most energy in a single step.

3. **Greedy Selection**
   - Choose the optimal index:
     $$\gamma_{n} = \arg\max_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\, \bigl\vert\langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma}\rangle\bigr\vert$$
   - The corresponding coefficient is $c_n = \langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle$.
   - **Why greedy?** A single best atom minimizes the *immediate* residual energy ŌĆö this is optimal for one step, not globally.

4. **Residual Update (Energy Subtraction)**
   - The residual is updated by removing the contribution of the chosen atom:
     $$R^{n+1} f \;=\; R^{n} f \;-\; \langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle \, g_{\gamma_{n}}$$
   - This is a **vector projection** formula: the residual becomes orthogonal to $g_{\gamma_n}$.

5. **Convergence Check**
   - Stop when either:
     (a) $\Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} < \varepsilon$ (energy threshold), **or**
     (b) $n \geq N_{\max}$ (sparsity budget).

6. **Sparse Reconstruction**
   - The final $N$-term approximation is:
     $$f \;\approx\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \langle R^{k} f,\, g_{\gamma_{k}}\rangle \, g_{\gamma_{k}} \;+\; R^{N} f$$
   - The chosen atoms $\{g_{\gamma_0}, g_{\gamma_1}, \dots, g_{\gamma_{N-1}}\}$ are called the **book of features** of the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mallat-Zhang Convergence Theorem (1993):** After $N$ iterations of MP, the residual energy satisfies
> $$\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; \bigl(1 - \alpha^{2}\bigr)^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}$$
> where $\alpha = \sup_{\gamma} \vert\langle f, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert / \Vert f\Vert$ is the *worst-case correlation*. This proves **exponential decay** of the residual ŌĆö a key KTU answer point.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Atom selection rule** | $\gamma_n = \arg\max_{\gamma} \vert\langle R^n f, g_\gamma\rangle\vert$ | Greedy best-atom picker |
| 2 | **Residual update** | $R^{n+1} f = R^n f - \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle\, g_{\gamma_n}$ | Removes explained energy |
| 3 | **Energy decay** | $\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} = \Vert R^n f\Vert^{2} - \vert\langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle\vert^{2}$ | Convergence monitor |
| 4 | **Mallat-Zhang bound** | $\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2} \leq (1 - \alpha^{2})^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}$ | Worst-case error bound |
| 5 | **Approximation** | $f \approx \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} c_k\, g_{\gamma_k} + R^{N} f$ | Sparse reconstruction |
| 6 | **Coefficient** | $c_k = \langle R^{k} f, g_{\gamma_k}\rangle$ | Amplitude of $k$-th atom |
| 7 | **Gabor atom** | $g_\gamma(t) = K_\gamma \cdot e^{-\pi (t-u)^{2}/s^{2}} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | Time-frequency atom |
| 8 | **Dictionary redundancy** | $K = \vert\Gamma\vert / N_{\text{sig}}$ | Higher $K$ ŌåÆ sparser codes |
| 9 | **OMP modification** | Re-orthogonalize all selected atoms at each step | Faster, no redundant picks |
| 10 | **Reconstruction SNR** | $\text{SNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\bigl(\Vert f\Vert^{2}/\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2}\bigr)$ | Quality metric in dB |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of MP in Biomedical Engineering

- **EEG Seizure Detection:** Decompose an EEG epoch into ~10 Gabor atoms; the **time-frequency "atoms-book"** becomes a seizure fingerprint for a classifier.
- **Evoked Potential Extraction:** MP separates the stimulus-locked response from ongoing EEG background, similar to *wavelet denoising* but data-adaptive.
- **Neural Spike Sorting:** Each action potential is a single Gabor atom; clustering of $\{u_k, s_k, \omega_k\}$ sorts spikes from different neurons.
- **ECG Late-Potential Analysis:** MP resolves low-amplitude micro-potentials hidden in QRS tails, used in post-MI risk stratification.
- **fMRI / BOLD Sparse Coding:** Sparse dictionary learning (generalization of MP) recovers task-related activation maps.

> [!TIP]
> **MP vs. DWT:** Both are sparse, but DWT uses a *fixed, orthogonal* basis (poor for chirps/transients), while MP uses a *redundant, data-adaptive* dictionary ŌĆö **much better for non-stationary biomedical signals**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Energy Decay After One MP Step

We start with residual $R^n f$ and pick the optimal atom $g_{\gamma_n}$ satisfying

$$c_n \;=\; \langle R^n f,\, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle \;=\; \max_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\, \vert\langle R^n f,\, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert$$

The updated residual is

$$R^{n+1} f \;=\; R^{n} f \;-\; c_{n}\, g_{\gamma_{n}}$$

To compute its energy, take the squared norm:

$$\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} \;=\; \langle R^{n+1} f,\, R^{n+1} f\rangle$$

Expand the right-hand side by substituting the residual update:

$$= \langle R^{n} f - c_{n} g_{\gamma_{n}},\, R^{n} f - c_{n} g_{\gamma_{n}} \rangle$$

Distribute the inner product (bilinearity):

$$= \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;-\; c_{n} \langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle \;-\; c_{n} \langle g_{\gamma_{n}},\, R^{n} f\rangle \;+\; \vert c_{n}\vert^{2} \Vert g_{\gamma_{n}}\Vert^{2}$$

Because atoms are real and unit-norm ($\Vert g_{\gamma_n}\Vert = 1$), and $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle$ is real-valued, the two cross terms collapse to $-2 c_n^{2}$. Thus:

$$= \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;-\; 2 c_{n}^{2} \;+\; c_{n}^{2}$$

$$= \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;-\; c_{n}^{2}$$

Substitute back $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle$:

$$\boxed{\;\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} \;=\; \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;-\; \bigl\vert\langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle\bigr\vert^{2}\;}$$

**Physical meaning:** Each iteration removes *exactly* $c_n^{2}$ units of signal energy, equal to the squared correlation with the chosen atom. The energy removed in one step is **at most** the energy removed by any other sparse-coding rule ŌĆö this is the *energy-optimality* of the greedy choice.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Mallat-Zhang Exponential Convergence Bound

We start from the energy-decay identity and introduce the *worst-case correlation ratio*:

$$\alpha \;=\; \sup_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\, \frac{\vert\langle f,\, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert}{\Vert f\Vert}$$

For any signal $f$, MP guarantees

$$\bigl\vert\langle R^{n} f,\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle\bigr\vert \;\geq\; \alpha\, \Vert R^{n} f\Vert$$

because if this were false for *all* atoms, the supremum definition of $\alpha$ would be contradicted. Therefore the *worst-case* energy removed per step is:

$$\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;-\; \alpha^{2}\, \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;=\; (1 - \alpha^{2})\,\Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2}$$

Apply this inequality recursively $N$ times starting from $\Vert R^{0} f\Vert = \Vert f\Vert$:

$$\Vert R^{1} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; (1 - \alpha^{2})\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}$$

$$\Vert R^{2} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; (1 - \alpha^{2})^{2}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}$$

Inductively, after $N$ iterations:

$$\boxed{\;\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; (1 - \alpha^{2})^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}\;}$$

**Practical interpretation:** Even for the *worst* signal-to-dictionary mismatch, MP still drives the residual to zero exponentially fast. For a redundancy factor of $K = 10$, typically $\alpha \approx 0.4$, so the bound gives a half-life of about $N_{1/2} = \ln(2)/\ln(1/(1-\alpha^{2})) \approx 4$ iterations ŌĆö exactly why only **~10ŌĆō20 atoms** are needed for an EEG epoch.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö OMP (Orthogonal Matching Pursuit) Re-Projection

Plain MP can re-select atoms that are **almost orthogonal to previously selected ones** but **not quite**, leaving residual energy. OMP fixes this by re-projecting the *original* signal onto the span of all selected atoms at every step. The OMP residual is defined as the orthogonal projection error:

$$R^{N}_{\text{OMP}} f \;=\; f \;-\; P_{V_N} f$$

where $P_{V_N}$ is the orthogonal projection onto the subspace $V_N = \text{span}(g_{\gamma_0}, g_{\gamma_1}, \dots, g_{\gamma_{N-1}})$. Using the projection formula:

$$P_{V_N} f \;=\; G\,(G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f$$

with $G = [g_{\gamma_0}\ \ g_{\gamma_1}\ \dots\ g_{\gamma_{N-1}}]$ the matrix of selected atoms. The least-squares coefficient vector is:

$$\mathbf{c} \;=\; (G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f$$

giving the OMP reconstruction:

$$\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; G\,(G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f \;+\; R^{N}_{\text{OMP}} f\;}$$

OMP requires solving a small linear system each step ($O(N^{2} d)$ for $N$-term code of $d$-dim atoms) but converges in **at most $d$ iterations** ŌĆö a hard guarantee MP lacks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Matching Pursuit Algorithm ŌĆö Block-Level Flow

The figure below shows the **functional architecture** of one MP iteration, including the dictionary, correlator, atom selector, and residual feedback loop. Biomedical instruments (EEG/ECG front-end) feed the analog signal $f(t)$ into the digitizer; the discrete samples enter the MP block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Discrete signal f of length L] --> B[Initialize residual R0 f equal to f]
    B --> C[Set iteration counter n to 0]
    C --> D[Correlator: Compute inner product of Rn f with every atom in dictionary D]
    D --> E[Selector: Find atom index gamma_n that maximizes absolute inner product]
    E --> F[Record coefficient c_n equal to inner product at gamma_n]
    F --> G[Subtract projection: update residual Rn+1 f]
    G --> H{Stopping rule: residual energy below threshold OR n equals Nmax}
    H -->|Not satisfied| I[Increment n by 1]
    I --> D
    H -->|Satisfied| J[Output sparse code: list of atom indices gamma_k and coefficients c_k]
    J --> K[Reconstructor: Build approximation f hat from selected atoms]
    K --> L[Biomedical Application Layer: Seizure detection or spike sorting or feature extraction]
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU Board Interpretation):**

- **Node AŌĆōC** ŌåÆ Initialization phase.
- **Node D** ŌåÆ *Correlator* (most computationally expensive ŌĆö for a 1000-atom dictionary, 1000 inner products per iteration).
- **Node E** ŌåÆ *Greedy selector* ŌĆö equivalent to finding the maximum of the correlogram.
- **Node G** ŌåÆ *Residual subtractor* ŌĆö this is the feedback that makes MP iterative.
- **Node H** ŌåÆ *Stopping rule* ŌĆö energy threshold or sparsity cap.
- **Node L** ŌåÆ Real biomedical output (e.g., classifier input).

---

## 4.2 Functional Pipeline ŌĆö From Raw EEG to Sparse Atoms

The diagram below maps the **engineering pipeline** for using MP on a single-channel EEG epoch: raw epoch ŌåÆ DC removal ŌåÆ MP decomposition ŌåÆ atom feature vector ŌåÆ classifier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw EEG epoch: 1 second, 256 samples] --> B[Preprocessing: bandpass 0.5 to 40 Hz and notch 50 Hz]
    B --> C[Gabor dictionary D with 5000 atoms: scale, position, frequency, phase]
    C --> D[MP engine: 20 iterations, residual threshold 0.01]
    D --> E[Sparse code: 20 tuples of scale, position, frequency, phase, amplitude]
    E --> F[Feature extractor: histogram of scales, mean frequency, atom density]
    F --> G[Classifier: SVM or random forest for seizure vs normal]
```

**Engineering insight:** The "feature vector" in node F is **derived from the atoms**, not from the raw samples ŌĆö this is why MP gives a *physically interpretable* sparse representation. The atom parameters $(s_k, u_k, \omega_k, \phi_k)$ are themselves the features.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: 2 questions ├Ś 3 marks = **6 marks**
> - Part B: 1 question (with internal choice) ├Ś 14 marks = **14 marks**
> - Total for this topic module weightage: **20 marks** (scaled from module-CSE pattern).

---

## ­¤ōØ PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PEBRT865]**
**Define Matching Pursuit. What is meant by a "redundant dictionary" in the context of MP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Matching Pursuit (MP) is a *greedy, iterative, adaptive sparse-decomposition algorithm* introduced by Mallat and Zhang (1993) that approximates a finite-energy signal $f \in \mathcal{H}$ as a linear combination of $N$ waveforms (atoms) chosen from a dictionary $\mathcal{D} = \{g_{\gamma}\}_{\gamma \in \Gamma}$. At each iteration, the algorithm picks the atom that maximizes the absolute inner product with the current residual, then subtracts its projection from the residual.

A **redundant dictionary** is one in which the number of atoms $\vert\Gamma\vert$ is much larger than the signal dimension $d$ (i.e., $K = \vert\Gamma\vert / d \gg 1$). This redundancy is essential because it allows MP to represent a wider variety of signal structures (chirps, transients, oscillations) using a *small* set of atoms, achieving sparse codes that an orthogonal basis (like DFT/DWT) cannot provide.

*(1 mark ŌĆö definition; 1 mark ŌĆö algorithm idea; 1 mark ŌĆö redundancy concept.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, PEBRT865]**
**State and explain the Mallat-Zhang convergence bound for Matching Pursuit. What does it guarantee?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

After $N$ iterations of MP on a signal $f$ with dictionary $\mathcal{D}$, the residual energy is bounded by:

$$\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; (1 - \alpha^{2})^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}$$

where $\alpha = \sup_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\, \frac{\vert\langle f, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert}{\Vert f\Vert}$ is the *worst-case normalized correlation* between the signal and any atom.

**Guarantee:** The bound states that the residual energy decays *exponentially* with iteration count $N$, regardless of the signal-to-dictionary mismatch. This means that for any finite-energy signal and any dictionary with $\alpha > 0$, MP will eventually drive the residual to zero (or below any threshold $\varepsilon$) in a finite number of iterations, making it a *universally convergent* sparse-decomposition method.

*(1 mark ŌĆö formula; 1 mark ŌĆö meaning of $\alpha$; 1 mark ŌĆö interpretation of exponential decay.)*

---

## ­¤ōØ PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Module-level choice is mandatory. Below, **Question A and Question B are both MP-centric, but at different cognitive levels** (per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).

---

### Ō£ģ Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, PEBRT865]

**Derive the residual-energy update equation for one iteration of the Matching Pursuit algorithm, starting from $R^{n+1} f = R^{n} f - \langle R^{n} f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle g_{\gamma_n}$. Hence obtain the Mallat-Zhang exponential convergence bound. Discuss its significance for biomedical signal decomposition (e.g., EEG seizure detection).**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of the Energy Decay Identity (7 marks)**

We begin with the residual update rule:

$$R^{n+1} f \;=\; R^{n} f \;-\; c_{n}\, g_{\gamma_{n}}\quad\text{where}\quad c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle$$

Compute the squared norm:

$$\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} = \langle R^{n+1} f,\, R^{n+1} f\rangle$$

Substitute the update:

$$= \langle R^{n} f - c_{n} g_{\gamma_{n}},\, R^{n} f - c_{n} g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle$$

Expand using bilinearity:

$$= \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} - 2 c_{n} \langle R^{n} f, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle + c_{n}^{2}\Vert g_{\gamma_{n}}\Vert^{2}$$

Since $\Vert g_{\gamma_n}\Vert = 1$ and $c_n = \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle$:

$$= \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} - 2 c_{n}^{2} + c_{n}^{2} = \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} - c_{n}^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} = \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} - \bigl\vert\langle R^{n} f, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle\bigr\vert^{2}\;}$$

This identity is the **energy bookkeeping law** of MP: each iteration removes *exactly* the squared inner product with the chosen atom. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for algebra, 2 for final boxed result and interpretation.)

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Mallat-Zhang Bound and Biomedical Significance (7 marks)**

Define the worst-case correlation ratio for the residual:

$$\alpha = \sup_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\, \frac{\vert\langle R^{n} f, g_{\gamma}\rangle\vert}{\Vert R^{n} f\Vert}$$

By definition of supremum, there exists at least one atom with $\vert\langle R^{n} f, g_{\gamma_{n}}\rangle\vert \geq \alpha \Vert R^{n} f\Vert$. Substituting into the energy law:

$$\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; \Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} - \alpha^{2}\,\Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2} \;=\; (1 - \alpha^{2})\,\Vert R^{n} f\Vert^{2}$$

Recursion over $N$ iterations yields:

$$\boxed{\;\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^{2} \;\leq\; (1 - \alpha^{2})^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^{2}\;}$$

**Biomedical Significance (EEG Seizure Detection):**

1. **Computational tractability:** A 1-second EEG epoch (256 samples) can be reconstructed with $\sim$10ŌĆō20 atoms at SNR $>$ 15 dB, since the half-life of the residual is only $\sim$4ŌĆō5 iterations. This is essential for real-time seizure monitoring.

2. **Physically interpretable features:** The selected atoms form a *sparse "fingerprint"* of the seizure epoch ŌĆö the histogram of atom scales reveals the slow-wave component (0.5ŌĆō4 Hz), the histogram of atom frequencies reveals the rhythmic discharge, and atom positions reveal the temporal evolution.

3. **Robustness to noise:** Because the residual decreases exponentially, MP automatically discards random noise (which has low correlation with *any* atom) and retains the dominant structured components ŌĆö exactly what is needed for EEG denoising.

4. **Generalization to dictionary learning:** Once the atoms are chosen for many epochs, the dictionary can be updated (K-SVD, MOD) for patient-specific MP, improving seizure-detection accuracy by 8ŌĆō15% over generic dictionaries.

(7 marks: 2 for bound derivation, 1 for interpretation, 4 for biomedical discussion ŌĆö break down by sub-points above.)

---

### Ō£ģ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PEBRT865]

**(a)** Explain the difference between Matching Pursuit (MP) and Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP). Derive the OMP reconstruction formula using the orthogonal-projection operator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a block diagram how MP is used in **neural spike sorting** from extracellular recordings. What role do Gabor atoms play, and what features of the atoms are used for clustering spikes from different neurons? **(7 marks)**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö MP vs. OMP and OMP Reconstruction (7 marks)**

**Differences (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Matching Pursuit (MP) | Orthogonal Matching Pursuit (OMP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Residual update | Subtract one atom's projection | Re-project on *all* selected atoms |
| Re-selection risk | Can pick near-orthogonal duplicates | Guaranteed no re-selection (until full basis) |
| Iteration count | May need many more iterations | Converges in $\leq d$ iterations |
| Cost per step | $O(d)$ | $O(N d + N^{2})$ (least-squares) |
| Optimality | Energy-optimal *per step*, not global | Energy-optimal *globally* over chosen atoms |

**OMP Reconstruction Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $G = [g_{\gamma_0}\ g_{\gamma_1}\ \dots\ g_{\gamma_{N-1}}]$ be the $d \times N$ matrix of selected atoms. The OMP approximation lies in the column space $V_N = \text{span}(G)$. The orthogonal projection of $f$ onto $V_N$ is:

$$P_{V_N} f \;=\; G\,(G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f$$

The least-squares coefficient vector is obtained by minimizing $\Vert f - G \mathbf{c}\Vert^{2}$:

$$\mathbf{c}^{\*} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{c}}\, \Vert f - G \mathbf{c}\Vert^{2} = (G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f$$

Therefore the OMP sparse approximation is:

$$\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; G\,(G^{T} G)^{-1}\, G^{T} f \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} c^{\*}_{k}\, g_{\gamma_{k}}\;}$$

The residual is **by construction orthogonal to all selected atoms**, i.e., $\langle R^{N}_{\text{OMP}} f, g_{\gamma_k}\rangle = 0$ for all $k$, which is why OMP avoids redundant picks. (Total 7 marks: 3 for comparison table, 1 for projection formula, 2 for least-squares coefficient, 1 for final OMP result.)

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö MP-Based Neural Spike Sorting (7 marks)**

**Pipeline:**

1. **Acquisition:** A microelectrode records extracellular voltage at 20ŌĆō30 kHz. Raw signal contains (a) neural action potentials ("spikes"), (b) background LFP, (c) thermal/line noise.

2. **Preprocessing:** Bandpass filter 300ŌĆō3000 Hz to isolate spike band; threshold at $-4\sigma$ to detect spike events.

3. **MP Decomposition:** For each detected spike window (typically 1ŌĆō2 ms = 32ŌĆō64 samples), decompose using a small Gabor dictionary with atoms:
   $$g_{\gamma}(t) = K_\gamma \cdot e^{-\pi (t-u)^{2}/s^{2}} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$
   Parameters: scale $s$ (spike width), position $u$ (spike time), frequency $\omega$ (after-hyperpolarization), phase $\phi$.

4. **Atom Feature Extraction:** For each spike, retain the *first* 2ŌĆō3 atoms and extract their parameters: $(s_k, u_k, \omega_k, c_k)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2$.

5. **Clustering:** Stack the feature vectors from many spikes and apply $K$-means or DBSCAN. Each cluster corresponds to a *single neuron*.

6. **Unit Identification:** Cluster centroids represent the average waveform of a single neuron. Cross-correlation with the original electrode data assigns spikes to units.

**Role of Gabor Atoms:** Their *time-frequency localization* matches the morphology of an action potential ŌĆö fast depolarization (high $\omega$) followed by slower repolarization (lower $\omega$, wider $s$). Unlike template-matching, MP is *adaptive*: it works even when spike shapes drift slowly over a recording session.

**Features used for clustering:** Width $s$, dominant frequency $\omega$, and the relative coefficient $c_1/c_0$ (which captures after-spike oscillation) are the three most discriminative features per KTU-board-recommended literature.

(7 marks: 2 for pipeline, 2 for atom role, 2 for clustering features, 1 for unit identification.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤Ü© KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting atom unit-norm:** In derivations, always state $\Vert g_{\gamma}\Vert = 1$. *Skipping this loses 1 mark in Part A and 2 marks in Part B derivations.*
> 2. **Mixing MP and OMP residuals:** Plain MP residual is *not* orthogonal to all selected atoms ŌĆö only to the *last* one. OMP residual *is* orthogonal to all. This is a favourite KTU trick question.
> 3. **Confusing the dictionary size $|\Gamma|$ with the sparsity $N$:** $\vert\Gamma\vert$ is the *number of atoms in the dictionary* (e.g., 5000), while $N$ is the *number actually used* to represent the signal (e.g., 20). Conflating them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions:** Mallat-Zhang bound requires $0 < \alpha \leq 1$. If $\alpha = 0$, the bound is trivial ($\Vert R^N f\Vert = \Vert f\Vert$ ŌĆö no progress!). State this explicitly for full marks.
> 5. **Writing $R^{n+1} f = R^{n} f + \dots$ (plus sign):** The correct sign is *minus*. This is a top-of-the-list silly mistake examiners mark strictly.

---

## ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ MP is a **greedy, adaptive, sparse** signal-decomposition algorithm introduced by **Mallat & Zhang (1993)**.
- Ō£ģ A **dictionary atom** $g_\gamma$ is a unit-norm elementary waveform, often a **Gabor atom** $g(t) = K e^{-\pi(t-u)^2/s^2}\cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
- Ō£ģ **Three core equations to memorize:**
  (i) Atom selection: $\gamma_n = \arg\max_\gamma \vert\langle R^n f, g_\gamma\rangle\vert$
  (ii) Residual update: $R^{n+1} f = R^n f - \langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle g_{\gamma_n}$
  (iii) Energy decay: $\Vert R^{n+1} f\Vert^2 = \Vert R^n f\Vert^2 - \vert\langle R^n f, g_{\gamma_n}\rangle\vert^2$
- Ō£ģ **Mallat-Zhang bound:** $\Vert R^{N} f\Vert^2 \leq (1 - \alpha^2)^{N}\,\Vert f\Vert^2$ ŌĆö exponential convergence, where $\alpha = \sup_\gamma \vert\langle f, g_\gamma\rangle\vert/\Vert f\Vert$.
- Ō£ģ **MP vs. OMP:** MP = greedy per-step optimal, may re-select; OMP = globally orthogonal projection, no re-selection, $O(d)$ iteration bound.
- Ō£ģ **Biomedical applications:** EEG seizure fingerprinting, evoked-potential extraction, neural spike sorting, ECG late-potential analysis, fMRI sparse coding.
- Ō£ģ **Dictionary redundancy factor $K = \vert\Gamma\vert / d$** must be $\gg 1$ for sparse codes (typical: 5ŌĆō20).
- Ō£ģ **Stopping rules:** (a) residual energy $\Vert R^n f\Vert^2 < \varepsilon$, or (b) maximum iteration $N_{\max}$.
- Ō£ģ **Convergence guarantee:** Exponential decay in $N$ means only $\sim$10ŌĆō30 atoms needed for biomedical epochs at SNR $>$ 10 dB.
- Ō£ģ **KTU answer must include:** the three core equations + the Mallat-Zhang bound + at least one biomedical application for full marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications to Biomedical Signals: Analysis of ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications to Biomedical Signals: Analysis of ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiogram (ECG) Signal Analysis** is the systematic application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques ŌĆö including filtering, wavelet transforms, feature extraction, template matching, and morphological analysis ŌĆö to the raw electrical potential recorded at the body surface, in order to extract clinically meaningful information such as heart rate, rhythm, conduction abnormalities, and ischemic events.

The ECG is a quasi-periodic, non-stationary biomedical signal with a typical bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** and a dynamic range of approximately **┬▒5 mV**. Standard clinical sampling rate is **500 Hz** (per AAMI/ANSI EC11 standard), and standard lead configurations include the 12-lead system (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1ŌĆōV6).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Heart's Drumbeat Letter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the heart as a drummer in a concert hall. Each beat sends a coded letter (the ECG waveform) through wires (the body's tissues) to a microphone (the electrodes). However, the hall is noisy ŌĆö there is crowd murmuring (baseline wander), a flickering fluorescent light (50/60 Hz powerline interference), and echoes (high-frequency muscle noise). **ECG analysis is the art of cleaning that letter, reading its content (P-QRS-T waves), and deciding whether the drummer is healthy, racing, skipping beats, or in danger.** Just as a postal inspector uses filters to remove smudges and stamps, a biomedical engineer uses digital filters and algorithms to clean and interpret the ECG.

### 1.3 Standard ECG Waveform Components

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Adult ECG Wave Parameters (Lead II, Normal Sinus Rhythm):**
> - **P Wave:** Atrial depolarization; duration **80ŌĆō120 ms**; amplitude **< 2.5 mm (0.25 mV)**
> - **PR Interval:** Onset of P to onset of Q; normal **120ŌĆō200 ms**
> - **QRS Complex:** Ventricular depolarization; duration **80ŌĆō120 ms**; amplitude **0.5ŌĆō2.0 mV**
> - **T Wave:** Ventricular repolarization; duration **160 ms**; amplitude **< 5 mm (0.5 mV)**
> - **ST Segment:** End of S to start of T; isoelectric normally; elevation/depression clinically critical
> - **QT Interval:** Onset of QRS to end of T; rate-corrected (QTc) **< 440 ms (male)**, **< 460 ms (female)**
> - **U Wave:** Sometimes seen after T; origin debated (Purkinje repolarization / mech-electrical feedback)
> - **RR Interval:** Peak-to-peak distance; fundamental for HRV and heart rate calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Heart Rate Formula:** $\text{HR (bpm)} = \dfrac{60}{\text{RR interval (seconds)}}$, where **normal resting HR = 60ŌĆō100 bpm**. A single cardiac cycle spans approximately **0.6ŌĆō1.0 s** (Ōēł 60ŌĆō100 bpm).

### 1.4 Common ECG Artifacts and Noise Sources

| Noise Type | Frequency Band | Physiological/Technical Origin | Clinical Consequence if Unremoved |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Baseline Wander | 0ŌĆō0.5 Hz | Respiration, body movement | False ST-segment shifts |
| Powerline Interference | 50/60 Hz | Electromagnetic induction from mains | False QRS triggering, R-wave jitter |
| EMG / Muscle Noise | 20ŌĆō500 Hz (broadband) | Skeletal muscle activity | Falsified high-frequency morphology |
| Motion Artifact | 0.1ŌĆō10 Hz | Electrode-skin impedance changes | Distorted P and T waves |
| Instrument Noise | Wideband | Amplifier thermal noise | Low SNR for QRS detection |
| Quantization Noise | DC to Fs/2 | Limited ADC resolution (typically 12-bit) | Subtle waveform degradation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student mistake is assuming that all ECG noise can be removed with a single low-pass filter. In reality, **noise must be treated by category** ŌĆö baseline wander needs a *high-pass* (or trend subtraction), powerline needs a *narrow notch* filter, and EMG needs a *low-pass* ŌĆö and these are usually cascaded.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ECG Signal Processing Pipeline (Architectural Logic)

The canonical ECG analysis chain follows five sequential stages:

1. **Pre-acquisition Analog Conditioning:** Instrumentation amplifier (gain Ōēł 1000), isolation, anti-aliasing low-pass filter (cutoff **150ŌĆō250 Hz**).
2. **Digitization:** ADC sampling at **360ŌĆō500 Hz** (clinical) or **1000ŌĆō5000 Hz** (research); resolution **12ŌĆō16 bits**.
3. **Digital Pre-processing (Cleaning):** Removal of baseline wander, powerline interference, high-frequency EMG, and motion artifact.
4. **Feature Extraction:** Detection of P, QRS, T waves; measurement of intervals, amplitudes, areas, slopes.
5. **Classification / Decision:** Beat typing (N, V, S, F, Q from AAMI), rhythm analysis, diagnostic output.

### 2.2 Baseline Wander Removal Techniques

**Method 1 ŌĆö Linear High-Pass FIR Filter (cutoff 0.5 Hz):**

For a sampling rate $F_s = 360 \text{ Hz}$ and cutoff $f_c = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
H_{\text{HP}}(z) = \dfrac{1 - \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} z^{-k}}{1 - \alpha \, z^{-1}}
$$

where $N = \dfrac{F_s}{f_c} = 720$ samples (a 1.5-second moving average subtracted from the input).

**Method 2 ŌĆö Cubic Spline Estimation (Recommended by KTU Examiners):**

Estimate isoelectric baseline by cubic-spline interpolation of PR-TP segments, then subtract from raw signal. Mathematically:

$$
B(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} c_i \, S_i(t) \quad \text{with} \quad y_{\text{clean}}(t) = x(t) - B(t)
$$

where $S_i(t)$ are cubic B-spline basis functions and $c_i$ are interpolation coefficients obtained by least-squares fitting.

**Method 3 ŌĆö IIR High-Pass (0.5 Hz, 2nd-order Butterworth):**

$$
H(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}
$$

Coefficients (for $F_s = 360$ Hz, $f_c = 0.5$ Hz, $Q = 0.707$): $b_0 = 0.9986,\ b_1 = -1.9972,\ b_2 = 0.9986,\ a_1 = -1.9972,\ a_2 = 0.9972$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Baseline wander removal is the first critical step in any Holter monitor (24-hour ambulatory ECG) and is mandatory for accurate ST-segment analysis in ischemia detection. A 1 mm ST shift at 0.5 Hz baseline drift is clinically meaningless and can trigger false alarms in CCU monitors.

### 2.3 Powerline Interference Removal

**Method 1 ŌĆö IIR Notch Filter (50/60 Hz, Q = 30):**

$$
H_{\text{notch}}(z) = \dfrac{1 - 2\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi f_0}{F_s}\right) z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 2r\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi f_0}{F_s}\right) z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2}}
$$

where $f_0$ is the interference frequency, $r \approx 0.99$ controls notch bandwidth, and poles are placed just inside the unit circle to ensure stability while keeping a sharp notch.

**Method 2 ŌĆö Adaptive Notch (Widrow-Hoff LMS):**

$$
y(n) = x(n) - w(n)\,\sin(\omega_0 n)
$$

$$
w(n+1) = w(n) + 2\mu\,x(n)\,\sin(\omega_0 n)
$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0/F_s$ and $\mu$ is the adaptation step-size.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why adaptive?** The mains frequency drifts (49ŌĆō51 Hz in real grids). A fixed notch can leave residual interference, while an LMS adaptive notch tracks frequency drift in real time.

### 2.4 Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm (Mandatory KTU Topic)

The Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985) is a **gold-standard** real-time QRS detector with detection sensitivity > 99% on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database. It consists of six stages:

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Bandpass Filtering (5ŌĆō15 Hz):**

Cascade of:
- **Lowpass (two-pole, 11 Hz cutoff):**
$$
H_{\text{LP}}(z) = \dfrac{(1 - z^{-6})^2}{(1 - z^{-1})^2}
$$

- **Highpass (two-pole, 5 Hz cutoff):**
$$
H_{\text{HP}}(z) = \dfrac{-1 + 32z^{-16} + z^{-32}}{1 + z^{-1}}
$$

Combined: $H_{\text{BP}}(z) = H_{\text{LP}}(z) \cdot H_{\text{HP}}(z)$, isolating QRS energy while suppressing P, T, baseline, and EMG.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Derivative Operator (Five-Point):**

$$
y(nT) = \dfrac{1}{8T}\bigl[\,-x(nT - 2T) - 2x(nT - T) + 2x(nT + T) + x(nT + 2T)\,\bigr]
$$

Transfer function: $H_d(z) = \dfrac{1}{8T}\bigl(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z + z^2\bigr)$ ŌĆö emphasizes the **steep slopes** of the QRS.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Squaring:**

$$
y(nT) = \bigl[x(nT)\bigr]^2
$$

Nonlinear operation that makes all data **positive** and **emphasizes large slopes** (large derivative values) while suppressing small P/T waves.

**Stage 4 ŌĆö Moving-Window Integration (MWI, 150 ms window):**

$$
y(nT) = \dfrac{1}{N}\bigl[x(nT - (N-1)T) + x(nT - (N-2)T) + \cdots + x(nT)\bigr]
$$

For $F_s = 200$ Hz, $N = 30$ samples (150 ms). This smooths the squared derivative, producing a single peak per QRS complex and providing an envelope whose peak location corresponds to the R-wave.

**Stage 5 ŌĆö Adaptive Thresholding:**

Two thresholds ŌĆö a primary **signal-level threshold** $SPKI$ and a secondary **noise-level threshold** $NPKI$ ŌĆö are updated recursively:

$$
SPKI(n) = 0.125\,y(n) + 0.875\,SPKI(n-1) \quad \text{(if signal detected)}
$$

$$
NPKI(n) = 0.125\,y(n) + 0.875\,NPKI(n-1) \quad \text{(if signal absent)}
$$

The decision threshold is $THR = NPKI + 0.25\,(SPKI - NPKI)$.

**Stage 6 ŌĆö Search Back and T-Wave Discrimination:**

If a QRS is not detected within 1.66├Ś the current RR interval, the algorithm **searches back** at a lower threshold (THR2). A T-wave whose amplitude exceeds THR but occurs within **360 ms** of the previous QRS is rejected as a T-wave (not classified as R).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does Pan-Tompkins work so well?** The cascaded bandpass filter removes out-of-band noise; squaring is a non-linear contrast enhancer; the moving-window integrator merges multiple slopes (Q, R, S) into one peak; and adaptive thresholds track QRS-amplitude changes due to respiration, posture, and arrhythmia.

### 2.5 Wavelet-Based ECG Analysis (KTU 2024 High-Priority Topic)

Wavelets overcome a fundamental limitation of STFT ŌĆö the **fixed time-frequency resolution**. Wavelets are well-suited to ECG because the signal contains **short, high-frequency transients (QRS)** and **longer, low-frequency components (P, T waves)**.

**Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT):**

$$
C(a, b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t - b}{a}\right) dt
$$

**Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) ŌĆö Mallat's Algorithm:**

$$
A_j[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\,A_{j+1}[k], \qquad D_j[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\,A_{j+1}[k]
$$

The DWT decomposes the ECG into **approximation (A)** and **detail (D)** coefficients at multiple scales. The **QRS energy is concentrated in scales D3 (4ŌĆō8 Hz) and D4 (8ŌĆō16 Hz)** for a typical MIT-BIH record.

**Modulus Maxima Method for R-Peak Detection:**

R-peaks correspond to **modulus maxima** (zero-crossings of the smoothed derivative) of the wavelet transform. The most common mother wavelet used is the **quadratic spline** or **Daubechies-4 (db4)** wavelet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wavelet-based methods are now the **clinical standard** for high-resolution ECG (late-potential detection, T-wave alternans, QT-dispersion mapping). They are also robust to non-stationarities, which the Fourier-based Pan-Tompkins is not.

### 2.6 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) ŌĆö Frequency-Domain Analysis

HRV quantifies the beat-to-beat variation in the RR interval series. Frequency-domain analysis decomposes the RR-tachogram $r(n)$ into:

| Band | Frequency Range | Physiological Origin |
|------|----------------|----------------------|
| **VLF** (Very Low Freq.) | 0.003ŌĆō0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, hormonal |
| **LF** (Low Freq.) | 0.04ŌĆō0.15 Hz | Sympathetic + Parasympathetic (baroreflex) |
| **HF** (High Freq.) | 0.15ŌĆō0.40 Hz | Parasympathetic (respiration, RSA) |

$$
\text{LF/HF ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Power in } [0.04, 0.15] \text{ Hz}}{\text{Power in } [0.15, 0.40] \text{ Hz}}
$$

A high LF/HF ratio indicates sympathetic dominance (stress/exercise); a low ratio indicates parasympathetic dominance (rest/recovery).

**PSD Estimation Methods:**
- Welch's Periodogram (windowed, averaged FFT)
- Autoregressive (AR) modeling ŌĆö gives smoother spectra, suitable for short records.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Heart Rate | $\text{HR} = 60 / \text{RR (s)}$ | bpm; normal 60ŌĆō100 |
| 2 | Sampling Theorem | $F_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $F_s \geq 300$ Hz clinically |
| 3 | QRS Bandpass | $f_{\text{BP}} \in [5, 15]$ Hz | Pan-Tompkins |
| 4 | Derivative Filter | $y(n) = \tfrac{1}{8T}[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)]$ | 5-point |
| 5 | MWI Length | $N = 0.15 \cdot F_s$ | 150 ms window |
| 6 | Notch Bandwidth | $\text{BW} \approx f_0 / Q$ | $Q = 30$ typical |
| 7 | LF/HF Ratio | $\text{LF/HF} = P_{\text{LF}} / P_{\text{HF}}$ | Sympathovagal balance |
| 8 | CWT | $C(a,b) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^{*}(\tfrac{t-b}{a}) dt$ | $a \neq 0$ |
| 9 | SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | $P_s$ signal power |
| 10 | Beat-to-Beat HRV | $\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N-1}\sum (RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$ | ms; normal 100ŌĆō180 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for Tables:** Inside table cells, never use the pipe `|` character for absolute value or conditionals ŌĆö use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead. The same applies to the vertical bar inside math expressions to prevent markdown parsing errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pan-Tompkins 5-Point Derivative Operator

**Goal:** Derive the five-point derivative transfer function used in Pan-Tompkins Stage 2.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Taylor Series Approximation.** Expand $x(t)$ around $t = nT$:

$$
x(nT + kT) = x(nT) + kT\,x'(nT) + \dfrac{(kT)^2}{2!} x''(nT) + \dfrac{(kT)^3}{3!} x'''(nT) + O(T^4)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Build the Linear Combination.** For the five points $k = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2$ with weights $-1, -2, 0, +2, +1$:

$$
y(nT) = \tfrac{1}{8T}\bigl[-x(nT-2T) - 2x(nT-T) + 0\cdot x(nT) + 2x(nT+T) + x(nT+2T)\bigr]
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute Taylor Expansions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(nT) &= \tfrac{1}{8T}\Bigl\{-\bigl[x - 2T x' + 2T^2 x'' - \tfrac{4}{3}T^3 x'''\bigr] \\
&\quad -2\bigl[x - T x' + \tfrac{T^2}{2} x'' - \tfrac{T^3}{6} x'''\bigr] \\
&\quad +2\bigl[x + T x' + \tfrac{T^2}{2} x'' + \tfrac{T^3}{6} x'''\bigr] \\
&\quad +\bigl[x + 2T x' + 2T^2 x'' + \tfrac{4}{3}T^3 x'''\bigr] \Bigr\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $x \equiv x(nT)$, $x' \equiv x'(nT)$, $x'' \equiv x''(nT)$, $x''' \equiv x'''(nT)$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine Terms by Order:**

- Coefficient of $x$: $(-1 - 2 + 0 + 2 + 1) = 0$ Ō£ō
- Coefficient of $x'$: $(2 + 2 + 2 + 2)T = 8T$ ŌåÆ multiplied by $\tfrac{1}{8T}$ ŌåÆ **1**
- Coefficient of $x''$: $(-2 - 1 + 0 + 1 + 2)T^2 = 0$ Ō£ō
- Coefficient of $x'''$: $(\tfrac{4}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{4}{3})T^3 = \tfrac{10}{3} T^3$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Expression:**

$$
y(nT) \approx x'(nT) + \dfrac{5T^2}{12} x'''(nT) + O(T^4)
$$

This is the **first derivative of the input signal**, with the second-order term canceled. The dominant residual is the **third derivative scaled by $\tfrac{5T^2}{12}$**, which is acceptable since the QRS has high first-derivative content.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Z-Domain Transfer Function:**

$$
\boxed{\,H_d(z) = \dfrac{1}{8T}\bigl(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z + z^{2}\bigr)\,}
$$

This derivative response **peaks near 15ŌĆō20 Hz** (as can be confirmed by substituting $z = e^{j\omega T}$), aligning with QRS spectral energy. Ō£ō

### 3.2 Derivation: Heart Rate from RR Interval

**Given:** A clean ECG with detected R-peaks at sample indices $n_1, n_2, n_3, \ldots, n_M$, sampled at $F_s$ Hz.

**Step 1 ŌĆö RR Interval in Samples:**

$$
\Delta N_i = n_{i+1} - n_i \quad \text{(samples)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö RR Interval in Seconds:**

$$
\text{RR}_i = \dfrac{\Delta N_i}{F_s} \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Instantaneous Heart Rate (bpm):**

$$
\text{HR}_i = \dfrac{60}{\text{RR}_i} = \dfrac{60\,F_s}{n_{i+1} - n_i}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mean Heart Rate over $M-1$ beats:**

$$
\boxed{\,\overline{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{60\,F_s}{\overline{\Delta N}} = \dfrac{60 (M-1) F_s}{n_M - n_1}\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $F_s = 360$ Hz and $\overline{\Delta N} = 540$ samples:

$$
\overline{\text{HR}} = \dfrac{60 \times 360}{540} = 40 \text{ bpm} \quad \text{(bradycardia)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) After Filtering

**Given:** Clean ECG $s(n)$ and additive noise $v(n)$ ŌåÆ observed $x(n) = s(n) + v(n)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Variance of Clean and Noisy Signals:**

$$
\sigma_s^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} s^2(n), \qquad \sigma_x^2 = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} x^2(n)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Estimate Noise Variance (assuming noise uncorrelated with signal):**

$$
\sigma_v^2 \approx \sigma_x^2 - \sigma_s^2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Input SNR (dB):**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_s^2}{\sigma_v^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö After Filter $H(z)$ with noise power gain $G_n$:**

$$
G_n = \dfrac{1}{F_s}\int_0^{F_s/2} \vert H(e^{j2\pi f / F_s}) \vert^2\,S_v(f)\,df \bigg/ \int_0^{F_s/2} S_v(f)\,df
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_s^2}{G_n \sigma_v^2}\right) = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} - 10\log_{10} G_n
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Improvement:**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta \text{SNR} = \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} - \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = -10\log_{10} G_n\,}
$$

For a Pan-Tompkins bandpass with white noise input, $G_n \approx 0.18$ ŌåÆ $\Delta \text{SNR} \approx +7.4$ dB improvement. Ō£ō

### 3.4 Derivation: Welch's Method for HRV PSD

**Step 1 ŌĆö Segment the RR-tachogram $r(n)$ of length $N$ into $K$ overlapping segments of length $L$, with overlap ratio $\alpha$ (typically 0.5).**

**Step 2 ŌĆö Window each segment with $w(n)$ (Hann or Hamming):**

$$
r_k(n) = r\!\left(n + k(1-\alpha)L\right) \cdot w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Periodogram of each segment:**

$$
P_k(f) = \dfrac{1}{L\,U}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} r_k(n) e^{-j2\pi f n / F_s} \right\vert^2
$$

where $U = \tfrac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window energy normalization.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Welch PSD = average of all segment periodograms:**

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{S}_{rr}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{k=0}^{K-1} P_k(f)\,}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute Band Powers:**

$$
P_{\text{LF}} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} \hat{S}_{rr}(f)\,df, \qquad P_{\text{HF}} = \int_{0.15}^{0.40} \hat{S}_{rr}(f)\,df
$$

$$
\text{LF/HF} = \dfrac{P_{\text{LF}}}{P_{\text{HF}}}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation: DWT-Based QRS Detection Threshold

**Step 1 ŌĆö Choose Mother Wavelet:** Quadratic spline $\psi(t)$ ŌĆö its first derivative is a Gaussian, ideal for detecting slope changes.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute DWT at scales $2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^5$.** QRS energy is concentrated in scales $2^3$ (8 samples) and $2^4$ (16 samples) for $F_s = 360$ Hz.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Modulus Maxima:** A modulus maximum at scale $a$ and position $b$ satisfies:

$$
\vert C(a, b) \vert > \vert C(a, b-1) \vert \quad \text{and} \quad \vert C(a, b) \vert > \vert C(a, b+1) \vert
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Threshold Logic:**

$$
\text{R-peak candidate} \iff \vert C(a_j, b) \vert > T \cdot \max_{a \in \{2^3, 2^4\}} \vert C(a, b) \vert_{\text{noise baseline}}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Zero-Crossing Refinement:** The R-peak is the **zero-crossing of the CWT between two opposite-polarity modulus maxima** ŌĆö this gives sub-sample-precision peak localization (Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 ms accuracy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a quadratic spline?** Its first derivative is a Gaussian, and the **CWT of a Gaussian-shaped QRS at scale $a$ produces a peak with amplitude proportional to the QRS's slope and width**. This provides robust detection even in noisy or arrhythmic records.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Method

| Condition | Recommended Method | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Clean ECG, real-time | Pan-Tompkins | Fast, deterministic, 99%+ sensitivity |
| Noisy ECG, MIT-BIH benchmarks | Wavelet (db4 / spline) | Multi-resolution, robust to noise |
| Holter (24-h ambulatory) | Pan-Tompkins + adaptive threshold | Long-term amplitude tracking |
| HRV analysis | Lomb-Scargle periodogram | Handles unevenly sampled RR series |
| ST-segment analysis | Cubic-spline baseline + low-pass | Preserves DC offset for ST-level |
| Late potentials (SAECG) | Wavelet denoising + time averaging | Detects sub-millivolt micro-signals |
| Atrial fibrillation detection | RR-irregularity features + ML | AF has no P-waves, irregular RR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ECG Waveform Morphology and Clinical Intervals

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Baseline] --> B[P Wave 80-120 ms]
    B --> C[PR Segment isoelectric]
    C --> D[QRS Complex 80-120 ms]
    D --> E[ST Segment isoelectric]
    E --> F[T Wave 160 ms]
    F --> G[U Wave optional]
    G --> H[Baseline next cycle]
    D --> D1[Q deflection downward]
    D --> D2[R deflection upward peak]
    D --> D3[S deflection downward]
    E --> E1[ST elevation = ischemia]
    E --> E2[ST depression = ischemia]
    F --> F1[T inversion = ischemia]
    F --> F2[T peaked = hyperkalemia]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ECG Pre-processing + Pan-Tompkins Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Raw ECG x n] --> B[Stage 1 Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Stage 2 Derivative Operator 5 point]
    C --> D[Stage 3 Squaring]
    D --> E[Stage 4 Moving Window Integration 150 ms]
    E --> F[Stage 5 Adaptive Thresholding]
    F --> G{Peak above THR1}
    G -- Yes --> H[Accept as R peak]
    G -- No --> I{Search back THR2}
    I -- Peak found --> J{Within 360 ms of previous}
    J -- Yes --> K[Reject as T wave]
    J -- No --> H
    I -- No peak --> L[No detection this window]
    H --> M[Compute RR and Heart Rate]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output RR series for HRV]
    N --> O[Welch PSD and LF HF ratio]
    O --> P[Clinical Report]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw ECG] --> B[Baseline Wander Removal]
    A1[0 to 0.5 Hz noise] --> B
    B --> C[Powerline Notch 50/60 Hz]
    A2[Mains interference] --> C
    C --> D[High Frequency EMG Low Pass 40 Hz]
    A3[EMG noise 20 to 500 Hz] --> D
    D --> E[Clean ECG]
    B -.->|Method 1| B1[IIR Highpass 0.5 Hz]

    C -.->|Method 1| C1[Fixed IIR Notch]
    C -.->|Method 2| C2[Adaptive LMS Notch]
    D -.->|Method 1| D1[FIR Lowpass 40 Hz]
    D -.->|Method 2| D2[Wavelet Thresholding]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Wavelet-Based QRS Detection

```mermaid

    A[Preprocessed ECG] --> B[Discrete Wavelet Transform DWT]
    B --> C1[Detail D1 scale 2]
    B --> C2[Detail D2 scale 4]
    B --> C3[Detail D3 scale 8 QRS band]
    B --> C4[Detail D4 scale 16 QRS band]
    C1 --> D1[Modulus Maxima Search]
    C2 --> D2[Modulus Maxima Search]
    C3 --> D3[Modulus Maxima Search]
    C4 --> D4[Modulus Maxima Search]
    D1 --> E[Combine candidates across scales]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    F --> G[R peak candidates]

```

```mermaid
    C1 --> D1[Statistical HRV metrics]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2]** Define an Electrocardiogram (ECG). List the standard ECG waves with their typical durations and clinical significance.

**Model Answer:**
The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive graphical recording of the electrical activity of the heart over time, obtained by placing electrodes on the body surface. It is a quasi-periodic, non-stationary signal in the bandwidth **0.05ŌĆō150 Hz** with amplitudes of approximately **┬▒5 mV**. The standard ECG waveform consists of:

- **P wave** (80ŌĆō120 ms) ŌĆö atrial depolarization.
- **QRS complex** (80ŌĆō120 ms) ŌĆö ventricular depolarization; dominant feature.
- **T wave** (~160 ms) ŌĆö ventricular repolarization.
- **U wave** (optional) ŌĆö possibly Purkinje repolarization.
- **PR interval** (120ŌĆō200 ms) ŌĆö atrioventricular conduction time.
- **QT interval** (rate-corrected < 440 ms male) ŌĆö total ventricular activity.
- **ST segment** ŌĆö isoelectric; deviations indicate ischemia.

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2]** What is baseline wander in ECG? Name any two methods used for its removal.

**Model Answer:**
**Baseline wander** is a low-frequency artifact (0ŌĆō0.5 Hz) superimposed on the ECG, caused by respiration, body movement, and electrode-skin impedance changes. It can falsely shift the ST segment and cause incorrect R-peak detection. Two standard removal methods are:

1. **Linear high-pass FIR filtering** (cutoff ~0.5 Hz) ŌĆö fast but may distort ST segment.
2. **Cubic-spline estimation** ŌĆö interpolates isoelectric PR/TP segments and subtracts the estimated baseline. Preferred clinically because it preserves ST morphology.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students are offered an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Full-step solutions are mandatory for scoring above 80%.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Pan-Tompkins Algorithm**

**[a] [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]** Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for real-time QRS detection. With a neat block diagram, describe each of its six stages.

**Model Solution:**

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985)** is a real-time, derivative-based QRS detector with > 99% sensitivity on the MIT-BIH database. It operates in six stages:

**Stage 1 ŌĆö Bandpass Filter (5ŌĆō15 Hz):**
A cascade of a **two-pole lowpass (11 Hz)** and a **two-pole highpass (5 Hz)** that isolates QRS energy while suppressing P, T, baseline wander, and high-frequency EMG noise.

**Stage 2 ŌĆö Derivative Operator:**
Implements $y(nT) = \tfrac{1}{8T}\bigl[-x(nT-2T) - 2x(nT-T) + 2x(nT+T) + x(nT+2T)\bigr]$ ŌĆö a five-point differentiator that emphasizes the steep QRS slopes and suppresses low-frequency P/T waves.

**Stage 3 ŌĆö Squaring:**
$y(nT) = [x(nT)]^2$ ŌĆö a non-linear operation that makes all values positive and amplifies large slopes (QRS) while suppressing small ones (P, T).

**Stage 4 ŌĆö Moving-Window Integration (MWI):**
A 150 ms moving average that smooths the squared derivative and produces an envelope with one peak per QRS, allowing location of the R-wave.

**Stage 5 ŌĆö Adaptive Thresholding:**
Two thresholds $SPKI$ (signal level) and $NPKI$ (noise level) are updated exponentially: $SPKI(n) = 0.125 y(n) + 0.875 SPKI(n-1)$ if signal detected, similar for $NPKI$. The decision threshold is $THR = NPKI + 0.25 (SPKI - NPKI)$.

**Stage 6 ŌĆö Search Back and T-Wave Discrimination:**
If no QRS is detected within **1.66├Ś the current RR interval**, the algorithm **searches back** at a lower threshold (THR2). A T-wave whose peak exceeds THR but is within **360 ms** of the previous QRS is rejected (a QRS would imply HR > 167 bpm, physiologically unusual).

[Block diagram shown in Section 4.2 above.]

**Justification for cascaded design:** Each stage targets a specific noise source ŌĆö the bandpass rejects out-of-band, the derivative emphasizes QRS slopes, squaring provides nonlinear contrast enhancement, MWI merges multi-slope QRS into one peak, and adaptive thresholding handles amplitude variability (respiration, posture, arrhythmia).

**[b] [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]** A 360 Hz-sampled ECG record has R-peaks detected at sample indices 720, 1260, 1800, 2340. Calculate the instantaneous heart rates, the mean HR, and the SDNN (a time-domain HRV metric). Comment on the rhythm.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö RR Intervals in samples:** $\Delta N = 540, 540, 540$ samples.

**Step 2 ŌĆö RR Intervals in seconds:** $\text{RR} = 540/360 = 1.5$ s.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Instantaneous HRs:**

$$
\text{HR}_1 = \text{HR}_2 = \text{HR}_3 = \dfrac{60}{1.5} = 40 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mean HR:** $\overline{\text{HR}} = 40$ bpm.

**Step 5 ŌĆö SDNN:**

$$
\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0+0+0}{2}} = 0 \text{ ms}
$$

**Comment:** A heart rate of 40 bpm indicates **bradycardia** (normal 60ŌĆō100 bpm). The SDNN of 0 ms indicates **no beat-to-beat variability** ŌĆö clinically significant, suggesting a possible **regularised rhythm** (e.g., complete heart block with junctional escape, or paced rhythm). Real ECG always shows some SDNN > 20 ms even at rest; SDNN < 50 ms is a poor prognostic indicator in post-MI patients.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Wavelet & HRV Analysis**

**[a] [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]** With the necessary mathematical expressions, explain the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) for ECG analysis. Discuss how modulus maxima are used for R-peak detection.

**Model Solution:**

**CWT Definition:**

$$
C(a, b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\dfrac{t - b}{a}\right) dt
$$

where $x(t)$ is the ECG signal, $\psi(t)$ is the **mother wavelet** (typically a quadratic spline for ECG), $a$ is the **scale** (inverse of frequency), $b$ is the **translation** (time location), and $*$ denotes complex conjugate. The normalization $1/\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$ keeps the energy constant across scales.

**Why Wavelets for ECG?** The ECG has **transient high-frequency components (QRS)** and **sustained low-frequency components (P, T, baseline)**. The CWT adapts its window length to frequency ŌĆö short window at high frequency, long window at low frequency ŌĆö providing optimal time-frequency resolution.

**Modulus Maxima for R-Peak Detection:** A point $(a, b)$ is a **modulus maximum** if:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \vert C(a, b) \vert}{\partial b} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \vert C(a, b) \vert > \vert C(a, b \pm \delta) \vert
$$

**Procedure:**
1. Apply CWT at scales $2^3$ and $2^4$ (the QRS energy bands for $F_s = 360$ Hz).
2. Find modulus maxima across these scales.
3. R-peaks correspond to **pairs of opposite-sign modulus maxima separated by a zero-crossing** ŌĆö the zero-crossing gives the **R-peak location with sub-sample precision** (Ōēł 1 ms).
4. Apply an adaptive threshold $T$ to reject noise-induced maxima.

**Advantages over Pan-Tompkins:** Wavelet methods are **robust to non-stationarities** (sudden amplitude changes in PVCs), handle **non-white noise** better, and provide **multi-scale features** useful for P and T wave detection as well.

**[b] [7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]** The following RR intervals (in seconds) were recorded: 0.80, 0.82, 0.78, 0.85, 0.79, 0.83. Calculate (i) Mean RR, (ii) SDNN, (iii) RMSSD, (iv) pNN50. Comment on the autonomic balance.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mean RR:**

$$
\overline{\text{RR}} = \dfrac{0.80 + 0.82 + 0.78 + 0.85 + 0.79 + 0.83}{6} = \dfrac{4.87}{6} = 0.812 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) SDNN:**

$$
\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{6}(RR_i - 0.812)^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0050}{5}} = \sqrt{0.0010} \approx 0.0244 \text{ s} = 24.4 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) RMSSD** (root mean square of successive differences):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSSD} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{(0.02)^2 + (0.04)^2 + (0.07)^2 + (0.06)^2 + (0.04)^2}{5}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0004 + 0.0016 + 0.0049 + 0.0036 + 0.0016}{5}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0121}{5}} = \sqrt{0.00242} \approx 0.0492 \text{ s} = 49.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) pNN50** (percentage of successive differences > 50 ms):

$$
\text{pNN50} = \dfrac{4}{5}\times 100\% = 80\%
$$

(Four of the five successive differences ŌĆö 20, 40, 70, 60, 40 ms ŌĆö actually only the **70 ms and 60 ms** exceed 50 ms, so $\text{pNN50} = \dfrac{2}{5}\times 100\% = 40\%$.)

**Clinical Comment:** Mean RR Ōēł 0.812 s ŌåÆ **HR Ōēł 74 bpm** (normal sinus). The **SDNN = 24.4 ms** is on the low side (normal 100ŌĆō180 ms for 24-h Holter; for short-term 30 s records, 30ŌĆō60 ms is typical). The **high RMSSD = 49.2 ms** and **pNN50 = 40%** indicate strong **parasympathetic (vagal) dominance**, consistent with a resting, healthy young adult. The autonomic balance is **parasympathetically dominant** ŌĆö typical of a relaxed state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö ECG Analysis Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the `2T` factor in derivative:** Many students write the five-point derivative as $y(n) = \tfrac{1}{8}[-x(n-2) - 2x(n-1) + 2x(n+1) + x(n+2)]$ **without the $T$** in the denominator. The correct form is $\tfrac{1}{8T}$ to normalize amplitude to a derivative (units of mV/s).
> 2. **Confusing Pan-Tompkins bandpass with simple low-pass:** The 5ŌĆō15 Hz filter is **bandpass**, not low-pass. Students who write "low-pass 11 Hz" lose 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up HRV time- and frequency-domain metrics:** SDNN/RMSSD/pNN50 are time-domain; LF/HF is frequency-domain. Confusing them costs full marks.
> 4. **Incorrect Welch formula:** Forgetting the window energy $U = \tfrac{1}{L}\sum w^2(n)$ in the periodogram normalization leads to incorrect band-power estimates.
> 5. **Stating QT interval as fixed:** It is **rate-corrected (QTc = QT/ŌłÜRR)**. Writing "QT < 440 ms" without correction is an incomplete answer.
> 6. **DWT vs CWT confusion:** DWT uses dyadic scales $2^j$ and Mallat's algorithm; CWT uses continuous scales. Examiners specifically test the Mallat pyramid.
> 7. **No units in numerical answers:** Always write "bpm", "ms", "Hz" ŌĆö bare numbers lose marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 ŌĆö ECG Analysis):**

- **ECG Standard:** Bandwidth 0.05ŌĆō150 Hz; sampling 360ŌĆō500 Hz; resolution 12-bit; leads I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1ŌĆōV6.
- **Wave Parameters:** P (80ŌĆō120 ms, < 0.25 mV); QRS (80ŌĆō120 ms, 0.5ŌĆō2.0 mV); T (~160 ms, < 0.5 mV); PR (120ŌĆō200 ms); QTc (< 440 ms male); ST (isoelectric).
- **Heart Rate Formula:** $\text{HR} = 60 / \text{RR (s)}$; normal 60ŌĆō100 bpm; bradycardia < 60; tachycardia > 100.
- **Noise Categories:** Baseline wander (0ŌĆō0.5 Hz) ŌåÆ high-pass; powerline (50/60 Hz) ŌåÆ notch; EMG (20ŌĆō500 Hz) ŌåÆ low-pass; motion (0.1ŌĆō10 Hz) ŌåÆ adaptive.
- **Pan-Tompkins Stages:** Bandpass (5ŌĆō15 Hz) ŌåÆ Derivative (5-point) ŌåÆ Squaring ŌåÆ MWI (150 ms) ŌåÆ Adaptive Thresholding ŌåÆ Search-back + T-wave rejection.
- **Derivative Filter:** $H_d(z) = \tfrac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z + z^2)$; approximates $\tfrac{dx}{dt}$.
- **Adaptive Threshold Recursion:** $\text{SPKI}(n) = 0.125\,y(n) + 0.875\,\text{SPKI}(n-1)$.
- **T-wave Rejection Rule:** Any peak within 360 ms of previous QRS is classified as T-wave (HR > 167 bpm not physiological).
- **CWT Formula:** $C(a,b) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\int x(t)\psi^{*}(\tfrac{t-b}{a})dt$; preferred mother wavelets: **quadratic spline** or **db4**.
- **DWT Energy Localization:** QRS energy concentrated in **D3 (4ŌĆō8 Hz)** and **D4 (8ŌĆō16 Hz)** for $F_s = 360$ Hz.
- **Modulus Maxima Method:** R-peak = zero-crossing between opposite-sign modulus maxima at scales $2^3, 2^4$.
- **HRV Frequency Bands:** VLF (0.003ŌĆō0.04 Hz), LF (0.04ŌĆō0.15 Hz ŌĆö sympathetic+parasympathetic), HF (0.15ŌĆō0.40 Hz ŌĆö parasympathetic / RSA).
- **LF/HF Ratio:** > 2 = sympathetic dominance; < 1 = parasympathetic dominance.
- **Welch PSD:** Windowed, overlapped, averaged periodogram; overlap 50%; window Hann/Hamming; $L = 256$ typical.
- **Time-Domain HRV:** SDNN (overall variability), RMSSD (short-term / vagal), pNN50 (% successive differences > 50 ms).
- **Clinical Normal Ranges:** SDNN 100ŌĆō180 ms (24-h); RMSSD 30ŌĆō80 ms (resting); pNN50 5ŌĆō25% normal; QTc Bazett.
- **Adaptive Notch LMS Update:** $w(n+1) = w(n) + 2\mu\,x(n)\sin(\omega_0 n)$.
- **SNR Improvement:** Pan-Tompkins bandpass gives Ōēł 7.4 dB improvement for white noise input.
- **Detection Sensitivity:** Pan-Tompkins reports > 99% sensitivity and > 99.5% positive predictivity on MIT-BIH database.
- **Application Areas:** Holter monitoring, ICU/CCU patient monitoring, ambulatory ECG, exercise stress testing, sleep studies (polysomnography), HRV research, atrial fibrillation detection, ischemic ST monitoring.
- **Most-Tested Topics in KTU:** Pan-Tompkins (every year), HRV PSD estimation (frequent), wavelet denoising (recent trend), numerical heart rate / SDNN calculation (every year).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Spectral Variability of Heart Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Spectral Variability of Heart Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is formally defined as the physiological phenomenon of variation in the time interval between consecutive heartbeats, measured as the variation in the **R-R intervals** (the time between successive R-peaks of the QRS complex in the electrocardiogram). When this variability is decomposed into its constituent frequency components, the procedure is termed the **Spectral Analysis of HRV** (also called Frequency-Domain Analysis of HRV).

The mathematical foundation of spectral HRV analysis rests upon estimating the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of the derived HRV time series, denoted as $P_x(f)$, which represents the distribution of signal power as a function of frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Spectral analysis of HRV quantifies autonomic nervous system (ANS) modulation of the heart by decomposing beat-to-beat interval variations into distinct physiological frequency bands (VLF, LF, HF), each linked to specific regulatory mechanisms (thermoregulation, baroreflex, respiration).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **skilled orchestra conductor** leading a symphony. The musicians (heart pacemaker cells) do not play at a rigidly mechanical metronomic pace; instead, the conductor (the autonomic nervous system) continuously fine-tunes their tempo based on the emotional dynamics of the piece (physical activity, stress, sleep, digestion). Two musicians playing the same note at slightly different moments produce a richer, more expressive performance than a robotic synthesizer locked to a single tempo.

**Spectral analysis of HRV** is akin to placing a **musical prism** in front of this orchestra. The prism splits the seemingly chaotic variation in tempo into a rainbow of frequencies:
- **Slow, deep oscillations** (VLF band): Represent long-term regulatory processes (like thermoregulation, hormonal cycles).
- **Medium oscillations** (LF band): Represent blood pressure regulation and baroreflex activity.
- **Fast, breath-paced oscillations** (HF band): Represent the natural acceleration and deceleration of the heart with each breath (respiratory sinus arrhythmia).

By quantifying the energy in each color band, physicians can diagnose whether the conductor is functioning properly, is over-stressed (sympathetic dominance), or is too relaxed (parasympathetic dominance).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physiological Constants

The following standardized metrics form the cornerstone of spectral HRV analysis (as per **Task Force of the European Society of Cardiology and the North American Society of Pacing and Electrophysiology, 1996**):

| Metric | Definition | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Power (TP)** | Variance of all NN intervals | $\text{ms}^2$ |
| **VLF Power** | Power in Very Low Frequency band | $\text{ms}^2$ |
| **LF Power** | Power in Low Frequency band | $\text{ms}^2$ |
| **HF Power** | Power in High Frequency band | $\text{ms}^2$ |
| **LF Norm** | LF / (TP - VLF) ├Ś 100 | **n.u. (normalized units)** |
| **HF Norm** | HF / (TP - VLF) ├Ś 100 | **n.u. (normalized units)** |
| **LF/HF Ratio** | Sympathovagal Balance Index | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Clarification:** "NN intervals" refer to **Normal-to-Normal** R-R intervals (sinus-origin beats), excluding ectopic or artifact-corrupted beats. The unit **$\text{ms}^2$** (milliseconds squared) represents signal power in the time-interval domain (analogous to $V^2/Hz$ in voltage PSDs).

### 1.4 Why Spectral (Frequency-Domain) Analysis is Indispensable

Time-domain HRV metrics (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50) provide only a single number summarizing overall variability. They cannot reveal **which physiological mechanism** is contributing to the variability. Spectral analysis provides this crucial mechanistic resolution:

- It separates **sympathetic** from **parasympathetic** influences quantitatively.
- It enables short-term (2ŌĆō5 minute) and ultra-short-term (less than 1 minute) assessment, which is impractical for time-domain methods like SDNN.
- It detects subtle autonomic dysregulation in diseases like **diabetic neuropathy, myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure**, and **hypertension**.
