## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Computational Families

Spectral estimation techniques applied to HRV fall into two fundamental families:

**A. Non-Parametric (Classical) Methods**
- Based directly on the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**.
- No prior model assumption about the data-generating process.
- Lower computational complexity; suitable for real-time implementations.
- **Subordinate methods:** Periodogram, Welch's Modified Periodogram, Blackman-Tukey Correlogram.

**B. Parametric (Model-Based) Methods**
- Assume the HRV signal is generated by a known **linear stochastic model** (e.g., AR, ARMA).
- Produce smoother, higher-resolution spectra, especially for short data records.
- **Subordinate methods:** Autoregressive (AR) modeling using the Yule-Walker / Burg algorithms.

### 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown

#### Step 1: ECG Pre-processing and R-peak Detection
- The raw ECG signal is band-pass filtered (typically **0.5ŌĆō40 Hz**) to remove baseline wander, powerline interference, and high-frequency noise.
- The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (or its modern variants) is applied to detect R-peaks with sub-sample precision.
- The instantaneous HRV time series is constructed as $\{RR_i\}$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$.

#### Step 2: Interpolation and Resampling
- Because RR intervals are unevenly sampled (the heart does not beat on a fixed grid), they must be **evenly resampled** (typically at **4 Hz**, though 1ŌĆō2 Hz is also used) using **cubic spline interpolation** or **linear interpolation**.
- This produces an evenly sampled signal $x(n)$ of length $N$ suitable for FFT-based spectral analysis.

#### Step 3: Mean Removal and Windowing
- The mean (DC component) is subtracted: $x'(n) = x(n) - \bar{x}$.
- A window function (Hann, Hamming, or Blackman) is applied to reduce spectral leakage.

#### Step 4: PSD Estimation
- For non-parametric: Compute the **periodogram** or **Welch's averaged periodogram**.
- For parametric: Fit an **AR model** of appropriate order and compute its frequency response.

#### Step 5: Band Power Integration
- Integrate the estimated PSD over the defined frequency bands (VLF, LF, HF) to obtain band powers.
- The integral is computed numerically using the **trapezoidal rule** or **Simpson's rule**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The following formulas are high-yield for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize the symbols, units, and the operational meaning of each.

| Symbol / Formula | Description | Domain of Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x(n) = x(nT_s)$ | Uniformly resampled HRV signal | $T_s = 0.25 \text{ s}$ (4 Hz) |
| $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 4 \text{ Hz}$ standard |
| $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | **Periodogram** (Bartlett) | $N \geq 256$ samples |
| $P_W(f) = \dfrac{1}{M \cdot L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert X_k(f) \vert^2$ | **Welch's Method** | Overlap 50% typical |
| $P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{\vert 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i e^{-j2\pi f i} \vert^2}$ | **AR(p) PSD** | Model order $p$ = 8 to 20 |
| $R(k) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x(n) x(n+k)$ | Biased autocorrelation estimate | $k = 0, 1, \dots, p$ |
| $LF_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{LF}{TP - VLF} \times 100$ | Normalized LF power | Expressed in **n.u.** |
| $HF_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{HF}{TP - VLF} \times 100$ | Normalized HF power | Expressed in **n.u.** |
| $R_{LF/HF} = \dfrac{LF_{\text{power}}}{HF_{\text{power}}}$ | Sympathovagal balance index | Dimensionless |
| $f_{\text{VLF}} \in [0.003, 0.04] \text{ Hz}$ | Very Low Frequency band | Long-term regulation |
| $f_{\text{LF}} \in [0.04, 0.15] \text{ Hz}$ | Low Frequency band | Baroreflex / Mixed |
| $f_{\text{HF}} \in [0.15, 0.4] \text{ Hz}$ | High Frequency band | Parasympathetic (RSA) |

### 2.4 Detailed Theoretical Notes on Each Method

#### A. The Periodogram

The periodogram is the most elementary non-parametric PSD estimator. For a zero-mean signal $x(n)$ of length $N$:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The periodogram is then:

$$P_{\text{per}}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(k) \vert^2$$

**Drawbacks:** The periodogram is **biased and inconsistent** ŌĆö its variance does not decrease with increasing $N$. It is also a noisy estimator with high variance. Rectangular windowing (implicit in the FFT) causes severe spectral leakage.

#### B. Welch's Modified Periodogram

Welch's method addresses the variance problem by **segmenting the data** into overlapping windows, computing a periodogram for each segment, and averaging:

1. Divide $x(n)$ into $K$ overlapping segments $x_i(n)$ of length $L$, with overlap ratio $\alpha \in [0, 0.75]$.
2. Apply a window $w(n)$ (typically Hann) to each segment.
3. Compute the FFT and periodogram of each windowed segment.
4. Average all $K$ periodograms.

The trade-off is **reduced frequency resolution** (proportional to $L$) in exchange for **reduced variance** (proportional to $1/K$). For HRV analysis, a segment length of **256 samples (64 seconds at 4 Hz)** with **50% overlap** is a common choice.

#### C. Autoregressive (AR) Modeling

In the **AR(p) model**, the current sample is expressed as a linear combination of its past $p$ samples plus white noise:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i x(n-i) + w(n)$$

where $a_i$ are the AR coefficients and $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ is white noise.

**Parameter Estimation:** The AR coefficients are obtained by solving the **Yule-Walker equations**:

$$\begin{bmatrix} R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(p-1) \\ R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ R(p-1) & R(p-2) & \cdots & R(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} R(1) \\ R(2) \\ \vdots \\ R(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Model Order Selection:** For HRV, the optimal AR order is typically determined by the **Akaike Information Criterion (AIC)**:

$$\text{AIC}(p) = N \ln(\sigma_p^2) + 2p$$

The selected $p$ minimizes $\text{AIC}(p)$. For HRV, this typically yields $p \in [8, 20]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why AR for HRV?** AR models are preferred for HRV because: (i) HRV exhibits sharp, well-defined spectral peaks (especially the HF peak) that AR models resolve better than FFT; (ii) AR provides a smooth, continuous spectrum; (iii) The center frequencies and powers of the spectral components can be extracted directly from the AR model poles.

### 2.5 Physiological Origin of the Frequency Bands

| Band | Frequency Range | Period | Physiological Correlate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **VLF** | 0.003 ŌĆō 0.04 Hz | 25 s ŌĆō 5 min | Thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin system, peripheral vasomotor activity |
| **LF** | 0.04 ŌĆō 0.15 Hz | 7 ŌĆō 25 s | Baroreflex control, **mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic**; predominant sympathetic modulation |
| **HF** | 0.15 ŌĆō 0.4 Hz | 2.5 ŌĆō 7 s | **Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA)**, exclusively parasympathetic (vagal) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** The LF band is often erroneously described as "purely sympathetic." Modern research (Task Force 1996; Goldstein et al. 2011) confirms that LF reflects **both** sympathetic and parasympathetic activity, with the baroreflex as the primary mediator. HF is the only band that can be unambiguously attributed to **parasympathetic (vagal) tone**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Clinical Cardiology:** Risk stratification post-myocardial infarction. Reduced HRV (especially LF and HF) is an independent predictor of mortality.
2. **Diabetic Autonomic Neuropathy:** Earliest detectable sign is loss of HF power, before symptoms manifest.
3. **Sports Medicine & Fitness:** Elite athletes exhibit higher HF power (better vagal tone) and greater total power.
4. **Mental Health & Stress Research:** Chronic stress shifts the LF/HF ratio upward, indicating sympathetic dominance.
5. **Wearable Technology:** Modern smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit, Garmin) implement Welch's method on 1-minute to 24-hour RR recordings to provide HRV metrics for wellness applications.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Periodogram as a Biased PSD Estimator

**Goal:** Show that the periodogram is the Fourier transform of the biased autocorrelation estimate.

**Step 1: Define the Biased Autocorrelation Estimate.**

For a finite-length signal $x(n)$ of length $N$, the biased autocorrelation estimate is:

$$r_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x(n) x(n+k), \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The "biased" qualifier indicates that the normalization factor is fixed at $1/N$ rather than $1/(N-k)$, making this estimator biased (the mean of $r_{xx}(k)$ does not equal the true $R_{xx}(k)$) but ensures the autocorrelation matrix is positive semi-definite.

**Step 2: Apply the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT).**

By the **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem**, the PSD is the DTFT of the true autocorrelation function. Applying this principle to the estimated autocorrelation:

$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} r_{xx}(k) e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the autocorrelation expression.**

$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x(n) x(n+k) \right] e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

**Step 4: Use the convolution-like manipulation.**

For $k \geq 0$, the summation is over $n$ from $0$ to $N-1-k$. The symmetry property of autocorrelation $r_{xx}(-k) = r_{xx}(k)$ allows us to handle the negative-$k$ terms identically. Substituting and rearranging:

$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^2$$

**Step 5: Recognize this as the magnitude-squared of the DTFT.**

Define $X(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}$, which is the DTFT of the finite-length signal (effectively a windowed version of the true signal). The periodogram is therefore:

$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$$

This is the **fundamental periodogram formula**. In practice, it is evaluated at discrete frequencies $f_k = k \cdot f_s / N$ using the FFT, yielding $P_{\text{per}}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(k) \vert^2$ where $X(k)$ is the $N$-point FFT of $x(n)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: AR Model PSD from the Yule-Walker Equations

**Goal:** Derive the AR(p) PSD formula from the model definition.

**Step 1: Start with the AR(p) difference equation.**

$$x(n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i x(n-i) + w(n)$$

where $w(n)$ is white noise with variance $\sigma_w^2$.

**Step 2: Take the Z-transform of both sides.**

$$X(z) = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i} X(z) + W(z)$$

$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i} \right] = W(z)$$

**Step 3: Solve for the transfer function $H(z) = X(z)/W(z)$.**

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i}}$$

**Step 4: Apply the PSD relationship for LTI systems driven by white noise.**

If $w(n)$ is white noise with PSD $P_w(f) = \sigma_w^2$, then the PSD of the output $x(n)$ is:

$$P_x(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2$$

**Step 5: Substitute $H(f)$ by evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j2\pi f}$.**

$$H(f) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i e^{-j2\pi f i}}$$

**Step 6: Final AR PSD expression.**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i e^{-j2\pi f i} \right\vert^2}}$$

This formula reveals the **physical meaning** of AR modeling: the spectrum is decomposed into a sum of **resonant peaks** determined by the poles of $H(z)$, which correspond to the dominant oscillatory components in the HRV signal.

### 3.3 Derivation: Yule-Walker Equations for AR Coefficient Estimation

**Goal:** Derive the linear system of equations that yields the AR coefficients.

**Step 1: Multiply the AR model by $x(n-k)$ and take the expectation.**

$$E[x(n) x(n-k)] = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i E[x(n-i) x(n-k)] + E[w(n) x(n-k)]$$

**Step 2: Recognize the autocorrelation terms.**

Since $w(n)$ is white noise uncorrelated with past samples $x(n-i)$ for $i \geq 1$:

$$E[w(n) x(n-k)] = \begin{cases} \sigma_w^2 & k = 0 \\ 0 & k \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

The remaining expectations are the autocorrelations $R(k-i) = E[x(n) x(n-k+i)]$:

$$R(k) = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i R(k-i) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(k)$$

**Step 3: Write the system in matrix form for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$.**

For $k \geq 1$, the $\sigma_w^2 \delta(k)$ term vanishes:

$$\begin{bmatrix} R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(p-1) \\ R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ R(p-1) & R(p-2) & \cdots & R(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} R(1) \\ R(2) \\ \vdots \\ R(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Solve the linear system using the Levinson-Durbin recursion (O($p^2$) complexity).**

This recursion exploits the **Toeplitz structure** of the autocorrelation matrix, enabling efficient computation without explicit matrix inversion:

For $m = 1$:
$$k_1 = -\frac{R(1)}{R(0)}, \quad a_1^{(1)} = k_1$$

For $m = 2, 3, \dots, p$:
$$k_m = -\frac{R(m) + \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)} R(m-i)}{R(0) + \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)} R(i)}$$

$$a_m^{(m)} = k_m$$

$$a_i^{(m)} = a_i^{(m-1)} + k_m a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}, \quad i = 1, \dots, m-1$$

The final AR coefficients are $a_i = a_i^{(p)}$, and the noise variance is updated as:

$$\sigma_m^2 = \sigma_{m-1}^2 (1 - k_m^2)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Levinson-Durbin Matters for HRV:** The reflection coefficients $k_m$ (also called PARCOR coefficients) are bounded by $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for a stable AR model. If any $\vert k_m \vert \geq 1$, the data is insufficient or the model is overfit, prompting a re-evaluation of the model order or data length.

### 3.4 Derivation: Welch's Method Variance Reduction

**Goal:** Quantify the variance reduction achieved by Welch's method.

**Step 1: Variance of a Single Periodogram.**

For a zero-mean Gaussian signal, the periodogram $P_{\text{per}}(f)$ at a non-zero frequency follows a chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom:

$$\text{Var}[P_{\text{per}}(f)] = P_x^2(f)$$

The relative variance is **100%** ŌĆö independent of $N$. This is the fundamental statistical weakness of the periodogram.

**Step 2: Welch's Averaging.**

Welch's method averages $K$ independent (or quasi-independent for 50% overlap) periodograms:

$$P_W(f) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} P_k(f)$$

**Step 3: Variance of the Welch Estimator.**

If the segments are uncorrelated:

$$\text{Var}[P_W(f)] = \frac{1}{K} \text{Var}[P_{\text{per}}(f)] = \frac{P_x^2(f)}{K}$$

The variance is reduced by a factor of $K$. For 50% overlap, segments are correlated, and the effective reduction is approximately:

$$K_{\text{eff}} \approx K \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \rho}$$

where $\rho$ is the correlation between adjacent overlapping segments (typically $\rho \approx 0.5$ for Hann windows with 50% overlap, yielding $K_{\text{eff}} \approx 0.67 K$).

**Step 4: Resolution vs. Variance Trade-off.**

The frequency resolution of Welch's method is $\Delta f = f_s / L$, where $L$ is the segment length. Increasing $K$ (more averaging) reduces variance but also reduces $L$ (if total data length is fixed), thereby reducing resolution. The optimal trade-off depends on the application:
- **HRV Analysis:** $L = 256$ samples (64 s at 4 Hz), $K$ chosen to cover the recording length.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Computing Band Powers

**Problem:** A 5-minute HRV recording is resampled at $f_s = 4 \text{ Hz}$, yielding $N = 1200$ samples. Welch's method with segment length $L = 256$ and 50% overlap produces the following integrated band powers:

- $P_{\text{VLF}} = 800 \text{ ms}^2$
- $P_{\text{LF}} = 1200 \text{ ms}^2$
- $P_{\text{HF}} = 600 \text{ ms}^2$

**Compute:** (a) Total Power (TP), (b) LF in normalized units, (c) HF in normalized units, (d) LF/HF ratio.

**Step 1: Total Power**

$$\text{TP} = P_{\text{VLF}} + P_{\text{LF}} + P_{\text{HF}} = 800 + 1200 + 600 = 2600 \text{ ms}^2$$

**Step 2: Power excluding VLF (used as denominator for normalization)**

$$TP - VLF = 2600 - 800 = 1800 \text{ ms}^2$$

**Step 3: Normalized LF**

$$LF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{P_{\text{LF}}}{TP - VLF} \times 100 = \frac{1200}{1800} \times 100 = 66.67 \text{ n.u.}$$

**Step 4: Normalized HF**

$$HF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{P_{\text{HF}}}{TP - VLF} \times 100 = \frac{600}{1800} \times 100 = 33.33 \text{ n.u.}$$

**Step 5: LF/HF Ratio**

$$R_{LF/HF} = \frac{P_{\text{LF}}}{P_{\text{HF}}} = \frac{1200}{600} = 2.0$$

**Step 6: Physiological Interpretation**

- $LF_{\text{norm}} = 66.67 \text{ n.u.}$ and $HF_{\text{norm}} = 33.33 \text{ n.u.}$ indicate **mixed autonomic activity with sympathetic predominance**.
- $LF/HF = 2.0$ is **within normal range** (typical resting values: 1.0 ŌĆō 4.0).
- The TP of 2600 ms┬▓ is **moderate**, indicating reasonably healthy overall HRV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $LF_{\text{norm}} + HF_{\text{norm}} = 66.67 + 33.33 = 100 \text{ n.u.}$ Ō£ō (This must always hold by construction of the normalization formula.)

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Spectral Estimation Methods

| Criterion | Periodogram | Welch's Method | AR Modeling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Spectral Resolution** | High ($f_s/N$) | Moderate ($f_s/L$) | Very High (model-based) |
| **Variance** | High (100%) | Low ($\sim 1/K$) | Very Low (smooth) |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(N \log L)$ | $O(Np + p^2)$ |
| **Bias** | Moderate | Slightly higher | Low |
| **Peak Detection** | Difficult | Moderate | Excellent (pole-based) |
| **Data Length Required** | Long ($N \geq 1024$) | Moderate | Short ($N \geq 256$) |
| **Suitability for HRV** | Poor | **Good (Standard)** | **Excellent (Gold Standard)** |
| **Decomposition into Bands** | Manual | Manual | **Automatic (pole clustering)** |

```mermaid
        F0[0 Hz]
        F1[0.04 Hz]
        F2[0.15 Hz]
        F3[0.4 Hz]
    end

    subgraph YAxis[Power Density ms squared per Hz]
        P0[Low Power VLF Plateau]
        P1[Rising LF Peak Around 0.1 Hz]
        P2[Sharp HF Peak Around 0.25 Hz]
        P3[Decay Beyond 0.4 Hz]
    end

    F0 --> P0
    F1 --> P1
    F2 --> P2
    F3 --> P3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1: Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List the three primary frequency bands used in its spectral analysis along with their frequency ranges and physiological origins.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is defined as the variation in the time interval between successive heartbeats, specifically measured from the R-peaks of normal sinus-origin QRS complexes in the ECG. It quantifies the beat-to-beat regulation imposed by the autonomic nervous system on the sinoatrial node.

The three primary frequency bands used in spectral HRV analysis are:

| Band | Frequency Range | Physiological Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **VLF** (Very Low Frequency) | 0.003 ŌĆō 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, renin-angiotensin system, peripheral vasomotor activity |
| **LF** (Low Frequency) | 0.04 ŌĆō 0.15 Hz | Baroreflex control; mixed sympathetic and parasympathetic modulation |
| **HF** (High Frequency) | 0.15 ŌĆō 0.4 Hz | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA); purely parasympathetic (vagal) |

**Keywords for Full Marks:** Definition of HRV, three bands with numerical frequency limits, mention of at least one physiological correlate per band.

---

#### Question A2: What is the significance of the LF/HF ratio in HRV spectral analysis? Under what physiological conditions is it elevated?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **LF/HF ratio** is a quantitative index of **sympathovagal balance**, representing the relative dominance of sympathetic nervous system activity (reflected in LF power) over parasympathetic activity (reflected in HF power). It is a **dimensionless quantity** computed as $R_{LF/HF} = P_{LF} / P_{HF}$.

**Physiological Interpretation:**
- **Normal resting state:** $R_{LF/HF} \approx 1.0$ to $4.0$ (sympathovagal equilibrium).
- **Elevated $R_{LF/HF}$ (> 4.0):** Sympathetic dominance, observed during physical exercise, mental stress, orthostatic challenge, and in patients with hypertension, anxiety disorders, or chronic heart failure.
- **Reduced $R_{LF/HF}$ (< 1.0):** Parasympathetic dominance, observed during deep relaxation, sleep (especially non-REM stages), meditation, and in trained athletes at rest.

**Keywords for Full Marks:** Definition as sympathovagal balance, dimensionless nature, elevated conditions (stress, exercise, hypertension), reduced conditions (relaxation, sleep, athletes).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2 | Choice A]`**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the **Autoregressive (AR) Power Spectral Density** from the AR(p) model definition. Explain the role of the Yule-Walker equations in estimating the AR coefficients and state the Yule-Walker equations in matrix form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **non-parametric (Welch)** and **parametric (AR)** spectral estimation methods for HRV analysis. Justify which method is more suitable for short-duration HRV recordings and explain why. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of AR PSD:**

The **AR(p) model** expresses the current sample $x(n)$ as a linear combination of $p$ past samples plus white noise $w(n)$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i x(n-i) + w(n)$$

where $a_i$ are the AR coefficients and $w(n) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is zero-mean white noise.

**Step 1:** Taking the Z-transform of both sides:

$$X(z) + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i} X(z) = W(z)$$

$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i} \right] = W(z)$$

**Step 2:** Solving for the transfer function $H(z) = X(z)/W(z)$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i}}$$

**Step 3:** For a linear time-invariant system driven by white noise with PSD $\sigma_w^2$, the output PSD is:

$$P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \vert H(f) \vert^2$$

**Step 4:** Evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j2\pi f}$:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i e^{-j2\pi f i} \right\vert^2}}$$

**Yule-Walker Equations in Matrix Form:**

Multiplying the AR model by $x(n-k)$ and taking expectations, using the whiteness of $w(n)$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(p-1) \\ R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ R(p-1) & R(p-2) & \cdots & R(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} R(1) \\ R(2) \\ \vdots \\ R(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

This **Toeplitz system** can be solved efficiently using the Levinson-Durbin recursion in $O(p^2)$ operations.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Comparative Analysis:**

| Aspect | Welch's Method (Non-Parametric) | AR Modeling (Parametric) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Theoretical Basis** | Direct FFT of windowed segments | Assumes an underlying linear stochastic model |
| **Data Length** | Requires longer records (N Ōēź 1024 for good resolution) | Works well with short records (N Ōēź 256) |
| **Spectral Resolution** | Limited by segment length $L$ | Model-based, can resolve closely spaced peaks |
| **Spectral Smoothing** | Achieved by averaging; may smear narrow peaks | Inherently smooth due to parametric form |
| **Peak Detection** | Requires peak-finding algorithms | Direct from model poles |
| **Computational Load** | $O(N \log L)$ per Welch segment | $O(Np + p^2)$ using Levinson-Durbin |
| **Model Order Selection** | Not required | Required (AIC, MDL, FPE, F-test) |

**Justification for Short-Duration HRV Recordings:**

AR modeling is **more suitable** for short-duration HRV recordings for the following reasons:

1. **Resolution:** AR models do not depend on the segment length for resolution; the spectral resolution is determined by the model order. Thus, a 256-sample record can yield a spectrum with resolution equivalent to a 4096-sample FFT.

2. **Smoothness:** AR spectra are inherently smooth and free from the noisy fluctuations of FFT-based periodograms, enabling reliable band power estimation from short data.

3. **Statistical Efficiency:** Parametric models extract the maximum statistical information from limited data, providing lower-variance estimates.

4. **Peak Detection:** The poles of the AR model directly identify the center frequencies of dominant oscillatory components, which is critical for HF peak detection (respiratory sinus arrhythmia).

5. **Clinical Practice:** Modern HRV analysis software (e.g., Kubios, HRV Analysis) defaults to AR modeling for recordings shorter than 5 minutes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 | Choice B]`**

**(a)** Explain the complete procedure for performing **spectral analysis of HRV** from raw ECG data. Include the pre-processing steps, resampling, and selection of an appropriate spectral estimation method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the following indices derived from the HRV power spectrum and state their clinical significance: **(i)** Total Power, **(ii)** LF in normalized units, **(iii)** HF in normalized units, **(iv)** LF/HF ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Complete Procedure for Spectral HRV Analysis:**

**Stage 1: ECG Acquisition and Pre-processing**
- Acquire ECG at sampling rate $f_s \geq 500$ Hz (clinical standard).
- Apply a **bandpass filter** in the range **0.5 ŌĆō 40 Hz** to remove baseline wander (high-pass) and high-frequency noise (low-pass).
- Apply a **50/60 Hz notch filter** to suppress powerline interference.

**Stage 2: R-peak Detection**
- Apply the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** (bandpass ŌåÆ derivative ŌåÆ squaring ŌåÆ moving window integration ŌåÆ adaptive thresholding).
- Verify each detected R-peak by checking inter-beat intervals for physiological plausibility (typically 0.4 ŌĆō 1.5 s).

**Stage 3: RR Interval Series Construction**
- Compute the RR interval sequence: $RR_i = t_{R}(i+1) - t_{R}(i)$ where $t_{R}(i)$ is the time of the $i$-th R-peak.
- Construct the instantaneous HRV time series.

**Stage 4: Artifact and Ectopic Beat Handling**
- Apply an **artifact detection algorithm** (e.g., Malik's adaptive filter, threshold-based detection at $\pm 20\%$ deviation from local mean).
- For detected artifacts or ectopic beats, apply either **deletion** (with subsequent gap handling) or **interpolation** using cubic splines from neighboring normal beats.

**Stage 5: Uniform Resampling**
- Resample the unevenly spaced RR series to a uniform sampling rate, typically **$f_s = 4 \text{ Hz}$** (Nyquist frequency = 2 Hz, sufficient for the highest HRV frequency of 0.4 Hz).
- Use **cubic spline interpolation** (preferred for smoothness) or **linear interpolation** (simpler).

**Stage 6: Spectral Estimation**
- **For long recordings (Ōēź 5 min):** Apply **Welch's method** with segment length 256 samples (64 s), 50% overlap, and Hann window. This provides a good balance of resolution and variance reduction.
- **For short recordings (< 5 min):** Apply **AR(p) modeling** with order $p$ selected by Akaike's Information Criterion (typically $p = 8$ to $20$).

**Stage 7: Band Power Computation**
- Integrate the estimated PSD over the three canonical bands:
  - VLF: 0.003 ŌĆō 0.04 Hz
  - LF: 0.04 ŌĆō 0.15 Hz
  - HF: 0.15 ŌĆō 0.4 Hz
- Use **trapezoidal numerical integration** in software or analytical integration in the AR case.

**Stage 8: Normalization and Reporting**
- Compute $LF_{\text{norm}} = LF / (TP - VLF) \times 100$
- Compute $HF_{\text{norm}} = HF / (TP - VLF) \times 100$
- Compute $R_{LF/HF} = LF / HF$
- Generate a clinical report with band powers, normalized values, ratios, and physiological interpretation.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Definitions and Clinical Significance of HRV Spectral Indices:**

**(i) Total Power (TP):**
$$TP = \int_{0.003}^{0.4} P_x(f) \, df \quad [\text{ms}^2]$$

**Definition:** The total power represents the **overall variance of the HRV signal** over the full frequency range of interest (0.003 to 0.4 Hz). It encompasses the contributions of all three bands.

**Clinical Significance:** TP is a global indicator of **overall autonomic activity and cardiovascular fitness**. Reduced TP is associated with increased mortality post-myocardial infarction, autonomic neuropathy, and chronic stress. Healthy young adults typically exhibit $TP > 2000 \text{ ms}^2$ in 5-minute recordings.

**(ii) LF in Normalized Units (LF n.u.):**
$$LF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{LF}{TP - VLF} \times 100 \quad [\text{n.u.}]$$

**Definition:** The LF power expressed as a percentage of the total power excluding VLF. This normalization removes the confounding influence of VLF and very-low-frequency noise.

**Clinical Significance:** $LF_{\text{norm}}$ primarily reflects **baroreflex-mediated sympathetic and parasympathetic modulation**. Elevated $LF_{\text{norm}}$ (> 60 n.u.) indicates sympathetic dominance or baroreflex overactivity, commonly seen in stress, anxiety, hypertension, and orthostatic challenge. Reduced $LF_{\text{norm}}$ (< 30 n.u.) is seen in well-trained athletes at rest and in parasympathetic dominance states.

**(iii) HF in Normalized Units (HF n.u.):**
$$HF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{HF}{TP - VLF} \times 100 \quad [\text{n.u.}]$$

**Definition:** The HF power expressed as a percentage of the total power excluding VLF.

**Clinical Significance:** $HF_{\text{norm}}$ is the most specific index of **parasympathetic (vagal) tone** and respiratory sinus arrhythmia. It is the earliest HRV index to be diminished in **diabetic autonomic neuropathy**, often before symptoms manifest. Reduced $HF_{\text{norm}}$ (< 20 n.u.) is a powerful predictor of cardiac mortality. The mathematical relationship $LF_{\text{norm}} + HF_{\text{norm}} = 100$ always holds by construction.

**(iv) LF/HF Ratio:**
$$R_{LF/HF} = \frac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}} \quad [\text{dimensionless}]$$

**Definition:** The ratio of absolute LF power to absolute HF power, representing the balance between sympathetic and parasympathetic activity.

**Clinical Significance:** $R_{LF/HF}$ is the **sympathovagal balance index**. Normal resting range is **1.0 to 4.0**. Elevated values (> 4.0) indicate sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise, hypertension, heart failure). Reduced values (< 1.0) indicate parasympathetic dominance (relaxation, meditation, sleep, athletic conditioning). Acute changes in $R_{LF/HF}$ during head-up tilt, exercise, or pharmacological blockade (atropine, propranolol) are used to assess autonomic reactivity in clinical autonomic function tests.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students frequently write band power in **Hz** instead of **$\text{ms}^2$**. Always specify **$\text{ms}^2$** for absolute power and **n.u.** for normalized units. Loss of **1 mark** per such error is common.
> 2. **Band Boundaries:** Memorize the exact frequency limits: VLF ends at **0.04 Hz** (not 0.05), and HF starts at **0.15 Hz** (not 0.20). Misquoting these limits costs **2 marks** in numerical problems.
> 3. **LF Misattribution:** Do not claim LF is "purely sympathetic." The correct statement is "mixed sympathetic and parasympathetic, predominantly sympathetic." Over-simplification loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping Pre-processing:** In derivation questions, students often jump directly to PSD estimation without mentioning **resampling and interpolation**. This is a significant omission; always include the full pipeline.
> 5. **Yule-Walker vs. Levinson-Durbin:** Levinson-Durbin is an **algorithm** for solving the Yule-Walker equations, not a separate method. Do not present them as alternatives.
> 6. **Normalization Formula:** The denominator is **$TP - VLF$**, NOT $TP$. Forgetting to subtract VLF is a very common error in 14-mark problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Examinations**

- **Definition:** HRV = variation in NN intervals; spectral analysis decomposes this variation into frequency components using PSD estimation.

- **Three Frequency Bands (Memorize the Limits Exactly):**
  - VLF: **0.003 ŌĆō 0.04 Hz** (thermoregulation, vasomotor)
  - LF: **0.04 ŌĆō 0.15 Hz** (baroreflex, mixed autonomic)
  - HF: **0.15 ŌĆō 0.4 Hz** (respiratory sinus arrhythmia, pure parasympathetic)

- **Standard Resampling Rate:** $f_s = 4 \text{ Hz}$ (Nyquist = 2 Hz Ōē½ 0.4 Hz HF limit).

- **Two Spectral Methods:**
  - **Non-Parametric (Welch):** Use for long recordings (Ōēź 5 min). Segment length 256, 50% overlap, Hann window.
  - **Parametric (AR):** Use for short recordings (< 5 min). Model order $p \in [8, 20]$ via AIC.

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Periodogram: $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$
  - Welch: $P_W(f) = \frac{1}{M \cdot L} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert X_k(f) \vert^2$
  - AR PSD: $P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i e^{-j2\pi f i} \vert^2}$
  - Normalization: $LF_{\text{norm}} = \frac{LF}{TP - VLF} \times 100$
  - Ratio: $R_{LF/HF} = LF / HF$

- **Units:** Band power in **$\text{ms}^2$**, normalized in **n.u.**, ratio is **dimensionless**.

- **Physiological Interpretation:**
  - Ōåæ LF/HF ŌåÆ Sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise, hypertension)
  - Ōåō LF/HF ŌåÆ Parasympathetic dominance (relaxation, sleep, athletes)
  - Ōåæ HF ŌåÆ Good vagal tone, healthy HRV
  - Ōåō HF ŌåÆ Autonomic dysfunction, neuropathy, cardiac risk

- **Yule-Walker Equations:** Linear system of $p$ equations solved by Levinson-Durbin recursion; autocorrelation matrix is Toeplitz.

- **Welch Variance Reduction:** $\text{Var}[P_W] = P_x^2(f) / K$ (factor of $K$ improvement over periodogram).

- **Clinical Gold Standard (1996 Task Force):** Reporting must include TP, VLF, LF, HF (absolute and normalized), and LF/HF ratio.

- **Common Pitfall:** Never confuse "NN intervals" (normal-to-normal sinus beats) with "RR intervals" (all beats including ectopics). Always work with NN intervals for HRV analysis.

- **Emerging Method:** **Lomb-Scargle periodogram** is preferred when resampling is undesirable (preserves original uneven sampling) or when data has significant gaps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of a Signal from a Laser Doppler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of a Signal from a Laser Doppler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Doppler Flowmetry (LDF)**, often called **Laser Doppler Perfusion Monitoring**, is a non-invasive, real-time biomedical signal acquisition technique used to quantify *microvascular blood perfusion* in tissues. The technique exploits the **Doppler shift** experienced by monochromatic laser light (typically at **785 nm** or **632.8 nm**) when it is scattered by moving red blood cells (RBCs) within the microcirculation. The resulting photocurrent at the photodetector is a **stochastic, non-stationary, wideband signal** composed of a **DC baseline component** (light scattered by static tissue) and an **AC fluctuating component** (light scattered by moving erythrocytes).

The processed parameters derived from the LDF signal are:
- **Perfusion (PU)** ŌĆö product of RBC concentration and mean RBC velocity.
- **Concentration (CMBC)** ŌĆö relative number of moving RBCs.
- **Velocity (V)** ŌĆö mean velocity of the RBC ensemble.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2, the analysis of a Laser Doppler signal is treated as a classic case study of a *stochastic biomedical signal* whose first-order statistics, autocorrelation, and power spectral density directly map to physiological parameters (perfusion, velocity, concentration).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing by a busy road holding a flashlight at night. Most of the light reflects off the parked cars and road (stationary objects) and returns unchanged ŌĆö this is the **DC component**. However, the headlights and tail-lights of moving cars return a slightly *blurred* or *flickering* light, with a small color/frequency shift caused by their motion ŌĆö this is the **AC component**. 

In an LDF system, the "flashlight" is a **low-power laser**, the "cars" are **red blood cells (RBCs)** flowing through capillaries, and the "detector" is a **photodiode** that mixes the Doppler-shifted light with the unshifted reference light. By analyzing the frequency content of the flicker, we infer *how fast* and *how many* cells are moving. This is essentially a **microscopic radar gun for blood flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in LDF:**
> - Laser wavelength $\lambda \approx 785\,\text{nm}$ (near-infrared, penetrates tissue well)
> - Speed of light in tissue $c \approx 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$
> - Refractive index of tissue $n \approx 1.33 - 1.4$
> - Photodetector bandwidth: typically $\mathbf{20\,\text{Hz}}$ to $\mathbf{20\,\text{kHz}}$
> - Sampling frequency: typically $\mathbf{44.1\,\text{kHz}}$ or higher

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

The Laser Doppler signal is one of the most instructive examples of a **non-deterministic, multi-component stochastic signal** encountered in biomedical engineering. It demonstrates how *time-domain* statistics (mean, RMS) and *frequency-domain* analysis (PSD, bandwidth) can be combined to extract clinically meaningful information. It serves as a benchmark for understanding **noise models, autocorrelation, and spectral estimation** techniques covered in Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the LDF Signal ŌĆö The Doppler Shift

When a photon of frequency $f_0$ strikes a moving RBC with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ between the velocity vector and the bisector of incident/scattered beams, the backscattered photon experiences a frequency shift $\Delta f$ given by the **optical Doppler equation**:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 \, n \, v}{\lambda} \cos\theta
$$

where:
- $n$ = refractive index of the medium
- $v$ = velocity of the scattering particle (RBC)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of the incident laser in vacuum
- $\theta$ = angle between velocity vector and observation direction

For typical microvascular flow ($v \approx 1\,\text{mm/s}$) and $\lambda = 785\,\text{nm}$, $\Delta f$ lies in the range $\mathbf{1\,\text{Hz}}$ to $\mathbf{20\,\text{kHz}}$ ŌĆö well within the audio range. This is why the detected photocurrent is often called the **"Doppler audio signal."**

### 2.2 Photomixing and Heterodyne Detection

The photodetector cannot measure the absolute optical frequency ($\sim 10^{14}\,\text{Hz}$). Instead, it performs **square-law detection**, producing a current proportional to the *intensity* of the sum of two optical fields:
- The **reference field** $E_r$ (light scattered by static tissue, unshifted).
- The **Doppler-shifted field** $E_d$ (light scattered by moving RBCs).

The total intensity is:

$$
I(t) = \langle \vert E_r + E_d(t) \vert^2 \rangle = \vert E_r \vert^2 + \vert E_d \vert^2 + 2 E_r E_d \cos(2\pi \Delta f \cdot t + \phi)
$$

- The first two terms combine to form the **DC baseline** ($I_{DC}$).
- The third term is the **AC beat signal** at frequency $\Delta f$, which carries the velocity information.

### 2.3 Statistical Signal Model

Since the microvasculature contains a *distribution* of RBC velocities, the LDF signal is modeled as a **sum of random sinusoids** (random-phase, random-frequency). Its mathematical model is:

$$
x(t) = I_{DC} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i)
$$

- $a_i$ = amplitude proportional to backscatter from $i$-th RBC
- $f_i$ = Doppler shift corresponding to $i$-th RBC velocity
- $\phi_i$ = uniformly random phase $\in [0, 2\pi)$
- $N$ = number of moving RBCs in the sample volume

This makes $x(t)$ a **wide-sense stationary (WSS) ergodic process** in short observation windows ŌĆö a key assumption for applying autocorrelation and PSD techniques.

### 2.4 Power Spectral Density and Velocity Distribution

A foundational result of LDF theory is the **direct proportionality** between the **normalized power spectral density (PSD)** of the AC component and the **velocity distribution of the RBCs**:

$$
S_x(f) \propto P(v) \quad \text{where} \quad v = \frac{\lambda f}{2 n \cos\theta}
$$

From this, the **first moment** of the spectrum gives the **mean velocity**, and the **zeroth moment** gives the **RBC concentration**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognize and apply in examination problems on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` in place of the raw pipe character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Doppler shift | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 n v \cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | Frequency shift $[\text{Hz}]$ |
| Velocity from frequency | $v = \dfrac{\lambda \, \Delta f}{2 n \cos\theta}$ | RBC velocity $[\text{m/s}]$ |
| Photocurrent model | $I(t) = I_{DC} + I_{AC}(t)$ | Total LDF signal $[\text{A}]$ |
| Instantaneous intensity | $I(t) = \langle \vert E_r + E_d(t) \vert^2 \rangle$ | Optical intensity $[\text{W/m}^2]$ |
| Autocorrelation | $R_x(\tau) = \lim\limits_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) x(t+\tau)\, dt$ | WSS assumption |
| Power Spectral Density | $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{ R_x(\tau) \}$ | Wiener-Khinchin theorem |
| Mean of PSD (zero moment) | $M_0 = \int_{0}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df$ | Proportional to RBC concentration |
| Mean frequency (first moment) | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{\infty} f \, S_x(f)\, df}{\int_{0}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df}$ | Proportional to mean velocity |
| RMS bandwidth (second moment) | $\sigma_f^2 = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{\infty} (f - \bar{f})^2 S_x(f)\, df}{\int_{0}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df}$ | Spectral spread $[\text{Hz}^2]$ |
| Perfusion (definition) | $P = K \cdot C \cdot V$ | Product in arbitrary Perfusion Units (PU) |
| Signal-to-Noise ratio | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\sigma_{AC}^2}{\sigma_{noise}^2} \right)$ | Power ratio in dB |
| RMS value of AC component | $x_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} (x(t) - \bar{x})^2 \, dt}$ | Standard deviation $\sigma_x$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LDF signal analysis paradigm is not confined to blood flow. The same theoretical framework ŌĆö heterodyne detection of Doppler-shifted scattered light ŌĆö is used in:
- **Laser Doppler Vibrometry (LDV)** ŌĆö measuring structural vibrations on turbine blades, MEMS devices, and bridges.
- **Doppler Radar** ŌĆö for weather forecasting and automotive speed guns.
- **Sonar and Ultrasound Doppler** ŌĆö fetal heart-rate monitors and echocardiography.
- **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)** ŌĆö high-resolution retinal imaging.

Thus, mastering LDF signal analysis equips the student with a transferable toolkit for any Doppler-based sensing modality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Maxwell's Wave Equation to the Optical Doppler Shift

We begin with a plane monochromatic wave of frequency $f_0$ and wavelength $\lambda$ incident on a moving particle. The particle moves with velocity $v$ along a line making angle $\theta$ with the direction of observation (backscattered configuration).

**Step 1: Wavefront arrival at the moving scatterer.**
In the rest frame of the scatterer, the incident wave frequency is shifted according to the relativistic (or for $v \ll c$, classical) Doppler formula:

$$
f' = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta_i}{c/n}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Re-emission in the lab frame.**
The scatterer re-emits the wave at frequency $f'$ back along the same direction. In the lab frame, the detector sees a second shift:

$$
f_{det} = f' \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta_s}{c/n}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Combine the two shifts (backscattered geometry, $\theta_i = \theta_s = \theta$).**

$$
f_{det} = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c/n}\right)\left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c/n}\right)
$$

$$
f_{det} = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{(c/n)^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Apply the non-relativistic approximation $v \ll c$ (true for RBCs, where $v \sim 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}$ vs $c \sim 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$).**
The $v^2/c^2$ term is negligible ($\sim 10^{-22}$), so:

$$
f_{det} \approx f_0
$$

**Step 5: Compute the *beat* frequency (difference between incident and detected).**

$$
\Delta f = f_0 - f_{det} = \frac{2 v f_0 \cos\theta}{c/n} = \frac{2 n v \cos\theta}{\lambda}
$$

This is the classical optical Doppler equation used universally in LDF textbooks.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** in the numerator arises because the light undergoes *two* Doppler shifts ŌĆö once on the way in, once on the way out. This distinguishes optical Doppler from acoustic Doppler, which has a factor of 2 only for stationary source and stationary receiver.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Photocurrent from Heterodyne Mixing

Let the reference field be $E_r = A_r \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ and the Doppler-shifted field be $E_d = A_d \cos[2\pi (f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi]$.

**Step 1:** Total optical field at the detector:

$$
E_{tot}(t) = A_r \cos(2\pi f_0 t) + A_d \cos[2\pi (f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi]
$$

**Step 2:** Square-law detection (photodiode current is proportional to optical intensity $\vert E_{tot} \vert^2$):

$$
I(t) \propto A_r^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_0 t) + A_d^2 \cos^2[2\pi (f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi] + 2 A_r A_d \cos(2\pi f_0 t) \cos[2\pi (f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi]
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $2\cos\alpha\cos\beta = \cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)$ to the cross-term:

$$
2 A_r A_d \cos(2\pi f_0 t) \cos[2\pi (f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi] = A_r A_d \left\{ \cos(2\pi \Delta f \, t + \phi) + \cos[2\pi (2f_0 + \Delta f) t + \phi] \right\}
$$

**Step 4:** The photodetector cannot respond at optical frequencies ($2f_0 \sim 10^{14}\,\text{Hz}$), so the high-frequency terms average to zero. The baseband photocurrent is:

$$
i(t) = \underbrace{A_r^2 + A_d^2}_{I_{DC}} + \underbrace{A_r A_d \cos(2\pi \Delta f \, t + \phi)}_{I_{AC}(t)}
$$

This confirms the LDF signal model: a DC baseline plus a beat-frequency AC component.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Autocorrelation of the Multi-RBC Signal

For a multi-RBC signal:

$$
x(t) = I_{DC} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i)
$$

**Step 1: Compute the autocorrelation function $R_x(\tau) = \mathbb{E}[x(t) x(t+\tau)]$.**
Substituting:

$$
x(t) x(t+\tau) = I_{DC}^2 + I_{DC} \sum_i a_i \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i) + I_{DC} \sum_j a_j \cos[2\pi f_j (t+\tau) + \phi_j] + \sum_i \sum_j a_i a_j \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i) \cos[2\pi f_j (t+\tau) + \phi_j]
$$

**Step 2: Take the expectation. Use $\mathbb{E}[\cos(\cdot + \phi_i)] = 0$ for uniformly distributed $\phi_i$, and the identity $\mathbb{E}[\cos\alpha \cos\beta] = \tfrac{1}{2}\cos(\alpha-\beta)$ when the phases are independent.**

$$
R_x(\tau) = I_{DC}^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2 \cos(2\pi f_i \tau)
$$

The cross-terms ($i \neq j$) vanish because $\phi_i$ and $\phi_j$ are statistically independent.

**Step 3: Recognize the Fourier pair.** Since $R_x(\tau)$ is a sum of cosines, its Fourier transform is a sum of delta functions weighted by $\tfrac{1}{2} a_i^2$:

$$
S_x(f) = I_{DC}^2 \delta(f) + \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2 \left[ \delta(f - f_i) + \delta(f + f_i) \right]
$$

**Step 4: For a continuous distribution of velocities (large $N$), the sum becomes an integral:**

$$
S_x(f) = I_{DC}^2 \delta(f) + \frac{1}{2} S_{AC}(f)
$$

where $S_{AC}(f)$ is the one-sided Doppler power spectrum directly proportional to the velocity distribution of the RBC ensemble.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Perfusion, Concentration, and Velocity from the PSD

Define the AC component's PSD as $S_{AC}(f)$ for $f \in [f_{min}, f_{max}]$.

**Step 1: RBC Concentration (CMBC).** Proportional to the zeroth moment:

$$
C \propto M_0 = \int_{f_{min}}^{f_{max}} S_{AC}(f)\, df
$$

**Step 2: Mean Velocity.** Proportional to the first moment normalized by $M_0$:

$$
V \propto \bar{f} = \frac{1}{M_0} \int_{f_{min}}^{f_{max}} f \, S_{AC}(f)\, df
$$

**Step 3: Perfusion.** Defined as the product of concentration and velocity, scaled by a constant $K$ that depends on optical geometry:

$$
P = K \cdot C \cdot V = K \int_{f_{min}}^{f_{max}} f \, S_{AC}(f)\, df
$$

**Step 4: Practical computation using the root-mean-square (RMS) value.** For real-time hardware, $P$ is often estimated directly in the time domain:

$$
P \propto \sigma_{AC} \cdot \bar{f}_{inst}
$$

where $\sigma_{AC}$ is the standard deviation of the AC component and $\bar{f}_{inst}$ is the instantaneous dominant frequency (estimated by zero-crossing counting or analog frequency-to-voltage conversion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of $f_{min}$ and $f_{max}$ is critical. Low-frequency motion artifacts (respiration, probe displacement) appear below ~$20\,\text{Hz}$ and must be filtered out. High-frequency shot noise dominates above ~$20\,\text{kHz}$. The usable LDF bandwidth is therefore $\mathbf{20\,\text{Hz}}$ to $\mathbf{20\,\text{kHz}}$, corresponding to physiological velocities of $\mathbf{0.1\,\text{mm/s}}$ to $\mathbf{100\,\text{mm/s}}$.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A Laser Doppler Flowmeter uses a $\lambda = 785\,\text{nm}$ laser in tissue with $n = 1.4$. An RBC moves at $v = 5\,\text{mm/s}$ at angle $\theta = 45^{\circ}$. Compute the Doppler shift.

**Step 1:** Insert values into the Doppler equation:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 n v \cos\theta}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 1.4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times \cos 45^{\circ}}{785 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\cos 45^{\circ} = 0.7071$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{2 \times 1.4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7071}{785 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

**Step 3:** Numerator:

$$
2 \times 1.4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.7071 = 9.8994 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $785 \times 10^{-9}$:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{9.8994 \times 10^{-3}}{7.85 \times 10^{-7}} = 12{,}611\,\text{Hz} \approx 12.6\,\text{kHz}
$$

This lies well within the LDF audio band, confirming the validity of the design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the LDF Signal Processing Chain

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete signal-flow architecture of a Laser Doppler Flowmeter, from the optical transducer to the clinical display. It maps the *physical* components (laser, probe, photodetector) to the *signal-processing* stages (amplification, filtering, PSD estimation, parameter extraction) covered in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LASER SOURCE 785 NM] --> B[OPTICAL FIBER PROBE]
    B --> C[TISSUE MICROVASCULATURE MOVING RBCS]
    C --> D[BACKSCATTERED LIGHT MIXED REFERENCE PLUS DOPPLER]
    D --> E[PHOTODETECTOR PHOTODIODE]
    E --> F[TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER]
    F --> G[HIGH PASS FILTER CUTOFF 20 HZ]
    G --> H[LOW PASS FILTER CUTOFF 20 KHZ]
    H --> I[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    I --> J[DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR]
    J --> K[PSD ESTIMATION WELCH METHOD]
    K --> L[MOMENT CALCULATION M0 M1 M2]
    L --> M[PERFUSION P]
    L --> N[CONCENTRATION C]
    L --> O[VELOCITY V]
    M --> P[CLINICAL DISPLAY PU UNITS]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    J --> Q[REAL TIME OSCILLOGRAPH WAVEFORM]
    P --> R[DATA LOGGING AND EXPORT]
```

### 4.2 Spectral Estimation Sub-Block (Detailed)

This auxiliary diagram zooms into the PSD estimation block, illustrating the **Welch method** (a standard non-parametric spectral estimation technique recommended by the KTU Module 2 syllabus).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[INPUT LDF SIGNAL XN] --> T[SEGMENT INTO OVERLAPPING WINDOWS]
    T --> U[APPLY WINDOW FUNCTION HAMMING]
    U --> V[COMPUTE FFT FOR EACH SEGMENT]
    V --> W[COMPUTE PERIODOGRAM PER SEGMENT]
    W --> X[AVERAGE PERIODOGRAMS]
    X --> Y[OUTPUT PSD SX OF F]
    Y --> Z[MOMENT EXTRACTION]
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Description (for Examination)

For a 14-mark question requiring a diagram, the student should describe each block as follows:

1. **Laser Source** ŌĆö single-mode, low-power (< 5 mW) near-infrared diode to ensure patient safety (ANSI MPE limits).
2. **Optical Fiber Probe** ŌĆö delivers laser to tissue and collects backscatter; separate transmit/receive fibers reduce specular reflection.
3. **Tissue Microvasculature** ŌĆö acts as the random scattering medium; the moving RBCs are the "Doppler targets."
4. **Photodetector** ŌĆö silicon PIN photodiode with high bandwidth and low dark current; performs square-law detection.
5. **Transimpedance Amplifier (TIA)** ŌĆö converts photodiode current to voltage with gain $\sim 10^5\,\text{V/A}$.
6. **Bandpass Filter (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz)** ŌĆö removes DC drift, 1/f noise, and high-frequency shot noise.
7. **ADC** ŌĆö typically 16-bit, sampling at $\geq 44.1\,\text{kHz}$ (Nyquist criterion satisfied).
8. **DSP Module** ŌĆö computes PSD, extracts moments, derives Perfusion / Concentration / Velocity.
9. **Display Unit** ŌĆö real-time perfusion trace, numerical readout, and alarm thresholds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
*Define Laser Doppler Flowmetry. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Laser Doppler Flowmeter for measuring microvascular blood perfusion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Laser Doppler Flowmetry (LDF) is a non-invasive technique used to measure microvascular blood perfusion based on the frequency shift (Doppler shift) experienced by laser light when scattered by moving red blood cells.
- **(1 mark) Principle:** The light scattered by moving RBCs undergoes a Doppler frequency shift $\Delta f = (2 n v \cos\theta)/\lambda$, which is proportional to RBC velocity. The backscattered light is mixed with reference (unshifted) light at the photodetector, producing an AC photocurrent at the beat frequency.
- **(1 mark) Block Diagram:** Laser source $\to$ optical fiber probe $\to$ tissue $\to$ photodetector $\to$ amplifier $\to$ filter (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz) $\to$ signal processor (PSD + moment analysis) $\to$ perfusion display.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
*List the three main output parameters of a Laser Doppler Flowmeter and explain how each is derived from the power spectral density (PSD) of the AC component of the LDF signal.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(1 mark) Perfusion (P):** $P = K \cdot C \cdot V$, derived as the first moment of the PSD, $P \propto \int f \cdot S(f) df$.
2. **(1 mark) Concentration (C):** Proportional to the zeroth moment of the PSD, $C \propto \int S(f) df$, representing the relative number of moving RBCs.
3. **(1 mark) Velocity (V):** Proportional to the first moment normalized by the zeroth moment, $V \propto \bar{f} = \int f S(f) df / \int S(f) df$, representing the mean Doppler frequency.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the optical Doppler shift equation $\Delta f = (2 n v \cos\theta)/\lambda$ for a Laser Doppler Flowmeter. State the assumptions made.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LDF system uses a 785 nm laser in tissue (refractive index $n = 1.4$). The Doppler signal has a flat (rectangular) power spectral density $S(f) = S_0$ for $20\,\text{Hz} \leq f \leq 20{,}000\,\text{Hz}$ and zero elsewhere. Compute:  
    (i) the **mean Doppler frequency** $\bar{f}$,  
    (ii) the **RMS bandwidth** $\sigma_f$,  
    (iii) the **mean RBC velocity** $V$ assuming $\theta = 0^{\circ}$ and $K = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question A**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the assumption of non-relativistic motion. Since $v \ll c/n$ for RBCs ($\sim 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}$ vs $\sim 10^8\,\text{m/s}$), classical Doppler theory applies.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Frequency observed in the moving frame of the scatterer:

$$
f' = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c/n}\right)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Frequency re-emitted and observed in the lab frame (backscatter geometry):

$$
f_{det} = f' \left(1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c/n}\right)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Multiply and apply $v \ll c$ approximation:

$$
f_{det} \approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{(c/n)^2}\right) \approx f_0
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Beat frequency:

$$
\Delta f = f_0 - f_{det} = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c/n} = \frac{2 n v \cos\theta}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Highlight assumptions: monochromatic laser, single-scatterer approximation, backscatter geometry, $v \ll c$.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note units: $\Delta f$ in Hz when $v$ in m/s, $\lambda$ in m.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Mean Doppler frequency (3 marks):**

By definition:

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{\int_{20}^{20000} f \cdot S_0 \, df}{\int_{20}^{20000} S_0 \, df}
$$

The constant $S_0$ cancels:

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{\int_{20}^{20000} f \, df}{\int_{20}^{20000} df} = \frac{\left[ f^2/2 \right]_{20}^{20000}}{[f]_{20}^{20000}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\frac{(20000)^2 - (20)^2}{2} = \frac{4 \times 10^8 - 400}{2} = 1.99998 \times 10^8
$$

Denominator:

$$
20000 - 20 = 19980
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{1.99998 \times 10^8}{19980} = 10{,}010.01\,\text{Hz} \approx 10.01\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(ii) RMS Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_f^2 = \frac{1}{M_0} \int_{20}^{20000} (f - \bar{f})^2 S_0 \, df = \frac{1}{19980} \int_{20}^{20000} (f - 10010.01)^2 \, df
$$

For a uniform distribution on $[a, b]$, the variance is $(b-a)^2/12$:

$$
\sigma_f^2 = \frac{(20000 - 20)^2}{12} = \frac{(19980)^2}{12} = \frac{3.992 \times 10^8}{12} = 3.327 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \sqrt{3.327 \times 10^7} \approx 5767.7\,\text{Hz} \approx 5.77\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(iii) Mean RBC Velocity (2 marks):**

Using $V = \lambda \bar{f} / (2 n \cos\theta)$ with $\theta = 0^{\circ}$:

$$
V = \frac{785 \times 10^{-9} \times 10010.01}{2 \times 1.4 \times 1} = \frac{7.8579 \times 10^{-3}}{2.8} = 2.806 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s}
$$

$$
V \approx 2.81\,\text{mm/s}
$$

This is consistent with typical microvascular flow rates in human skin.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a Laser Doppler Flowmeter. Describe the role of the photodetector and the signal conditioning stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Doppler signal from an LDF is modeled as $x(t) = I_{DC} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i)$, where $\phi_i$ are independent and uniformly distributed on $[0, 2\pi)$.  
    (i) Derive the **autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$ of $x(t)$.  
    (ii) Apply the **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** to obtain the **power spectral density** $S_x(f)$.  
    (iii) Explain how the **perfusion parameter** is obtained from $S_x(f)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution ŌĆö Question B**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Block Diagram and Description (7 marks):**

| **Block** | **Function (approx. 1 mark each)** |
|---|---|
| Laser diode (785 nm) | Produces coherent, monochromatic light. |
| Optical fiber probe | Delivers laser to tissue; collects backscatter. |
| Photodetector (PIN diode) | Square-law detection; produces AC photocurrent at beat frequency. |
| Transimpedance amplifier | Converts current to voltage; provides gain. |
| Bandpass filter (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz) | Removes DC drift, 1/f noise, and high-frequency shot noise. |
| ADC + DSP | Digital sampling, PSD computation (Welch), moment extraction. |
| Display | Real-time perfusion value in PU. |

**Part (b) ŌĆö Stochastic Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Autocorrelation (3 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.3:

$$
R_x(\tau) = I_{DC}^2 + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2 \cos(2\pi f_i \tau)
$$

The cross-terms vanish because $\mathbb{E}[\cos(\alpha + \phi_i)\cos(\beta + \phi_j)] = 0$ for $i \neq j$ when $\phi_i, \phi_j$ are independent and uniform.

**(ii) Power Spectral Density (2 marks):**

By the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$:

$$
S_x(f) = I_{DC}^2 \, \delta(f) + \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^2 \left[ \delta(f - f_i) + \delta(f + f_i) \right]
$$

For continuous velocity distribution, the discrete sum becomes the smooth spectrum $S_{AC}(f)$.

**(iii) Perfusion Parameter (2 marks):**

Perfusion is the first moment of the AC PSD:

$$
P = K \int_{f_{min}}^{f_{max}} f \cdot S_{AC}(f) \, df
$$

Equivalently, $P = K \cdot C \cdot V$ where $C = M_0$ and $V \propto \bar{f}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Doppler shift equation** ŌĆö students often write $\Delta f = (n v \cos\theta)/\lambda$, omitting the backscatter factor. This is the single most common error and costs 1ŌĆō2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing acoustic and optical Doppler formulas** ŌĆö acoustic Doppler uses $f_s$ and $f_o$ as separate source/observer frequencies, while optical Doppler for backscatter always yields the compact $2 n v \cos\theta/\lambda$ form.
> 3. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ with raw pipe characters in markdown tables** ŌĆö this breaks the table and the examiner may mark the answer as incomplete. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.
> 4. **Neglecting the refractive index $n$** ŌĆö some students treat $n = 1$ in the equation, which gives an answer ~33% lower than the correct value for soft tissue. The KTU examiner specifically looks for $n = 1.33$ŌĆō$1.4$.
> 5. **Mixing up zeroth, first, and second moments** ŌĆö Concentration $\propto M_0$, Velocity $\propto M_1 / M_0$, Bandwidth $\propto \sqrt{M_2/M_0 - (M_1/M_0)^2}$. Confusing these definitions will cost full marks on the numerical part.
> 6. **Not stating the WSS / ergodic assumption explicitly** ŌĆö KTU valuation scheme penalizes derivations where the stationarity assumption is left unstated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List for KTU 2024 ŌĆö Laser Doppler Signal Analysis**

- [x] **LDF = non-invasive microvascular perfusion monitor** based on optical Doppler shift.
- [x] **Wavelength of choice:** $\lambda = 785\,\text{nm}$ (NIR, safe, deep tissue penetration).
- [x] **Core equation:** $\Delta f = (2 n v \cos\theta)/\lambda$ ŌĆö **never forget the factor of 2**.
- [x] **Signal structure:** $x(t) = I_{DC} + I_{AC}(t)$ ŌĆö DC from static tissue, AC from moving RBCs.
- [x] **Detection principle:** Heterodyne / square-law photomixing ŌĆö photodiode cannot see optical frequencies.
- [x] **Usable bandwidth:** $20\,\text{Hz}$ to $20\,\text{kHz}$ (motion artifacts below, shot noise above).
- [x] **Three output parameters:** Perfusion $P$, Concentration $C$, Velocity $V$, with $P = K \cdot C \cdot V$.
- [x] **Spectral interpretation:** $S_{AC}(f) \propto$ velocity distribution of RBCs.
- [x] **First moment of PSD = mean velocity; Zeroth moment = concentration; First moment (un-normalized) = perfusion.**
- [x] **Welch's method** is the recommended PSD estimator for real LDF signals (averaged periodograms).
- [x] **Autocorrelation** of the multi-RBC model reduces to $R_x(\tau) = I_{DC}^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum a_i^2 \cos(2\pi f_i \tau)$ due to random phases.
- [x] **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** links $R_x(\tau) \leftrightarrow S_x(f)$ ŌĆö foundational for all spectral analysis.
- [x] **Assumptions to declare in any derivation:** non-relativistic ($v \ll c$), WSS, ergodic, single-scattering regime, uniform random phase.
- [x] **Refractive index of tissue:** $n \approx 1.4$ ŌĆö required for correct velocity estimation.
- [x] **Clinical applications:** skin perfusion (burn assessment, diabetes), cerebral blood flow, intestinal ischemia, dental pulp vitality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geometrical Scaling and Self-Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geometrical Scaling and Self-Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Geometrical Scaling

In the context of **wavelet theory** and **biomedical signal processing**, *geometrical scaling* refers to the mathematical operation of **dilating** (stretching) or **contracting** (compressing) a function along the time (or independent variable) axis. For a continuous-time signal $f(t)$, a geometrical scale change by a factor $a \in \mathbb{R}^+$, $a \neq 0$ produces a new function:

$$f_a(t) = f(at)$$

The parameter $a$ governs the **geometrical transformation**:
- When $a > 1$ ŌåÆ the function is **contracted** (compressed in time, expanded in frequency).
- When $0 < a < 1$ ŌåÆ the function is **dilated** (stretched in time, compressed in frequency).
- When $a < 0$ ŌåÆ the function is **time-reversed** and scaled.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Geometrical scaling is a continuous affine transformation of the independent variable, $t \rightarrow at$, that produces a new waveform geometrically similar to the original but rescaled along the time axis. Self-similarity is the property by which a function (or its sub-structures) is invariant (up to amplitude scaling) under such a geometrical scaling operation.*

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Self-Similarity

A function $f(t)$ is said to be **self-similar** if there exists a non-trivial scaling factor $a$ and an amplitude scaling factor $c$ such that:

$$f(t) = c \cdot f(at), \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R}$$

In a weaker (and more practically useful) sense, a *family* of functions is self-similar if every member of the family can be obtained from a single *mother* function $\psi(t)$ by a combination of **scaling** and **translation**:

$$\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\,\psi(2^j t - k), \quad j, k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-similarity in Wavelets:** The wavelet basis $\{\psi_{j,k}(t)\}$ is self-similar because the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is reused at every scale $j$ ŌĆö only the geometrical extent and amplitude change. This is the **core property** that makes wavelets powerful for analyzing non-stationary biomedical signals such as ECG, EEG, and EMG.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a **fern leaf** under a magnifying glass. As you zoom in on a small frond of the leaf, you see the **same structure repeated** ŌĆö each tiny sub-frond looks like the whole leaf. The fern is *self-similar*. A similar phenomenon happens in biomedical signals:

| Real-World Analogy | Biomedical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Zooming into a coastline from a satellite | Zooming into QRS complex of an ECG |
| Fractal branching of a fern | Fibrillation patterns in EEG |
| Russian-doll nesting | Self-similar basis of wavelets |

Consider an **ECG signal** ŌĆö the QRS complex has a sharp spike. If you zoom into the rising edge of that spike, you may find smaller kinks that have the *same general shape*. This *approximate* self-similarity is precisely what wavelet bases exploit: by using a single prototype waveform at multiple scales, the analyzer can capture both the broad PQRST morphology and the fine notches within the QRS.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **geometrical scaling as a "zoom knob"** on a microscope. Turning the knob magnifies the signal in time, but the *shape* of the wavelet template remains identical ŌĆö that is the essence of self-similarity.

## 1.4 Key Notational Conventions Used in KTU 2024 Scheme

- $f(t)$ ŌĆö original continuous-time biomedical signal.
- $a, s$ ŌĆö continuous scaling parameters.
- $j$ ŌĆö discrete scale index (integer) used in DWT.
- $k$ ŌĆö discrete translation index (integer).
- $\phi(t)$ ŌĆö **scaling function** (father wavelet), which generates the approximation subspace.
- $\psi(t)$ ŌĆö **wavelet function** (mother wavelet), which generates the detail subspace.
- $L^2(\mathbb{R})$ ŌĆö the Hilbert space of finite-energy signals, the natural home of biomedical signals.
- Standard metric used: **unitless scaling factor**, with $\Vert f \Vert_2 = \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(t) \vert^2 \, dt\right)^{1/2}$ defining signal energy.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Geometrical Operations

### 2.1.1 Time Scaling (Dilation / Contraction)

Given $f(t)$ and scale $a > 0$:

$$f_a(t) = f(at)$$

**Effect on signal energy:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(at) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{a}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau \quad \text{(using } \tau = at \text{)}$$

This is why wavelets must be **normalized by $\sqrt{a}$** to preserve energy (Parseval's theorem).

### 2.1.2 Time Translation

$$f_{t_0}(t) = f(t - t_0)$$

In wavelets, translation is *discrete*: $k \in \mathbb{Z}$, so the shift is by an integer multiple of the sampling period.

## 2.2 The Wavelet Basis ŌĆö Self-Similarity in Action

The Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) uses:

$$CWT_x(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,\psi^*\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

The set of atoms $\{\psi_{a,b}(t)\}$ where $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\,\psi((t-b)/a)$ is the **continuous family of self-similar copies** of $\psi(t)$.

For the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)**, we restrict $a = 2^j$ and $b = k \cdot 2^j$ (dyadic sampling):

$$\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\,\psi(2^j t - k)$$

This produces an **orthonormal basis of $L^2(\mathbb{R})$**, in which every basis function is a self-similar replica of the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$.

## 2.3 The Dilation (Two-Scale) Equation

The **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ satisfies the *fundamental self-similarity recursion*:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,\phi(2t - n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Dilation Equation** (also called the *refinement equation* or *two-scale relation*). It states that the scaling function at scale $2^0$ is a weighted sum of half-scale, shifted copies of itself. The coefficients $h[n]$ are the *scaling filter* coefficients.

Equivalently, in the **frequency domain**:

$$\Phi(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,H\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\Phi\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

where $H(\omega) = \sum_{n} h[n]e^{-j\omega n}$ is the discrete-time Fourier transform of the scaling filter.

## 2.4 Self-Similarity and Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA)

MRA requires nested subspaces $V_j \subset V_{j+1}$ such that:

1. $\overline{\bigcup_{j} V_j} = L^2(\mathbb{R})$
2. $\bigcap_{j} V_j = \{0\}$
3. $f(t) \in V_j \iff f(2t) \in V_{j+1}$ ŌåÉ **self-similarity condition**
4. $f(t) \in V_0 \iff f(t-k) \in V_0$ ŌåÉ translation invariance
5. There exists $\phi(t)$ such that $\{\phi(t-k)\}$ is a Riesz basis for $V_0$.

The orthogonality between scales is enforced by the **wavelet function** $\psi(t)$:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} g[n]\,\phi(2t - n), \quad g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$$

where $g[n]$ are the *wavelet filter* coefficients.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_a(t) = f(at)$ | Geometrical scaling of $f$ | Defining dilated signal |
| 2 | $\int \vert f(at)\vert^2 dt = a^{-1}\int \vert f(\tau)\vert^2 d\tau$ | Energy under scaling | Justifies $a^{-1/2}$ normalization |
| 3 | $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\,\psi(2^j t - k)$ | DWT basis atom | Discrete wavelet analysis |
| 4 | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\,\sum_n h[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$ | Dilation (two-scale) equation | Defines scaling function |
| 5 | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\,\sum_n g[n]\,\phi(2t-n)$ | Wavelet-from-scaling relation | Defines mother wavelet |
| 6 | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ | QMF relation between filters | Orthogonal wavelet design |
| 7 | $CWT_x(a,b) = a^{-1/2}\int x(t)\psi^*((t-b)/a)dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform | Time-scale decomposition |
| 8 | $V_j \subset V_{j+1},\; f(t)\in V_j \Leftrightarrow f(2t)\in V_{j+1}$ | MRA self-similarity | MRA framework |
| 9 | $\sum_n \vert h[n]\vert^2 = 2$ | Energy conservation of $h$ | Filter design validity |
| 10 | $\sum_n h[n] = \sqrt{2}$ | Low-pass DC condition | Approximation property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, all absolute-value bars use the LaTeX `\vert` command (rendered as `$\vert \cdot \vert$`) so the table syntax is preserved. In your KTU answer scripts, you may freely use `|x|` notation.

## 2.6 Why Self-Similarity Matters in Biomedical Engineering

1. **Multi-scale feature extraction:** Self-similar wavelet bases naturally decompose signals into multiple resolution layers ŌĆö perfect for detecting both macro (e.g., heart-rate variability) and micro (e.g., R-peak notches) features.
2. **Compact representation:** Many biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are *approximately* self-similar; wavelet coefficients are sparse, enabling efficient compression and denoising.
3. **Translation invariance:** A QRS complex can occur anywhere on the time axis ŌĆö the translation property of the wavelet basis makes detection shift-invariant.
4. **Constant-Q analysis:** Because the relative bandwidth $\Delta f / f$ is preserved under scaling, wavelets are ideally suited for analyzing biomedical signals whose dominant frequencies change with time.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Energy Transformation Under Geometrical Scaling

**Goal:** Show that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(at) \vert^2 \, dt = \dfrac{1}{a} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau$.

**Step 1.** Let $\tau = at$. Then $d\tau = a\,dt$, so $dt = d\tau / a$.

**Step 2.** Substitute into the integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(at) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(\tau) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\tau}{a}$$

**Step 3.** Pull out the constant $1/a$:

$$= \frac{1}{a}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert f(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, to keep the energy of $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\psi((t-b)/a)$ equal to that of $\psi(t)$, the factor $a^{-1/2}$ is mandatory:

$$\Vert \psi_{a,b} \Vert_2^2 = \int \left\vert a^{-1/2}\psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) \right\vert^2 dt = a^{-1}\cdot a \int \vert\psi(\tau)\vert^2 d\tau = \Vert\psi\Vert_2^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **mathematical justification** for the $\sqrt{a}$ normalization in the wavelet atom. Without it, energy would *blow up* or *vanish* depending on scale.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö The Dilation Equation from MRA Self-Similarity

**Goal:** Derive $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n]\phi(2t - n)$ from the MRA nesting property.

**Step 1.** By the MRA property, $V_0 \subset V_1$, and $\{\phi(t-k)\}_{k\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is a basis for $V_0$ while $\{\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t - n)\}_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}$ is a basis for $V_1$.

**Step 2.** Hence $\phi(t)$, which lies in $V_0 \subset V_1$, can be expanded in the $V_1$ basis:

$$\phi(t) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 3.** Recognizing that $h[n] = \langle\phi(t),\sqrt{2}\,\phi(2t-n)\rangle$ are the projection coefficients (the *scaling filter*), we obtain:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} h[n]\,\phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 4.** Apply the Fourier transform to both sides:

$$\Phi(\omega) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n} h[n]\int \phi(2t-n)e^{-j\omega t}dt$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $\tau = 2t - n \Rightarrow t = (\tau + n)/2,\; dt = d\tau/2$:

$$= \sqrt{2}\sum_n h[n] \frac{1}{2}\int \phi(\tau) e^{-j\omega(\tau+n)/2}d\tau$$

**Step 6.** Group terms:

$$= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(\sum_n h[n]e^{-j\omega n/2}\right)\int \phi(\tau) e^{-j\omega\tau/2}d\tau$$

**Step 7.** Identify the discrete-time Fourier transform $H(\omega/2) = \sum_n h[n]e^{-j\omega n/2}$ and the integral as $\Phi(\omega/2)$:

$$\Phi(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}H\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)\Phi\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 8.** Iterating $J$ times (self-similarity under repeated scaling):

$$\Phi(\omega) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^J \prod_{m=1}^{J} H\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2^m}\right)\Phi\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2^J}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $J \to \infty$, $\Phi(\omega/2^J) \to \Phi(0) = 1$ (for a properly normalized $\phi$), giving the **infinite product** formula that completely characterizes the scaling function from its filter $H(\omega)$. This is the deepest expression of self-similarity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Orthogonality of the QMF Condition

**Goal:** Show that $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ makes $\{\phi_{0,k},\psi_{j,k}\}$ mutually orthogonal.

**Step 1.** For an orthogonal MRA, the wavelet filter $G(\omega)$ and scaling filter $H(\omega)$ must satisfy the **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) condition**:

$$H(\omega)G^*(\omega) + H(\omega+\pi)G^*(\omega+\pi) = 0$$

**Step 2.** Substituting $G(\omega) = -e^{-j\omega}H^*(\omega+\pi)$ (one valid choice), we test the LHS:

$$H(\omega)\!\left(-e^{j\omega}H(\omega+\pi)\right) + H(\omega+\pi)\!\left(-e^{j(\omega+\pi)}H(\omega+2\pi)\right)$$

**Step 3.** Simplify using $H(\omega+2\pi) = H(\omega)$ (periodicity) and $e^{j\pi} = -1$:

$$-e^{j\omega}H(\omega)H(\omega+\pi) - e^{j\omega}(-1)H(\omega+\pi)H(\omega)$$

$$= -e^{j\omega}H(\omega)H(\omega+\pi) + e^{j\omega}H(\omega)H(\omega+\pi) = 0 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4.** In the time domain, the condition $G(\omega) = -e^{-j\omega}H^*(\omega+\pi)$ corresponds to:

$$g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$$

which is exactly the **QMF relation** between scaling and wavelet filters.

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Haar Wavelet Self-Similarity

The **Haar scaling function** is $\phi(t) = 1$ for $t \in [0,1)$, and 0 elsewhere. Its filter coefficients are $h[0] = h[1] = 1/\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 1.** Verify the dilation equation:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum_{n=0}^{1} h[n]\phi(2t - n) = \sqrt{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\phi(2t) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\phi(2t-1)\right]$$

**Step 2.** For $t \in [0, 1/2)$: $\phi(2t) = 1$, $\phi(2t-1) = 0$, so RHS $= 1$ Ō£ō
For $t \in [1/2, 1)$: $\phi(2t) = 0$, $\phi(2t-1) = 1$, so RHS $= 1$ Ō£ō
Otherwise: both terms are 0, so RHS $= 0$ Ō£ō

**Step 3.** Self-similar scaling property: $\phi(t)$ at scale $2^j$ is supported on an interval of length $2^{-j}$ and has height 1, so the **area** (and thus DC content) is preserved ŌĆö a hallmark of self-similarity.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which Concept

| Scenario | Concept Applied | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Designing a filter bank for ECG denoising | Dilation equation | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h[n]\phi(2t-n)$ |
| Computing time-frequency map of EEG | Continuous self-similar atoms | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = a^{-1/2}\psi((t-b)/a)$ |
| Verifying filter orthogonality | QMF relation | $g[n] = (-1)^n h[1-n]$ |
| Implementing 1-level DWT | Dyadic scaling & shift | $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\psi(2^j t - k)$ |
| Characterizing fractal ECG dynamics | Iterative self-similarity | $\Phi(\omega) = \prod_m 2^{-1/2} H(\omega/2^m)$ |
