## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define self-similarity in the context of biomedical signal processing. Distinguish between deterministic and statistical self-similarity.**

**Model Answer**:
A signal $x(t)$ is said to be **self-similar** if a scaling of the time axis by a factor $a$ results in a statistically equivalent version of the original signal after appropriate amplitude rescaling. Mathematically: $x(at) = b^{H} x(t)$.

- **Deterministic self-similarity**: The signal looks *exactly* the same at every scale (e.g., Weierstrass function, Koch curve). Mathematically strict and rarely seen in biology.
- **Statistical self-similarity**: Only the *statistical properties* (mean, variance, PSD shape) are preserved across scales. This is the regime relevant to **ECG, EEG, and HRV** signals.

*Course Outcome mapped*: CO3 ŌĆö Apply advanced signal-processing concepts to biomedical data.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Hurst exponent? Explain its significance in characterizing the scaling behavior of biomedical signals.**

**Model Answer**:
The **Hurst exponent** $H \in (0, 1)$ is the parameter that quantifies the degree of self-similarity in a stochastic process. It is defined via the scaling relation:

$$\text{Var}[X(at)] = a^{2H} \text{Var}[X(t)]$$

- $H = 0.5$: uncorrelated (white noise).
- $H > 0.5$: **persistent** (long-range positive correlations; healthy fractal physiology).
- $H < 0.5$: **anti-persistent** (mean-reverting; loss of complexity, often pathological).

In biomedical applications, $H$ serves as a **single-scalar biomarker** distinguishing healthy from diseased states. For instance, healthy HRV exhibits $H \approx 0.7$, while congestive heart failure patients show significantly reduced $H$.

*Course Outcome mapped*: CO3, CO5 ŌĆö Analyze and interpret biomedical signals using advanced metrics.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the power spectral density exponent $\beta$ and the Hurst exponent $H$ for a stationary self-similar process with stationary increments (fractional Gaussian noise). Clearly state all assumptions used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 10-minute HRV recording of a healthy subject is analyzed using Detrended Fluctuation Analysis. The fluctuation function $F(n)$ yields the following data:

| Block size $n$ (beats) | 4 | 8 | 16 | 32 | 64 | 128 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F(n)$ | 1.42 | 2.18 | 3.32 | 5.05 | 7.72 | 11.74 |

Compute the Hurst exponent $H$ from the logŌĆōlog regression of $F(n)$ vs $n$. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution**:

**(a)** Derivation as in Section 3.1, Steps 1ŌĆō6:

We begin from the self-similarity axiom of fractional Gaussian noise (fGn), defined as the increment process of fractional Brownian motion (fBm) with Hurst exponent $H$. The autocorrelation of fGn is:

$$R_{\text{fGn}}(\tau) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2} \left[ \vert \tau+1 \vert^{2H} - 2\vert \tau \vert^{2H} + \vert \tau-1 \vert^{2H} \right]$$

**Assumption 1**: Wide-sense stationarity (fGn is stationary; fBm is not).

**Assumption 2**: $0 < H < 1$, $H \neq 0.5$ (to avoid degenerate cases).

Apply the WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem:

$$S_{\text{fGn}}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{\text{fGn}}(\tau)\}$$

Using the Fourier pair $\mathcal{F}\{\vert t \vert^{2H}\} \propto \vert f \vert^{-(2H+1)}$ and applying the second-difference asymptotic expansion for large $\vert \tau \vert$:

$$R_{\text{fGn}}(\tau) \sim \sigma^{2} H(2H-1) \vert \tau \vert^{2H-2}$$

Taking the Fourier transform of this asymptotic form yields:

$$S_{\text{fGn}}(f) \propto \frac{1}{\vert f \vert^{2H-1}}$$

Therefore, identifying the spectral exponent:

$$\boxed{\beta = 2H - 1}$$

This is the celebrated **$1/f^{\beta}$ power-law** governing all fGn-type self-similar processes.

**(b)** Computation of $H$ from the DFA data:

Step 1 ŌĆö Form the logŌĆōlog table:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(n): & \quad 0.602, \; 0.903, \; 1.204, \; 1.505, \; 1.806, \; 2.107 \\
\log_{10}(F): & \quad 0.152, \; 0.338, \; 0.521, \; 0.703, \; 0.888, \; 1.070
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the linear regression slope. Using the standard least-squares formula $\alpha = \dfrac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{n \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}}$:

With $n = 6$, $\sum x = 8.127$, $\sum y = 3.672$, $\sum x^{2} = 12.652$, $\sum xy = 5.987$:

$$\alpha = \frac{6(5.987) - (8.127)(3.672)}{6(12.652) - (8.127)^{2}} = \frac{35.922 - 29.842}{75.912 - 66.048} = \frac{6.080}{9.864} \approx 0.616$$

Step 3 ŌĆö For DFA, the slope $\alpha$ equals the Hurst exponent $H$:

$$\boxed{H \approx 0.62}$$

**Interpretation**: This value lies in the persistent range ($H > 0.5$), consistent with healthy fractal heart-rate dynamics. (Healthy HRV typically has $H \in [0.65, 0.85]$; a slightly lower value may indicate sub-clinical autonomic dysregulation.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of long-range dependence in self-similar biomedical signals. Show mathematically that for $0.5 < H < 1$, the autocorrelation function of fractional Gaussian noise is non-summable. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The Higuchi fractal dimension (HFD) of a 5-minute EEG segment from an epileptic patient is computed as $D = 1.18$, while a healthy control subject shows $D = 1.42$. Compute the corresponding Hurst exponents for both cases and interpret the clinical significance. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution**:

**(a)** Long-range dependence (LRD):

A stationary process exhibits **long-range dependence** if its autocorrelation function $R(k)$ decays so slowly that:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} R(k) = \infty$$

For fGn with $H \in (0.5, 1)$:

$$R_{\text{fGn}}(k) \sim H(2H-1)\sigma^{2} \vert k \vert^{2H-2}$$

The summability test:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \vert k \vert^{2H-2} \quad \text{converges iff} \quad 2H - 2 < -1 \iff H < 0.5$$

For $H > 0.5$, the exponent $2H - 2 \in (-1, 0)$, so the sum **diverges**. Therefore, fGn with $H > 0.5$ exhibits **LRD**. This non-summability is the mathematical signature distinguishing fractal biomedical signals from short-range-dependent (Markovian) processes, and is the theoretical basis for using $H$ as a clinical biomarker.

**(b)** Hurst exponent from fractal dimension:

Using $D = 2 - H \Rightarrow H = 2 - D$:

- **Epileptic patient**: $H_{\text{epi}} = 2 - 1.18 = 0.82$
- **Healthy control**: $H_{\text{ctrl}} = 2 - 1.42 = 0.58$

**Clinical interpretation**:
The epileptic EEG shows **high persistence** ($H = 0.82$), indicating an overly regular, predictable signal ŌĆö consistent with the hyper-synchronized neural activity during pre-ictal/ictal states. The healthy control shows moderate persistence ($H = 0.58$) characteristic of normal, complex brain dynamics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. **Do not** confuse $H$ with the *spectral exponent* $\beta$ in the final answer. The relation is $\beta = 2H - 1$ for fGn, not $\beta = H$.
> 2. When computing $D$ from $H$ (or vice versa), always use $D = 2 - H$ for **1-D time series**. The formula $D = 1 - H$ is for 2-D surfaces and is a common error.
> 3. **Show the units in the logŌĆōlog regression**: explicitly state that the slope is dimensionless and the intercept carries the amplitude units.
> 4. **Distinguish** $H$ for fBm (non-stationary, $H \in (0,1)$) from $H$ for fGn (stationary increments, $H \in (0,1)$). Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Self-similarity** means statistical properties are preserved under combined timeŌĆōamplitude scaling: $x(at) = b^{H} x(t)$.
- [ ] **Deterministic** self-similarity is exact (mathematical fractals); **statistical** self-similarity is distributional (biomedical signals).
- [ ] The **Hurst exponent** $H \in (0,1)$ is the master scaling parameter.
- [ ] $H > 0.5$ ŌćÆ **persistent** (healthy fractal physiology); $H < 0.5$ ŌćÆ **anti-persistent** (loss of complexity); $H = 0.5$ ŌćÆ white noise.
- [ ] **Power-law PSD**: $S_{X}(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$ with $\beta = 2H - 1$ for fGn and $\beta = 2H + 1$ for fBm.
- [ ] **Variance scaling**: $\text{Var}[X(at)] = a^{2H} \text{Var}[X(t)]$.
- [ ] **Fractal dimension (1-D signal)**: $D = 2 - H$.
- [ ] **Autocorrelation** of fGn: $R(k) \propto \vert k \vert^{2H-2}$ ŌĆö non-summable for $H > 0.5$ (long-range dependence).
- [ ] **Practical estimators**: R/S analysis, DFA, wavelet-based, Higuchi FD, Petrosian FD, Katz FD.
- [ ] **Biomedical biomarkers**: HRV ($H \approx 0.7$ healthy, $\downarrow$ in CHF); EEG ($H \uparrow$ in epilepsy); aging ($\downarrow$ complexity).
- [ ] For KTU 14-mark problems, always **derive** the $\beta$ŌĆō$H$ relation from first principles and **show all logŌĆōlog regression steps**.
- [ ] Always specify whether $H$ refers to **fBm** (non-stationary) or **fGn** (stationary) ŌĆö examiners reward this precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Theoretical Signals Having Statistical Similarity** are mathematically synthesized, non-physiological waveforms whose first-order (e.g., mean, variance, higher moments) and second-order (e.g., autocorrelation, power spectral density) statistical descriptors are deliberately designed to match those of an authentic biomedical signal. Unlike real biosignals (acquired through electrodes or sensors from a living system), these signals are generated from stochastic, parametric, or deterministic-stochastic hybrid models. They are essential tools in **algorithm benchmarking**, **Monte-Carlo simulation**, **receiver-operating-characteristic (ROC) analysis**, and **closed-loop validation** of biomedical signal-processing pipelines. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic bridges Module 2 (Classical Biomedical Signal Acquisition) with Module 3 (Statistical & Stochastic Modeling) and Module 4 (Modern Adaptive Filtering), since every adaptive filter, denoiser, or detector must be tested on a *known* signal before being trusted on a *real* patient record. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Decoding** > - **Theoretical Signal** ŌåÆ Mathematically constructed; no patient was ever involved. > - **Statistical Similarity** ŌåÆ Moments, PDFs, and PSDs closely match the target biological signal. > - **Stationarity Assumption** ŌåÆ Required for most classical estimators; many biomedical signals are quasi-stationary, so the assumption holds only over short windows. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Digital Twin" of a Biosignal Imagine you are a cardiologist who has developed a brand-new algorithm that detects a subtle arrhythmia from an ECG. Before testing on real patients (ethical, legal, and statistical risks), you want a *known* ECG that contains the arrhythmia, embedded in realistic noise, with a ground-truth label. This is exactly what a **theoretical signal with statistical similarity** provides ŌĆö it is a **digital twin** of a real ECG, EEG, or EMG, created from a mathematical model whose parameters are tuned so that the *statistical fingerprint* (histogram, autocorrelation, spectrum) of the synthetic signal is indistinguishable from a real one. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why not just use real signals?** Real biomedical signals are: > 1. **Non-repeatable** (you cannot record the *exact* same heartbeat twice). > 2. **Unlabelled** (the ground truth is often unknown without invasive procedures). > 3. **Noisy in unknown ways** (mixing real signal with *known* noise is impossible). > 4. **Scarce** (pathological recordings are rare, especially for novel diseases). > > Theoretical signals solve all four problems simultaneously. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are universally used to quantify "statistical similarity" between a theoretical and real signal: - **Mean** ($\mu$): $0$ (most biosignals are AC-coupled). - **Standard Deviation** ($\sigma$): typically $0.1$ to $1.0$ mV for ECG, $10$ to $100$ $\mu$V for EEG. - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**: $0$ to $40$ **dB** for clinical recordings. - **Sampling Frequency** ($F_s$): $360$ Hz (ECG standard), $500$ Hz (EEG standard), $1000$ Hz (EMG standard). - **Probability Density Function (PDF)**: Gaussian, Laplacian, or Generalized Gaussian depending on the biosignal. - **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**: Band-limited (e.g., $0.5$ to $40$ Hz for ECG). > [!NOTE] > A theoretical signal is said to be "statistically similar" to a real biosignal when its first four moments (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis) and its PSD shape are within an acceptable tolerance (commonly $\le 5\%$ relative error) of the target. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Classification of Theoretical Signals Theoretical signals with statistical similarity are classified along three orthogonal axes: #### A. By Determinism 1. **Purely Deterministic** (e.g., sum of sinusoids ŌĆö McSharryŌĆōClifford ECG model). 2. **Purely Stochastic** (e.g., filtered white noise mimicking EMG). 3. **Hybrid DeterministicŌĆōStochastic** (deterministic morphology + stochastic noise; the most common in practice). #### B. By Stationarity 1. **Strict-Sense Stationary (SSS)**: All joint PDFs time-invariant. 2. **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)**: Only mean and autocorrelation time-invariant. 3. **Cyclostationary**: Statistics periodic in time (e.g., ECG is cyclostationary with heart-period $T$). 4. **Non-stationary**: Statistics vary with time (most EEG during seizures). #### C. By Underlying Statistical Distribution - **Gaussian** (central limit, white noise baseline). - **Laplacian** (sharp-peaked, matches EEG). - **Generalized Gaussian** $\mathcal{G}(\alpha, \beta)$ ŌĆö family controlled by shape parameter $\alpha$. - **Rayleigh** (envelope of narrowband signals). - **Gamma** (heart-rate variability distributions). ### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Statistical Similarity To declare a theoretical signal "statistically similar" to a real one, three pillars must be matched: | Pillar | Mathematical Object | Matching Strategy | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Pillar 1 ŌĆö Time Domain** | PDF of amplitudes, histogram, moments | Match shape (Gaussian, Laplacian, etc.) and parameters ($\mu$, $\sigma$, $\gamma_3$, $\gamma_4$) | | **Pillar 2 ŌĆö Frequency Domain** | Power Spectral Density $S_x(f)$ | Match peak frequencies, bandwidth, and roll-off (e.g., $-20$ dB/dec) | | **Pillar 3 ŌĆö Time-Frequency Domain** | Spectrogram, WignerŌĆōVille, wavelet scalogram | Match transient energy distribution and modulation patterns | ### 2.3 White Noise and Colored Noise ŌĆö The Building Blocks All theoretical biosignals are constructed by **shaping** (filtering) a fundamental random process. The most fundamental is **white Gaussian noise (WGN)**. **White Gaussian Noise (WGN):** - Samples are i.i.d. $\sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. - Autocorrelation $R_{ww}(\tau) = \sigma^2 \delta(\tau)$. - PSD $S_w(f) = \sigma^2$ (flat across all frequencies). - **Theoretical infinite bandwidth** ŌĆö in practice truncated by sampling to $F_s/2$. **Colored Noise (Shaped WGN):** Generated by passing WGN through a shaping filter $H(f)$. | Noise Type | Spectral Slope | Typical Use in Biomedical Modeling | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **White** | $0$ dB/octave | Baseline sensor noise floor | | **Pink** ($1/f$) | $-3$ dB/octave | EEG background activity, HRV long-term variability | | **Brown / Red** ($1/f^2$) | $-6$ dB/octave | Drift, baseline wander, integrated noise | | **Blue** ($f$) | $+3$ dB/octave | Differentiated white noise; rarely used | | **Violet** ($f^2$) | $+6$ dB/octave | Double-differentiated noise | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. **All variables are dimensionless unless otherwise stated.** | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Use | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **WGN Autocorrelation** | $R_w(\tau) = \sigma^2 \, \delta(\tau)$ | Impulse at zero lag, zero elsewhere | | 2 | **WGN PSD** | $S_w(f) = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \;\; \forall f$ | Two-sided PSD, $N_0$ in W/Hz | | 3 | **Ergodic Mean** | $\hat{\mu}_x = \displaystyle\lim_{N\to\infty}\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]$ | Time-average equals ensemble-average | | 4 | **Sample Variance** | $\hat{\sigma}_x^2 = \dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-\hat{\mu}_x)^2$ | Second central moment | | 5 | **Skewness** | $\gamma_3 = \dfrac{E[(x-\mu)^3]}{\sigma^3}$ | Asymmetry of PDF | | 6 | **Kurtosis** | $\gamma_4 = \dfrac{E[(x-\mu)^4]}{\sigma^4}$ | "Tailedness" of PDF | | 7 | **Autocorrelation (WSS)** | $R_x(\tau) = E[x(t)\,x(t+\tau)]$ | Second-order moment | | 8 | **PSD via WienerŌĆōKhinchin** | $S_x(f) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau)\,e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\,d\tau$ | Fourier transform of autocorrelation | | 9 | **AR($p$) Model** | $x[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + w[n]$ | $w[n]\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_w^2)$ | | 10 | **AR PSD** | $S_{AR}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\big\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s}\big\vert^{2}}$ | All-pole spectral model | | 11 | **MA($q$) Model** | $x[n] = w[n] + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k\, w[n-k]$ | All-zero spectral model | | 12 | **ARMA($p,q$) Model** | $x[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x[n-k] + w[n] + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k\, w[n-k]$ | Pole-zero model | | 13 | **McSharryŌĆōClifford ECG** | $z'(t) = 1 - \sqrt{x(t)^2 + y(t)^2}$ | Dynamical model for synthetic ECG | | 14 | **Gaussian PDF** | $p(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\tfrac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Symmetric, light-tailed | | 15 | **Laplacian PDF** | $p(x) = \dfrac{1}{2b} e^{-\tfrac{\vert x-\mu\vert}{b}}$ | Sharp-peaked, used for EEG | | 16 | **SNR (dB)** | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | $P = E[x^2]$ average power | | 17 | **KL Divergence** | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \displaystyle\int p(x)\log\dfrac{p(x)}{q(x)}\,dx$ | Asymmetric similarity measure | | 18 | **Wold Decomposition** | $x[n] = x_{\text{det}}[n] + x_{\text{stoch}}[n]$ | Any WSS process = deterministic + stochastic part | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **absolute value** $\vert x \vert$ in the Laplacian PDF is rendered as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX. **Never** use the keyboard pipe $\vert$ inside markdown tables ŌĆö it breaks the column delimiter. The same rule applies to the denominator of the AR PSD. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Theoretical signals with statistical similarity are the **backbone of evidence-based medical device certification**. International standards such as **IEC 60601-2-47** (ambulatory ECG), **IEC 60601-2-26** (EEG), and **FDA 510(k) Guidance for ECG algorithms** explicitly require performance testing on synthetic signals with known ground truth *before* clinical trials. Without such signals, regulatory approval of new QRS detectors, arrhythmia classifiers, or seizure predictors would be practically impossible. > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 Scheme, the expected student outcome for this topic is **CO3 ŌĆö *Apply statistical modeling techniques to synthesize and analyze theoretical biomedical signals for algorithm validation.*** ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Spectral Density of an AR($p$) Process The **autoregressive (AR) model** is the single most important parametric model for theoretical biomedical signals. It is the workhorse behind synthetic EEG, HRV, and EMG generation. **Given:** An AR($p$) process driven by WGN $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_w^2)$: $$ x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] + w[n] $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate the innovation term.** Move the deterministic part to the LHS: $$ x[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x[n-k] = w[n] $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Express in convolution form.** Recognize that the LHS is a *linear filter* applied to $x[n]$, with impulse response $h[k]$ given by: $$ h[k] = \begin{cases} 1, & k = 0 \\ -a_k, & 1 \le k \le p \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ Therefore: $$ \sum_{k=0}^{p} h[k]\, x[n-k] = w[n] $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Take the discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) of both sides.** Use the time-shift property $\text{DTFT}\{x[n-k]\} = X(f)\,e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s}$: $$ H(f) \, X(f) = W(f) $$ where the transfer function of the AR filter is: $$ H(f) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $X(f)$.** Algebraic inversion: $$ X(f) = \frac{W(f)}{H(f)} = \frac{W(f)}{1 - \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s}} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the PSD using $|X(f)|^2$ and the WGN PSD.** Since $S_W(f) = \sigma_w^2$ (constant), and using the spectral property $S_X(f) = \vert X(f)/W(f) \vert^{2} \, S_W(f)$: $$ S_X(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 - \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s}\right\vert^{2}} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation.** The AR model produces a spectrum that is **concentrated near the poles** of $H(z)$ in the $z$-plane. When poles lie close to the unit circle, sharp spectral peaks emerge ŌĆö exactly mimicking narrowband physiological rhythms such as the alpha rhythm of EEG ($8$ŌĆō$13$ Hz). > [!NOTE] > The denominator $\left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2$ is the **squared magnitude** of a complex number. When written in LaTeX inside a markdown table, render it as `\left\vert \cdot \right\vert` or `\vert \cdot \vert` ŌĆö never as a raw pipe. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: McSharryŌĆōClifford Synthetic ECG Model This is the most celebrated **deterministic-dynamical** model for theoretical ECG signals, originally proposed by McSharry and Clifford (2003) and adopted as a standard benchmark by PhysioNet. **Given:** A 2-D dynamical system in the $(x, y)$-plane representing the trajectory of the heart's electrical vector: $$ \dot{x} = \alpha \, x - \omega \, y $$ $$ \dot{y} = \alpha \, y + \omega \, x $$ with $\alpha = 1 - \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ where $T$ is the instantaneous RR interval (heart period). **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the radius of the limit cycle.** At equilibrium, $\dot{r} = 0$ implies $r^2 = x^2 + y^2 = 1$, so the trajectory traces a **unit circle** in the phase plane. **Step 2 ŌĆö Parameterize a Gaussian function for each waveform peak.** The P-wave, QRS complex, and T-wave are modeled as Gaussians of varying amplitude, width, and center time: $$ \sum_j a_j \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\theta - \theta_j)^2}{2 b_j^2}\right) $$ where $\theta$ is the angular position on the unit circle (linearly related to time $t$ via $t = T\theta/2\pi$), $a_j$ is the amplitude, $\theta_j$ is the angular center, and $b_j$ is the angular width of the $j$-th wave ($j \in \{P, Q, R, S, T\}$). **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the radial displacement.** The radial deviation $z(t)$ from the unit circle produces the ECG: $$ z(t) = -\sum_{j \in \{P,Q,R,S,T\}} a_j \, \Delta\theta_j \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta\theta_j^2}{2 b_j^2}\right) $$ where $\Delta\theta_j = (\theta - \theta_j) \mod 2\pi$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Add stochastic noise to achieve statistical similarity.** Finally, additive WGN is added to obtain the realistic ECG: $$ x_{\text{ECG}}(t) = z(t) + w(t), \quad w(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2) $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Match statistical descriptors.** By tuning the seven parameters $(a_P, a_Q, a_R, a_S, a_T, b_P, \ldots, b_T, T, \sigma_w)$, the first four moments and the PSD of $x_{\text{ECG}}(t)$ can be matched to a target real ECG within $\le 3\%$ relative error. > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU examinations, you may be asked to **derive the radial deviation** $z(t)$ or to **list the parameters** that must be tuned to match a target ECG. Memorize the structure: *unit circle trajectory + 5 Gaussians + additive noise*. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: KL Divergence Between Two Gaussian PDFs The **KullbackŌĆōLeibler (KL) divergence** is the canonical measure of statistical dissimilarity between two PDFs. Although it is asymmetric, it is widely used in biomedical signal validation. **Given:** Two zero-mean Gaussians, $p(x) = \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_1^2)$ and $q(x) = \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_2^2)$: $$ p(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma_1 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right), \quad q(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma_2 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{2\sigma_2^2}\right) $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the definition.** $$ D_{KL}(p \Vert q) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} p(x) \, \log\!\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right) dx $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the log ratio.** Using $\log(p/q) = \log p - \log q$: $$ \log\!\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right) = \log\!\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\right) + \frac{x^2}{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_2^2} - \frac{1}{\sigma_1^2}\right) $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into the integral.** $$ D_{KL}(p \Vert q) = \int p(x) \log\!\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\right) dx + \int p(x) \frac{x^2}{2}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sigma_2^2} - \frac{1}{\sigma_1^2}\right) dx $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the two integrals separately.** The first integral yields $\log(\sigma_2/\sigma_1)$ since $\int p(x) dx = 1$. The second integral equals $\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_1^2 \cdot (1/\sigma_2^2 - 1/\sigma_1^2)$ because $E_p[x^2] = \sigma_1^2$: $$ D_{KL}(p \Vert q) = \log\!\left(\frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1}\right) + \frac{\sigma_1^2}{2\sigma_2^2} - \frac{1}{2} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Final form (after combining with natural log convention).** Multiplying out: $$ D_{KL}\!\left(\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_1^2) \,\Big\Vert\, \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_2^2)\right) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} - 1 - \log\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}\right) $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Interpretation.** $D_{KL} = 0$ iff $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. As $\sigma_1 / \sigma_2 \to \infty$, $D_{KL} \to \infty$, indicating maximum statistical dissimilarity. This is used in KTU labs to **quantify** how close a theoretical signal's PDF is to a real one. > [!NOTE] > Always state the unit of $D_{KL}$ as **nats** (natural log) or **bits** (log base 2). In KTU 2024, the default is **nats**. ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 End-to-End Functional Block Diagram of Theoretical Signal Generation The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional architecture for synthesizing and validating a theoretical signal with statistical similarity to a real biosignal. It models the *data flow* ŌĆö physical drawings of, e.g., the inside of an ECG generator are not required for this topic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ParamSpec
Parameter Specification] --> B[WhiteNoiseGen
WGN Generator] A --> C[ShapeFilter
Spectral Shaping Filter] A --> D[Modulator
Modulation Block] B --> C C --> E[PathOne
Stationary Component] D --> F[PathTwo
Non-stationary Component] E --> G[Adder
Combine] F --> G A --> H[NoiseModel
Additive Noise] H --> G G --> I[TheoSignal
Theoretical Signal Output] I --> J[StatAnalyzer
Statistical Analyzer] K[RealSignal
Real Biosignal] --> J J --> L[SimScore
Similarity Metric] L --> M[Decision
Pass or Reject] A --> N[ParamTuner
Parameter Tuner] M -->|Reject| N N --> A M -->|Pass| O[ApprovedSignal
Validated Theoretical Signal] style A fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333 style B fill:#A6F1A6,stroke:#333 style C fill:#A6F1A6,stroke:#333 style D fill:#A6D8F1,stroke:#333 style H fill:#F4B6B6,stroke:#333 style I fill:#E0C3FC,stroke:#333 style J fill:#FFD9A6,stroke:#333 style L fill:#FFD9A6,stroke:#333 style M fill:#F4B6B6,stroke:#333 style O fill:#B6F4D8,stroke:#333 style N fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333 style K fill:#E0C3FC,stroke:#333 ``` **Block-by-Block Explanation:** 1. **ParamSpec (Parameter Specification):** Holds the target statistical descriptors ŌĆö desired PDF, PSD peaks, sampling rate, mean heart period $T$, etc. 2. **WhiteNoiseGen (WGN Generator):** Produces the fundamental i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ driving sequence. 3. **ShapeFilter (Spectral Shaping Filter):** An IIR/FIR filter $H(f)$ that converts WGN into colored noise with the desired PSD. For AR($p$) models, this filter is $1/A(z)$ where $A(z) = 1 - \sum a_k z^{-k}$. 4. **Modulator (Modulation Block):** Introduces cyclostationarity by amplitude-modulating the colored noise with a periodic envelope derived from the McSharryŌĆōClifford waveform generator. 5. **PathOne / PathTwo (Stationary / Non-stationary Branches):** Decomposition of the signal into a wide-sense stationary background and a transient non-stationary event (e.g., a QRS complex). 6. **NoiseModel (Additive Noise):** Adds a calibrated WGN or pink-noise component to match the real signal's SNR. 7. **Adder (Combine):** Sums all branches to form the composite theoretical signal. 8. **TheoSignal (Theoretical Signal Output):** The final synthetic waveform at the test point. 9. **StatAnalyzer (Statistical Analyzer):** Computes PDF, PSD, autocorrelation, skewness, and kurtosis of both signals. 10. **SimScore (Similarity Metric):** Evaluates $D_{KL}$, correlation coefficient $\rho$, and mean-square-error (MSE). 11. **Decision (Pass or Reject):** Compares the similarity metric against a threshold $\epsilon$ (commonly $D_{KL} \le 0.1$ nats). 12. **ParamTuner (Parameter Tuner):** Adjusts parameters and feeds back if rejected ŌĆö closes the validation loop. ### 4.2 Comparative Architecture: Real vs. Theoretical Signal Path ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RealPath R1[Patient] --> R2[Sensor Electrode] R2 --> R3[Analog Front End] R3 --> R4[ADC Sampler] R4 --> R5[Real Biosignal] end subgraph TheoPath T1[Math Model] --> T2[Numerical Solver] T2 --> T3[Statistical Shaping] T3 --> T4[Noise Injection] T4 --> T5[Theoretical Signal] end R5 --> COMP[Statistical Comparison] T5 --> COMP COMP --> OUT[Validated Synthetic Signal] style R1 fill:#E0C3FC,stroke:#333 style T1 fill:#A6F1A6,stroke:#333 style COMP fill:#FFD9A6,stroke:#333 style OUT fill:#B6F4D8,stroke:#333 ``` This side-by-side diagram emphasizes the **functional equivalence** between physiological acquisition and theoretical synthesis ŌĆö the comparison block at the center is what guarantees "statistical similarity." ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1(a)]** **Q1(a).** Define a "theoretical signal having statistical similarity" to a real biomedical signal. List any two first-order and two second-order statistical descriptors used to characterize such similarity. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A theoretical signal with statistical similarity is a mathematically constructed waveform whose statistical properties (PDF, PSD, moments) match those of a real biomedical signal within a defined tolerance. *First-order descriptors (2 marks, 1 mark each):* 1. **Mean** $\mu = E[x]$ ŌĆö measures DC offset; typically $0$ for AC-coupled biosignals. 2. **Variance** $\sigma^2 = E[(x-\mu)^2]$ ŌĆö measures signal power. *Second-order descriptors (1 mark for both):* 3. **Autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau) = E[x(t)x(t+\tau)]$ ŌĆö captures periodicity. 4. **Power spectral density** $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ ŌĆö captures frequency content. --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Q1(b)]** **Q1(b).** Differentiate between **wide-sense stationarity (WSS)** and **strict-sense stationarity (SSS)**. State one biomedical example of a non-stationary signal. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | SSS | WSS | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Condition | All joint PDFs invariant under time shift | Only mean and autocorrelation invariant | | Strength | Stronger | Weaker | | Implication | Implies WSS | Does not imply SSS | *Example of non-stationary biomedical signal (1 mark):* **EEG during an epileptic seizure** ŌĆö its PSD evolves rapidly in time as ictal rhythms emerge and propagate. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Q2, 14 Marks]** **Q2(a). [7 Marks]** With neat mathematical formulation, explain the **McSharryŌĆōClifford dynamical model** for the synthesis of a theoretical ECG signal. List all parameters that must be tuned to achieve statistical similarity to a real ECG. **Model Solution:** The McSharryŌĆōClifford model (2003) is a 2-D dynamical system representing the trajectory of the cardiac electrical vector in the $(x, y)$-plane. It produces a synthetic ECG whose morphology is mathematically controlled by a small set of parameters, enabling perfect ground-truth labelling. The governing differential equations are: $$ \dot{x} = \alpha x - \omega y, \qquad \dot{y} = \alpha y + \omega x $$ where $\alpha = 1 - \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and $\omega = 2\pi / T$ is the angular frequency, with $T$ being the instantaneous RR interval. At equilibrium, the radius $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ tends to $1$, so the trajectory traces a unit circle. The synthetic ECG is generated as the **radial deviation** from this circle: $$ z(t) = -\sum_{j \in \{P, Q, R, S, T\}} a_j \, \Delta\theta_j \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta\theta_j^2}{2 b_j^2}\right) $$ where: - $a_j$ ŌåÆ amplitude of the $j$-th Gaussian peak. - $b_j$ ŌåÆ angular width of the $j$-th peak. - $\Delta\theta_j = (\theta - \theta_j) \mod 2\pi$ ŌåÆ angular displacement. - $\theta = 2\pi t / T$ ŌåÆ instantaneous angle. **Parameters to be tuned for statistical similarity (3 marks ŌĆö list at least 6):** 1. RR interval $T$ (typically $0.6$ to $1.2$ s). 2. Five amplitudes $(a_P, a_Q, a_R, a_S, a_T)$ ŌĆö set R-wave dominance. 3. Five angular widths $(b_P, b_Q, b_R, b_S, b_T)$ ŌĆö set sharpness. 4. Five angular centers $(\theta_P, \theta_Q, \theta_R, \theta_S, \theta_T)$ ŌĆö set timing. 5. Noise standard deviation $\sigma_w$ ŌĆö match SNR. 6. Sampling rate $F_s$ ŌĆö match real ECG acquisition (typically $360$ Hz). *(3 marks for the dynamical equations, 1 mark for the radial deviation, 3 marks for the parameter list.)* --- **Q2(b). [7 Marks]** Derive the **power spectral density of an AR(2) process** $x[n] = a_1 x[n-1] + a_2 x[n-2] + w[n]$, where $w[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_w^2)$. Identify the location of spectral peaks in terms of the AR parameters. **Model Solution:** Starting from the AR($2$) difference equation: $$ x[n] - a_1 x[n-1] - a_2 x[n-2] = w[n] $$ Taking the discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) of both sides, using the linearity and time-shift properties: $$ X(f) \left[1 - a_1 e^{-j 2\pi f / F_s} - a_2 e^{-j 4\pi f / F_s}\right] = W(f) $$ Solving for $X(f)$: $$ X(f) = \frac{W(f)}{1 - a_1 e^{-j 2\pi f / F_s} - a_2 e^{-j 4\pi f / F_s}} $$ The PSD is the magnitude-squared, scaled by the input WGN PSD $S_W(f) = \sigma_w^2$: $$ S_{AR(2)}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 - a_1 e^{-j 2\pi f / F_s} - a_2 e^{-j 4\pi f / F_s}\right\vert^{2}} $$ **Spectral Peak Location (3 marks):** The denominator $|H(f)|^2$ is minimized when the complex exponential term in the denominator aligns constructively with the real part. In the $z$-plane, the AR filter $H(z) = 1 - a_1 z^{-1} - a_2 z^{-2}$ has poles at: $$ z_{1,2} = \frac{a_1 \pm \sqrt{a_1^2 + 4 a_2}}{2} $$ For **complex-conjugate poles** (needed for oscillatory peaks), the discriminant $a_1^2 + 4a_2 < 0$, i.e., $a_2 < -a_1^2 / 4$. The peak frequency is: $$ f_{\text{peak}} = \frac{F_s}{2\pi} \arccos\!\left(\frac{-a_1}{2\sqrt{-a_2}}\right) $$ A larger $|a_2|$ (poles closer to the unit circle) produces a sharper, taller peak ŌĆö used to mimic, e.g., the alpha rhythm of EEG. --- > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Q3, 14 Marks ŌĆö INTERNAL CHOICE]** **ALTERNATIVE ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks):** **Q3A(a). [7 Marks]** Compare **white**, **pink**, and **brown** noise in terms of PSD slope and biomedical application. Generate the theoretical PSD plot of each (use a sketch with axes). **Q3A(b). [7 Marks]** A biomedical signal $x(t)$ is modelled as $x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, where $s(t)$ is the clean biosignal and $n(t)$ is WGN with PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-6}$ W/Hz. If the signal power is $P_s = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ W, compute the SNR in dB. **Model Solution 3A(b):** Given $P_s = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ W and $N_0/2 = 10^{-6}$ W/Hz. For a baseband signal of bandwidth $B$ (assume $B = 40$ Hz for ECG): **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute noise power.** $$ P_n = \int_{-B}^{B} \frac{N_0}{2} \, df = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute linear SNR.** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{lin}} = \frac{P_s}{P_n} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 5 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to dB.** $$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(5) \approx 6.99 \text{ dB} \approx 7 \text{ dB} $$ --- **ALTERNATIVE ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks):** **Q3B(a). [7 Marks]** Derive the **KullbackŌĆōLeibler divergence** between two zero-mean Gaussian PDFs $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_1^2)$ and $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_2^2)$. Show that it is non-negative and zero iff $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. **Q3B(b). [7 Marks]** A theoretical ECG is generated with PDF parameters $(\mu_t, \sigma_t) = (0, 0.25)$ mV. A real ECG segment has $(\mu_r, \sigma_r) = (0.01, 0.27)$ mV. Compute the KL divergence and comment on whether the theoretical signal is "statistically similar" to the real one. **Model Solution 3B(b):** Using the derived formula: $$ D_{KL} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_t^2}{\sigma_r^2} - 1 - \log\frac{\sigma_t^2}{\sigma_r^2}\right) $$ Substituting $\sigma_t = 0.25$ and $\sigma_r = 0.27$: **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the ratio.** $$ \frac{\sigma_t^2}{\sigma_r^2} = \frac{0.0625}{0.0729} \approx 0.8573 $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the log term.** $$ \log(0.8573) \approx -0.1542 $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into the KL formula.** $$ D_{KL} = \frac{1}{2}(0.8573 - 1 - (-0.1542)) = \frac{1}{2}(0.0115) = 0.00575 \text{ nats} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Comment.** Since $D_{KL} \approx 0.006$ nats $\ll 0.1$ nats threshold, the theoretical ECG is **statistically similar** to the real one. The slight mean offset ($0.01$ mV) is negligible compared to $\sigma_r$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting to specify the sampling rate $F_s$ in the AR PSD formula.** The formula $\frac{\sigma_w^2}{|H(f)|^2}$ is incomplete without $F_s$ in the exponent. > 2. **Using $\log$ instead of $\log_{10}$ for dB conversion.** The SNR formula is $10 \log_{10}$, not $10 \ln$. Examiner deducts 1 mark for the wrong base. > 3. **Confusing cyclostationary with non-stationary.** ECG is *cyclostationary* (periodic statistics), not strictly non-stationary. > 4. **Not closing the parametric tuning loop.** When asked "how is similarity achieved?", students often list the metrics but forget the *iterative parameter adjustment* step. > 5. **Treating $D_{KL}$ as a true distance metric.** It is *not* symmetric: $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \Vert P)$. Examiners deduct marks for this oversight. > 6. **Mixing the Laplacian PDF with the Gaussian PDF** when describing EEG ŌĆö EEG background is typically Laplacian, not Gaussian. Wrong attribution costs 1ŌĆō2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 ESE):** > > - **Definition (memorize verbatim):** Theoretical signals with statistical similarity are mathematically constructed waveforms whose PDF, PSD, and moments match a real biosignal within a defined tolerance. > - **Three Pillars:** Time-domain PDF matching, Frequency-domain PSD matching, Time-frequency spectrogram matching. > - **The Big Four Statistical Descriptors:** Mean $\mu$, Variance $\sigma^2$, Skewness $\gamma_3$, Kurtosis $\gamma_4$. > - **WienerŌĆōKhinchin Theorem:** $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ ŌĆö always cite this when relating autocorrelation to PSD. > - **White Noise Constants:** $R_w(\tau) = \sigma^2 \delta(\tau)$, $S_w(f) = \sigma^2$ flat, i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ samples. > - **Colored Noise Slopes:** White $= 0$, Pink $= -3$ dB/octave, Brown $= -6$ dB/octave. > - **AR($p$) Formula:** $x[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x[n-k] + w[n]$. Poles determine spectral peaks; closer to unit circle = sharper peak. > - **AR PSD Form:** $S_{AR}(f) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k/F_s}\right\vert^{2}}$. > - **McSharryŌĆōClifford Model:** Unit-circle trajectory + 5 Gaussians (P, Q, R, S, T) + additive WGN. 5 amplitudes, 5 widths, 5 centers, RR interval $T$, noise $\sigma_w$. > - **SNR Formula:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. Use log base $10$ for dB. > - **KL Divergence Form:** $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \int p(x) \log[p(x)/q(x)] dx$. For Gaussians: $\frac{1}{2}(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2 - 1 - \log \sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2)$. > - **Stationarity Hierarchy:** SSS $\Rightarrow$ WSS $\Rightarrow$ Cyclostationary $\Rightarrow$ Non-stationary. > - **Ergodicity:** Time averages $\equiv$ ensemble averages. Required for parameter estimation from a single recording. > - **Wold Decomposition:** Any WSS process $=$ deterministic part $+$ stochastic part. > - **Real-World Tie-in:** IEC 60601-2-47, FDA 510(k) require synthetic signals for algorithm certification. > - **Sanity check on KL:** $D_{KL} \le 0.1$ nats $\Rightarrow$ "statistically similar." Above $0.5$ nats $\Rightarrow$ rejected. > - **CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO3** of PEBRT865 (Apply statistical modeling for signal synthesis and validation).

topics.title: Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Advanced Biomedical Signal Processing, **Measures of Statistical Similarity for Real Signals** refer to a family of quantitative metrics that mathematically characterize the degree of resemblance, correspondence, or pattern match between two real-valued discrete-time signals (or between a reference signal and a test signal). These measures operate in the statistical domain ŌĆö i.e., they exploit the **second-order statistics (moments)**, **distribution properties**, or **joint statistical dependencies** of the signals, rather than purely deterministic (point-by-point) comparisons.

For two real signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ of length $N$, a statistical similarity measure $S(x,y)$ is a scalar (or function of lag $\tau$) that satisfies the symmetry, boundedness, and scale-invariance properties appropriate to the application (template matching, QRS detection, EEG coherence, ECG beat classification, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The course explicitly requires students to derive, interpret, and apply at least the following statistical similarity measures for real biomedical signals: **(i) Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, **(ii) Cross-Correlation Coefficient**, **(iii) Normalized Cross-Correlation**, **(iv) Cosine Similarity**, **(v) Coherence Function**, and **(vi) Mutual Information (conceptual).**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Fingerprint Match Problem**
> Imagine you are a forensic expert comparing two fingerprints on a crime-scene glass. You do **not** overlay them pixel-by-pixel; instead, you look at *ridge-spacing statistics*, *minutiae density*, and *pattern flow angle*. Two fingerprints are "similar" if their statistical signatures ŌĆö not their exact ridges ŌĆö overlap strongly.
> 
> In the same way, statistical similarity measures compare **ECG templates** or **EEG epochs** by analyzing the *mean*, *variance*, *covariance*, and *joint distribution* of sample values, instead of demanding sample-by-sample identity. This makes them **robust to amplitude scaling, baseline wander, and minor time shifts** ŌĆö all of which are pervasive in real biomedical recordings.

### 1.3 Why Statistical (and Not Purely Deterministic)?

Real biomedical signals are inherently:
- **Noisy** (thermal noise, motion artifact, 50/60 Hz interference).
- **Non-stationary** (statistics change with time ŌĆö e.g., ECG varies with respiration).
- **Amplitude-scaled** (electrode impedance varies between sessions).

Pure Euclidean distance $\|x-y\|_2$ fails under these conditions. Statistical measures **discount** the noise floor and **normalize** the scale, yielding a meaningful similarity score.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Statistical Similarity Measures

The measures covered in Module 3 are organized below by the statistical depth they exploit.

| Tier | Statistical Order Used | Measures | When Preferred |
|------|------------------------|----------|----------------|
| **Tier-1: 0th Moment (Energy)** | Energy $\sum x^2$ | SNR, MSE | Quick quality check |
| **Tier-2: 1st & 2nd Moments** | Mean $\mu$, Variance $\sigma^2$, Covariance | Correlation Coefficient, Cosine Similarity, Coherence | Template matching, QRS detection |
| **Tier-3: Full Distribution** | PDF / Entropy | Mutual Information, KL Divergence | Non-linear EEG/EMG coupling |

### 2.2 Measure 1 ŌĆö Mean Squared Error (MSE)

The simplest (deterministic + statistical) similarity indicator. It is **inverse similarity** ŌĆö lower is better.

$$MSE = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n] - y[n]\bigr)^2$$

> **Statistical meaning:** $MSE = \sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 - 2\,\text{Cov}(x,y)$ when $\mu_x = \mu_y$.

- **Units:** Square of the signal units (e.g., $\text{mV}^2$ for ECG).
- **Range:** $[0, \infty)$.
- **Limitation:** Sensitive to amplitude scaling; a louder signal always looks "more different" even if shape is identical.

### 2.3 Measure 2 ŌĆö Cross-Correlation $r_{xy}(\tau)$

The unnormalized cross-correlation at lag $\tau$ is:

$$r_{xy}(\tau) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,y[n+\tau]$$

For finite-length signals of length $N$:

$$r_{xy}(\tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert\tau\vert} x[n]\,y[n+\tau] \quad \text{for } \tau \in [-N+1,\,N-1]$$

- **Statistical meaning:** Estimates the joint second moment $E\{X[n]Y[n+\tau]\}$ at lag $\tau$.
- **Peak location** $\tau^*$ tells the *time alignment* between $x$ and $y$.
- **Peak value** indicates strength of linear association.

### 2.4 Measure 3 ŌĆö Pearson Correlation Coefficient (Normalized Cross-Correlation)

The **gold-standard** statistical similarity measure for KTU board questions. It normalizes the cross-covariance by the individual standard deviations:

$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\sigma_x\,\sigma_y} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)^2 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties (must be memorised for KTU viva):**
> 1. $\rho_{xy} \in [-1, +1]$.
> 2. $\rho = +1$: Perfect positive linear similarity.
> 3. $\rho = -1$: Perfect anti-similarity.
> 4. $\rho = 0$: No linear statistical similarity.
> 5. **Invariant to affine transformation** $y = a\,x + b$ (with $a>0$).
> 6. Symmetric: $\rho_{xy} = \rho_{yx}$.

### 2.5 Measure 4 ŌĆö Cosine Similarity

Treats each signal as a vector in $\mathbb{R}^N$ and computes the angle between them:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{\langle x, y\rangle}{\|x\|_2 \cdot \|y\|_2} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\,y[n]}{\sqrt{\sum x^2[n] \cdot \sum y^2[n]}}$$

- **Difference from Pearson:** Cosine similarity does **not** subtract the means; it compares the raw "direction" of the signal vector.
- **Use case:** When baseline offsets are meaningful (e.g., comparing resting vs. task EEG power levels).

### 2.6 Measure 5 ŌĆö Coherence Function (Frequency-Domain Similarity)

For stochastic signals, the **magnitude-squared coherence** $\gamma_{xy}^2(f)$ is the frequency-domain analog of $\rho^2$:

$$\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2}}{S_{xx}(f)\,S_{yy}(f)}$$

where $S_{xy}(f)$ is the cross-spectral density and $S_{xx},S_{yy}$ are auto-spectra.

- **Range:** $[0,1]$.
- **Interpretation:** Fraction of power in $x$ at frequency $f$ that is linearly related to power in $y$ at $f$.
- **Use case in biomedicine:** **EEG-EEG** or **ECG-EMG** coupling analysis, detecting cortico-muscular coherence.

### 2.7 Measure 6 ŌĆö Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

If $y = x + \eta$ (clean signal + noise):

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum_{n} x^2[n]}{\sum_{n}\eta^2[n]}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}}\right)$$

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Measure | Formula | Range | Units | Invariant to Scale? | Invariant to Offset? |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|----------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | MSE | $\frac{1}{N}\sum(x-y)^2$ | $[0,\infty)$ | Signal┬▓ | ŌØī | ŌØī |
| 2 | Cross-Correlation $r_{xy}(\tau)$ | $\sum x[n]y[n+\tau]$ | $[-\infty,\infty)$ | Signal┬▓ | ŌØī | ŌØī |
| 3 | Pearson $\rho_{xy}$ | $\text{Cov}(x,y)/(\sigma_x\sigma_y)$ | $[-1,+1]$ | Dimensionless | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| 4 | Cosine Similarity | $\langle x,y\rangle / (\|x\|\|y\|)$ | $[-1,+1]$ | Dimensionless | Ō£ģ | ŌØī |
| 5 | Coherence $\gamma^2(f)$ | $\vert S_{xy}\vert^2/(S_{xx}S_{yy})$ | $[0,1]$ | Dimensionless | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |
| 6 | SNR (dB) | $10\log_{10}(P_s/P_\eta)$ | $(-\infty,\infty)$ dB | Decibels | ŌĆö | ŌĆö |
| 7 | Mutual Info $I(X;Y)$ | $\sum p(x,y)\log\frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)p(y)}$ | $[0,\infty)$ | Bits | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KRUT 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **Pearson coefficient** (subtracts means) with the **Cosine similarity** (does not). Forgetting to demean $x$ and $y$ before computing $\rho$ is a **3-mark deduction** in ESE valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Cross-Covariance to Pearson's $\rho$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define sample means.**

$$\mu_x = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n], \qquad \mu_y = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define zero-mean versions.**

$$\tilde{x}[n] = x[n] - \mu_x, \qquad \tilde{y}[n] = y[n] - \mu_y$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define sample variances and covariance.**

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \tilde{x}^2[n], \quad \sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \tilde{y}^2[n]$$

$$\text{Cov}(x,y) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \tilde{x}[n]\,\tilde{y}[n]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Form the normalised ratio.**

$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute and simplify.**

$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)^2}}$$

The $\frac{1}{N}$ factor cancels identically in numerator and denominator, giving the working formula.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality** to prove $\vert\rho_{xy}\vert \leq 1$.

By the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality:

$$\left\vert \sum_{n} \tilde{x}[n]\,\tilde{y}[n] \right\vert \leq \sqrt{\sum_{n}\tilde{x}^2[n] \cdot \sum_{n}\tilde{y}^2[n]}$$

Dividing both sides by the (positive) denominator yields $\vert\rho_{xy}\vert \leq 1$. **Equality holds iff $\tilde{y} = k\,\tilde{x}$** (perfect linear relationship).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** Two ECG-template signals of length $N=5$ are:
> $x = [1,\;2,\;3,\;4,\;5]$, $y = [2,\;4,\;6,\;8,\;10]$.
> Compute (a) MSE, (b) Pearson $\rho_{xy}$, (c) Cosine similarity, and comment on which is the most reliable similarity indicator.

**Solution:**

**Step A ŌĆö Means.**

$$\mu_x = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \qquad \mu_y = \frac{2+4+6+8+10}{5} = 6$$

**Step B ŌĆö Zero-mean signals.**

$$\tilde{x} = [-2,-1,0,1,2], \qquad \tilde{y} = [-4,-2,0,2,4]$$

**Step C ŌĆö MSE.**

$$MSE = \frac{1}{5}\bigl[(1-2)^2+(2-4)^2+(3-6)^2+(4-8)^2+(5-10)^2\bigr]$$

$$= \frac{1}{5}\bigl[1+4+9+16+25\bigr] = \frac{55}{5} = 11$$

> Comment: MSE is large because $y$ is amplitude-scaled version of $x$ ŌĆö MSE penalises scaling unfairly.

**Step D ŌĆö Numerator of $\rho$.**

$$\sum \tilde{x}\,\tilde{y} = (-2)(-4)+(-1)(-2)+(0)(0)+(1)(2)+(2)(4)$$

$$= 8+2+0+2+8 = 20$$

**Step E ŌĆö Denominator pieces.**

$$\sum \tilde{x}^2 = 4+1+0+1+4 = 10, \qquad \sum \tilde{y}^2 = 16+4+0+4+16 = 40$$

$$\sqrt{10 \times 40} = \sqrt{400} = 20$$

**Step F ŌĆö Pearson $\rho$.**

$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{20}{20} = +1$$

> Interpretation: Perfect positive linear similarity (since $y = 2x$ exactly).

**Step G ŌĆö Cosine similarity.**

$$\sum x[n]y[n] = 2+8+18+32+50 = 110$$

$$\sum x^2 = 1+4+9+16+25 = 55, \qquad \sum y^2 = 4+16+36+64+100 = 220$$

$$\|x\|\,\|y\| = \sqrt{55 \times 220} = \sqrt{12100} = 110$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{110}{110} = +1$$

> Comment: Both $\rho$ and cosine return $1$, but for different reasons. Cosine used *raw* signals (picks up the proportional amplitude), while Pearson first de-meaned (relying on shape).

**Conclusion for KTU board:** For two *purely proportional* signals, $\rho$ and cosine agree. For signals that differ in **DC offset** (e.g., baseline-shifted ECG), **only $\rho$ remains $=1$**, while cosine drops. Hence $\rho$ is the more robust statistical similarity measure for biomedical templates.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Measure

| Scenario | Best Measure | Reason |
|----------|--------------|--------|
| QRS template matching (clean ECG) | Pearson $\rho$ | Robust to baseline wander; bounded $[-1,1]$ |
| EEG channel coupling | Coherence $\gamma^2(f)$ | Reveals frequency-specific synchronization |
| Detrended heart-rate variability | Cosine similarity on RR-interval vectors | Preserves amplitude information |
| Denoising filter evaluation | SNR (dB) | Direct physical interpretability |
| Non-linear coupling (e.g., anaesthetic depth EEG) | Mutual Information | Captures non-Gaussian dependencies |
| Quick noise-floor estimate | MSE | Computationally trivial |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic Statistical Similarity Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x n] --> B[Mean Removal]
    C[Reference Signal y n] --> D[Mean Removal]
    B --> E[Compute Covariance]
    D --> E
    B --> F[Compute Variance sigma x squared]
    D --> G[Compute Variance sigma y squared]
    E --> H[Numerator of rho]
    F --> I[Denominator sqrt]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Pearson Correlation rho xy]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Decision}
    K -->|rho near 1| L[Strong Positive Similarity]
    K -->|rho near 0| M[No Linear Similarity]
    K -->|rho near minus 1| N[Strong Inverse Similarity]
```

> **Figure 4.1:** Functional block diagram of a Pearson correlation coefficient computation pipeline applied to two biomedical signals. The two parallel mean-removal branches ensure offset invariance; the covariance and variance branches meet at the normalisation junction.

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Frequency-Domain Coherence Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Segment x n into K windows] --> B1[Window with Hanning]
    A2[Segment y n into K windows] --> B2[Window with Hanning]
    B1 --> C1[FFT to get X k f]
    B2 --> C2[FFT to get Y k f]
    C1 --> D1[S xx f = average of X star X]
    C2 --> D2[S yy f = average of Y star Y]
    C1 --> E1[Cross spectrum S xy f = average of X star Y]
    C2 --> E1
    D1 --> F[Compute gamma squared f]
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
    F --> G{Coh at target band}
    G -->|gamma squared near 1| H[Strong Frequency Coupling]
    G -->|gamma squared near 0| I[Independent at f]
```

> **Figure 4.2:** Welch-based magnitude-squared coherence pipeline. Each signal is windowed, transformed via FFT, and the cross-spectral density is normalised by the geometric mean of the auto-spectra. Used in Module 3 to detect, e.g., 10 Hz alpha-band EEGŌĆōEMG coupling.

### 4.3 Conceptual Map of Similarity Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statistical Similarity Measures] --> B[Time Domain]
    A --> C[Frequency Domain]
    A --> D[Information Theoretic]
    B --> B1[Cross Correlation]
    B --> B2[Pearson rho]
    B --> B3[Cosine Similarity]
    B --> B4[MSE]
    C --> C1[Coherence gamma squared]
    C --> C2[Spectral Correlation]
    D --> D1[Mutual Information]
    D --> D2[KL Divergence]
    B1 --> E[QRS Template Match]
    B2 --> E
    C1 --> F[EEG EMG Coupling]
    D1 --> G[Nonlinear EEG Analysis]
```

> **Figure 4.3:** Hierarchical taxonomy of statistical similarity measures covered in Module 3 and their canonical biomedical applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the Pearson correlation coefficient for two real signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$. State any two of its key properties relevant to biomedical signal comparison.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Pearson correlation coefficient is defined as the normalised cross-covariance of two zero-mean real signals:

$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)}{\sqrt{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(x[n]-\mu_x\bigr)^2 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\bigl(y[n]-\mu_y\bigr)^2}}$$

**Two key properties:**
1. $\rho_{xy} \in [-1,+1]$; value $+1$ indicates perfect positive linear similarity.
2. **Offset-invariant:** Adding a constant to $x$ or $y$ does not change $\rho_{xy}$, which makes it ideal for baseline-shifted ECG/EEG recordings.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each property = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Retest]**
*Differentiate between Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Pearson correlation coefficient as similarity measures. In which biomedical situation is MSE preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | MSE | Pearson $\rho$ |
|--------|-----|----------------|
| Range | $[0,\infty)$ | $[-1,+1]$ |
| Scale-invariant | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Offset-invariant | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes |
| Indicates | Absolute error | Linear statistical association |

**MSE is preferred** when we require a *physical* measure of error magnitude in the same units as the signal ŌĆö e.g., quantifying reconstruction error of a denoised ECG in $\text{mV}^2$, where reporting the actual error power is meaningful for diagnostic thresholding.

*(1.5 marks table + 1.5 marks application = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö ESE Module Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024] ŌĆö Question A (14 Marks)**
> 
> **(a)** Derive the Pearson correlation coefficient from the definitions of sample mean, sample variance, and sample covariance. Prove that $\vert\rho_{xy}\vert \leq 1$ using the CauchyŌĆōSchwarz inequality. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** For the two finite-length biomedical signals $x = [3, 5, 2, 8, 6]$ and $y = [4, 6, 3, 9, 7]$, compute (i) MSE, (ii) Pearson $\rho_{xy}$, and (iii) Cosine similarity. Comment on which measure is most suitable for comparing two ECG template beats. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

1. *Sample means* (1 mark):
$$\mu_x = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}x[n], \quad \mu_y = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}y[n]$$

2. *Sample covariance* (1 mark):
$$\text{Cov}(x,y) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-\mu_x)(y[n]-\mu_y)$$

3. *Sample variances* (1 mark):
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(x[n]-\mu_x)^2, \quad \sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}(y[n]-\mu_y)^2$$

4. *Pearson definition* (1 mark):
$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x,y)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$$

5. *CauchyŌĆōSchwarz statement* (1 mark):
$$\left\vert \sum_n a_n b_n \right\vert \leq \sqrt{\sum_n a_n^2}\sqrt{\sum_n b_n^2}$$

6. *Application* with $a_n = \tilde{x}[n]$, $b_n = \tilde{y}[n]$ (1 mark):
$$\left\vert \sum_n \tilde{x}[n]\,\tilde{y}[n] \right\vert \leq \sqrt{\sum_n \tilde{x}^2[n]}\sqrt{\sum_n \tilde{y}^2[n]}$$

7. *Dividing both sides* by $\sqrt{\sum \tilde{x}^2}\sqrt{\sum \tilde{y}^2}$ yields $\vert\rho_{xy}\vert \leq 1$, with equality iff $\tilde{y} = k\tilde{x}$ (1 mark).

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Means (1 mark):**
$$\mu_x = \frac{3+5+2+8+6}{5} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8$$
$$\mu_y = \frac{4+6+3+9+7}{5} = \frac{29}{5} = 5.8$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Zero-mean vectors (0.5 mark):**
$$\tilde{x} = [-1.8,\;0.2,\;-2.8,\;3.2,\;1.2]$$
$$\tilde{y} = [-1.8,\;0.2,\;-2.8,\;3.2,\;1.2]$$

(Note: $\tilde{y} = \tilde{x}$ here ŌĆö the two signals differ only by a constant offset of $1$.)

**Step 3 ŌĆö MSE (1 mark):**
$$MSE = \frac{1}{5}\bigl[(3-4)^2+(5-6)^2+(2-3)^2+(8-9)^2+(6-7)^2\bigr]$$
$$= \frac{1}{5}(1+1+1+1+1) = 1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Covariance (1 mark):**
$$\sum \tilde{x}\tilde{y} = 3.24+0.04+7.84+10.24+1.44 = 22.8$$
$$\text{Cov}(x,y) = \frac{22.8}{5} = 4.56$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Variances (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{5}(3.24+0.04+7.84+10.24+1.44) = \frac{22.8}{5} = 4.56$$
$$\sigma_y^2 = 4.56 \text{ (same)}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Pearson (0.5 mark):**
$$\rho_{xy} = \frac{4.56}{\sqrt{4.56 \times 4.56}} = \frac{4.56}{4.56} = +1$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Cosine similarity (1 mark):**
$$\sum xy = 12+30+6+72+42 = 162$$
$$\sum x^2 = 9+25+4+64+36 = 138, \quad \sum y^2 = 16+36+9+81+49 = 191$$
$$\|x\|\,\|y\| = \sqrt{138 \times 191} = \sqrt{26358} \approx 162.35$$
$$\cos\theta = \frac{162}{162.35} \approx 0.9979$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Comment (1 mark):**
$\rho = 1$ exactly (since the signals are identical in shape, differing only by an additive constant), whereas cosine $\approx 0.998$ is slightly less than $1$ because it is *not* offset-invariant. Hence, **Pearson $\rho$ is the most suitable** for comparing ECG templates, where baseline wander (offset variation) is unavoidable.

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024] ŌĆö Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative)**
> 
> **(a)** Define the **magnitude-squared coherence** $\gamma_{xy}^2(f)$ for two real biomedical signals. Explain each term in the formula and discuss its range and physical interpretation. Why is coherence preferred over raw cross-correlation in EEGŌĆōEMG coupling studies? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Two signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ of length $N=4$ are $x=[2, 4, 6, 8]$ and $y=[1, 3, 5, 7]$. Compute (i) the zero-lag cross-correlation $r_{xy}(0)$, (ii) the lag-1 cross-correlation $r_{xy}(1)$, (iii) the Pearson correlation coefficient, and (iv) the cosine similarity. State the lag at which $x$ and $y$ are most similar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

The magnitude-squared coherence is defined as (1 mark):

$$\gamma_{xy}^{2}(f) = \frac{\vert S_{xy}(f) \vert^{2}}{S_{xx}(f)\,S_{yy}(f)}$$

where:
- $S_{xx}(f) = E\{\vert X(f)\vert^2\}$ ŌĆö auto-spectral density of $x$ (1 mark)
- $S_{yy}(f) = E\{\vert Y(f)\vert^2\}$ ŌĆö auto-spectral density of $y$ (1 mark)
- $S_{xy}(f) = E\{X^*(f)Y(f)\}$ ŌĆö cross-spectral density (1 mark)
- The expectation $E\{\cdot\}$ is implemented by averaging over short-time segments (Welch method) (1 mark)

**Range:** $\gamma_{xy}^2(f) \in [0,1]$ (1 mark). It represents the fraction of power in $x$ at frequency $f$ that is linearly predictable from $y$ at $f$.

**Why preferred over cross-correlation for EEGŌĆōEMG:** Time-domain cross-correlation collapses all frequency information into a single lag, whereas coherence is a *function of frequency* and can isolate the **beta-band (13ŌĆō30 Hz) cortico-muscular coupling** that is clinically meaningful, suppressing DC and noise bands. (2 marks)

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

Given: $x=[2,4,6,8]$, $y=[1,3,5,7]$, $N=4$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö $r_{xy}(0)$ (2 marks):**
$$r_{xy}(0) = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n]\,y[n] = 2\cdot1 + 4\cdot3 + 6\cdot5 + 8\cdot7$$
$$= 2+12+30+56 = 100$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö $r_{xy}(1)$ (2 marks):**
$$r_{xy}(1) = \sum_{n=0}^{2} x[n]\,y[n+1] = 2\cdot3 + 4\cdot5 + 6\cdot7 = 6+20+42 = 68$$

(For completeness, $r_{xy}(-1) = 1\cdot2 + 3\cdot4 + 5\cdot6 = 2+12+30 = 44$; the maximum correlation occurs at $\tau = 0$.)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Means (0.5 mark):**
$$\mu_x = 5, \quad \mu_y = 4$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pearson $\rho$ (1 mark):**
$$\tilde{x}=[-3,-1,1,3], \quad \tilde{y}=[-3,-1,1,3]$$
$$\sum \tilde{x}\tilde{y} = 9+1+1+9 = 20$$
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{20}{4}=5,\quad \sigma_y^2=5$$
$$\rho = \frac{5}{\sqrt{5 \times 5}} = +1$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Cosine similarity (1 mark):**
$$\sum xy = 100, \quad \sum x^2 = 4+16+36+64 = 120, \quad \sum y^2 = 1+9+25+49 = 84$$
$$\|x\|\|y\| = \sqrt{120 \times 84} = \sqrt{10080} \approx 100.40$$
$$\cos\theta = \frac{100}{100.40} \approx 0.996$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The peak cross-correlation occurs at $\tau^* = 0$, indicating that $x$ and $y$ are **most similar when aligned without any lag**, with $r_{xy}(0) = 100$. Pearson $\rho = 1$ confirms perfect linear similarity (the signals are perfectly proportional with $y = x - 1$).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark Pitfalls (costing up to 4 marks each):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to demean the signal** before computing $\rho$. If you write $\sum x[n]y[n]$ in the numerator instead of $\sum (x[n]-\mu_x)(y[n]-\mu_y)$, you will compute **cosine similarity**, not Pearson. This is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 
> 2. **Conflating cross-correlation with convolution.** Cross-correlation does **not** time-reverse the second signal: $r_{xy}(\tau) = \sum x[n]y[n+\tau]$, *not* $\sum x[n]y[\tau-n]$. Mark loss: 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Off-by-one lag indexing** in finite-length cross-correlation. The valid lag range is $\tau \in [-(N-1), +(N-1)]$, and the summand index $n$ must be clipped: $n \in [\max(0,-\tau), \min(N-1, N-1-\tau)]$.
> 
> 4. **Incoherence in coherence formula:** The denominator is $S_{xx}(f) \cdot S_{yy}(f)$, *not* $\sqrt{S_{xx} S_{yy}}$ squared. Some students incorrectly write the geometric mean, leading to an extra square root in the expression.
> 
> 5. **Using $\log_e$ where $\log_2$ is required** in mutual information, or forgetting to specify the base.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **MSE** ŌĆö quick error metric; sensitive to scale; units = signal┬▓.
- [x] **Cross-correlation $r_{xy}(\tau)$** ŌĆö locates optimal time-alignment lag; peak gives similarity.
- [x] **Pearson $\rho$** ŌĆö *always demean* before computing; range $[-1,1]$; offset- and scale-invariant.
- [x] **Cosine similarity** ŌĆö does *not* demean; range $[-1,1]$; preserves amplitude information.
- [x] **Coherence $\gamma^2(f)$** ŌĆö frequency-domain $[0,1]$; requires Welch-averaged spectra; ideal for EEG-EMG/EEG-EEG coupling.
- [x] **SNR (dB)** ŌĆö uses $10\log_{10}$; measures noise contamination.
- [x] **Mutual Information** ŌĆö captures *non-linear* dependencies; uses joint and marginal PDFs.
- [x] **CauchyŌĆōSchwarz** is the foundation of $\vert\rho\vert \leq 1$.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark questions** always involve (a) a derivation/proof + (b) a numerical computation.
- [x] **Biomedical preference rule:** Use **Pearson** for template beats; use **coherence** for oscillatory coupling; use **SNR** for filter evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthetic Fractal Signals** are artificially generated stochastic or deterministic time series that exhibit **statistical self-similarity** and **long-range dependence (LRD)** across multiple time scales. In the context of biomedical signal processing, they serve as mathematically tractable surrogates for physiological signals (e.g., heart rate variability, EEG background activity, gait dynamics) that possess **non-integer (fractal) dimensions** and **1/f^╬▓ power spectral behavior**.

Mathematically, a continuous-time process $X(t)$ is classified as a **scaling (self-similar) process** if there exists a parameter $H > 0$ such that for any scaling factor $a > 0$:

$$X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$$

where $\stackrel{d}{=}$ denotes equality in finite-dimensional distributions, and **H** is the **Hurst exponent** (or **self-similarity parameter**).

### 1.2 Key Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Canonical Fractal Signal Families:**
> - **Fractional Brownian Motion (fBm):** A non-stationary, zero-mean Gaussian process $B_{H}(t)$ with $B_{H}(0) = 0$ and stationary increments.
> - **Fractional Gaussian Noise (fGn):** The derivative (increments) of fBm ŌĆö a *stationary* long-range dependent process with autocorrelation decaying as a power law.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a coastline viewed from a satellite at 10 km, then re-viewed from a helicopter at 1 km, then walked along on foot. The roughness pattern *looks statistically the same* at every zoom level ŌĆö only the units change. This is **self-similarity**.

Now, a patient's heart rate at rest, when recorded over 1 hour, 1 minute, or 1 second, also shows a similar "wriggliness" pattern at each scale. **Synthetic fractal signals are lab-generated signals that mimic this scale-invariant, "wriggly-but-predictable-in-statistics" behaviour**, allowing engineers to test algorithms, filters, and Hurst-estimation techniques on signals whose ground-truth fractal parameters are *known a priori*.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Hurst Exponent (H):** $0 < H < 1$ (typically $0.5$ = classical Brownian motion, $H > 0.5$ = persistent/long-memory, $H < 0.5$ = anti-persistent)
- **Fractal Dimension (D):** For a 1-D time series, $D = 2 - H$
- **Spectral Exponent (╬▓):** Power spectral density decays as $1/f^{\beta}$, where $\beta = 2H - 1$ (for fBm) and $\beta = 2H + 1$ (for fGn with stationary interpretation using spectral measure).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation ŌĆö Why Synthetic Generation?

Real biomedical recordings are *contaminated* with noise, artifacts, and non-stationarities. To validate fractal-analysis algorithms (DFA, Higuchi, Multifractal DFA), researchers require:

1. **Ground-truth control** ŌĆö exact knowledge of $H$, $\beta$, and $D$.
2. **Arbitrary length** ŌĆö generate any number of samples.
3. **Statistical reproducibility** ŌĆö average over many realizations.

### 2.2 The Three Principal Generation Paradigms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation Paradigms for Synthetic Fractal Signals:**
> 1. **Spectral (Frequency-Domain) Synthesis** ŌĆö inverse FFT of shaped spectral coefficients (VossŌĆōMcCartney algorithm).
> 2. **Spatial (Recursive) Synthesis** ŌĆö Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD) and successive random additions.
> 3. **Algebraic / Closed-Form Synthesis** ŌĆö WeierstrassŌĆōMandelbrot function (deterministic).

### 2.3 Step-Wise Logic of Spectral Synthesis (FFT-Based)

- **Why:** A fractal signal's defining property is its **power spectral density** $S(f) \propto 1/f^{\beta}$. If we can manufacture a complex spectrum whose magnitude matches this law and whose phase is random, the inverse FFT will yield a real-valued time series with the desired fractal statistics.
- **How:**
  1. Define target PSD: $S(k) = C \cdot \vert k \vert^{-\beta}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, N/2$.
  2. Generate complex Fourier coefficients: $X(k) = \sqrt{S(k)} \cdot e^{j\phi(k)}$, where $\phi(k) \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 2\pi)$.
  3. Enforce Hermitian symmetry: $X(N-k) = X^{*}(k)$ (to ensure real-valued output).
  4. Apply $x[n] = \text{Re}\{\text{IFFT}(X(k))\}$.
  5. Optionally normalize variance to unity.

### 2.4 Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD) ŌĆö The Spatial Method

- **Why:** A self-similar curve, when zoomed in, must show a *scaled copy* of the original roughness. RMD recursively inserts midpoints displaced by a Gaussian random amount whose variance shrinks as $\sigma^{2} \propto (\Delta t)^{2H}$.
- **How:** At each refinement level $m$, the displacement variance is:
  $$\sigma_{m}^{2} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot (2^{-m})^{2H} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot 2^{-2mH}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for KTU 2024**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X(at) \stackrel{d}{=} a^{H} X(t)$ | Self-similarity definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | $D = 2 - H$ | Fractal dimension from Hurst exponent | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\beta = 2H + 1$ | Spectral exponent (fGn, stationary) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\beta = 2H - 1$ | Spectral exponent (fBm, non-stationary) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S(f) = C \cdot \vert f \vert^{-\beta}$ | Power Spectral Density of fractal process | $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ |
| 6 | $R(\tau) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}\left(\vert \tau \vert^{2H} - 1\right)$ | Autocorrelation of fGn increments | $\text{V}^2$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{m}^{2} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot 2^{-2mH}$ | RMD displacement variance at level $m$ | $\text{V}^2$ |
| 8 | $W(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma^{-nH} \sin(2\pi \gamma^{n} t + \phi_{n})$ | WeierstrassŌĆōMandelbrot function | dimensionless |
| 9 | $H = 0.5$ | Classical (uncorrelated) Brownian limit | dimensionless |
| 10 | $0 < H < 1$ | Valid Hurst range for fBm | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** In the formula table, the autocorrelation expression is written as $R(\tau) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}(\vert \tau \vert^{2H} - 1)$. Always use the `\vert` LaTeX command in your answer sheets to denote absolute value ŌĆö KTU scripts use MathJax-compatible LaTeX.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Algorithm benchmarking:** Generating reference signals to test Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA), Higuchi's method, and wavelet-based Hurst estimators.
- **Biomedical modelling:** Synthesizing realistic heart-rate variability (HRV) traces, EEG micro-states, and gait cycle timing.
- **Communications & image processing:** Fractal image compression, network traffic modelling, and noise generation in MEMs sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Spectral Density of fBm

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the self-similar increment definition.**

For a fractional Brownian motion $B_{H}(t)$ with stationary increments, the increment process over lag $\tau$ is:

$$\Delta_{\tau} B_{H}(t) = B_{H}(t+\tau) - B_{H}(t)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the variance of the increment.**

By the definition of fBm, the variance of $B_{H}(t)$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[B_{H}^{2}(t)] = \sigma^{2} \cdot \vert t \vert^{2H}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Derive the variance of the increment.**

The increment variance is a classical identity of fBm:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(\Delta_{\tau} B_{H})^{2}\right] = \sigma^{2} \cdot \vert \tau \vert^{2H}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Relate to the autocorrelation function.**

The stationary increment process $X(t) = \Delta_{1} B_{H}(t)$ has autocorrelation:

$$R_{X}(\tau) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\mathbb{E}[(X(t+\tau) - X(t))^{2}]\right] - \text{var}(X)$$

Applying the increment-variance identity and simplifying gives the **canonical fGn autocorrelation**:

$$R_{X}(\tau) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}\left(\vert \tau + 1 \vert^{2H} - 2 \vert \tau \vert^{2H} + \vert \tau - 1 \vert^{2H}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Take the Fourier transform to obtain PSD.**

Using the WienerŌĆōKhinchin theorem $S(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_{X}(\tau)\}$, the asymptotic behavior for large $\vert f \vert$ is:

$$S_{X}(f) \propto \sigma^{2} \cdot \vert f \vert^{-(2H+1)}$$

Therefore, the spectral exponent of stationary fGn is $\beta = 2H + 1$, **and of fBm is $\beta = 2H - 1$** (because integration in time-domain corresponds to $1/f^{2}$ roll-off).

$$\boxed{\;S_{B_{H}}(f) \propto \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\vert f \vert^{2H-1}}, \qquad S_{X}(f) \propto \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\vert f \vert^{2H+1}}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Variance Decay in Random Midpoint Displacement (RMD)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial condition.** Start with endpoints $x(0) = 0$ and $x(1) = 0$ (or any baseline). Define base variance $\sigma_{0}^{2}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö First refinement (level $m = 1$).** The midpoint at $t = 0.5$ is set to:

$$x(0.5) = \frac{x(0) + x(1)}{2} + \delta_{1}, \qquad \delta_{1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{1}^{2})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Second refinement (level $m = 2$).** Insert midpoints of each sub-interval, e.g., at $t = 0.25$ and $t = 0.75$:

$$x(0.25) = \frac{x(0) + x(0.5)}{2} + \delta_{2}, \qquad \delta_{2} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{2}^{2})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Self-similarity constraint.** The increment distribution at any scale must be statistically identical up to a power of the scale factor. If the new sub-interval length is $\Delta t_{m} = 2^{-m}$, then:

$$\sigma_{m} = \sigma_{0} \cdot (\Delta t_{m})^{H} = \sigma_{0} \cdot 2^{-mH}$$

Squaring both sides gives the variance recursion:

$$\sigma_{m}^{2} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot 2^{-2mH}$$

This is the canonical RMD variance law; it guarantees that the resulting curve has Hausdorff dimension $D = 2 - H$.

### 3.3 Derivation: WeierstrassŌĆōMandelbrot Function Generation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Closed-form definition.** The classical WeierstrassŌĆōMandelbrot function is:

$$W(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma^{-nH} \left[1 - e^{i \gamma^{n} t}\right] e^{i \phi_{n}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Truncation for implementation.** For a finite signal of $N$ samples, truncate to $n = 0, 1, \dots, N_{max}$:

$$W(t) \approx \sum_{n=0}^{N_{max}} \gamma^{-nH} \sin(\gamma^{n} t + \phi_{n})$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Parameter selection.**
- Base frequency: $\gamma > 1$ (typically $\gamma = 1.5$).
- Phase set: $\phi_{n} \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 2\pi)$ i.i.d.
- $N_{max}$: chosen so that the highest frequency component $\gamma^{N_{max}}$ is at least $2 \times$ the sampling rate of interest.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verification of fractal dimension.** The Hausdorff dimension of $W(t)$ is rigorously:

$$D = 2 - H + 1 = 2 - H \quad \text{(for a graph in 2-D space)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "derive the HurstŌĆōdimension relation," always start from the **self-similarity definition**, derive the **variance law** of increments, then map to **dimension** via the box-counting or Hausdorff relation $D = 2 - H$. Marks are awarded for *each* logical bridge ŌĆö not just the final equation.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Generate 8 samples of a synthetic fractal signal using RMD with $H = 0.7$ and $\sigma_{0} = 1$. The recursion formula is $\sigma_{m}^{2} = \sigma_{0}^{2} \cdot 2^{-2mH}$.

**Solution:**

Level 0: $\sigma_{0}^{2} = 1.000$, $\sigma_{0} = 1.000$

Level 1: $\sigma_{1}^{2} = 1 \cdot 2^{-1.4} = 0.379$, $\sigma_{1} = 0.616$

Level 2: $\sigma_{2}^{2} = 1 \cdot 2^{-2.8} = 0.144$, $\sigma_{2} = 0.379$

Level 3: $\sigma_{3}^{2} = 1 \cdot 2^{-4.2} = 0.054$, $\sigma_{3} = 0.233$

These four standard deviations are the random-injection magnitudes for the four levels of midpoint refinement, producing a 9-point (or with boundary, 17-point) signal of Hurst exponent $0.7$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
