# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, PEBRT865]**
> Define *Instantaneous Frequency* of a biomedical signal. Why is the Hilbert Transform not directly applicable to a multi-component EEG epoch containing both alpha spindles and beta bursts?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Instantaneous Frequency (IF) is the time-derivative of the instantaneous phase of the **analytic signal**: $f_i(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$ (Hz). It represents the *momentary* dominant oscillation frequency at time $t$. The Hilbert Transform assumes the signal is **mono-component** ŌĆö only one dominant IF at any instant. A multi-component EEG epoch contains *multiple simultaneous IFs* (alpha Ōēł 10 Hz and beta Ōēł 20 Hz), so applying $\mathcal{H}\{\cdot\}$ directly produces a meaningless *average* IF. The correct procedure is to first apply **Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD)** or **Band-Pass Filtering** to isolate intrinsic mode functions (IMFs), and then compute IF on each IMF separately. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, PEBRT865]**
> State the Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty principle in the context of time-frequency analysis. What is the clinical significance of the *Gaussian window* in STFT-based ECG analysis?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Heisenberg-Gabor principle states $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ (s┬ĘHz), where $\Delta t$ and $\Delta f$ are the standard deviations of a signal's energy in time and frequency. No joint TFR can localize a signal better than this bound; the Gaussian window **achieves equality** and is therefore the optimal STFT window in the information-theoretic sense. Clinically, for ECG analysis the Gaussian window minimizes leakage of the QRS complex into the ST-T segment, improving detection of late potentials and T-wave alternans. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **Instantaneous Frequency** $f_i(t)$ from the analytic signal $x_a(t) = A(t)\, e^{j\phi(t)}$. Show all intermediate steps.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a synthetic ECG-like chirp signal $x(t) = \cos\!\left(2\pi(5t + 0.5 t^2)\right)$ sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz, compute: (i) the analytic signal, (ii) the instantaneous frequency as a function of $t$ over the interval $t \in [0, 4]$ s, and (iii) sketch the IF trajectory in the time-frequency plane. Comment on the Nyquist compliance.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of IF (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the analytic signal as $x_a(t) = x(t) + j\mathcal{H}\{x(t)\}$ and its polar form as $x_a(t) = A(t)\, e^{j\phi(t)}$, where $A(t) \geq 0$ is the instantaneous amplitude and $\phi(t)$ the instantaneous phase.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take the natural logarithm: $\ln x_a(t) = \ln A(t) + j\phi(t)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate w.r.t. $t$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\ln x_a(t) = \frac{1}{A(t)}\,\frac{dA(t)}{dt} + j\,\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equivalently, $\dfrac{x_a'(t)}{x_a(t)} = \dfrac{A'(t)}{A(t)} + j\dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$, so the imaginary part is the *angular* instantaneous frequency:

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\phi(t)}{dt} = \text{Im}\!\left[\frac{x_a'(t)}{x_a(t)}\right] \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** To convert to Hz, divide by $2\pi$ because the Fourier kernel is $e^{j 2\pi f t}$:

$$f_i(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\frac{d\phi(t)}{dt} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks):** Discuss the *mono-component requirement* (Cohen, 1995): IF is physically meaningful only if $A(t)$ and $f_i(t)$ vary *slower* than $\phi(t)$ (Bedrosian theorem) and the signal has a *single* dominant oscillation at each instant. For multi-component biosignals (e.g., EEG with mixed rhythms), pre-decomposition via EMD is mandatory.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö IF of a Chirp Signal (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the instantaneous phase. Compare $x(t) = \cos\!\left(2\pi(5t + 0.5t^2)\right)$ with the standard form $\cos\phi(t)$:

$$\phi(t) = 2\pi\!\left(5t + 0.5 t^2\right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Differentiate to get angular frequency:

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\phi}{dt} = 2\pi(5 + t) \quad \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert to Hz:

$$f_i(t) = 5 + t \quad \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Evaluate the IF trajectory over $t \in [0, 4]$ s. Tabulate:

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_i(t)$ (Hz) | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |

The IF rises **linearly** from 5 Hz to 9 Hz ŌĆö a classic linear chirp (FM sweep).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Nyquist check. $f_s = 200$ Hz gives $f_N = 100$ Hz. The maximum IF = 9 Hz satisfies $9 < 100$ by a wide margin, so the signal is safely oversampled (no aliasing of the IF).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Question A:**
> A common 2-mark deduction occurs when students write $\omega_i = d\phi/dt$ *without* dividing by $2\pi$ to obtain Hz. KTU examiners expect the **final IF in Hz**; mixing rad/s with Hz in the same answer is a frequent mistake. Always state units explicitly.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2024 Model Paper, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** and the **Spectrogram**. Derive the relationship between window length and the time-frequency resolution trade-off.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EMG signal is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. A Hamming window of length $L = 256$ samples is used. Compute: (i) the time resolution $\Delta t$, (ii) the frequency resolution $\Delta f$, (iii) verify Heisenberg-Gabor compliance, and (iv) state the consequence if $L$ is increased to 1024.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) ŌĆö STFT and Spectrogram (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The STFT slides a windowed Fourier Transform across the signal:

$$X_{\text{STFT}}(t, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\, w(\tau - t)\, e^{-j 2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

The Spectrogram (energy density) is its squared magnitude:

$$S_x(t, f) = \vert X_{\text{STFT}}(t, f) \vert^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The window $w(\tau - t)$ localizes $x(\tau)$ around time $t$, allowing the *instantaneous* spectrum to be measured. Common windows: Rectangular, Hamming, Hann, Gaussian.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Resolution trade-off derivation. Let $\sigma_t$ be the time-standard deviation of $w(t)$. By Fourier duality, the frequency-spread $\sigma_f$ satisfies $\sigma_f = \dfrac{1}{4\pi \sigma_t}$. Therefore:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \sigma_t \cdot \sigma_f = \frac{1}{4\pi} \quad \text{(Gaussian, equality)}$$

For any other window, the inequality $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ holds strictly. *Short windows* improve $\Delta t$ but worsen $\Delta f$; *long windows* do the reverse.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö EMG Window Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert $L$ to seconds: $\Delta t = L / f_s = 256 / 1000 = 0.256$ s.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Frequency resolution: $\Delta f = f_s / L = 1000 / 256 \approx 3.906$ Hz. (Equivalently $\Delta f = 1/\Delta t \approx 3.9$ Hz.)

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Heisenberg-Gabor check:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f = 0.256 \times 3.906 \approx 1.0 \;\text{s┬ĘHz}$$

Compare with the lower bound: $\dfrac{1}{4\pi} \approx 0.0796$ s┬ĘHz. Since $1.0 \gg 0.0796$, the Hamming window is **not optimal** (a Gaussian window with the same $\Delta t$ would have $\Delta f = 0.311$ Hz, giving a product of 0.0796). The excess factor 12.5 is the cost of the Hamming's lower side-lobe leakage.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Consequence of $L = 1024$:
- New time resolution: $\Delta t = 1024 / 1000 = 1.024$ s (4├Ś worse ŌĆö too long to track individual MUAPs in dynamic EMG).
- New frequency resolution: $\Delta f = 1000 / 1024 \approx 0.977$ Hz (4├Ś better ŌĆö useful for fatigue-shift detection in the median frequency).
- The product is again $\approx 1.0$ s┬ĘHz (a property of all windows of the same family), so Heisenberg compliance is preserved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Question B:**
> Many students forget to **convert samples to seconds** when computing $\Delta t$. Writing $\Delta t = 256$ is a 1-mark penalty; the correct unit is **seconds**. Similarly, students often write "resolution increases" without specifying whether time or frequency resolution ŌĆö be explicit: "**Frequency** resolution improves, **time** resolution degrades."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Analytic Signal** is built using the Hilbert Transform: $x_a(t) = x(t) + j\hat{x}(t)$.
- Ō£ģ **Instantaneous Frequency** $f_i(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\,\dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ ŌĆö always in **Hz**, requires mono-component signals.
- Ō£ģ **STFT** is *linear*; **Spectrogram** and **WVD** are *quadratic* (energy distributions).
- Ō£ģ **WVD** is the only TFR that satisfies *both* marginals exactly but suffers from **cross-terms** for multi-component signals.
- Ō£ģ **Heisenberg-Gabor Limit:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ ŌĆö equality holds for the **Gaussian window** only.
- Ō£ģ **Nyquist check** is mandatory before IF estimation; maximum IF must satisfy $f_i^{\max} < f_s / 2$.
- Ō£ģ For multi-component biosignals (EEG/EMG), always pre-filter or apply **EMD** before computing IF.
- Ō£ģ In KTU 14-mark answers, *always derive*, *state units*, and *justify* the choice of TFR for the clinical application.
- Ō£ģ Negative values in WVD are **mathematically permissible** (not noise!) ŌĆö they encode the phase relationship between signal components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poincar├® Sections and Return Maps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Poincar├® Sections and Return Maps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Poincar├® Section** is a lower-dimensional hypersurface (typically one dimension less than the original phase space) that is transverse to the flow of a continuous-time dynamical system. Intersections of a continuous trajectory with this carefully chosen transverse section are recorded, effectively converting a continuous flow into a **discrete-time mapping**.

The corresponding **Poincar├® Map (also called the First Return Map)** is a discrete dynamical system:

$$\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_n)$$

where $\mathbf{x}_n \in \Sigma \subset \mathbb{R}^{n-1}$ represents the state of the system at the $n$-th crossing of the section $\Sigma$, and $\mathbf{P}$ is the discrete evolution operator derived from the continuous flow $\phi_t$:

$$\mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_n) = \phi_{\tau}(\mathbf{x}_n) \quad \text{where} \quad \phi_{\tau}(\mathbf{x}_n) \in \Sigma$$

A **Return Map** is the geometric construction obtained by plotting successive intersection points of a trajectory with a prescribed surface of section, revealing the long-term skeleton of the system's dynamics (fixed points, periodic orbits, quasi-periodicity, and chaos).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of biomedical signal processing, Poincar├® sections and return maps are used as **nonlinear feature extraction tools** for analyzing aperiodic physiological signals such as cardiac RR intervals, EEG epochs, and respiratory waveforms, where linear Fourier methods fail to capture the underlying dynamical structure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are standing beside a busy multi-lane highway and you want to study the traffic pattern. Instead of recording the continuous positions of every car on the road (impossible!), you place a single **invisible line across the highway** and simply **count and record the position and speed of every car each time it crosses that line**.

- The highway = the full phase space trajectory
- The invisible line = the **Poincar├® section**
- The list of (position, speed) at each crossing = the **return map**
- A car that returns to the same point at the same speed = a **fixed point** (stable pattern)
- A car that returns to the same point after two laps = a **period-2 orbit**
- A car that never repeats = **chaos**

This simple act of "slicing" a continuous flow reduces an impossibly complex continuous record into a tidy, analyzable list ŌĆö and that is exactly the genius of Henri Poincar├®'s method.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Quantitative Markers Used in Poincar├® Analysis of Biomedical Signals:**
> - **SD1, SD2** ŌĆö short-term and long-term variability descriptors of the **Poincar├® plot** of RR intervals (units: **ms**).
> - **Determinant of the covariance matrix** of the Poincar├® plot ŌĆö denoted **DET** (no unit, dimensionless).
> - **Sample entropy (SampEn)** of the return map ŌĆö dimensionless.
> - **Correlation dimension (D2)** of the attractor reconstructed from return points.
> - **Fixed point stability criterion** ŌĆö magnitude of the **Floquet multiplier** $|\lambda| < 1$ (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Setting

A continuous-time nonlinear dynamical system is described by an autonomous vector field:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}(t)), \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

The **flow map** $\phi_t : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ satisfies $\phi_0(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x}$ and $\frac{d}{dt}\phi_t(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{F}(\phi_t(\mathbf{x}))$.

### Construction of the Poincar├® Map (Structured Steps)

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Choose a transverse surface:** Select an $(n-1)$-dimensional hypersurface $\Sigma$ such that for every $\mathbf{x} \in \Sigma$, the vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ is **not tangent** to $\Sigma$ (transversality condition: $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$, where $\mathbf{n}$ is the unit normal to $\Sigma$).
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Define the first hitting time:** For an initial $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \Sigma$, define $\tau(\mathbf{x}_0) = \inf\{t > 0 : \phi_t(\mathbf{x}_0) \in \Sigma\}$.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the discrete map:** Set $\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_n) = \phi_{\tau(\mathbf{x}_n)}(\mathbf{x}_n)$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Iterate:** Plot the sequence $\{\mathbf{x}_0, \mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \ldots\}$ ŌĆö this is the **return map**.

### Why This Works (The "Why" and "How")

The continuous trajectory in $n$-dimensional space becomes a **discrete sequence of points** in $(n-1)$-dimensional space. This reduction in dimension makes:
- Visualization possible (2D or 3D maps are easy to plot)
- Bifurcation analysis tractable (one-parameter families of 1D maps)
- Periodicity detection immediate (fixed points of $\mathbf{P}$ = periodic orbits of $\mathbf{F}$)
- Chaos quantification feasible (Lyapunov exponents, fractal dimension of the return set)

## 2.2 Fixed Points and Stability

A **fixed point** of the Poincar├® map satisfies $\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ and corresponds to a **closed periodic orbit** of the original continuous system.

The **linearization** about $\mathbf{x}^*$ is governed by the **Floquet matrix** (monodromy matrix):

$$\mathbf{M} = \left.\frac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}^*}$$

with eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}$ called **Floquet multipliers**. The orbit is **stable** if and only if:

$$|\lambda_i| < 1 \quad \forall i$$

For a 1D return map $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$, the fixed point $x^*$ with $f'(x^*) = \lambda$ is:
- **Asymptotically stable** if $|\lambda| < 1$
- **Unstable** if $|\lambda| > 1$
- **Marginal (NeimarkŌĆōSacker bifurcation)** if $|\lambda| = 1$ (complex conjugate pair)

## 2.3 Period-Doubling Bifurcation

As a system parameter is varied, a stable fixed point may lose stability and give rise to a **period-2 orbit** when $\lambda = -1$. Successive period-doublings follow **Feigenbaum universality**:

$$\delta = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\mu_{n-1} - \mu_{n-2}}{\mu_n - \mu_{n-1}} \approx 4.6692016\ldots$$

This is the well-known **period-doubling route to chaos** observed in cardiac tissue (e.g., onset of ventricular fibrillation) and neural populations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| Continuous system | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ | Phase space evolution |
| Poincar├® map | $\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{P}(\mathbf{x}_n)$ | Discrete iteration on $\Sigma$ |
| Transversality | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$ | Section not tangent to flow |
| Monodromy matrix | $\mathbf{M} = \partial \mathbf{P} / \partial \mathbf{x} \big\vert_{\mathbf{x}^*}$ | Linearized return map |
| Stability | $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ | Asymptotically stable orbit |
| Period-doubling | $f'(x^*) = -1$ | Flip bifurcation onset |
| Feigenbaum constant | $\delta \approx 4.6692$ | Universality of chaos onset |
| SD1 (Poincar├® plot) | $\mathrm{SD1} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1}-RR_n)}$ | Short-term HRV (ms) |
| SD2 (Poincar├® plot) | $\mathrm{SD2} = \sqrt{2\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1}-RR_n)}$ | Long-term HRV (ms) |
| Cardiac ratio | $\mathrm{SD1/SD2}$ | Autonomic balance index |
| Lyapunov exponent | $\lambda = \lim_{N\to\infty}\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\ln \vert f'(x_n) \vert$ | Chaos detector (bit$^{-1}$) |
| Correlation dimension | $D_2 = \lim_{\epsilon\to 0}\tfrac{\ln C(\epsilon)}{\ln \epsilon}$ | Attractor complexity |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology:** Distinguishing healthy sinus rhythm (compact Poincar├® plot) from congestive heart failure (large SD1, small SD2) and atrial fibrillation (diffuse cloud).
- **Neurology:** Detecting pre-seizure states in EEG via return map geometry.
- **Anesthesia depth monitoring:** Loss of return map structure correlates with burst suppression.
- **Sleep staging:** Transition from REM to NREM alters the EEG return map topology.
- **Mechanical ventilation:** Return maps of respiratory flow reveal patientŌĆōventilator dyssynchrony.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reduction of a 2D Continuous System to a 1D Return Map

Consider the prototypical nonlinear oscillator (Van der Pol model relevant to cardiac pacemakers):

$$\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0, \quad \mu > 0$$

### Step A ŌĆö Write as a first-order 2D system

Let $x_1 = x$ and $x_2 = \dot{x}$. The system becomes:

$$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$
$$\dot{x}_2 = \mu(1 - x_1^2)\,x_2 - x_1$$

### Step B ŌĆö Choose a Poincar├® section

Take the **positive $x_1$-axis** as the transversal section:

$$\Sigma = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2 : x_2 = 0,\; x_1 > 0\}$$

The transversality condition is satisfied because $\dot{x}_2 \big\vert_{x_2=0} = -x_1 \neq 0$ for $x_1 \neq 0$.

### Step C ŌĆö Define the return map

Let $x_n$ denote the $n$-th positive crossing of the $x_1$-axis. The return map is then implicitly:

$$x_{n+1} = f_\mu(x_n)$$

For the **relaxation Van der Pol oscillator** ($\mu \gg 1$), analytical approximation yields the **logistic-like map** (Norman Baker's canonical reduction):

$$x_{n+1} \approx \alpha - x_n^2$$

with $\alpha = \alpha(\mu)$ being a monotonically increasing function of $\mu$. This is the **quadratic map**, the simplest route to chaos.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stability of a Period-1 Fixed Point

Let the return map be $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$ and suppose a fixed point $x^*$ exists such that $f(x^*) = x^*$.

### Step A ŌĆö Linearize around $x^*$

Let $x_n = x^* + \eta_n$ with $\eta_n$ small. A first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^* + \eta_{n+1} &= f(x^* + \eta_n) \\
&= f(x^*) + f'(x^*)\,\eta_n + \mathcal{O}(\eta_n^2) \\
&= x^* + f'(x^*)\,\eta_n + \mathcal{O}(\eta_n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B ŌĆö Subtract $x^*$ from both sides

$$
\eta_{n+1} = f'(x^*)\,\eta_n + \mathcal{O}(\eta_n^2)
$$

### Step C ŌĆö Iterate the linearized map

$$
\eta_n = \big[f'(x^*)\big]^n \eta_0
$$

### Step D ŌĆö Stability criterion

For $\eta_n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, we need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big|f'(x^*)\big| &< 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{stable} \\
\big|f'(x^*)\big| &> 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{unstable} \\
f'(x^*) &= -1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{flip (period-doubling) bifurcation}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SD1 and SD2 of the RR-Interval Poincar├® Plot

Let $RR_n$ denote the $n$-th normal-to-normal RR interval. Define the rotated coordinates:

$$u_n = \frac{RR_n - RR_{n+1}}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad v_n = \frac{RR_n + RR_{n+1}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

These represent axes along and perpendicular to the **identity line** $RR_{n+1} = RR_n$. The variances are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Var}(u) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n) = \mathrm{SD1}^2 \\
\mathrm{Var}(v) &= 2\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n) = \mathrm{SD2}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathrm{SD1} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n)}\;}, \quad
\boxed{\;\mathrm{SD2} = \sqrt{2\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n)}\;}
$$

**Geometric interpretation:**
- **SD1** ŌŖź to the identity line ŌćÆ captures **beat-to-beat** (short-term) variability, dominated by **parasympathetic** activity.
- **SD2** Ōłź to the identity line ŌćÆ captures **long-term** variability, dominated by **sympathetic + parasympathetic** activity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Map Type vs. Dynamics

| Return map signature | Underlying dynamics | Clinical/biological example |
|----------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|
| Single tight cluster on diagonal | Stable period-1 limit cycle | Healthy resting sinus rhythm |
| Two clusters on diagonal | Period-2 orbit | Bigeminy (alternating PVCs) |
| Dense 1D closed curve | Quasi-periodicity (torus) | CheyneŌĆōStokes respiration |
| Cloud of points (no structure) | Low-dimensional chaos | Atrial fibrillation |
| Filamentary fractal | Strange attractor | Epileptic seizure EEG |
| Sparse returns | Transient or unstable orbit | Post-PVC compensatory pause |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Poincar├® Analysis Pipeline for Biomedical Signals

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **end-to-end processing pipeline** for generating a Poincar├®/return map from a raw physiological signal ŌĆö a structure expected in KTU board answers for application-oriented questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Biomedical Signal] --> B[Bandpass Filtering 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[Event Detection R peaks or Spike Times]
    C --> D[Inter-event Interval Sequence I sub n]
    D --> E[Phase Space Embedding using Takens Theorem]
    E --> F[Define Transverse Section Sigma]
    F --> G[Poincare Intersection Detection]
    G --> H[Return Map Construction]
    H --> I[Fixed Point and Period Analysis]
    I --> J[Feature Extraction SD1, SD2, D2, SampEn]
    J --> K[Classification Normal, Pathological, Chaotic]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mermaid Diagram: Construction of a Return Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase Space Trajectory] -->|crosses section| S1[Section Sigma]
    S1 -->|point x0| M1[Return Map]
    S1 -->|point x1| M1
    S1 -->|point x2| M1
    S1 -->|point x3| M1
    M1 --> F[Fixed Point Detector]
    M1 --> PD[Period Doubling Detector]
    M1 --> L[Lyapunov Exponent]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stability Classification of a Fixed Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Return Map x sub n plus 1 equals f of x sub n] --> FP[Locate Fixed Point x star]
    FP --> D[Compute Derivative f prime at x star]
    D --> Q{Absolute f prime less than 1}
    Q -->|Yes| S[Stable Limit Cycle]
    Q -->|No| U[Unstable]
    U --> Q2{f prime equals minus 1}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PD[Period Doubling Bifurcation]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Complex pair magnitude 1}
    Q3 -->|Yes| NS[Neimark Sacker Torus Birth]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Poincar├® Plot Geometry

A clean ASCII schematic of an RR-interval Poincar├® plot with axes, identity line, and SD1/SD2 ellipse:

```
        RR(n+1)
          ^
          |
   SD2    |        . . .
  (long)  |     .       .   <-- elongated ellipse
          |    .  ___    .
          |   . /   \   .
          |  .  |  *  |  .     * = mean RR
          |   . \___/   .
          |    .       .
          |     . . . 
          |______________|____> RR(n)
                 ^
                SD1 (short, perpendicular to identity line)
```

The major axis (length $4 \times \mathrm{SD2}$) lies along the identity line, and the minor axis (length $4 \times \mathrm{SD1}$) lies perpendicular to it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**Define a Poincar├® section. Why is transversality an essential condition for its construction?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Poincar├® section** is a lower-dimensional hypersurface $\Sigma$ embedded in the phase space of a continuous dynamical system, chosen such that the system's trajectory **transversely intersects** $\Sigma$ during its evolution.

**Transversality is essential** because:

- It guarantees that the trajectory actually **crosses** $\Sigma$ rather than merely skimming along it.
- The vector field $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})$ at any point of $\Sigma$ must satisfy $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$ (where $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal), ensuring a **nonzero, well-defined return time** to $\Sigma$.
- Without transversality, the first-return map $\mathbf{P}$ is not differentiable, and Floquet stability analysis becomes invalid.

In the **biomedical context**, the section is often chosen as the plane $x = 0$ with $\dot{x} > 0$ in reconstructed cardiac phase space, allowing successive R-peak returns to be analysed for HRV.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a fixed point and a period-2 orbit in a 1D Poincar├® return map $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fixed Point (Period-1) | Period-2 Orbit |
|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| Defining equation | $x^* = f(x^*)$ | $x_a = f(x_b),\; x_b = f(x_a)$ with $x_a \neq x_b$ |
| Trajectory shape | Single point in return map | Two alternating points |
| Continuous-time equivalent | A closed limit cycle (period $T$) | A limit cycle traversed in time $2T$ |
| Stability | $\vert f'(x^*) \vert < 1$ | $\vert f'(x_a)\cdot f'(x_b) \vert < 1$ |
| Cardiac analogue | Steady sinus rhythm | Bigeminy / trigeminy |
| Emergence | Generic attractor | Arises via **flip bifurcation** when $f'(x^*) = -1$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **end-to-end procedure for constructing a Poincar├® return map** from a raw ECG signal. Identify the choice of section and justify it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **SD1 and SD2** descriptors of the RR-interval Poincar├® plot and interpret their physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Acquire the ECG:** Sample at 250ŌĆō1000 Hz to resolve R-peaks.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pre-process:** Apply a 0.5ŌĆō40 Hz bandpass filter to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise.

**Step 3 ŌĆö R-peak detection:** Use the **PanŌĆōTompkins algorithm** to obtain fiducial points $\{t_n\}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö RR-interval extraction:** Compute $RR_n = t_{n+1} - t_n$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Phase-space embedding (Takens):** Construct vectors $\mathbf{x}_n = [RR_n,\; RR_{n+\tau},\; RR_{n+2\tau}]$ with embedding delay $\tau$ chosen via the **first minimum of the mutual information** function.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Section choice:** The natural Poincar├® section for cardiac dynamics is the **plane $\Sigma = \{x_2 = 0,\; \dot{x}_2 > 0\}$** in the embedded phase space, equivalent in the time domain to **consecutive R-peaks** (i.e., $n \mapsto n+1$).

**Step 7 ŌĆö Return map:** Plot the pairs $(RR_n,\; RR_{n+1})$ ŌĆö this is the **Poincar├® plot**.

**Justification:** R-peak crossings are physiologically meaningful, align with the cardiac cycle's natural return, and satisfy transversality because the phase-space velocity at the R-peak is dominantly along the $x_2$-direction (changing RR interval).

#### Model Solution (b)

Refer to **Section 3.3** of these notes for the full derivation. The final results are:

$$
\mathrm{SD1} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n)}, \qquad
\mathrm{SD2} = \sqrt{2\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_n) - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{Var}(RR_{n+1} - RR_n)}
$$

**Physiological interpretation:**

- **SD1** (perpendicular to identity line): reflects **short-term, beat-to-beat variability**, mediated primarily by the **parasympathetic (vagal)** nervous system via respiratory sinus arrhythmia.
- **SD2** (along identity line): reflects **long-term variability**, jointly modulated by **sympathetic and parasympathetic** systems as well as thermoregulatory and circadian influences.
- **Ratio SD1/SD2:** a quantitative index of **autonomic balance** ŌĆö high in young healthy adults, reduced in elderly, diabetics, and post-myocardial-infarction patients.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and prove the **linear stability criterion** for a fixed point of a 1D Poincar├® map $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **period-doubling route to chaos** with reference to the **Feigenbaum constant**, and discuss its relevance in identifying cardiac arrhythmias. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Statement:** A fixed point $x^*$ of the map $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$ (with $f$ continuously differentiable) is **asymptotically stable** if and only if $\vert f'(x^*) \vert < 1$.

**Proof** ŌĆö see Section 3.2, Steps AŌĆōD. The linearized map $\eta_{n+1} = f'(x^*)\,\eta_n$ has solution $\eta_n = [f'(x^*)]^n \eta_0$, which converges to zero iff $\vert f'(x^*) \vert < 1$. ŌłÄ

**Corollaries:**
- $f'(x^*) > 1$: monotonic divergence (unstable node).
- $f'(x^*) < -1$: oscillatory divergence (flip instability).
- $f'(x^*) = -1$: **period-doubling (flip) bifurcation**.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Period-doubling route to chaos:**

As a system parameter $\mu$ increases through a sequence of critical values $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3, \ldots$, a stable fixed point of $f_\mu$ undergoes successive period-doublings, producing orbits of period $2, 4, 8, \ldots, 2^k, \ldots$ until an accumulation point $\mu_\infty$ is reached beyond which the dynamics is **chaotic**.

**Feigenbaum universal constant:**

$$
\delta = \lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\mu_{k-1} - \mu_{k-2}}{\mu_k - \mu_{k-1}} \approx 4.6692016\ldots
$$

This constant is **universal** ŌĆö it does not depend on the specific map, only on the quadratic maximum (single-hump) nature of $f_\mu$. It was first discovered by Mitchell Feigenbaum in 1975.

**Cardiac relevance:**

- Experimentally observed in **isolated cardiac tissue** and **numerical heart models** as the pacing rate or sympathetic drive is increased.
- The onset of **ventricular fibrillation** has been linked to a period-doubling cascade in certain rabbit and pig heart preparations (Witkowski, Chialvo, et al.).
- Clinically, observing period-doubling in the return map of RR intervals can serve as an **early warning biomarker** of impending arrhythmia ŌĆö occurring hours before fibrillation onset.
- This motivates **closed-loop defibrillators** that detect Feigenbaum-like scaling in real time and deliver preemptive pacing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do **not** confuse the **Poincar├® section** (a geometric surface in phase space) with the **Poincar├® plot** (a 2D scatter of $RR_{n+1}$ vs $RR_n$ in cardiology). The former is a construction tool; the latter is a diagnostic display.
> - The stability criterion uses $\vert f'(x^*) \vert$, **not** $f'(x^*) < 0$ alone. Many students write only the negative-slope condition and lose 2 marks.
> - SD1 measures variability **perpendicular to the identity line**, SD2 measures **along** it ŌĆö confusing this orientation costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> - Feigenbaum's constant is **universal** for 1D quadratic maps; do not state it depends on the biological system.
> - The cardiac ratio SD1/SD2 is **dimensionless**; do not write units of ms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Poincar├® section** $\Sigma$: an $(n-1)$-dimensional hypersurface in the $n$-dimensional phase space, chosen so that the flow crosses it **transversally**.
- **Poincar├® map** $\mathbf{P}$: the discrete map obtained by recording successive intersections of the trajectory with $\Sigma$.
- **Transversality condition:** $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) \cdot \mathbf{n}(\mathbf{x}) \neq 0$ on $\Sigma$.
- A **fixed point** of $\mathbf{P}$ corresponds to a **periodic orbit** of the original continuous system.
- **Stability criterion:** $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1$ for all Floquet multipliers.
- **Period-doubling bifurcation** occurs when a real multiplier passes through $-1$.
- **Feigenbaum constant** $\delta \approx 4.6692$ is **universal** for quadratic maps.
- **Return map signature ŌåÆ dynamics:**
  - Compact cloud on diagonal ŌćÆ stable rhythm
  - Multiple clusters ŌćÆ higher-period orbit
  - Diffuse cloud ŌćÆ chaos / fibrillation
- **SD1** ŌŖź identity line ŌćÆ short-term HRV (parasympathetic).
- **SD2** Ōłź identity line ŌćÆ long-term HRV (sympathetic + parasympathetic).
- **Cardiac ratio** SD1/SD2 reflects autonomic balance.
- **Biomedical applications:** HRV analysis, EEG seizure detection, anesthesia depth, respiratory dynamics, neural spike trains.
- **Takens' embedding theorem** justifies reconstructing the phase space from a single time series via delay coordinates $(x_n, x_{n+\tau}, x_{n+2\tau}, \ldots)$.
- The return map is a **dimension-reduction tool** ŌĆö turning an intractable continuous flow into a finite, visualizable discrete dataset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chaos
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chaos

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Chaos (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> In the context of biomedical signal processing, **chaos** refers to the deterministic, bounded, and aperiodic behavior exhibited by certain nonlinear dynamical systems that exhibit **extreme sensitivity to initial conditions**. A chaotic signal is generated by a fully deterministic rule (governed by nonlinear differential or difference equations), yet its long-term evolution appears random and is practically unpredictable, even though all governing equations are precisely known.

A signal is classified as **chaotic** if it satisfies the following three mathematical properties (the Devaney triad, widely adopted in KTU signal-processing modules):

1. **Determinism with Aperiodicity:** The system is governed by deterministic equations (no random input), yet its trajectory never repeats itself exactly.
2. **Sensitive Dependence on Initial Conditions (SDIC):** Two trajectories starting arbitrarily close in state space diverge exponentially with time.
3. **Topological Mixing / Dense Orbits:** Every open region of the state space will eventually be visited by any trajectory (topological transitivity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Within biomedical engineering, chaos theory is applied to demonstrate that signals such as **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)**, **EEG rhythms**, and **respiratory patterns** are not merely "noise" but rather the output of low-dimensional nonlinear deterministic systems. This has direct clinical implications for **epileptic seizure detection**, **cardiac arrhythmia prediction**, and **sleep-stage classification**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Butterfly & The Pendulum Analogy**
> Imagine two perfectly identical grandfather clocks hung side by side, started with weights that differ by the tiniest, invisible amount (say, the weight of a single grain of sand). After just one day, the pendulums swing in sync. After one month, they drift apart. After one year, they behave in **completely unrelated ways**, even though both were started from nearly identical conditions. This is **sensitivity to initial conditions**.
>
> Now, a chaotic biomedical signal (e.g., a healthy heart's RR-interval series) behaves like this pendulum: it is **deterministic** (the heart's pacemaker obeys strict nonlinear biophysical laws), **bounded** (heart rate stays between ~40ŌĆō180 bpm), and **aperiodic** (no two consecutive heartbeats are mathematically identical). A diseased heart, in contrast, often loses this rich nonlinear richness and becomes more **periodic** or **random** ŌĆö both deviations from healthy chaos.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Markers of Chaos

The following **invariant metrics** are used throughout the KTU module to objectively classify a signal as chaotic:

- **Lyapunov Exponent ($\lambda$):** Measures the rate of exponential divergence of nearby trajectories. $\lambda > 0 \Rightarrow$ chaos.
- **Correlation Dimension ($D_2$):** Measures the complexity/degrees of freedom of the underlying dynamical system.
- **Approximate Entropy ($ApEn$):** Quantifies the regularity and unpredictability of fluctuations in a time series.
- **Hurst Exponent ($H$):** Distinguishes persistent ($H > 0.5$), random ($H = 0.5$), and anti-persistent ($H < 0.5$) behavior.
- **Fractal Dimension ($D_f$):** Quantifies the self-similarity of the strange attractor in phase space.

### 1.4 Distinguishing Chaos from Randomness and Periodicity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Student Confusion ŌĆö Resolved**
>
> | Property | Periodic | Random | **Chaotic** |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
> | Deterministic rule? | Yes | No | **Yes** |
> | Long-term predictable? | Yes | No | **No** |
> | Bounded trajectory? | Yes | No (can diverge) | **Yes** |
> | Power spectrum | Discrete peaks (harmonics) | Broadband (flat) | **Broadband with structure (1/f-like)** |
> | Sensitive to initial conditions? | No | Yes (irrelevantly) | **Yes (exponentially)** |
> | Phase space portrait | Closed loop (limit cycle) | Diffuse cloud | **Strange attractor (fractal)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase Space Reconstruction (Takens' Embedding Theorem)

A scalar biomedical time series $x(1), x(2), \ldots, x(N)$ cannot directly reveal the underlying dynamics. We must reconstruct the **phase space** using delay coordinates.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Choose Time Delay ($\tau$):**
The optimal time delay is selected as the **first minimum of the Average Mutual Information (AMI)** function:

$$AMI(\tau) = \sum_{i,j} p_{ij}(\tau) \cdot \log_2 \left[ \frac{p_{ij}(\tau)}{p_i \cdot p_j} \right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Choose Embedding Dimension ($m$):**
The minimum embedding dimension is found using the **False Nearest Neighbors (FNN)** method, where the embedding dimension is the smallest $m$ for which the FNN percentage drops below ~5ŌĆō10%.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Construct Delay Vectors:**
The reconstructed phase-space vectors (orbits) are formed as:

$$\mathbf{X}(n) = \bigl[ x(n),\ x(n+\tau),\ x(n+2\tau),\ \ldots,\ x(n+(m-1)\tau) \bigr]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Takens' Theorem (1981):**
> If the embedding dimension $m \geq 2d + 1$ (where $d$ is the true box-counting dimension of the attractor), then the delay-coordinate map is a *diffeomorphism* of the true underlying dynamics. In practice, $m \geq 5$ suffices for most biological signals.

### 2.2 Lyapunov Exponent ŌĆö The Heart of Chaos Detection

The **largest Lyapunov exponent** $\lambda_1$ is the principal indicator of chaos. Given two initially close trajectories separated by $\delta_0$ at $t=0$, the separation at time $t$ evolves as:

$$\vert \delta(t) \vert \approx \vert \delta_0 \vert \cdot e^{\lambda_1 t}$$

The Wolf algorithm (used by MATLAB's `lyapunovExponent`) estimates $\lambda_1$ via:

$$\lambda_1 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N \cdot \Delta t} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \ln \frac{\vert \delta(t_k) \vert}{\vert \delta(t_{k-1}) \vert}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\lambda_1 > 0 \Rightarrow$ **Chaos** (exponential divergence ŌĆö the hallmark of chaos).
- $\lambda_1 = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Limit cycle** (purely periodic behavior).
- $\lambda_1 < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Fixed point** (stable equilibrium).

### 2.3 Correlation Dimension ($D_2$) ŌĆö GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia Algorithm

The **correlation integral** $C(r)$ counts the fraction of pairs of points on the reconstructed attractor that lie within distance $r$ of each other:

$$C(r) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} \Theta\bigl(r - \Vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \Vert \bigr)$$

where $\Theta(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function. For small $r$, $C(r)$ obeys a power law:

$$C(r) \propto r^{D_2}$$

Hence, the **correlation dimension** is computed as the slope of the linear region in the log-log plot:

$$D_2 = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$$

### 2.4 Approximate Entropy ($ApEn$)

A real-world, finite-data-friendly measure of irregularity. For embedding dimension $m$ and tolerance $r$:

$$ApEn(m, r, N) = \Phi^m(r) - \Phi^{m+1}(r)$$

where $\Phi^m(r)$ is the average logarithmic conditional probability that two sequences of length $m$ that are similar remain similar when one more sample is appended.

- $ApEn \to 0 \Rightarrow$ Highly regular / periodic.
- $ApEn \to 2 \Rightarrow$ Highly random / uncorrelated noise.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Form | KTU Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Lyapunov exponent | $\lambda_1 = \lim\limits_{t \to \infty} \frac{1}{t} \ln \dfrac{\vert \delta(t) \vert}{\vert \delta_0 \vert}$ | Distinguishing chaos from noise in EEG |
| 2 | Correlation integral | $C(r) = \dfrac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i<j} \Theta(r - \Vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \Vert)$ | Computing $D_2$ (HRV complexity) |
| 3 | Correlation dimension | $D_2 = \lim\limits_{r \to 0} \dfrac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$ | Estimating degrees of freedom of cardiac system |
| 4 | Takens delay vector | $\mathbf{X}(n) = [x(n), x(n+\tau), \ldots, x(n+(m-1)\tau)]$ | Phase space reconstruction |
| 5 | Average Mutual Info | $AMI(\tau) = \sum p_{ij} \log_2 \dfrac{p_{ij}}{p_i p_j}$ | Optimal $\tau$ selection |
| 6 | Approximate Entropy | $ApEn = \Phi^m(r) - \Phi^{m+1}(r)$ | Regularity in RR-intervals |
| 7 | Hurst Exponent | $R/S = (c \cdot n)^H$ | Long-range dependence in gait signals |
| 8 | KaplanŌĆōYorke dimension | $D_{KY} = j + \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} \lambda_i}{\vert \lambda_{j+1} \vert}$ | Linking Lyapunov spectrum to fractal dim |
| 9 | R├Čssler system | $\dot{x} = -y - z$, $\dot{y} = x + ay$, $\dot{z} = b + z(x - c)$ | Canonical chaotic reference model |
| 10 | Logistic map | $x_{n+1} = r \cdot x_n (1 - x_n)$, chaotic for $r \in [3.57, 4]$ | Simplest 1-D chaotic generator |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology:** Reduced $D_2$ of HRV is a clinical warning sign for patients at risk of sudden cardiac death.
- **Neurology:** Increased $\lambda_1$ and altered $D_2$ in EEG mark transition into epileptic seizure.
- **Sleep Medicine:** Distinct $ApEn$ signatures across wake/REM/NREM stages.
- **Wearable Devices:** Real-time chaos-based features in fitness ECG patches for arrhythmia warning.
- **Biomechanics:** Hurst exponent of gait signals distinguishes healthy vs. Parkinson's-affected walking patterns.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Logistic Map to Lyapunov Exponent

The **logistic map** is the simplest 1-D nonlinear system that exhibits chaos. It models population dynamics:

$$x_{n+1} = f(x_n) = r \cdot x_n (1 - x_n), \quad x_n \in [0, 1],\ r \in [0, 4]$$

**Step A: Identify the derivative of the map.**
The sensitivity of $x_{n+1}$ to perturbations in $x_n$ is governed by the magnitude of $f'(x_n)$:

$$f'(x_n) = r \cdot (1 - 2x_n)$$

**Step B: Construct the perturbation evolution.**
Let two initial conditions be $x_0$ and $x_0 + \epsilon_0$. After one iteration, their separation becomes:

$$\epsilon_1 = f'(x_0) \cdot \epsilon_0 = r(1 - 2x_0)\,\epsilon_0$$

**Step C: Iterate $N$ times.**
After $N$ iterations, using the chain rule (since each step multiplies the separation by the local derivative):

$$\epsilon_N = \epsilon_0 \cdot \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} f'(x_k) = \epsilon_0 \cdot \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} r(1 - 2x_k)$$

**Step D: Take the natural logarithm and the limit.**
Applying $\ln$ to both sides and dividing by $N$:

$$\frac{1}{N} \ln \left\vert \frac{\epsilon_N}{\epsilon_0} \right\vert = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \ln \left\vert r(1 - 2x_k) \right\vert$$

**Step E: Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**
The Birkhoff Ergodic Theorem guarantees convergence to the time-average (since the orbit visits the attractor ergodically):

$$\lambda_1 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \ln \left\vert r(1 - 2x_k) \right\vert$$

This is the closed-form expression for the **largest Lyapunov exponent** of the logistic map, which becomes positive for $r > r_\infty \approx 3.5699$ (the Feigenbaum accumulation point), confirming the onset of chaos.

**Step F: Interpretation.**
For $r = 4$, the orbit is fully chaotic and:

$$\lambda_1 = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \ \text{nats/iteration}$$

This is the maximum achievable Lyapunov exponent for a 1-D map on $[0, 1]$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia Correlation Dimension

**Step A: Set up the pair-distance count.**
Given $N$ reconstructed delay vectors $\{\mathbf{X}_1, \mathbf{X}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_N\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^m$, define the Heaviside-weighted pair count:

$$C_m(r) = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq N} \Theta\!\left(r - \Vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \Vert \right)$$

**Step B: Identify the scaling law.**
For a true fractal attractor of dimension $D_2$, the number of points within a ball of radius $r$ grows as $r^{D_2}$. Therefore, for sufficiently small $r$ and sufficiently large $N$:

$$C_m(r) \propto r^{D_2}$$

**Step C: Linearize via logarithms.**
Taking $\ln$ of both sides:

$$\ln C_m(r) = D_2 \cdot \ln r + \text{const.}$$

**Step D: Compute the slope.**
The correlation dimension is the slope of the linear (scaling) region:

$$D_2 = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{\partial \ln C_m(r)}{\partial \ln r}$$

**Step E: Saturation test for chaos.**
A key diagnostic ŌĆö if $D_2$ **saturates** to a finite, non-integer value as $m$ increases, the signal is **deterministic chaos**. If $D_2$ keeps growing with $m$, the signal is **stochastic (random)**. This is the famous GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia test used to detect low-dimensional chaos in HRV and EEG.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö R├Čssler Strange Attractor (Canonical 3-D Chaotic Model)

The R├Čssler system is given by:

$$\dot{x} = -y - z$$

$$\dot{y} = x + a \cdot y$$

$$\dot{z} = b + z(x - c)$$

**Step A: Equilibria.**
Setting the RHS to zero, equilibrium requires $y = -z$ and $x = -ay = az$. Substituting into the third equation:

$$b + az \cdot (az - c) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a^2 z^2 - acz + b = 0$$

$$z_{\pm} = \frac{ac \pm \sqrt{a^2 c^2 - 4 a^2 b}}{2 a^2} = \frac{c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4b}}{2a}$$

For the standard parameters $a = 0.2$, $b = 0.2$, $c = 5.7$, the equilibrium is unstable, and trajectories spiral outward and fold back in, producing the famous **R├Čssler strange attractor**.

**Step B: Linearization around equilibrium.**
The Jacobian matrix at any state is:

$$J = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 & -1 \\ 1 & a & 0 \\ z & 0 & x - c \end{pmatrix}$$

At the equilibrium, the eigenvalues of $J$ determine stability. For the standard parameters, one eigenvalue has a positive real part, causing the exponential divergence (positive $\lambda_1$) that defines chaos.

**Step C: Lyapunov spectrum.**
Numerically (e.g., Benettin's algorithm), the three Lyapunov exponents of the R├Čssler system are:

$$\lambda_1 \approx 0.0714, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.0000, \quad \lambda_3 \approx -5.3943 \ \text{(time-units)}^{-1}$$

**Step D: KaplanŌĆōYorke Dimension.**
The KaplanŌĆōYorke conjecture provides the connection between the Lyapunov spectrum and the fractal dimension of the attractor:

$$D_{KY} = j + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} \lambda_i}{\vert \lambda_{j+1} \vert}$$

Ordering $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \lambda_3$:

- $j = 1$: $\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 0.0714$. Since $\vert \lambda_3 \vert = 5.3943 \gg 0.0714$, we have:

$$D_{KY} = 1 + \frac{0.0714}{5.3943} \approx 1 + 0.0132 \approx 1.0132$$

- $j = 2$: $\sum_{i=1}^{2} \lambda_i = 0.0714$ is barely positive, so $D_{KY}$ stays just above 1, matching numerical estimates of the R├Čssler attractor dimension (which is "almost" a 2-D manifold with a fractal folding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU Board Exams:**
> Examiners love testing whether students know that **a 3-D continuous system can produce chaos** (R├Čssler, Lorenz) while **2-D continuous systems cannot** (Poincar├®ŌĆōBendixson theorem). This is a high-yield fact for 7-mark derivations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Wolf Algorithm Step Trace (Lyapunov Exponent from Real Data)

This is the algorithm examiners expect students to describe for **practical biomedical signal analysis**:

**Step 1:** Reconstruct the phase space using Takens' embedding with the chosen $\tau$ and $m$.

**Step 2:** Pick a reference point $\mathbf{X}_0$ on the trajectory. Find its nearest neighbor $\mathbf{X}_0'$ at distance $\delta_0 = \Vert \mathbf{X}_0 - \mathbf{X}_0' \Vert$.

**Step 3:** Advance both points by a fixed evolution time $T$ along the trajectory to get $\mathbf{X}_1$ and $\mathbf{X}_1'$.

**Step 4:** Record the new separation $\delta_1 = \Vert \mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_1' \Vert$. The local stretching factor is $\delta_1 / \delta_0$.

**Step 5:** At time $T$, replace $\mathbf{X}_1'$ with a new nearest neighbor of $\mathbf{X}_1$ (preserving direction of separation as far as possible) and re-measure $\delta_1$.

**Step 6:** Repeat for the entire trajectory length and average:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{k \cdot T} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \ln \frac{\delta_i'}{\delta_{i-1}}$$

where $\delta_i'$ is the replacement-pair distance and $k$ is the number of replacement steps.

**Step 7:** Sanity check ŌĆö for purely periodic data, $\lambda_1 \to 0$; for white noise, $\lambda_1 \to \infty$; for chaos, $\lambda_1$ converges to a finite positive value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Chaos-Based Biomedical Analyzer

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **complete processing pipeline** used in KTU-aligned chaos analysis of ECG/EEG signals. It maps the data flow from raw acquisition through phase-space reconstruction to clinical decision support.

```mermaid

    A[Raw Biomedical Signal] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B1 --> B2[Baseline Wander Removal]
    B2 --> B3[R Peak / Spike Detection]
    B3 --> C[Feature Extraction Stage]

    C --> D1[Takens Delay Embedding]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Average Mutual Info]
    D2 --> D3[False Nearest Neighbors FNN Test]

    D3 --> E1[Lyapunov Exponent Lambda 1]
    D3 --> E2[Correlation Dimension D2]
    D3 --> E3[Approximate Entropy ApEn]
    D3 --> E4[Hurst Exponent H]

    E1 --> F[Classifier / Decision Layer]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F

    F --> G1[Normal Healthy State]
    F --> G2[Pathological State]
    F --> G3[Pre-seizure / Pre-arrhythmia Warning]

    G2 --> H[Clinical Decision Support]
    G3 --> H

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff

```

---

### 4.2 Conceptual State Diagram ŌĆö Healthy vs. Pathological Dynamics

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> HealthyChaos
    HealthyChaos : Healthy Chaos\nHigh D2, lambda1 approx 0.07, ApEn approx 1.2
    HealthyChaos --> PrePathological : Stress / Early Disease
    PrePathological : Reduced Complexity\nD2 falls, lambda1 falls, ApEn falls
    PrePathological --> Pathological : Full Disease Onset
    Pathological : Periodic or Random\nD2 approx 1, lambda1 near 0, ApEn very low
    Pathological --> CriticalEvent : Sudden Cardiac Death / Seizure
    CriticalEvent : Loss of Complexity\nApproaching Fixed Point Behavior
    CriticalEvent --> [*]
    PrePathological --> HealthyChaos : Recovery / Treatment
```

### 4.3 Workflow Diagram ŌĆö GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1D Time Series] --> B[Choose tau via AMI]
    B --> C[Choose m via FNN]
    C --> D[Build Delay Vectors]
    D --> E[Compute Pairwise Distances]
    E --> F[Count Pairs within Radius r]
    F --> G[Compute Correlation Integral C r]
    G --> H[Log Log Plot of C r vs r]
    H --> I{Linear Region Exists?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Slope gives D2]
    I -- No --> K[Increase m and Retry]
    J --> L{D2 Saturates with m?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Low Dimensional Chaos Confirmed]
    L -- No --> N[Stochastic Random Signal]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 4, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a). Define chaos in the context of biomedical signals. List any two characteristic properties of a chaotic system.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Chaos refers to the deterministic, bounded, and aperiodic long-term behavior of a nonlinear dynamical system that is highly sensitive to initial conditions. In biomedical signal processing, chaos theory is used to characterize signals such as HRV and EEG, which appear random but are governed by deterministic nonlinear rules.

**Two characteristic properties:**
1. **Sensitive Dependence on Initial Conditions (SDIC):** Trajectories starting from arbitrarily close initial conditions diverge exponentially with time. Quantified by a positive largest Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_1 > 0$.
2. **Aperiodicity combined with Determinism:** The signal never repeats exactly, yet the underlying system is fully deterministic with no random input.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 4, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b). What is a strange attractor? How is its dimension related to the Lyapunov spectrum of the underlying chaotic system?** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
A **strange attractor** is the bounded set in phase space toward which a chaotic trajectory asymptotically converges, while never settling into a periodic orbit. Geometrically, it has a non-integer (fractal) dimension and exhibits self-similar structure at every scale.

The dimension of the strange attractor is linked to the Lyapunov spectrum via the **KaplanŌĆōYorke conjecture**:

$$D_{KY} = j + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} \lambda_i}{\vert \lambda_{j+1} \vert}$$

where the $\lambda_i$ are sorted in descending order and $j$ is the largest integer for which the partial sum $\sum_{i=1}^{j} \lambda_i$ remains non-negative. This relation connects the geometric complexity of the attractor to the dynamical stretching/contracting rates along its principal axes.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Style ŌĆö Internal Choice Format**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **phase-space reconstruction** of a 1-D biomedical time series using **Takens' delay-coordinate embedding theorem**. State clearly the role of (i) the time delay $\tau$ and (ii) the embedding dimension $m$, and describe one method each for their selection. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Derive the largest Lyapunov exponent expression for the **logistic map** $x_{n+1} = r x_n (1 - x_n)$ and discuss the onset of chaos as a function of the parameter $r$. Compute $\lambda_1$ for the special case $r = 4$. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Block Diagram of Phase-Space Reconstruction:**

```
[1-D Time Series x(1)..x(N)]
          |
          v
  +----------------------+
  |  Compute AMI(tau)    |  --->  tau* = first minimum of AMI
  +----------------------+
          |
          v
  +----------------------+
  |  FNN Test (vary m)   |  --->  m* = smallest m with FNN < 5%
  +----------------------+
          |
          v
[Delay-coordinate vectors]
  X(n) = [x(n), x(n+tau), ..., x(n+(m-1)tau)]
          |
          v
[Reconstructed Phase Space in R^m]
```

**Selection of $\tau$:**
- **Average Mutual Information (AMI) method:** Compute $AMI(\tau) = \sum p_{ij}(\tau) \log_2[p_{ij}(\tau)/(p_i p_j)]$. Choose $\tau$ as the first minimum of $AMI(\tau)$. This choice maximizes the *new information* added by each delayed coordinate, avoiding both redundancy (small $\tau$) and irrelevance (large $\tau$).

**Selection of $m$:**
- **False Nearest Neighbors (FNN) method:** Compute the percentage of points whose neighbors in dimension $m$ become "false" (i.e., far apart) when projected into dimension $m+1$. Choose the smallest $m$ for which FNN < 5ŌĆō10%.

**Role of Takens' Theorem:**
For a smooth dynamical system with attractor of dimension $d$, an embedding of dimension $m \geq 2d + 1$ is a *diffeomorphism* of the true dynamics. In practice $m \geq 5$ is sufficient for biological signals.

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Write the map and its derivative:

$$f(x) = r x (1 - x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad f'(x) = r(1 - 2x)$$

**Step 2:** Express the $N$-step perturbation growth. Let $\epsilon_0$ be the initial separation between two close trajectories. After one iteration:

$$\epsilon_1 = f'(x_0)\,\epsilon_0$$

**Step 3:** After $N$ iterations (chain rule):

$$\epsilon_N = \epsilon_0 \cdot \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} f'(x_k) = \epsilon_0 \prod_{k=0}^{N-1} r(1 - 2x_k)$$

**Step 4:** Take the logarithm:

$$\ln\left\vert \frac{\epsilon_N}{\epsilon_0} \right\vert = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \ln\left\vert r(1 - 2x_k) \right\vert$$

**Step 5:** Define the Lyapunov exponent as the time-average:

$$\lambda_1 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \ln\left\vert r(1 - 2x_k) \right\vert$$

**Step 6:** Discuss onset of chaos.
- For $r \in [1, 3]$: stable fixed point, $\lambda_1 < 0$.
- For $r \in [3, 3.5699]$: period-doubling cascade, $\lambda_1 = 0$ on the periodic windows.
- For $r > r_\infty \approx 3.5699$: chaos, $\lambda_1 > 0$.

**Step 7:** Special case $r = 4$. For $r=4$, the invariant density is $\rho(x) = 1/[\pi\sqrt{x(1-x)}]$ on $[0,1]$. Compute the average:

$$\lambda_1 = \int_0^1 \ln\bigl\vert 4(1 - 2x) \bigr\vert \cdot \rho(x)\,dx = \ln 4 \cdot \int_0^1 \rho(x)\,dx + \int_0^1 \ln\vert 1 - 2x \vert \cdot \rho(x)\,dx$$

By symmetry $\int \ln\vert 1 - 2x\vert \cdot \rho(x)\,dx = 0$ (odd under $x \to 1-x$). Since $\int_0^1 \rho(x)\,dx = 1$:

$$\lambda_1 = \ln 4 - \ln 2 = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931 \ \text{nats/iteration}$$

Hence the logistic map is maximally chaotic at $r=4$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia algorithm** for computing the **correlation dimension $D_2$** of a reconstructed attractor. Show mathematically that saturation of $D_2$ with increasing embedding dimension $m$ confirms low-dimensional chaos, whereas non-saturation indicates stochastic randomness. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Apply the **Wolf algorithm** conceptually to a 300-sample EEG epoch and describe how you would obtain the largest Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_1$. State the clinical interpretation of $\lambda_1$ for distinguishing pre-ictal, ictal, and inter-ictal EEG states in epilepsy. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Reconstruct the attractor in $\mathbb{R}^m$ using delay vectors $\mathbf{X}_i$ with optimal $\tau$ and varying $m$.

**Step 2:** Define the correlation integral:

$$C_m(r) = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{1 \leq i < j \leq N} \Theta\bigl(r - \Vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \Vert\bigr)$$

**Step 3:** For a fractal attractor with correlation dimension $D_2$, $C_m(r) \sim r^{D_2}$ for small $r$. Hence:

$$\ln C_m(r) = D_2 \cdot \ln r + \text{const.} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_2 = \frac{d \ln C_m(r)}{d \ln r}$$

**Step 4:** Saturation logic. As $m$ is increased:
- **Low-dimensional chaos:** $D_2$ grows initially but **saturates** at a finite non-integer value (the true dimension of the attractor) once $m \geq m_\text{crit}$. This is the GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia chaos signature.
- **Stochastic signal:** $D_2$ **does not saturate**; it continues to grow with $m$ (ultimately approaching $m$ itself, since random data has no intrinsic low-dimensional structure).

**Step 5:** Procedure to confirm chaos:
1. Plot $\ln C_m(r)$ vs $\ln r$ for multiple $m$ values.
2. Identify the linear (scaling) region in each plot and extract its slope.
3. Plot $D_2$ vs $m$. Saturation = chaos; non-saturation = noise.

**Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):**

**Wolf Algorithm applied to a 300-sample EEG epoch:**

**Step 1:** Reconstruct EEG phase space using Takens' embedding with $m=5$ and $\tau$ from AMI.

**Step 2:** Choose a reference point $\mathbf{X}_0$ and its nearest neighbor $\mathbf{X}_0'$ at distance $\delta_0 = \Vert \mathbf{X}_0 - \mathbf{X}_0' \Vert$.

**Step 3:** Advance both points along the trajectory by an evolution time $T$ (typically one period or 5ŌĆō10 samples). Record new distance $\delta_1 = \Vert \mathbf{X}_1 - \mathbf{X}_1' \Vert$.

**Step 4:** Replace $\mathbf{X}_1'$ with a new nearest neighbor of $\mathbf{X}_1$ that preserves the direction of separation. Record $\delta_1'$.

**Step 5:** Repeat until trajectory is exhausted. Aggregate:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{k \cdot T} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \ln \frac{\delta_i'}{\delta_{i-1}}$$

**Clinical interpretation for epilepsy:**

| EEG State | $\lambda_1$ (typical) | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inter-ictal** (normal) | $\lambda_1 > 0$ but moderate ($\approx 0.01$ to $0.05$ per sample) | Healthy nonlinear dynamics |
| **Pre-ictal** (minutes before seizure) | $\lambda_1$ **decreases** (loss of complexity) | Pre-seizure warning ŌĆö clinically valuable for implantable warning devices |
| **Ictal** (during seizure) | $\lambda_1$ approaches **0** or even slightly negative | Hypersynchronous, near-periodic discharge dominates |
| **Post-ictal** | $\lambda_1$ gradually recovers to baseline | Brain returning to complex dynamics |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse chaos with noise.** A common error is reporting positive $\lambda_1$ for white noise and concluding "chaos detected." White noise gives an *unbounded* apparent $\lambda_1$ that grows with the number of samples and does not saturate. Always validate using the correlation dimension saturation test.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the Takens prerequisites.** Examiners specifically deduct marks if you compute $D_2$ or $\lambda_1$ without first stating that the data is stationary, sampled at sufficient frequency, and reconstructed using optimal $\tau$ and $m$.
> 3. **Do NOT misreport units.** Lyapunov exponent is reported in **nats per sample** (or **bits/sample** if $\log_2$ is used). Mixing units loses 1 mark.
> 4. **The KaplanŌĆōYorke relation is a conjecture, not a theorem.** Examiners penalize if you present it as mathematically proven.
> 5. **Embedding dimension $m$ must be chosen via FNN, not by guessing.** Hard-coding $m=3$ or $m=10$ without justification is a frequent deduction trigger.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**
>
> - **Chaos** = deterministic + bounded + aperiodic + **SDIC** (positive $\lambda_1$).
> - **Three classical properties:** aperiodicity, sensitive dependence, topological transitivity (Devaney).
> - **Takens' delay embedding:** $\mathbf{X}(n) = [x(n), x(n+\tau), \ldots, x(n+(m-1)\tau)]$.
> - **Optimal $\tau$** = first minimum of **Average Mutual Information (AMI)**.
> - **Optimal $m$** = smallest dimension where **False Nearest Neighbors (FNN) < 5%**.
> - **Largest Lyapunov exponent** $\lambda_1$ quantifies chaos; positive $\lambda_1 \Leftrightarrow$ chaos.
> - **Wolf algorithm** is the practical way to estimate $\lambda_1$ from real biomedical data.
> - **Correlation dimension $D_2$** via **GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia** algorithm; saturation with $m$ confirms low-dimensional chaos.
> - **Approximate Entropy ($ApEn$):** $0 =$ regular, $\to 2 =$ random.
> - **Hurst Exponent $H$:** $H > 0.5$ persistent, $H = 0.5$ random, $H < 0.5$ anti-persistent.
> - **R├Čssler / Lorenz** systems are canonical continuous 3-D chaotic models; logistic map is the canonical 1-D discrete chaotic model.
> - **Logistic map at $r = 4$:** $\lambda_1 = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ nats/iteration (maximum chaos).
> - **KaplanŌĆōYorke dimension** $D_{KY}$ links the Lyapunov spectrum to the attractor's fractal dimension.
> - **Biomedical applications:** HRV analysis (cardiology), EEG seizure detection (neurology), gait analysis (Parkinson's), sleep staging.
> - **Clinical insight:** Healthy physiology = **rich chaos**; pathology = **reduced complexity** (towards periodicity or randomness).
> - **Common pitfall:** chaotic signal $\neq$ random signal; chaotic = deterministic rule, low-dimensional attractor; random = no rule, infinite-dimensional.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measures of Nonlinear Signals and Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measures of Nonlinear Signals and Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Nonlinear Signals and Systems

A **nonlinear signal** is one whose underlying generating dynamics cannot be described by a linear superposition principle ŌĆö the whole is not equal to the sum of its parts. A **nonlinear system** is one whose output does not scale proportionally with its input, and whose behaviour depends on the *state* of the system, not just the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A nonlinear dynamical system is one whose evolution is governed by equations in which the dependent variable (or its derivatives) appear in a non-linear fashion (e.g., products, powers, exponentials, trigonometric functions). In biomedical signal processing, most physiological signals ŌĆö ECG, EEG, EMG, HRV, gait signals ŌĆö originate from nonlinear biological systems.

### 1.2 Linear vs. Nonlinear ŌĆö The Core Distinction

| Property | Linear System | Nonlinear System |
|---|---|---|
| Superposition | Holds ($y = a y_1 + b y_2$) | Violated |
| Frequency response | New frequencies absent | New frequencies generated |
| Phase space trajectory | Fixed points / limit cycles only | Strange attractors, chaos possible |
| Predictability | Long-term deterministic | Sensitive dependence on initial conditions |
| Output-input relation | Proportional (homogeneous) | State-dependent, history-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in biomedical engineering:**
> The human heart, brain, and respiratory system are governed by feedback loops, saturation effects, and threshold behaviours. Treating ECG, EEG, or HRV signals as purely linear (via FFT and PSD) discards clinically rich information hidden in their nonlinear structure.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Pendulum Story

Imagine two pendulums:

- **Linear pendulum (small angle):** The restoring force is proportional to the angle ($F = -k\theta$). Swing it from $10┬░$ and from $20┬░$ ŌĆö the bigger swing takes exactly twice as long. Everything scales linearly.

- **Nonlinear pendulum (large angle):** The true restoring force is $F = -mg\sin(\theta)$. The two swings take *different* proportions of time because $\sin(20┬░) \neq 2\sin(10┬░)$. Worse, give the same pendulum a tiny tap and it might spin around forever ŌĆö a behaviour the linear model can never predict.

**This is exactly the difference between linear and nonlinear biomedical signals.** A linear model of the heart assumes a clean sinus rhythm. A nonlinear model captures arrhythmias, chaos, and sudden state transitions.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Mathematical Quantities

- **Embedding dimension ($m$):** Integer defining the size of the reconstructed phase-space vectors.
- **Time delay ($\tau$):** Lag (in samples) used when reconstructing state vectors.
- **Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda$):** Rate of exponential divergence (or convergence) of nearby trajectories ŌĆö measured in **nats/sample** or **bits/sample**.
- **Correlation dimension ($D_2$):** A fractal (non-integer) measure of the dimensionality of the underlying attractor.
- **Approximate Entropy (ApEn) and Sample Entropy (SampEn):** Non-negative scalars quantifying the *regularity* of a time series.
- **Hurst exponent ($H$):** Indicator of long-range dependence ($0 < H < 1$).
- **Bispectrum ($B(f_1, f_2)$):** Third-order (cumulant) frequency statistic retaining phase information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 explicitly tests the student's ability to (a) reconstruct phase space from a 1-D time series, (b) compute Lyapunov exponent and correlation dimension, (c) apply entropy-based regularity measures, and (d) interpret higher-order spectra for biomedical signals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Nonlinear Measures?

Linear methods (mean, variance, FFT, PSD) only capture **second-order statistics**. They are blind to:

1. **Phase coupling** between frequency components (handled by bispectrum).
2. **Deterministic chaos** masquerading as random noise (Lyapunov exponents).
3. **Fractal scaling** in self-similar physiological signals (DFA, Hurst exponent).
4. **Complexity/regularity** differences between healthy and pathological states (ApEn, SampEn).

## 2.2 Phase-Space Reconstruction ŌĆö Takens' Embedding Theorem

Given a 1-D time series $x(1), x(2), \ldots, x(N)$, we reconstruct a *multi-dimensional* state vector:

$$\mathbf{X}(n) = \left[x(n),\ x(n+\tau),\ x(n+2\tau),\ \ldots,\ x(n+(m-1)\tau)\right]$$

where:
- $m$ = embedding dimension
- $\tau$ = time delay (in samples)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takens' Theorem (1981):** If the original dynamical system has a manifold of dimension $d$, then the reconstructed attractor is topologically equivalent to the original provided $m \geq 2d + 1$. The reconstructed dynamics preserve invariants such as Lyapunov exponents and correlation dimension.

### 2.2.1 Choosing the Time Delay $\tau$

**Method 1 ŌĆö Average Mutual Information (AMI):**
The first minimum of the AMI function $I(\tau)$ gives an optimal $\tau$.

$$I(\tau) = \sum_{i,j} p_{ij}(\tau)\ \log_2 \frac{p_{ij}(\tau)}{p_i\ p_j}$$

**Method 2 ŌĆö Autocorrelation:** First zero-crossing or first 1/e drop.

### 2.2.2 Choosing the Embedding Dimension $m$

**False Nearest Neighbours (FNN) algorithm:** Increase $m$ until the percentage of false neighbours falls below a threshold (typically 5ŌĆō10%).

## 2.3 Lyapunov Exponent ŌĆö Quantifying Chaos

The largest Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_1$ measures the average rate of exponential divergence of infinitesimally close trajectories:

$$d(t) \approx d(0)\ e^{\lambda_1 t}$$

- $\lambda_1 > 0$ ŌåÆ **chaos** (sensitive dependence on initial conditions)
- $\lambda_1 = 0$ ŌåÆ marginal stability (limit cycle)
- $\lambda_1 < 0$ ŌåÆ fixed point (convergent)

### 2.3.1 Wolf et al. (1985) Algorithm ŌĆö Practical Computation

1. Reconstruct phase space with embedding dimension $m$ and time delay $\tau$.
2. Pick a reference point $\mathbf{X}(t_0)$; locate its nearest neighbour $\mathbf{X}(t_0')$.
3. Track the evolution of the distance $L(t_0) = \vert \mathbf{X}(t_0) - \mathbf{X}(t_0') \vert$ over a short time $\Delta t$.
4. After evolution, find a new replacement neighbour that preserves direction and length.
5. Compute:
$$\lambda_1 = \frac{1}{t_M - t_0}\ \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \log_2 \frac{L'(t_{k+1})}{L(t_k)}$$

### 2.3.2 Rosenstein et al. (1993) Algorithm

Track the mean divergence of nearest neighbours over time and fit a line to:

$$y(i) = \frac{1}{\Delta t}\ \langle \ln d_j(i) \rangle_j$$

The slope of $y(i)$ versus $i$ in the linear region gives $\lambda_1$.

## 2.4 Correlation Dimension ($D_2$) ŌĆö GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia Algorithm

The **correlation integral** $C(r)$ counts the fraction of pairs of points within distance $r$:

$$C(r) = \frac{2}{N(N-1)}\ \sum_{i<j} \Theta(r - \vert \mathbf{X}(i) - \mathbf{X}(j) \vert)$$

where $\Theta(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function. For a fractal attractor:

$$C(r) \propto r^{D_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_2 = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Compute $C(r)$ for a range of $r$ values, for each embedding dimension $m$.
2. Plot $\log C(r)$ vs $\log r$ ŌĆö the slope in the linear scaling region gives $D_2(m)$.
3. Plot $D_2(m)$ vs $m$ ŌĆö saturation gives the true correlation dimension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical insight:** The healthy heart has $D_2 \approx 3.6$ŌĆō$5.2$ (complex attractor). Congestive heart failure patients show $D_2 \approx 2.0$ŌĆō$3.0$ (loss of complexity). Reduced $D_2$ is a hallmark of pathological rigidity.

## 2.5 Approximate Entropy (ApEn) ŌĆö Pincus (1991)

ApEn$(m, r, N)$ measures the *log-likelihood* that runs of patterns that are close for $m$ points remain close on the next incremental comparison.

**Steps:**
1. Form template vectors $\mathbf{x}_i^m$ of length $m$.
2. For each $i$, count $C_i^m(r)$ = number of $j$ such that $d(\mathbf{x}_i^m, \mathbf{x}_j^m) \leq r$ (Chebyshev distance).
3. Compute:
$$C_i^m(r) = \frac{n_i^m(r)}{N - m + 1}$$
4. Average the natural log: $\Phi^m(r) = \frac{1}{N-m+1} \sum_i \ln C_i^m(r)$.
5. ApEn = $\Phi^m(r) - \Phi^{m+1}(r)$.

**Typical values:** $m = 2$, $r = 0.1$ŌĆō$0.25$ times the standard deviation of the data.

## 2.6 Sample Entropy (SampEn) ŌĆö Richman & Moorman (2000)

Improves ApEn by removing self-matches and reducing bias:

$$\text{SampEn}(m, r, N) = -\ln \frac{A}{B}$$

- $B$ = number of template matches of length $m$.
- $A$ = number of template matches of length $m+1$.

**SampEn is more independent of record length and remains stable for short biosignals** (e.g., 5-min HRV).

## 2.7 Hurst Exponent ($H$) and Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA)

### 2.7.1 Hurst Exponent

For a self-similar time series of length $N$, the rescaled range $R/S$ scales as:

$$\frac{R}{S} \propto N^H$$

| Value of $H$ | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| $0 < H < 0.5$ | Anti-persistent (mean-reverting) |
| $H = 0.5$ | Uncorrelated (white noise) |
| $0.5 < H < 1$ | Persistent (long-range correlated) |
| $H \approx 1$ | $1/f$ "pink" noise (healthy HRV) |

### 2.7.2 Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA)

1. Integrate the mean-removed series: $Y(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (x(i) - \bar{x})$.
2. Divide $Y(k)$ into non-overlapping windows of length $n$.
3. In each window, fit a local polynomial trend $Y_n(k)$; compute the fluctuation:
$$F(n) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \left[Y(k) - Y_n(k)\right]^2}$$
4. Repeat for multiple $n$. The scaling exponent $\alpha$ is the slope of $\log F(n)$ vs $\log n$:
$$F(n) \propto n^\alpha$$
- $\alpha_1$ (short-term, 4ŌĆō16 beats) and $\alpha_2$ (long-term, 16ŌĆō64 beats) are the two clinical DFA indices.

## 2.8 Higher-Order Spectra ŌĆö Bispectrum

The **bispectrum** is the 2-D Fourier transform of the third-order cumulant (triple correlation):

$$B(f_1, f_2) = E\left[X(f_1)\ X(f_2)\ X^*(f_1 + f_2)\right]$$

- Retains **phase information** (unlike PSD).
- **Bicoherence** (normalised bispectrum):
$$\text{bic}(f_1, f_2) = \frac{\vert B(f_1, f_2) \vert^2}{P(f_1)\ P(f_2)\ P(f_1 + f_2)}, \quad 0 \leq \text{bic} \leq 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical use of bispectrum:** Quadratic phase coupling in EEG (e.g., seizure signatures), detection of nonlinearity in HRV, and EMG fatigue analysis.

## 2.9 Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA)

Build the **recurrence plot** ŌĆö a binary matrix $R_{i,j}$:

$$R_{i,j} = \Theta(\varepsilon - \vert \mathbf{X}(i) - \mathbf{X}(j) \vert)$$

**RQA metrics:**
- **Recurrence Rate (RR):** Density of recurrence points.
- **Determinism (DET):** Fraction of points forming diagonal lines of length $\geq l_{min}$.
- **Laminarity (LAM):** Fraction forming vertical lines.
- **Longest diagonal line ($L_{max}$):** Related to inverse of largest Lyapunov exponent.
- **Entropy of diagonal line lengths (ENTR):** Complexity of the recurrence structure.

## 2.10 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reconstructed state vector | $\mathbf{X}(n) = [x(n), x(n+\tau), \ldots, x(n+(m-1)\tau)]$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Time delay (AMI) | First min of $I(\tau) = \sum p_{ij}\log_2(p_{ij}/(p_i p_j))$ | Samples |
| 3 | Lyapunov exponent | $\lambda_1 = \lim_{t\to\infty} \frac{1}{t} \ln \frac{d(t)}{d(0)}$ | bits/sample |
| 4 | Correlation integral | $C(r) = \frac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i<j} \Theta(r - \vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \vert)$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Correlation dimension | $D_2 = \lim_{r\to 0} \frac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$ | Non-integer |
| 6 | ApEn | $\text{ApEn}(m,r,N) = \Phi^m(r) - \Phi^{m+1}(r)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | SampEn | $\text{SampEn} = -\ln(A/B)$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | Hurst scaling | $R/S \propto N^H$ | $0 < H < 1$ |
| 9 | DFA fluctuation | $F(n) \propto n^\alpha$ | $\alpha_1$, $\alpha_2$ |
| 10 | Bispectrum | $B(f_1, f_2) = E[X(f_1)X(f_2)X^*(f_1+f_2)]$ | Complex |
| 11 | Bicoherence | $\text{bic}(f_1, f_2) = \vert B \vert^2 / (P_1 P_2 P_{1+2})$ | $[0,1]$ |
| 12 | RQA recurrence | $R_{i,j} = \Theta(\varepsilon - \vert \mathbf{X}_i - \mathbf{X}_j \vert)$ | $\{0,1\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These measures collectively power clinical decision support for cardiac arrhythmia detection (HRV), seizure prediction (EEG), sleep staging, EMG fatigue analysis, and gait-disorder assessment (Parkinson's, ALS).
