- **Step 1:** For each trial $D_e$ from 1 upward, delay-embed the signal.
- **Step 2:** For each $X_i$, identify its nearest neighbour $X_j^{(D_e)}$.
- **Step 3:** Project both into $\mathbb{R}^{D_e+1}$ using the next delay coordinate $s(i + D_e\tau)$. Compute the new distance $\lVert X_i^{D_e+1} - X_j^{D_e+1} \rVert$.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Two criteria for "false" status:**
  - **Distance ratio criterion:** If
  $$\frac{\lVert X_i^{D_e+1} - X_j^{D_e+1} \rVert}{\lVert X_i^{D_e} - X_j^{D_e} \rVert} > R_{tol} \approx 15$$
  then the neighbour is false.
  - **Size criterion:** If $\lVert X_i^{D_e+1} - X_j^{D_e+1} \rVert > R_A$ (the typical attractor radius), the neighbour is false because two genuinely close attractor points should never separate by more than the attractor size.
- **Step 5:** Plot **PFNN (%)** vs. $D_e$. The $D_e$ at which PFNN first falls below ~ 5 % is the **minimum embedding dimension** $D_e^{min}$.

*Mark split:* [Definition 1, Two criteria 2, Procedure 2, Plot interpretation 1, Diagram 1]

**(b) KaplanŌĆōYorke dimension [7 marks]**

Block diagram ŌĆö Lyapunov spectrum calculation: collect trajectory, reconstruct Jacobian along the orbit, perform QR decomposition at fixed steps, accumulate the log-stretch factors, then apply the KaplanŌĆōYorke formula. **[1 mark for the block diagram, 2 marks for the procedural explanation]**

**Numerical calculation:**

Cumulative sums:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \lambda_1 = +0.42 \\
S_2 &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 0.50 \\
S_3 &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.35 \\
S_4 &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4 = -0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

Identify the largest $j$ with $S_j \ge 0$: $j = 3$ (since $S_3 = 0.35 > 0$ and $S_4 = -0.60 < 0$).

Apply the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{KY} &= j + \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{j} \lambda_i}{\lvert \lambda_{j+1} \rvert} \\
&= 3 + \frac{S_3}{\lvert \lambda_4 \rvert} \\
&= 3 + \frac{0.35}{0.95} \\
&\approx 3.37
\end{aligned}
$$

**[2 marks for the cumulative sum table, 2 marks for the final numerical value]**

**Clinical interpretation [1 mark]:** A KaplanŌĆōYorke dimension of $\approx 3.37$ indicates the cardiac-cell model has a **chaotic, low-dimensional attractor** ŌĆö consistent with published values for coupled-oscillator cardiac models and slightly higher than pure periodic dynamics ($D_{KY}=1$ for limit cycles). This complexity reflects the physiological richness of cardiac electrical activity.

**Final answer [1 mark]:** $\boxed{D_{KY} \approx 3.37}$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the Theiler window** in the correlation sum ŌĆö examiners *expect* a discussion of autocorrelation bias. Missing this costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Reporting $\hat{D}_2$ without specifying the scaling region** ŌĆö the slope is meaningless without $[\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2]$.
> 3. **Confusing the *embedding* dimension $D_e$ with the *intrinsic* dimension $D$** ŌĆö the most common conceptual error. Use Takens' theorem $D_e \ge 2D + 1$ to remember the difference.
> 4. **Forgetting to justify the lag $\tau$** ŌĆö either quote the AMI first-minimum criterion or the autocorrelation 1/e criterion. State it.
> 5. **Miscounting Theiler pairs** in the correlation sum ŌĆö the divisor is $N_p(N_p - 1)/2$, *not* $N_p^2$.
> 6. **Sign errors** in KaplanŌĆōYorke: remember to use $\lvert \lambda_{j+1} \rvert$ (absolute value) in the denominator.

### 5.4 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Three "dimensions"** to keep separate: ambient ($D_{obs}$), embedding ($D_e$), intrinsic ($D$).
- [ ] **Takens' lower bound** $D_e \ge 2D + 1$ (or $2D_0 + 1$ for fractal attractors) is the key theoretical statement.
- [ ] **GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia** is the workhorse estimator: $C(\varepsilon) \propto \varepsilon^{D_2}$, slope in log-log plot gives $D_2$.
- [ ] **Theiler window** $W$ is mandatory to avoid autocorrelation-induced bias in $C(\varepsilon)$.
- [ ] **Box-counting $D_0$** is the easiest to compute (raster image / histogram approach) but least accurate.
- [ ] **Information $D_1$** weights boxes by probability mass ŌĆö more efficient use of data.
- [ ] **FNN method** determines the *minimum* embedding dimension ŌĆö smallest $D_e$ with PFNN Ōēł 0.
- [ ] **KaplanŌĆōYorke** dimension uses the *Lyapunov spectrum*; integer or fractional.
- [ ] **Practical tips for KTU lab:** sampling rate Ōēź 5├Ś maximum signal frequency; $N$ Ōēź $10^D$ for reliable $D_2$; use Theiler window $W \ge$ autocorrelation time.
- [ ] **Clinical correlates:** healthy HRV $D_2 \approx 3$ŌĆō$4$, post-MI HRV $D_2 \downarrow$, epileptic seizure EEG $D_2 \downarrow$, deep anaesthesia $D_2 \approx 1$.
- [ ] **Examiner's last word:** Always quote the *scaling range*, the *embedding lag*, the *embedding dimension*, the *Theiler window*, and the *sample size* $N$ ŌĆö these five parameters fully document a KTU-acceptable dimension estimate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tests of Null Hypotheses Based on Surrogate Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tests of Null Hypotheses Based on Surrogate Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Biomedical Signal Processing**, the **Tests of Null Hypotheses Based on Surrogate Data** is a class of non-parametric, Monte-CarloŌĆōbased statistical procedures used to rigorously test whether a given observed time series (e.g., EEG, ECG, HRV) possesses a specific structure ŌĆö most commonly **nonlinearity**, **non-Gaussianity**, or **deterministic chaos** ŌĆö that cannot be explained by a simpler linear stochastic null hypothesis.

A **surrogate dataset** is an artificially generated time series that *preserves* a chosen set of linear statistical properties of the original signal (e.g., power spectrum, mean, variance, autocorrelation) but is otherwise *randomized* with respect to the property being tested. By computing a discriminating statistic (a *test statistic*) on both the original and many surrogate realizations, one constructs a *null distribution* empirically and tests the **null hypothesis (HŌéĆ)**.

Formally, the null hypothesis tested is:

> **HŌéĆ:** The observed time series is consistent with a *linear, Gaussian, stationary stochastic process* having the same power spectrum (and/or amplitude distribution) as the data.

If the test statistic computed on the original data is statistically distinguishable from the surrogate distribution, **HŌéĆ is rejected**, indicating evidence of nonlinearity, non-Gaussianity, or deterministic structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Surrogate data testing is the *gold-standard* hypothesis-testing framework in nonlinear biomedical signal analysis. The procedure is rooted in the classical works of **Theiler et al. (1992)** and **Prichard & Theiler (1994)**, and is examined under the broader module of "Nonlinear Dynamics in Biomedical Signals."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy ŌĆö The Art Forgery Test**
>
> Imagine an art expert is given a painting and is asked: *"Is this painting an original Monet, or a sophisticated forgery by a linear artist who only knows how to replicate brushstroke statistics (color histograms) but not the deeper nonlinear creative style?"*
>
> - The original painting = the **observed biomedical signal** (e.g., EEG).
> - The forgeries = **surrogate datasets** (paintings that *match* the same color distribution and canvas size but lack the genuine artistic style).
> - The discriminating test = a *statistical test statistic* (e.g., a nonlinear measure such as **Correlation Dimension** or **Lyapunov Exponent**).
> - The comparison = measure the test statistic on the original and on **N (typically 19, 39, or 99)** forgeries.
>
> If the original is clearly *off the chart* compared to the forgeries ŌåÆ reject HŌéĆ ŌåÆ the signal contains *genuine* nonlinear structure.
> If the original blends in with the forgeries ŌåÆ fail to reject HŌéĆ ŌåÆ a linear model is sufficient.

### 1.3 Why Surrogate Data? (The Motivation)

Standard parametric tests (e.g., Student's t-test, ANOVA) assume a known distribution of the test statistic under HŌéĆ. However, for **nonlinear measures** (correlation dimension, Lyapunov exponents, approximate entropy, sample entropy, bispectrum, etc.), the distribution under HŌéĆ is generally **unknown analytically**. Surrogate data methods bypass this by *empirically* building the null distribution through Monte Carlo resampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Desirable Properties of a Good Surrogate Generator:**
> 1. **Preservation** of the null hypothesis properties (power spectrum, amplitude distribution, etc.).
> 2. **Destruction** of the feature being tested (nonlinear coupling, phase relationships, etc.).
> 3. **Computational tractability** for long biomedical records (e.g., 8-hour EEG).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded)

- **N_s** = number of surrogate realizations (typical: **19, 39, 99, or 199**).
- **╬▒** = significance level (typical: **0.05** or **0.01**).
- **z-score** = standardized deviation of original test statistic from surrogate mean.
- **Rank-based p-value** = position of original statistic in the ordered surrogate distribution.
- **False Discovery Rate (FDR)** = expected proportion of false rejections across multiple channels/windows.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Algorithmic Framework (Bulleted Logic Steps)

The test of a null hypothesis using surrogate data proceeds in **five canonical steps**:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Choose the Null Hypothesis (HŌéĆ).**  
   Decide which class of linear stochastic process is to be mimicked. Common choices:  
   - *HŌéĆ: linear Gaussian (white) noise.*  
   - *HŌéĆ: linear Gaussian correlated noise (matched power spectrum).*  
   - *HŌéĆ: linear stationary (possibly non-Gaussian) noise (matched spectrum + amplitude distribution).*

2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Select a Discriminating Statistic (T).**  
   A scalar function sensitive to the property of interest but *insensitive* to preserved null properties.  
   Examples: **Correlation Dimension (DŌéé)**, **Largest Lyapunov Exponent (╬╗Ōéü)**, **Approximate Entropy (ApEn)**, **Sample Entropy (SampEn)**, **Bispectrum**, **Phase Coherence**.

3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Generate Surrogate Realizations {SŌüĮŌü▒ŌüŠ}, i = 1 ŌĆ” N_s.**  
   Using one of the following algorithms (chosen to match HŌéĆ):  
   - **Algorithm 1:** Phase-randomized (Fourier Transform) Surrogates ŌĆö preserves **power spectrum**, destroys **phase coupling**.  
   - **Algorithm 2:** Amplitude-Adjusted Fourier Transform (AAFT) Surrogates ŌĆö preserves **power spectrum + amplitude distribution**, destroys **nonlinear structure**.  
   - **Algorithm 3:** Iterative AAFT (IAAFT) Surrogates ŌĆö *strictly* matches both spectrum and amplitude distribution.  
   - **Algorithm 4:** Shuffle / IAAFT-Windowed variants.

4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute T on Original and on Each Surrogate.**  
   Let **T_data** be the statistic on the original, and **T_surrŌüĮŌü▒ŌüŠ** on surrogate *i*. Construct the empirical null distribution.

5. **Step 5 ŌĆö Test the Hypothesis.**  
   Use a **rank-based** or **z-scoreŌĆōbased** decision rule. Reject HŌéĆ at level ╬▒ if the original statistic lies outside the **╬▒/2** tails of the empirical null distribution.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why preserve the power spectrum?** Because under HŌéĆ of linear Gaussian noise, the *autocorrelation function* and hence the *power spectral density* fully characterize the second-order statistics. Any discriminating statistic that is *invariant* to second-order structure will then test only for higher-order (nonlinear) information.
- **Why preserve the amplitude distribution (AAFT)?** Because in *real* biomedical data (EEG spikes, ECG R-peaks, EMG bursts), the amplitude distribution is *non-Gaussian* by nature. Preserving it ensures that the surrogate null hypothesis is realistic, not artificially constrained.
- **Why use Monte Carlo rather than analytic null distributions?** The distributions of nonlinear statistics (DŌéé, ╬╗Ōéü, ApEn) under HŌéĆ are mathematically intractable for finite, noisy, non-stationary biomedical records.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Definition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T_{\text{data}}$ | Test statistic on observed signal | scalar $\in \mathbb{R}$ | unitless |
| $\bar{T}_{\text{surr}}$ | Mean of surrogate test statistics | $\bar{T}_{\text{surr}} = \dfrac{1}{N_s}\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} T_{\text{surr}}^{(i)}$ | same as T |
| $\sigma_{\text{surr}}$ | Std. dev. of surrogate statistics | $\sigma_{\text{surr}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N_s-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N_s}\left(T_{\text{surr}}^{(i)}-\bar{T}_{\text{surr}}\right)^{2}}$ | same as T |
| $Z_{\text{score}}$ | Standardized z-score | $Z_{\text{score}} = \dfrac{\vert T_{\text{data}} - \bar{T}_{\text{surr}} \vert}{\sigma_{\text{surr}}}$ | unitless |
| $p_{\text{rank}}$ | Rank-based p-value (two-sided) | $p_{\text{rank}} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \min(r,\; N_s+1-r)}{N_s+1}$ | unitless |
| $S_{x}(f)$ | Power spectrum of signal $x(t)$ | $S_{x}(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^{2}$ | V┬▓/Hz |
| $X(f)$ | Fourier transform of $x(t)$ | $X(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n/N}$ | V |
| $\hat{x}_{\text{FT}}$ | Phase-randomized surrogate (FT method) | $\hat{X}(f) = \vert X(f) \vert \, e^{j\phi_{\text{unif}}(f)}$ | V |
| $\hat{x}_{\text{AAFT}}$ | Amplitude-adjusted FT surrogate | rank-match the rescaled FT surrogate to original amplitude CDF | V |
| $D_{2}$ | Correlation dimension (GrassbergerŌĆōProcaccia) | $D_{2} = \lim_{r \to 0} \dfrac{\log C(r)}{\log r}$ | unitless |
| $C(r)$ | Correlation integral | $C(r) = \dfrac{2}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i<j} \Theta(r - \vert \vec{x}_i - \vec{x}_j \vert)$ | unitless |
| $\text{ApEn}(m,r,N)$ | Approximate entropy | regularity statistic on template length $m$, tolerance $r$ | unitless |
| $\lambda_{1}$ | Largest Lyapunov exponent | exponential divergence rate of nearby trajectories | 1/sample |
| $\alpha$ | Significance level | typically **0.05** | unitless |
| $N_s$ | Number of surrogates | typically **19, 39, 99, or 199** | integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In clinical biomedical engineering, surrogate data testing is the *de facto* validation step for any nonlinear measure claimed to detect pathology ŌĆö e.g., seizure onset in EEG, ventricular fibrillation in ECG, or muscular fatigue in EMG. Without it, a "nonlinear finding" may simply be an artifact of non-Gaussian noise or non-stationarity.

### 2.4 The Three Canonical Surrogate Algorithms

**Algorithm 1 ŌĆö Fourier Transform (FT) / Phase-Randomized Surrogates (Theiler, 1992)**

Preserves: Power spectrum | Destroys: Phase relationships, amplitude distribution.

1. Compute $X(f) = \text{FFT}\{x(n)\}$.
2. Randomize the phase: $\phi(f) \sim \text{Uniform}(-\pi, \pi)$, ensuring conjugate symmetry $\phi(-f) = -\phi(f)$.
3. Reconstruct: $\hat{X}(f) = \vert X(f) \vert \, e^{j\phi(f)}$.
4. Inverse FFT: $\hat{x}(n) = \text{IFFT}\{\hat{X}(f)\}$.

**Algorithm 2 ŌĆö Amplitude-Adjusted Fourier Transform (AAFT) Surrogates (Theiler et al., 1992)**

Preserves: Power spectrum + amplitude distribution | Destroys: Nonlinear phase structure.

1. Normalize original $x(n)$ to have unit variance (Gaussianize amplitude).
2. Apply FT surrogate procedure (Algorithm 1) on the Gaussianized data.
3. Rank-order the surrogate and re-map amplitudes to match the *original* empirical CDF.
4. Optional: iterate to converge (yields IAAFT).

**Algorithm 3 ŌĆö Iterative AAFT (IAAFT) Surrogates (Schreiber & Schmitz, 1996)**

Preserves *exactly*: Power spectrum + amplitude distribution.

1. Initialize $\hat{x}_0$ via AAFT.
2. **Loop until convergence:**
   - (a) $\hat{X}_k(f) = \text{FFT}\{\hat{x}_k\}$ ŌåÆ replace magnitudes with $\vert X(f) \vert$ ŌåÆ keep phases.
   - (b) $\hat{x}_{k+1} = \text{IFFT}\{\hat{X}_k\}$ ŌåÆ rank-order to match original amplitude distribution.
3. Stop when $\sum (\hat{X}_k(f) - \vert X(f)\vert)^{2}$ is below threshold.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rank-Based Two-Sided p-value

> **Problem:** Given $N_s$ surrogate realizations and a single observed test statistic $T_{\text{data}}$, derive the rank-based two-sided p-value used in surrogate hypothesis testing.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Pool the statistics.**  
Construct the multiset:

$$
\mathcal{T} = \{T_{\text{data}},\; T_{\text{surr}}^{(1)},\; T_{\text{surr}}^{(2)},\; \dots,\; T_{\text{surr}}^{(N_s)}\}
$$

The total cardinality is $\vert \mathcal{T} \vert = N_s + 1$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Rank the data statistic.**  
Sort $\mathcal{T}$ in ascending order. Let $r$ denote the **rank** of $T_{\text{data}}$ in this ordered list (i.e., $r=1$ if $T_{\text{data}}$ is the smallest, $r = N_s+1$ if the largest). The convention is that ties are assigned the average rank.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Form the one-sided tail probabilities.**  
The probability that $T_{\text{data}}$ is *at least this extreme* in the *upper* tail:

$$
p_{\text{upper}} = \frac{N_s + 1 - r}{N_s + 1}
$$

Similarly, the lower tail probability:

$$
p_{\text{lower}} = \frac{r}{N_s + 1}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine for a two-sided test.**  
For a two-sided null hypothesis, take twice the smaller of the two tails:

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = 2 \cdot \min\left(\frac{r}{N_s+1},\; \frac{N_s+1-r}{N_s+1}\right)
$$

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(r,\; N_s+1-r)}{N_s+1}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Cap at 1 to ensure validity.**  
The two-sided p-value must lie in $[0, 1]$. By construction it does, but numerically:

$$
p_{\text{final}} = \min\left(p_{\text{two-sided}},\; 1\right)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Decision rule.**  
Reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ if and only if:

$$
p_{\text{final}} < \alpha
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on `N_s+1` denominator:** Including $T_{\text{data}}$ in the ranking (rather than the standard $N_s$ denominator) is the *Davison & Hinkley* correction. It is the most widely adopted convention in the surrogate data literature (Theiler & Prichard, 1996).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the z-Score Test Statistic

> **Problem:** Derive the z-score standardization used in Gaussian-approximation surrogate tests.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the surrogate mean and standard deviation.**

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{surr}} = \frac{1}{N_s} \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} T_{\text{surr}}^{(i)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{surr}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_s - 1} \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \left( T_{\text{surr}}^{(i)} - \bar{T}_{\text{surr}} \right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Standardize the observed statistic.**  
Subtract the surrogate mean and divide by the surrogate standard deviation:

$$
Z_{\text{score}} = \frac{T_{\text{data}} - \bar{T}_{\text{surr}}}{\sigma_{\text{surr}}}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Two-sided absolute value form.**  
Because we test for *extremity* in either tail:

$$
Z_{\text{score, two-sided}} = \frac{\vert T_{\text{data}} - \bar{T}_{\text{surr}} \vert}{\sigma_{\text{surr}}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Decision rule (Gaussian approximation).**  
For large $N_s$, by the central limit theorem, the surrogate statistics are approximately Gaussian. Therefore:

$$
\text{Reject } H_0 \iff Z_{\text{score, two-sided}} > \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{\alpha}{2}\right)
$$

For $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical value is $\Phi^{-1}(0.975) \approx 1.96$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: AAFT Surrogate Generation (8-Point Signal)

> **Problem:** Let $x = [1.2,\; 0.5,\; 2.1,\; 0.8,\; 1.5,\; 0.3,\; 1.9,\; 0.7]$. Generate one AAFT surrogate.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Rank the original signal.**  
Sorted ranks: $x_{\text{sorted}} = [0.3,\; 0.5,\; 0.7,\; 0.8,\; 1.2,\; 1.5,\; 1.9,\; 2.1]$.  
Rank vector $R = [5,\; 2,\; 8,\; 4,\; 6,\; 1,\; 7,\; 3]$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Gaussianize via rank-matching to $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.**  
Using standard normal quantile values at equally-spaced probabilities:

$$
\tilde{x} = [\Phi^{-1}(5.5/9),\; \Phi^{-1}(2.5/9),\; \dots]
$$

Approximately: $\tilde{x} \approx [0.34,\; -0.34,\; 1.07,\; 0.00,\; 0.43,\; -1.07,\; 0.75,\; -0.75]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply phase randomization (FT surrogate on $\tilde{x}$).**  
Generate $\phi \sim \text{Uniform}(-\pi, \pi)$, enforce conjugate symmetry, compute:

$$
\hat{X}(f) = \vert \tilde{X}(f) \vert \, e^{j\phi(f)}
$$

$$
\hat{\tilde{x}} = \text{IFFT}\{\hat{X}(f)\}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Re-rank to original amplitude distribution.**  
Rank-order $\hat{\tilde{x}}$ and replace with the corresponding original $x_{\text{sorted}}$ values. The result $\hat{x}_{\text{AAFT}}$ has the same amplitude distribution and (approximately) the same power spectrum as $x$, but destroyed nonlinear phase structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check (KTU Board-Examiner Style):** A correct AAFT surrogate must satisfy (i) $\text{mean}(\hat{x}) \approx \text{mean}(x)$, (ii) $\text{var}(\hat{x}) = \text{var}(x)$ exactly, and (iii) $\text{Empirical CDF}(\hat{x}) \equiv \text{Empirical CDF}(x)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: p-value Computation (N_s = 19 surrogates)

> **Problem:** Suppose $T_{\text{data}} = 3.45$ and the 19 surrogate values are $T_{\text{surr}} = \{2.10, 2.45, 2.67, 2.80, 2.95, 3.05, 3.12, 3.20, 3.28, 3.30, 3.35, 3.42, 3.50, 3.60, 3.72, 3.85, 4.00, 4.15, 4.30\}$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the rank of $T_{\text{data}}$ in the combined set of 20 values.**  
$T_{\text{data}} = 3.45$ falls between $3.42$ (rank 12) and $3.50$ (rank 13). So $r = 13$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the two-sided formula.**

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(13,\; 20-13)}{20} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(13,\; 7)}{20} = \frac{14}{20} = 0.70
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Decision.**  
At $\alpha = 0.05$, $p = 0.70 \gg 0.05$. **Fail to reject HŌéĆ.** The signal is consistent with linear Gaussian noise with the same power spectrum.

### 4.1 High-Level Surrogate Data Testing Framework

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `stepA`, `procB`). No reserved keywords used as node IDs. Labels contain clean uppercase text only, no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[STEP 1: OBSERVED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL x of n] --> stepB[STEP 2: DEFINE NULL HYPOTHESIS H0]
    stepB --> stepC{WHICH NULL?}
    stepC -->|White Gaussian Noise| stepC1[Use SHUFFLE Surrogates]
    stepC -->|Linear Correlated Gaussian| stepC2[Use FT PHASE RANDOMIZED Surrogates]
    stepC -->|Linear Stationary Non Gaussian| stepC3[Use AAFT or IAAFT Surrogates]
    stepC1 --> stepD
    stepC2 --> stepD
    stepC3 --> stepD[STEP 3: GENERATE Ns SURROGATE REALIZATIONS]
    stepD --> stepE[STEP 4: COMPUTE DISCRIMINATING STATISTIC T]
    stepE --> stepE1[T data on Original]
    stepE --> stepE2[T surr 1 ... T surr Ns on Surrogates]
    stepE1 --> stepF[STEP 5: BUILD EMPIRICAL NULL DISTRIBUTION]
    stepE2 --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[STEP 6: COMPUTE Z SCORE OR RANK p VALUE]
    stepG --> stepH{p value less than alpha}
    stepH -->|YES| stepI[REJECT H0: EVIDENCE OF NONLINEARITY]
    stepH -->|NO| stepJ[FAIL TO REJECT H0: LINEAR MODEL SUFFICIENT]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AAFT Surrogate Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Original Signal x of n] --> proc1[Compute Empirical CDF Fx]
    in1 --> proc2[Rank Order x]
    proc2 --> proc3[Gaussianize: Match Ranks to N 0 1]
    proc3 --> proc4[FFT: Compute Xtilde of f]
    proc4 --> proc5[Randomize Phase: phi of f uniform minus pi to pi]
    proc5 --> proc6[Reconstruct: Xhat of f equals magnitude times exp j phi]
    proc6 --> proc7[IFFT: Inverse Fourier Transform]
    proc7 --> proc8[Rank Order Surrogate]
    proc8 --> proc9[Re Map to Original Amplitudes via Fx]
    proc9 --> out1[AAFT Surrogate x hat of n]
```

### 4.3 Decision-Theoretic Tree for the Rejection Rule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Compute p value from rank of T data] --> comp{p value less than alpha 0.05}
    comp -->|YES| rej[REJECT H0]
    comp -->|NO| acc[FAIL TO REJECT H0]
    rej --> outA[Conclude: Nonlinear or Non Gaussian Structure Detected]
    acc --> outB[Conclude: Linear Stochastic Model Adequate]
    rej --> nextA[Report z score and p value]
    acc --> nextB[Report z score and p value]
    nextA --> endA[END]
    nextB --> endA
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]:** Define the term "surrogate data" as used in the nonlinear analysis of biomedical signals. Why is it necessary in the test of null hypotheses for nonlinear time series?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** A surrogate dataset is a computer-generated time series that preserves one or more linear statistical properties of an observed signal ŌĆö most commonly its power spectrum and/or amplitude distribution ŌĆö but otherwise randomizes the higher-order structure being tested. Surrogates are constructed to mimic the *null hypothesis* of a linear stochastic process.
>
> **Necessity (1 mark):** For most nonlinear discriminating statistics (e.g., correlation dimension, Lyapunov exponent, approximate entropy, bispectrum), the sampling distribution under the null hypothesis is *analytically intractable*. Surrogate data methods bypass this difficulty by empirically building the null distribution via Monte-Carlo resampling, enabling statistically rigorous hypothesis testing.

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]:** Distinguish between FT surrogates and AAFT surrogates. Under what null hypothesis would you choose AAFT over FT?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | FT Surrogates | AAFT Surrogates |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Preserves** | Power spectrum only | Power spectrum + amplitude distribution |
> | **Destroys** | All phase relationships; amplitude also randomized | Nonlinear phase structure only |
> | **Suitability** | Linear Gaussian null | Linear stationary (possibly non-Gaussian) null |
>
> **When to choose AAFT (1 mark):** When the observed signal is non-Gaussian (e.g., spike-laden EEG, R-peakŌĆōdominated ECG), and one wishes to test for *nonlinearity* without confounding the test with the signal's intrinsic non-Gaussian amplitude statistics. AAFT is therefore the standard choice for most *real* biomedical recordings.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 4]:**

**(a)** Explain in detail the algorithm for generating **Amplitude-Adjusted Fourier Transform (AAFT) surrogate data**. Clearly state what is preserved and what is destroyed, and derive the rank-based two-sided p-value used in the hypothesis test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a given EEG segment, the test statistic $T_{\text{data}} = 4.82$ and 19 surrogate statistics yielded:

$$
T_{\text{surr}} = \{3.10,\; 3.45,\; 3.78,\; 3.92,\; 4.05,\; 4.12,\; 4.20,\; 4.28,\; 4.35,\; 4.42,\; 4.50,\; 4.58,\; 4.65,\; 4.78,\; 4.88,\; 5.02,\; 5.15,\; 5.30,\; 5.50\}
$$

Compute the two-sided rank-based p-value, the z-score, and state your conclusion at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö AAFT Algorithm and p-value Derivation (7 marks):**

The AAFT procedure preserves both the **power spectrum** and the **empirical amplitude distribution** of the original signal $x(n)$, $n=0, 1, \dots, N-1$, while destroying any **nonlinear phase coupling** that may exist.

**Step 1 (1 mark): Gaussianization.**  
Sort $x(n)$ in ascending order to obtain $x_{\text{sorted}}$. Compute the ranks $R_n$ of each $x(n)$. Replace each sample with the standard normal quantile corresponding to its rank:

$$
\tilde{x}(n) = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_n - 0.5}{N}\right)
$$

This yields a Gaussianized signal $\tilde{x}(n)$ with the *same linear autocorrelation* as $x(n)$.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Phase randomization.**  
Compute $\tilde{X}(f) = \text{FFT}\{\tilde{x}(n)\}$. Generate independent uniform phases $\phi(f) \sim U(-\pi, \pi)$, subject to the conjugate-symmetry constraint $\phi(-f) = -\phi(f)$ to ensure a real-valued output. Construct:

$$
\hat{X}(f) = \vert \tilde{X}(f) \vert \, e^{j\phi(f)}
$$

Compute $\hat{\tilde{x}}(n) = \text{IFFT}\{\hat{X}(f)\}$. This phase-randomized signal preserves the *power spectrum* of $\tilde{x}$ but destroys nonlinear phase structure.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Amplitude re-mapping.**  
Rank-order $\hat{\tilde{x}}(n)$ to obtain new ranks. Replace each value of $\hat{\tilde{x}}$ with the corresponding element of $x_{\text{sorted}}$ so that the empirical CDF of the surrogate exactly matches that of $x(n)$. The result is the AAFT surrogate $\hat{x}_{\text{AAFT}}(n)$.

**Preserved (1 mark):** Power spectrum (approximately), amplitude distribution (exactly), mean, variance.  
**Destroyed (1 mark):** Nonlinear phase coupling, all higher-order (polyspectral) structure, deterministic nonlinear dynamics.

**Rank-based p-value derivation (1 mark):**  
[Refer to Section 3.1 above for the full derivation.] The two-sided p-value is:

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(r,\; N_s+1-r)}{N_s + 1}
$$

where $r$ is the rank of $T_{\text{data}}$ in the pooled set of $N_s + 1$ values.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks): Rank computation.**  
Pool the 20 statistics and sort in ascending order. We determine the rank $r$ of $T_{\text{data}} = 4.82$:

Sorted surrogate list with $T_{\text{data}}$ inserted:
$\{3.10, 3.45, 3.78, 3.92, 4.05, 4.12, 4.20, 4.28, 4.35, 4.42, 4.50, 4.58, 4.65, 4.78, \mathbf{4.82}, 4.88, 5.02, 5.15, 5.30, 5.50\}$.

$T_{\text{data}} = 4.82$ lies between $4.78$ and $4.88$, giving rank $r = 15$.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Two-sided p-value.**

$$
p = \frac{2 \cdot \min(15,\; 20-15)}{20} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(15,\; 5)}{20} = \frac{10}{20} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): z-score computation.**

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{surr}} = \frac{1}{19}\sum_{i=1}^{19} T_{\text{surr}}^{(i)}
$$

Sum: $3.10+3.45+3.78+3.92+4.05+4.12+4.20+4.28+4.35+4.42+4.50+4.58+4.65+4.78+4.88+5.02+5.15+5.30+5.50 = 86.03$.

$$
\bar{T}_{\text{surr}} = \frac{86.03}{19} \approx 4.528
$$

Compute $\sigma_{\text{surr}}$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{surr}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{18} \sum_{i=1}^{19} (T_i - 4.528)^2} \approx \sqrt{0.366} \approx 0.605
$$

$$
Z_{\text{score, two-sided}} = \frac{\vert 4.82 - 4.528 \vert}{0.605} = \frac{0.292}{0.605} \approx 0.483
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Conclusion.**  
At $\alpha = 0.05$, $p = 0.50 \gg 0.05$ and $\vert Z \vert = 0.483 \ll 1.96$. **Fail to reject HŌéĆ.** The EEG segment is statistically consistent with a linear Gaussian process with the same spectrum ŌĆö no significant evidence of nonlinearity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 4]:**

**(a)** Discuss the **Theiler procedure** for testing the null hypothesis of a linear Gaussian correlated process. Compare FT surrogates, AAFT surrogates, and IAAFT surrogates in terms of preserved/destroyed properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinician computes the correlation dimension $D_2$ on a 30-minute ECG segment to detect cardiac arrhythmia. The $D_2$ of the original is $2.78$, while 99 IAAFT surrogates have mean $\bar{D}_2 = 2.10$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 0.18$. Test the null hypothesis at $\alpha = 0.01$ using both the z-score and rank-based approaches (assume the rank corresponds to the 96th percentile of the surrogate distribution). Comment on the clinical implication. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Theiler Procedure and Algorithm Comparison (7 marks):**

**Theiler Procedure (3 marks):**  
The Theiler procedure (Theiler et al., 1992) tests the null hypothesis:

> $H_0$: The observed time series is a realization of a *linearly-filtered Gaussian noise* process.

The procedure consists of:  
(i) Choose a discriminating statistic $T$ (e.g., $D_2$, $\lambda_1$, ApEn).  
(ii) Generate $N_s$ phase-randomized surrogate datasets $\hat{x}^{(i)}$ such that each has the *same power spectrum* as the original $x$.  
(iii) Compute $T$ on $x$ and on each $\hat{x}^{(i)}$.  
(iv) Construct the empirical null distribution and test via z-score or rank.

**Comparison (4 marks):**

| Algorithm | Preserves | Destroys | Iterative? | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FT (Phase-Randomized)** | Power spectrum | Phase, amplitude | No | Strictly Gaussian linear HŌéĆ |
| **AAFT** | Spectrum + amplitude | Nonlinear phase | No (one iteration) | Non-Gaussian linear HŌéĆ |
| **IAAFT** | Spectrum + amplitude *exactly* | Nonlinear phase | Yes (until convergence) | Gold-standard non-Gaussian linear HŌéĆ |

**Key Insight:** IAAFT converges to surrogates that *simultaneously* match the empirical power spectrum *and* the empirical amplitude distribution to numerical precision, making it the most stringent test of nonlinearity under realistic biomedical conditions.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö z-score and Rank-Based Test (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State hypotheses.  
$H_0$: ECG segment is consistent with linear stationary stochastic process.  
$H_1$: ECG segment contains nonlinear structure indicative of arrhythmia.

**Step 2 (2 marks): z-score computation.**

$$
Z = \frac{\vert D_2^{\text{data}} - \bar{D}_2^{\text{surr}} \vert}{\sigma_{\text{surr}}} = \frac{\vert 2.78 - 2.10 \vert}{0.18} = \frac{0.68}{0.18} \approx 3.778
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): z-score decision at $\alpha = 0.01$.**  
Critical value (two-sided): $\Phi^{-1}(0.995) \approx 2.576$.  
$Z = 3.778 > 2.576 \Rightarrow$ **Reject $H_0$** with very high confidence.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Rank-based p-value.**

The 96th percentile rank (with $N_s = 99$ surrogates, $r = 96$):

$$
p_{\text{one-sided, upper}} = \frac{99 + 1 - 96}{99 + 1} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04
$$

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = 2 \times 0.04 = 0.08
$$

Hmm ŌĆö at $\alpha = 0.01$, the rank-based p-value alone would *not* reject. However, the z-score with its Gaussian approximation indicates extreme deviation. The discrepancy is a known phenomenon with low $N_s$ and skewed surrogate distributions; the z-score is more powerful here, but rank-based is non-parametrically valid.

**Step 5 (1 mark): Clinical Implication.**  
The correlation dimension is *significantly higher* in the original ECG than in linear surrogates, indicating that the cardiac dynamics contain **genuine nonlinear structure** that cannot be explained by linear filtering alone. This supports the **nonlinear dynamical theory of arrhythmogenesis** and suggests that nonlinear measures (e.g., $D_2$, $\lambda_1$) may serve as biomarkers for arrhythmia risk stratification. However, the clinician should confirm with **multiple complementary statistics** and **longer surrogate ensembles** ($N_s \geq 199$) to ensure robustness.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator:** Students often use $N_s$ instead of $N_s + 1$ in the rank-based p-value. KTU expects the **DavisonŌĆōHinkley correction** ($N_s + 1$) ŌĆö losing 1 mark otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting conjugate symmetry:** In FT surrogate generation, students sometimes assign independent phases at both $+f$ and $-f$, producing a *complex-valued* surrogate. KTU deducts 1 mark for this oversight.
> 3. **Confusing AAFT with IAAFT:** AAFT is a *single-pass* algorithm; IAAFT is *iterative*. Examiners expect precise terminology ŌĆö 1 mark lost for misuse.
> 4. **Two-sided vs one-sided confusion:** Students sometimes quote only the upper-tail p-value. KTU board examiners explicitly require the **two-sided** form for two-sided tests.
> 5. **Miscounting the rank:** Off-by-one errors in rank computation are extremely common. Always sort and recount explicitly ŌĆö show your sorted list in the answer booklet.
> 6. **Forgetting to state HŌéĆ and HŌéü:** The KTU marking scheme allocates 1 mark for clearly stating both hypotheses. Omit them at your peril.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- Ō£ģ **Surrogate data** = synthetic time series preserving a chosen set of linear statistics of the original.
- Ō£ģ **Primary purpose:** to *empirically* construct the null distribution of any nonlinear test statistic.
- Ō£ģ **Null hypothesis HŌéĆ** tested: signal is a realization of a *linear stationary stochastic process* (with specified power spectrum and/or amplitude distribution).
- Ō£ģ **Three canonical algorithms:** **FT** (spectrum), **AAFT** (spectrum + amplitude), **IAAFT** (exact spectrum + amplitude, iterative).
- Ō£ģ **Standard discriminating statistics:** $D_2$, $\lambda_1$, ApEn, SampEn, bispectrum, phase coherence.
- Ō£ģ **Two decision rules:** **Rank-based p-value** (non-parametric) and **z-score** (Gaussian approximation).
- Ō£ģ **Two-sided rank p-value formula:**

$$
p_{\text{two-sided}} = \frac{2 \cdot \min(r,\; N_s+1-r)}{N_s + 1}
$$

- Ō£ģ **Critical z-score values:** $\alpha = 0.05 \rightarrow 1.96$; $\alpha = 0.01 \rightarrow 2.576$.
- Ō£ģ **Typical $N_s$:** 19 (minimum), 39, 99 (recommended), 199 (high-precision).
- Ō£ģ **FT surrogate recipe:** FFT ŌåÆ randomize phase (uniform $[-\pi, \pi]$) with conjugate symmetry ŌåÆ IFFT.
- Ō£ģ **AAFT surrogate recipe:** Gaussianize ŌåÆ FT phase randomize ŌåÆ re-rank to original amplitude CDF.
- Ō£ģ **IAAFT surrogate recipe:** Iterate (replace spectrum, then re-rank) until convergence.
- Ō£ģ **Validation checks:** surrogate must have the same mean, variance, and (for AAFT/IAAFT) identical amplitude histogram.
- Ō£ģ **Clinical applications:** seizure detection in EEG, arrhythmia in ECG, fatigue in EMG, sleep-stage transitions in polysomnography.
- Ō£ģ **Pitfall to avoid:** phase randomization *without* conjugate symmetry produces a complex-valued surrogate (invalid).
- Ō£ģ **Pitfall to avoid:** using a small $N_s$ (e.g., $N_s = 9$) leads to coarse p-values; the minimum *p* achievable is $2/(N_s+1) = 0.20$.
- Ō£ģ **Foundational references** (KTE-style): Theiler, Eubank, Longtin, Galdrikian & Farmer (1992); Theiler & Prichard (1996); Schreiber & Schmitz (1996); Prichard & Theiler (1994).
- Ō£ģ **Engineering utility:** surrogate data testing is the *validation gate* for any claimed nonlinear biomarker in clinical biomedical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Bifurcations in a Model of a Cardiac Pacemaker Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT865 - ADVANCED BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications: Bifurcations in a Model of a Cardiac Pacemaker Cell ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **cardiac pacemaker cell** is a specialized myocyte (most notably in the **Sino-Atrial (SA) node**, Atrio-Ventricular (AV) node, and the His-Purkinje system) that generates spontaneous, rhythmic action potentials without external neural input. This property is termed **automaticity** and is governed by a slow, cyclic depolarization of the membrane potential during **Phase 4** of the cardiac action potential. In the framework of **nonlinear dynamics and bifurcation theory**, the pacemaker cell is mathematically represented as an **autonomous nonlinear dynamical system**: $$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}), \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n, \ \boldsymbol{\mu} \in \mathbb{R}^p$$ where the vector $\mathbf{x}$ contains state variables (typically transmembrane voltage $V$ and one or more **gating/recovery variables** $w$), and $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ contains **bifurcation parameters** (e.g., maximum conductances $\bar{g}_{Na}, \bar{g}_{K}, \bar{g}_{Ca}$, or external current $I_{stim}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bifurcation** is defined as a *qualitative* change in the topology of the system's phase portrait that occurs when a control (bifurcation) parameter crosses a critical value $\mu_c$. The normal rhythm of the heart is a **stable limit cycle**; bifurcation analysis explains how this oscillation is *born*, *maintained*, or *terminated* under physiological and pathological conditions (e.g., ischemia, drug action, electrolyte imbalance). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine a *pendulum clock* mounted on a wall. | Analogy Element | Cardiac Counterpart | |---|---| | The swinging pendulum | The pacemaker cell's oscillating membrane voltage | | The driving weight (hanging chain) | Inward depolarizing currents ($I_{Ca,L}, I_f$) that supply energy | | Friction (air resistance) | Outward repolarizing currents ($I_K$) that dissipate energy | | Lengthening the chain (small parameter change) | Reducing $\bar{g}_{K}$ or increasing $\bar{g}_{Ca}$ | | The pendulum suddenly stopping | **Saddle-Node on Invariant Circle (SNIC)** bifurcation ŌåÆ cessation of firing (cardiac arrest/standstill) | | The pendulum swinging into chaotic, irregular motion | **Period-doubling / Homoclinic bifurcation** ŌåÆ arrhythmia (Tachycardia/Fibrillation) | In short, the pacemaker cell is a **biological limit-cycle oscillator**. Bifurcation theory is the *mathematical microscope* that lets us see *exactly* which "knob" of the cell, when turned by how much, can switch the heart from a steady rhythm to a chaotic one. > [!NOTE] > The standard clinical metrics that act as bifurcation parameters are the **resting heart rate (60ŌĆō100 bpm)**, **action potential duration (APD Ōēł 200ŌĆō300 ms in ventricular cells)**, and the **slope of Phase 4 depolarization (Ōēł 0.1 V/s in SA node)**. A change in *slope* of the Phase-4 depolarization is mathematically equivalent to a change in the real part of the dominant eigenvalue. ### 1.3 Why a Two-Variable Reduction is Sufficient The HodgkinŌĆōHuxley (HH) cardiac models can have 4ŌĆō17 state variables. However, for bifurcation analysis of *automaticity*, a 2-dimensional reduction (FitzHugh-Nagumo / Van der Pol type) captures the essential **slow-fast dynamics** between the membrane voltage and a recovery current. $$\dot{V} = f(V, w) = \frac{1}{C_m}\left(-\sum I_{ion}(V, w) + I_{ext}\right)$$ $$\dot{w} = g(V, w) = \epsilon \cdot h(V, w), \quad 0 < \epsilon \ll 1$$ This is the **canonical form** used throughout the rest of this note. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Universally Relevant Bifurcations in Pacemaker Models Cardiac pacemaker dynamics are dominated by **two codimension-1 local bifurcations**: 1. **Hopf Bifurcation** (Supercritical) ŌĆö Creates a small-amplitude stable limit cycle around an unstable focus. Mathematically equivalent to *soft excitation onset*. 2. **Saddle-Node on an Invariant Circle (SNIC)** ŌĆö A real eigenvalue of the fixed point crosses zero. The fixed point and a saddle point collide and annihilate on a circular manifold, leaving the system to circulate forever. Mathematically equivalent to *class-1 excitability* and abrupt firing onset at the SA node. > [!TIP] > The SA node exhibits the **SNIC bifurcation** (Class-1 excitability), whereas Purkinje fibers exhibit a **Hopf bifurcation** (Class-2 excitability). This distinction was proven rigorously by **Guevara & Jongsma (1992)** for the BeelerŌĆōReuter model. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework To analyze a bifurcation, follow this four-stage logical ladder: 1. **Reduce the system** to a 2D autonomous ODE (preserving fast voltage, slow recovery). 2. **Locate the fixed points** by solving $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^*, \mu) = \mathbf{0}$. 3. **Linearize** at the fixed point using the Jacobian $J = D_\mathbf{x}\mathbf{F}\big|_{\mathbf{x}^*}$. 4. **Compute eigenvalues** of $J$. The bifurcation type is read directly from the eigenvalue crossing pattern: | Eigenvalue Crossing Pattern | Bifurcation Type | Physiological Consequence | |---|---|---| | $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha(\mu) \pm i\beta$, with $\alpha(\mu_c) = 0$, $\beta \neq 0$ | **Hopf Bifurcation** | Gradual onset of low-amplitude oscillation | | $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 < 0$ | **Saddle-Node (on invariant circle)** | All-or-none firing onset | | $\lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 = 0$ (other root negative) | **Saddle-Node** | Loss/creation of quiescent state | | $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 0$ | **BogdanovŌĆōTakens** | Mixed dynamics; linked to early afterdepolarizations | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Symbol / Symbol Group | Meaning | Constraint / Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $V$ | Membrane potential | $-90$ to $+40$ | mV | | $w$ | Recovery / gating variable (slow) | $0$ to $1$ | dimensionless | | $I_{ion}$ | Total ionic current density | $\pm 100$ typical | $\mu$A/cm┬▓ | | $C_m$ | Membrane capacitance | $\approx 1$ | $\mu$F/cm┬▓ | | $\bar{g}_{ion}$ | Maximal ionic conductance | $0.1$ to $36$ | mS/cm┬▓ | | $\mu$ (or $I_{ext}$) | Bifurcation parameter | $-50$ to $+50$ | $\mu$A/cm┬▓ | | $J = D_\mathbf{x}\mathbf{F}$ | Jacobian matrix at fixed point | $2 \times 2$ | ŌĆö | | $\tau = \mathrm{tr}(J)$ | Trace of Jacobian | Hopf: $\tau = 0$ | sŌü╗┬╣ | | $\Delta = \det(J)$ | Determinant of Jacobian | Hopf: $\Delta > 0$ | sŌü╗┬▓ | | $\lambda^{2} - \tau \lambda + \Delta = 0$ | Characteristic polynomial | always | ŌĆö | | $\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2}$ | Eigenvalues (2D) | ŌĆö | sŌü╗┬╣ | | $\epsilon$ | Time-scale separation | $0.01 \le \epsilon \le 0.1$ | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Hopf Theorem (1942)** states: *If $\tau(\mu_c) = 0$, $\Delta(\mu_c) > 0$, and $d\tau/d\mu \big|_{\mu_c} \neq 0$, then a limit cycle is born at $\mu = \mu_c$.* This single theorem is the engine behind ~60% of bifurcation problems in cardiac electrophysiology. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pacemaker design**: Implantable pacemakers must mimic the bifurcation properties of a healthy SA node. The hysteresis algorithm used in rate-responsive pacemakers is, mathematically, an engineered **Hopf bifurcation** with adjustable critical frequency. - **Anti-arrhythmic drug screening**: Class III antiarrhythmics (e.g., amiodarone) shift the Hopf bifurcation point by blocking $I_K$, prolonging APD. - **Defibrillation modeling**: A strong electric shock moves the system far from its limit cycle across a **bifurcation manifold**; understanding this geometry reduces the energy required for cardioversion. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Convention for Section 3:** We use the dimensionless FitzHughŌĆōNagumo (FHN) pacemaker model, which is the *universal* reduced form cited in KTU references for this topic. ### 3.1 The Reference Model (FitzHughŌĆōNagumo) $$ \dot{V} = V - \frac{V^{3}}{3} - W + I_{ext} $$ $$ \dot{W} = \epsilon \left( V + \alpha - \beta W \right) $$ where $V$ is the (dimensionless) membrane potential, $W$ is the recovery variable, $\epsilon \in (0,1)$ is the time-scale separation, and $I_{ext}$ is the **bifurcation parameter** $\mu$. The constants $\alpha, \beta$ are positive (typical values $\alpha = 0.7$, $\beta = 0.8$). ### 3.2 Derivation: Location of the Fixed Point **Step 1 ŌĆö Set the right-hand side to zero.** At equilibrium, $\dot{V} = 0$ and $\dot{W} = 0$, which gives the algebraic system: $$0 = V^{*} - \frac{(V^{*})^{3}}{3} - W^{*} + I_{ext}$$ $$0 = \epsilon \left( V^{*} + \alpha - \beta W^{*} \right)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the second equation for $W^*$.** Since $\epsilon \neq 0$: $$W^{*} = \frac{V^{*} + \alpha}{\beta}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into the first equation.** $$0 = V^{*} - \frac{(V^{*})^{3}}{3} - \frac{V^{*} + \alpha}{\beta} + I_{ext}$$ Multiply through by $3\beta$ to clear the denominators: $$0 = 3\beta V^{*} - \beta (V^{*})^{3} - 3(V^{*} + \alpha) + 3\beta I_{ext}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange into a depressed cubic in $V^*$.** $$ \beta (V^{*})^{3} - (3\beta - 3)V^{*} - (3\alpha - 3\beta I_{ext}) = 0 $$ Dividing by $\beta$: $$ (V^{*})^{3} - \left( 3 - \frac{3}{\beta} \right)V^{*} - \left( \frac{3\alpha}{\beta} - 3I_{ext} \right) = 0 $$ For $\beta = 0.8$ and $\alpha = 0.7$, the cubic is of the form $V^{*3} - 0.75 V^{*} - (2.625 - 3 I_{ext}) = 0$. This cubic has **three real roots** for $I_{ext}$ in an intermediate range, which is exactly the geometric reason the FHN model exhibits a **cubic V-nullcline** and produces an S-shaped curve in the phase plane (essential for both Hopf and SNIC behavior). ### 3.3 Derivation: Jacobian Matrix The Jacobian of the system with respect to $(V, W)$ is: $$ J(V, W) = \begin{pmatrix} \dfrac{\partial \dot{V}}{\partial V} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{V}}{\partial W} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial \dot{W}}{\partial V} & \dfrac{\partial \dot{W}}{\partial W} \end{pmatrix} $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute each partial derivative.** From $\dot{V} = V - \frac{V^3}{3} - W + I_{ext}$: $$\frac{\partial \dot{V}}{\partial V} = 1 - V^{2}, \qquad \frac{\partial \dot{V}}{\partial W} = -1$$ From $\dot{W} = \epsilon(V + \alpha - \beta W)$: $$\frac{\partial \dot{W}}{\partial V} = \epsilon, \qquad \frac{\partial \dot{W}}{\partial W} = -\epsilon\beta$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Assemble the matrix.** $$ J(V, W) = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - V^{2} & -1 \\ \epsilon & -\epsilon \beta \end{pmatrix} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation: Hopf Bifurcation Condition **Step 1 ŌĆö Trace and determinant.** $$\tau = \mathrm{tr}(J) = (1 - V^{*2}) - \epsilon\beta = 1 - (V^{*})^{2} - \epsilon\beta$$ $$\Delta = \det(J) = (1 - (V^{*})^{2})(-\epsilon\beta) - (-1)(\epsilon) = -\epsilon\beta + \epsilon\beta(V^{*})^{2} + \epsilon$$ $$\Delta = \epsilon \left[ 1 + \beta(V^{*})^{2} - \beta \right] = \epsilon \left[ 1 - \beta + \beta(V^{*})^{2} \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Hopf condition $\tau = 0$.** $$1 - (V^{*})^{2} - \epsilon\beta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (V^{*})^{2} = 1 - \epsilon\beta$$ For the square root to be real, we need $\epsilon\beta < 1$ ŌĆö which is always satisfied since both are small. **Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for the critical $V^*$ at the bifurcation.** $$V^{*}_{H} = \pm \sqrt{1 - \epsilon\beta}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back to obtain the critical external current $I_{ext,H}$.** We use the fixed-point equation $0 = V^* - \frac{V^{*3}}{3} - W^* + I_{ext}$, with $W^* = \frac{V^* + \alpha}{\beta}$. $$I_{ext, H} = -V^{*}_{H} + \frac{(V^{*}_{H})^{3}}{3} + \frac{V^{*}_{H} + \alpha}{\beta}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Verify the transversality condition (non-degeneracy).** We need $d\tau/d\mu \neq 0$ at $\mu = \mu_c$. Differentiating $\tau$ with respect to $I_{ext}$ (via implicit differentiation of the cubic in $V^*$) gives a non-zero value except at isolated parameter points, so the bifurcation is **non-degenerate** and the periodic orbit is born smoothly (supercritical Hopf). **Step 6 ŌĆö Verify the second Lyapunov coefficient (sign of branching).** For FHN with $\epsilon \ll 1$ and $\alpha, \beta \in (0,1)$, the second Lyapunov coefficient is **negative**, confirming the Hopf bifurcation is **supercritical** ŌĆö a *stable* limit cycle is born as $\mu$ crosses $\mu_c$. > [!NOTE] > **Numerical illustration (KTU-style):** Taking $\alpha = 0.7$, $\beta = 0.8$, $\epsilon = 0.1$: > - $V^*_H = \pm\sqrt{1 - 0.08} = \pm 0.959$ > - $I_{ext, H} = -0.959 + \frac{(0.959)^3}{3} + \frac{0.959 + 0.7}{0.8} = -0.959 + 0.294 + 2.074 = +1.409$ $\mu$A/cm┬▓ > This is the **Hopf bifurcation current** ŌĆö below it, the cell is quiescent; above it, the cell fires repetitively. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Summary of All Conditions | Mathematical Object | Symbol | Required Expression | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | V-nullcline | $W = V - V^3/3 + I_{ext}$ | Cubic in $V$ | Slow manifold | | W-nullcline | $W = (V+\alpha)/\beta$ | Linear in $V$ | Recovery nullcline | | Jacobian | $J$ | $2\times 2$ matrix above | Local linearization | | Trace | $\tau$ | $1 - V^{*2} - \epsilon\beta$ | Sum of eigenvalues | | Determinant | $\Delta$ | $\epsilon(1 - \beta + \beta V^{*2})$ | Product of eigenvalues | | Hopf Condition | $\tau = 0$ | $V^{*2} = 1 - \epsilon\beta$ | Birth of oscillation | | Hopf Condition (Stability) | $\Delta > 0$ | $1 - \beta + \beta V^{*2} > 0$ | Limit cycle exists | | Eigenvalues | $\lambda_{1,2}$ | $\frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^2 - 4\Delta}}{2}$ | Oscillation frequency | | Limit cycle frequency | $f$ | $\beta/2\pi$ approx | Pacemaker rate | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Phase Portrait of a Cardiac Pacemaker Cell (Schematic Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["Phase Plane: V vs W"] A1["Stable Fixed Point
Silent / Quiescent state
mu less than mu_c"] A2["Unstable Focus
at Hopf threshold mu = mu_c"] A3["Stable Limit Cycle
Repetitive Firing
mu greater than mu_c"] A4["V-nullcline
Cubic S-curve"] A5["W-nullcline
Linear line"] A6["Trajectory orbits
in the V-W plane"] end A1 -->|"Parameter mu increases"| A2 A2 -->|"Supercritical Hopf
bifurcation"| A3 A4 --- A5 A6 -.-> A3 ``` ### 4.2 Bifurcation Diagram ŌĆö Birth of the Pacemaker Limit Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BD["Bifurcation Diagram amplitude vs mu"] B1["Lower branch
Stable fixed point
solid line"] B2["Upper branch
Unstable fixed point
dashed line"] B3["Limit cycle amplitude
grows as sqrt of mu minus mu_c
solid curve"] B4["Critical point
mu = mu_c
Hopf"] end B1 -->|"mu increases"| B4 B2 -->|"mu increases"| B4 B4 -->|"limit cycle born"| B3 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cardiac Pacemaker Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph C["Cardiac Pacemaker Cell Model"] C1["Input I_ext
Bifurcation parameter"] C2["Fast Subsystem
Membrane Voltage V
dV/dt = f of V and W"] C3["Slow Subsystem
Recovery W
dW/dt = epsilon g of V and W"] C4["Ionic Currents
I_Na, I_CaL, I_K, I_f"] C5["Phase Portrait
V dot W plane"] C6["Output
Action Potential Waveform"] C7["Bifurcation Analyzer
Eigenvalues of Jacobian"] end C1 --> C2 C2 <--> C3 C2 --> C4 C3 --> C4 C4 --> C5 C5 --> C6 C5 --> C7 C7 -->|"Hopf: tau=0 Delta>0"| C5 ``` ### 4.4 Eigenvalue Trajectory in the Complex Plane (Hopf Crossing) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph D["Complex Plane Re of lambda vs Im of lambda"] D1["Region 1
mu less than mu_c
Both eigenvalues with negative real part
Stable node or focus"] D2["Region 2
mu = mu_c
Eigenvalues on imaginary axis
Purely imaginary pair"] D3["Region 3
mu greater than mu_c
Positive real part pair
Unstable focus + limit cycle"] D4["Trajectory of eigenvalues
crosses imaginary axis
transversally"] end D1 -->|"mu increases"| D2 D2 -->|"mu increases"| D3 D4 -.-> D2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper Style]** #### **Q1. Define bifurcation in the context of a cardiac pacemaker cell. Mention the two bifurcation types relevant to SA node and Purkinje fibers.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** A bifurcation is a qualitative change in the phase-portrait topology of a dynamical system when a control parameter crosses a critical value. In a cardiac pacemaker cell, the membrane voltage $V$ and recovery variable $W$ form a 2D autonomous system; bifurcation parameters (e.g., $\bar{g}_{K}$ or $I_{ext}$) can either create or destroy the **limit cycle** that corresponds to the rhythmic action potential. - **SA node:** Saddle-Node on an Invariant Circle (SNIC) ŌĆö Class-1 excitability. - **Purkinje fiber:** Hopf bifurcation ŌĆö Class-2 excitability. #### **Q2. What is automaticity? State the Hopf bifurcation condition in terms of Jacobian trace and determinant.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **Automaticity** is the ability of certain specialized cardiac cells (SA, AV, His-Purkinje) to generate spontaneous, rhythmic action potentials without external innervation, due to a slow Phase-4 depolarization driven by $I_f$ ("funny current") and $I_{Ca,T}$. **Hopf Bifurcation Condition:** At the bifurcation point $\mu_c$, $$\tau(\mu_c) = 0, \quad \Delta(\mu_c) > 0, \quad \frac{d\tau}{d\mu}\bigg|_{\mu_c} \neq 0$$ where $\tau = \mathrm{tr}(J)$ and $\Delta = \det(J)$ of the $2\times 2$ Jacobian evaluated at the fixed point. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 / Model Pattern: Two alternative 14-mark questions, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the dimensionless FitzHughŌĆōNagumo pacemaker model $$\dot{V} = V - \frac{V^{3}}{3} - W + I_{ext}, \quad \dot{W} = \epsilon(V + \alpha - \beta W)$$ derive the steady-state equilibrium and obtain the conditions on the system parameters for a Hopf bifurcation to occur. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Equilibrium.** Setting $\dot{V} = 0$, $\dot{W} = 0$: $$W^{*} = \frac{V^{*} + \alpha}{\beta}, \quad (V^{*})^{3} - \left(3 - \tfrac{3}{\beta}\right)V^{*} - \tfrac{3\alpha}{\beta} + 3I_{ext} = 0.$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Jacobian.** $$J = \begin{pmatrix} 1 - V^{*2} & -1 \\ \epsilon & -\epsilon\beta \end{pmatrix}.$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Trace and determinant.** $$\tau = 1 - V^{*2} - \epsilon\beta, \quad \Delta = \epsilon(1 - \beta + \beta V^{*2}).$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Hopf conditions.** $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow V^{*2} = 1 - \epsilon\beta$ and $\Delta > 0 \Rightarrow 1 - \beta + \beta V^{*2} > 0$. Substituting $V^{*2}$ into the cubic gives the critical $I_{ext,H}$. Both conditions are satisfied when $\epsilon\beta < 1$ and $\beta < 1$. **(b)** For $\alpha = 0.7$, $\beta = 0.8$, $\epsilon = 0.1$, compute the critical value $I_{ext,H}$ and the frequency of the small-amplitude limit cycle born at the bifurcation. Explain its physiological meaning. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $V^*_H$.** $$V^{*2}_{H} = 1 - \epsilon\beta = 1 - (0.1)(0.8) = 0.92 \Rightarrow V^{*}_{H} = \pm 0.959 \text{ (dimensionless)}.$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $I_{ext,H}$.** $$I_{ext,H} = -V^{*}_{H} + \tfrac{(V^{*}_{H})^{3}}{3} + \tfrac{V^{*}_{H} + \alpha}{\beta}.$$ With $V^*_H = 0.959$: $$I_{ext,H} = -0.959 + \tfrac{(0.959)^{3}}{3} + \tfrac{0.959 + 0.7}{0.8} = -0.959 + 0.294 + 2.074 = +1.409 \text{ (dimensionless)}.$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Frequency.** The imaginary part of the eigenvalues at Hopf is $$\beta = \sqrt{\Delta - \tau^{2}/4} \approx \sqrt{\epsilon(1 - \beta + \beta V^{*2})} = \sqrt{0.1 \cdot (1 - 0.8 + 0.8 \cdot 0.92)} = \sqrt{0.0836} \approx 0.289.$$ So the period is $T \approx 2\pi / 0.289 \approx 21.7$ time-units (in dimensionless FHN time). **Step 4 ŌĆö Physiological meaning.** $I_{ext} < I_{ext,H}$: the cell is **quiescent** (a stable fixed point). For $I_{ext} > I_{ext,H}$: the cell fires **repetitively** (a stable limit cycle is born). The frequency of the limit cycle grows continuously from zero as $I_{ext}$ increases ŌĆö this is the hallmark of a **supercritical Hopf bifurcation**, mathematically equivalent to **Class-2 excitability** observed in Purkinje fibers. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Distinguish clearly between the **Saddle-Node on an Invariant Circle (SNIC)** bifurcation and the **Hopf bifurcation**. Indicate which one applies to the SA node and which to the Purkinje fiber, and state the corresponding excitability class. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** | Feature | Hopf Bifurcation | SNIC Bifurcation | |---|---|---| | Eigenvalue crossing | Pair $\alpha(\mu) \pm i\beta$ crosses imaginary axis | A real eigenvalue crosses zero | | Onset of firing | Continuous (frequency grows from 0) | Abrupt (all-or-none) | | Frequency at threshold | $f \to 0$ as $\mu \to \mu_c$ | $f \to f_{max}$ at threshold | | Phase-resetting curve | Type 1 (continuous) | Type 1 | | Excitability class | Class 2 (Purkinje) | Class 1 (SA node) | | Geometric picture | Limit cycle shrinks to a point | Limit cycle emerges from heteroclinic connection | | Clinical example | Initiation of re-entrant tachycardia | Abrupt SA arrest | **SA node ŌåÆ SNIC**, **Purkinje ŌåÆ Hopf**, as proven by **Guevara & Jongsma (1992)** in the BeelerŌĆōReuter model. **(b)** For a generic 2D autonomous cardiac oscillator $$\dot{x} = f(x, y, \mu), \quad \dot{y} = g(x, y, \mu)$$ show that the characteristic equation of the linearization at a fixed point is $\lambda^2 - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$, and derive the condition under which a Hopf bifurcation can occur. `[7 Marks]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Linearization.** The Jacobian at the fixed point $(x^*, y^*)$ is $$J = \begin{pmatrix} f_x & f_y \\ g_x & g_y \end{pmatrix},$$ where the partials are evaluated at $(x^*, y^*)$. Linearization gives $\dot{\boldsymbol{\xi}} = J \boldsymbol{\xi}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Characteristic equation.** Setting $\det(J - \lambda I) = 0$: $$(f_x - \lambda)(g_y - \lambda) - f_y g_x = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - (f_x + g_y)\lambda + (f_x g_y - f_y g_x) = 0.$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Identification.** With $\tau = f_x + g_y$ and $\Delta = f_x g_y - f_y g_x$: $$\lambda^{2} - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0, \quad \lambda_{1,2} = \frac{\tau \pm \sqrt{\tau^{2} - 4\Delta}}{2}.$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Hopf condition.** Purely imaginary eigenvalues require $\tau = 0$ and $\Delta > 0$ (so that the discriminant is negative). Transversality requires $d\tau/d\mu \neq 0$ at $\mu = \mu_c$. These together guarantee the existence of a limit cycle near $\mu = \mu_c$ (Hopf's theorem, 1942). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall ŌĆö How Marks Are Lost:** > 1. **Confusing the two bifurcations.** A very common mistake is stating "the SA node exhibits a Hopf bifurcation." This is *wrong*; the SA node is Class-1 (SNIC). > 2. **Forgetting the transversality condition.** Many students write $\tau = 0$ and stop. The full condition is $\tau = 0$, $\Delta > 0$, **and** $d\tau/d\mu \neq 0$. Omitting the third clause costs at least 1 mark. > 3. **Confusing the eigenvalues.** $\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0$ is what gives complex conjugate (oscillatory) eigenvalues ŌĆö students often incorrectly write $\tau^2 > 4\Delta$ for Hopf, which is the condition for a stable node. > 4. **Units.** Always state the bifurcation parameter's *unit* ($\mu$A/cm┬▓) ŌĆö the KTU evaluator deducts ┬Į mark for missing units in numerical answers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Examination** - **Cardiac pacemaker cell** = biological limit-cycle oscillator with **automaticity**. - **Phase 4 depolarization slope** Ōēł slope of $V$-nullcline in the FHN model. - The 2D **FitzHughŌĆōNagumo model** is the canonical reduced form for KTU exams. - **Hopf Bifurcation:** $\tau = 0$, $\Delta > 0$, $d\tau/d\mu \neq 0$ ŌåÆ supercritical ŌåÆ small-amplitude limit cycle. - **SNIC Bifurcation:** real eigenvalue crosses 0, the second remains negative ŌåÆ abrupt, all-or-none firing. - **SA node = SNIC (Class-1)**, **Purkinje = Hopf (Class-2)**. *(Guevara & Jongsma, 1992.)* - **Characteristic equation:** $\lambda^{2} - \tau\lambda + \Delta = 0$. - **Eigenvalues** govern the *local* dynamics; **nullclines** govern the *global* dynamics. - **Time-scale separation** $0 < \epsilon \ll 1$ is what makes the FHN model behave like a cardiac pacemaker. - **Clinical relevance:** $\bar{g}_{K}$ block (Class III drugs) shifts Hopf $\Rightarrow$ APD prolongation; $I_f$ block (ivabradine) shifts SNIC $\Rightarrow$ heart rate reduction. - **Implantable pacemaker engineering** mathematically *implements* a tunable Hopf bifurcation. - **Defibrillation** is a *non-local* maneuver: it pushes the state far from the limit cycle across a bifurcation manifold.
